Antoninus Augustus and sent into readiness for the Germanic expedition to escort by the Danube the supplies for the supply of both armies, overseer of detachments of the Misenian praetorian fleet, also the Ravennate, also the British fleet, also of the elite African and Moorish cavalry for the task of scouting Pannonia, prefect of the I Ala of Aravacorum in the Germanic readiness, honored in person by the Emperor Antoninus Augustus with a horse, phalerai, and arms, because he killed the leader of the Naristae, Valao, with his own hand, and in the same ala obtaining the honor of the fourth military service, prefect of the ala of the Contarii, honored with gifts in the Germanic and Sarmatian wars, overseer of the knights of the Naristae, Quadi, proceeding to avenge the uprising in the East, increased in honor with centenarian rank and salary, he obtained the procuratorship of Lower Moesia, in the same time overseer of detachments and thus sent by the emperor to destroy a band of Briseian brigands on the border of Macedonia and Thrace, procurator of Upper Moesia, procurator of the province of Dacia Porolissensis, selected into the most distinguished order among the praetorians by the most sacred emperors, and then legate of the I Adiutrix legion, also legate of the II Adiutrix legion, overseer of detachments of wintering troops, also legate of the V Macedonica legion, also legate of the I Italica legion, also legate of the XIII Gemina legion, also legate of Augustus as pro praetor [of the III Augusta legion], honored with gifts by the most noble prince Marcus Aurelius Commodus Augustus in the second Germanic expedition, by the distinguished order of Dianensian veterans, through a contribution of bronze.
EDCS-13600417.summary: An exceptionally detailed career summary of Marcus Valerius Maximianus, detailing his extensive military and administrative service under various emperors and in numerous campaigns, culminating in his selection into the senatorial order and high military commands, likely dating to the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-13700376.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto sacrum Marcus Iulius Cai filius Papiria Felicianus vir optimus praefectus pro IIviris quinquennalibus sacerdos Liberi Patris responso monituve eius sua pecunia cum base ceterisque omnibus ponsuit ide<m=N>que victimis athibitis sic dedicavit curantibus Iulio Valerio Gaiano nepote et Rutilia Eugamia coniuge et Flavia Felicissima nuru et Iulia Septimina filia votum solvit libens animo Posita III Idus Maias Perpetuo et Corneliano consulibus \n
EDCS-13700376.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Marcus Iulius Felicianus, son of Caius, of the tribus Papiria, a most excellent man, prefect and acting duovir for five years, priest of Liber Pater, by his response or instruction, placed this bridge with its base and all other things at his own expense, and dedicated it with the appropriate sacrifices. This was done with the care of his grandson Iulius Valerius Gaius, his wife Rutilia Eugamia, his daughter-in-law Flavia Felicissima, and his daughter Iulia Septimina. He fulfilled his vow willingly. Placed on the 3rd day before the Ides of May, during the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelius.
EDCS-13700376.summary: An inscription dedicating a bridge and its associated structures, built by Marcus Iulius Felicianus, likely in honor of Hercules Augustus and the local magistracy, dating to the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelius (likely 237 AD).

EDCS-13700377.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Auggustis sacrum signum Saturisci quem Iulia Urbana somnio praemonita promiserat Quintus Cominius Successus vir eius cum Cominiis Urbano Cominiano Cominia Quintiane sic filis eius reddere curaverunt idemque victimis ath<i=A>bitis sic dedicaverunt votum solverunt libentes animo Perpetuo et Corneliano consulibus III Idus Maias posita \n
EDCS-13700377.translation: Sacred to Liber Pater and Libera Augusta. The statue of Saturiscus, which Iulia Urbana had promised in a dream, her husband Quintus Cominius Successus, along with his sons Cominius Urbanus, Cominianus, and Cominia Quintiana, took care to return, and they dedicated it with the appropriate sacrifices. They fulfilled their vow willingly. Placed on the 3rd day before the Ides of May, during the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelius.
EDCS-13700377.summary: An inscription dedicating a statue of Saturiscus to Liber Pater and Libera Augusta, fulfilled by a family, likely dating to the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelius (likely 237 AD).

EDCS-13800128.summary: A brief dedication to the deified Emperor Aurelian, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-13800133.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Tiberi filio Quirina Gordiano Tyanae ex Cappadocia quaestori provinciae Cypri legato provinciae eiusdem proconsuli provinciae Macedoniae legato legionis XI Claudiae legato legionis III Augustae praefecto aerarii Saturni consuli designato sacerdoti fetiali Publius Iulius Theodorus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae praesidi optimo\n
EDCS-13800133.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Gordianus, son of Tiberius, of the Quirina tribe, from Tyana in Cappadocia, quaestor of the province of Cyprus, legate of the same province, proconsul of the province of Macedonia, legate of the XI Claudia legion, legate of the III Augusta legion, prefect of the treasury of Saturn, designated consul, pontifical priest. Publius Julius Theodorus, (centurion) of the III Augusta legion, erected (this) to the best governor.
EDCS-13800133.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Publius Julius Theodorus to the governor Tiberius Claudius Gordianus, detailing his career, likely erected as a mark of respect by a subordinate officer.

EDCS-13800175.text_edition: \n Fortunae Reduci Augusti divi Alexandri divi Antonini Magni Pii fili divi Pii Severi [nepotis] divi Marci Antonini [pronepotis] divi Pii [et divi Hadriani] abnepotis \n
EDCS-13800175.translation: To Fortune the Bringer-Back of the Emperor, descendant of the divine Alexander, son of the divine Antoninus Magnus Pius, grandson of the divine Pius Severus, great-grandson of the divine Marcus Antoninus, great-great-grandson of the divine Pius and the divine Hadrian.
EDCS-13800175.summary: This is a dedication to Fortuna Redux, listing the divine lineage of the emperors down to Hadrian, likely from the Severan period.

EDCS-13800215.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepote Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate VIII imperatore IIII consule III patre patriae Sextus Iulius Maior legatus Augusti pro praetore patronus coloniae dicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-13800215.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, acclaimed emperor for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country. Sextus Julius Maior, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul, patron of the colony, dedicated (this) by decree of the decurions with public funds.
EDCS-13800215.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Sextus Julius Maior, imperial legate, to Emperor Hadrian, funded by the local council, likely from Hadrian's reign.

EDCS-13800217.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Britanico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis XVIII imperatori III consuli III proconsuli patri patriae a Lambaese sic milia passuum XXVIIII\n
EDCS-13800217.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, conqueror of Parthia, conqueror of Britain, conqueror of Germany, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 18th time, acclaimed emperor for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, father of the country, from Lambaesis, 29 miles.
EDCS-13800217.summary: This inscription records a milestone or marker indicating the distance of 29 miles from Lambaesis, dedicated to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix (Caracalla) during his reign.

EDCS-15000064.inscription_type: tituli fabricationis, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-15000064.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Cai Maximini Pii Felicis Augusti vexillarii legionis III Augustae piae vindicis Maximianae morantes castello Dimmidi sub cura Cai Ponti Aureliani |(centurionis) legionis eiusdem devoti numini eius aram Cerei consecratam V Nonas Maias Quintiano et Severo consulibus curantibus Marco Lisinio sic Cassiano stratore et Lucio Aurelio Emerito tesserario sculpsit Lucius Marius Namphamo immunis\n
EDCS-15000064.translation: To Jupiter, the Best and Greatest, for the safety of our lord Emperor Caesar Gaius Maximinius Pius Felix Augustus. The standard-bearers of the III Augusta legion, devoted and victorious Maximian, stationed at the fort of Dimmidi, under the care of Gaius Pontius Aurelianus, (centurion) of the same legion, consecrated this altar to his divine power on the 5th day before the Nones of May, during the consulship of Quintianus and Severus, with Marcus Lisinius Cassianus, officer of the escort, and Lucius Aurelius Emeritus, tesserarius, overseeing. Lucius Marius Namphamo, exempt from service, carved it.
EDCS-15000064.summary: This is an altar dedicated to Jupiter for the safety of Emperor Maximinus Thrax by soldiers of the III Augusta legion stationed at Dimmidi fort, likely from Maximinus' reign (235-238 AD).

EDCS-15000094.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-15000094.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate X consuli III cohors I Chalcidenorum equitata devotissima ipsi statuam de suo posuit Sexto Iulio Maiore legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-15000094.translation: The very devoted I Chalcidenorum cavalry cohort erected this statue at their own expense to Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 10th time, consul for the 3rd time, to Sextus Julius Maior, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul.
EDCS-15000094.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue by the I Chalcidenorum cavalry cohort to Sextus Julius Maior, the imperial legate, during Hadrian's reign.

EDCS-15000095.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVI consul III pater patriae Lucio Vario Ambibulo legato Augusti pro praetore legionis III Augustae\n
EDCS-15000095.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, (writes) to Lucius Varius Ambibulus, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul, of the III Augusta legion.
EDCS-15000095.summary: This inscription is likely a rescript or letter from Emperor Hadrian to Lucius Varius Ambibulus, legate of the III Augusta legion, dating to Hadrian's reign.

EDCS-15200105.text_edition: \n Iovi conservatori fortissimorum Imperatorum Severi et Antonini et Getae Augustorum et Iuliae Augustae matris castrorum et Auggustorum Quintus Cornelius Valens legatus eorum pro praetore posuit\n
EDCS-15200105.translation: Quintus Cornelius Valens, their legate with the power of a proconsul, set up (this) to Jupiter the preserver of the most valiant Emperors Severus and Antoninus and Geta Augustus, and Julia Augusta, mother of the camps, and the Augusti.
EDCS-15200105.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Conservator by Quintus Cornelius Valens, legate, for the well-being of the Severan emperors and Julia Domna, likely during the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15200108.text_edition: \n ORI X Kalendas Martias Claudio Iuliano II et Bruttio Crispino consulibus\n
EDCS-15200108.translation: On the 10th day before the Kalends of March, during the consulship of Claudius Julianus II and Bruttius Crispinus.
EDCS-15200108.summary: This is a dating clause, recording the consuls for the year, likely serving as a reference point for another inscription or event.

EDCS-15200110.text_edition: \n Deo numini Malagbelo pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Invicti Pii Felicis Augusti divi Magni Antonini fili et Iuliae Mamaeae Augustae matris Augusti et [castrorum ------\n
EDCS-15200110.translation: To the god, the divine Malagbel, for the safety of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the divine Magnus Antoninus, and Julia Mamaea Augusta, mother of the emperor and [of the camps ------
EDCS-15200110.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Malagbel for the safety of Emperor Severus Alexander and his mother Julia Mamaea, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15200125.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Agrippa Themi filius Palmyra qui fuit |(centurio) cohortis III Thracum Syria item translatus in cohortem I Chalcidenorum iusso sic Imperatoris curam egit Palmyrenorum sagittariorum nnorum X militavit nnos XXIII vixit annos LV es libertus et procurator\n
EDCS-15200125.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Agrippa, son of Themi from Palmyra, who served as a centurion in the III Thracum cohort and was transferred to the I Chalcidenorum cohort by imperial order, oversaw the Palmyrene archers. He served 10 years in the [army], served 23 years, and lived 55 years. He was [a freedman] and procurator.
EDCS-15200125.summary: A funerary inscription for Agrippa, a former centurion and procurator, detailing his military service and lifespan.

EDCS-15300545.text_edition: \n Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati filio patrono Publius Iulius Cai filius Papiria Iunianus\n
EDCS-15300545.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Macro Saturninus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macro, governor with the rank of propraetor, and consul-designate, his patron, Publius Iulius Iunianus, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe.
EDCS-15300545.summary: An honorific inscription dedicating a monument to Marcus Aemilius Macro Saturninus, likely by his client or a beneficiary of his patronage.

EDCS-15600011.text_edition: \n Silvano sancto Marcus Porcius Iustus praefectus legionis III Augustae posuit Dedicata Idibus Ianuariis Bruttio Praesente II et Condiano consulibus\n
EDCS-15600011.translation: Marcus Porcius Iustus, prefect of the III Augusta legion, erected this to the holy Silvanus. Dedicated on the Ides of January, with Bruttius Praesens II and Condianus as consuls.
EDCS-15600011.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus by Marcus Porcius Iustus, prefect of a legion, dating to January during the consulship of Bruttius Praesens II and Condianus.

EDCS-15900105.summary: A very brief inscription identifying Rogatus as a servant of God, possibly indicating early Christian affiliation.

EDCS-16000488.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico maximo Britannico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis XVII imperatori III patri patriae consuli IIII procons(uli) a Calceo milia passuum III\n
EDCS-16000488.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, the greatest conqueror of Parthia, Britain, and Germany, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 17th time, acclaimed emperor for the 3rd time, father of the country, consul for the 4th time, proconsul, 3 miles from Calceus.
EDCS-16000488.summary: An milestone inscription marking a distance of 3 miles, dedicated to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus with a full list of his titles and honors.

EDCS-16100225.text_edition: \n Deo Soli ortum sic constitutum sic per Caio sic Iulio sic Aelurione sic |(centurionem) legionis III Augustae Antoninianae praeposito numeri Hemesenorum\n
EDCS-16100225.translation: To the Sun God, established thus and dedicated by Caius Iulius Aelurio, a centurion of the III Augusta Antoniniana legion, prefect of the Hemeseni auxiliary unit.
EDCS-16100225.summary: A dedication to the Sun God, initiated by Caius Iulius Aelurio, a centurion and prefect.

EDCS-16100379.text_edition: \n Matri Deum Magnae Ideae Augustae sacrum pro salute [] [] Hortensia Fortunata sacerdos tauribolium sic et criobolium motum sancit mandante Marco Tullio Pudente patre\n
EDCS-16100379.translation: Sacred to the Great Mother of the Gods, Cybele Augusta. Hortensia Fortunata, priestess, established the bull sacrifice and ram sacrifice, by order of Marcus Tullius Pudens, father.
EDCS-16100379.summary: A religious dedication for the well-being of the indicated individuals, involving sacrifices, commissioned by Hortensia Fortunata, priestess, and her father.

EDCS-16100444.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepote divi Nervae pronepote pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XII consule III patre patriae dedicante Lucio Novio Crispino Martiale Saturnino legato Augusti pro praetore legionis III Augustae consule designato patrono municipi\n
EDCS-16100444.translation: During the reign of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 12th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, dedicated by Lucius Novius Crispinus Martialis Saturninus, governor with the rank of imperial propraetor of the III Augusta legion, consul-designate, patron of the municipality.
EDCS-16100444.summary: An inscription commemorating the dedication of a monument under Emperor Antoninus Pius, with extensive titles for the emperor and details about the dedicating official, Lucius Novius Crispinus Martialis Saturninus.

EDCS-16200714.text_edition: \n Deo Patrio Libero Patri conservatori dominorum nn[nostrorum] Augg[gustorum] Quintus Ranius Cassianus tribunus militum legionis III Augustae votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-16200714.translation: To the Native God Liber Pater, protector of our lords, the Augusti. Quintus Ranius Cassianus, tribune of the soldiers of the III Augusta legion, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16200714.summary: A votive inscription by Quintus Ranius Cassianus, a military tribune, dedicating a monument to the god Liber Pater.

EDCS-16200900.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Augusti filio Caesari consuli II decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-16200900.translation: To Marcus Aurelius, son of the Emperor, Caesar, consul for the 2nd time, by decree of the town council, at public expense.
EDCS-16200900.summary: A public dedication to Marcus Aurelius, son of the emperor and Caesar, for his second consulship, funded by the municipality.

EDCS-16201575.text_edition: \n Pro salute et incolumitate Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Augusti Caius Iulius Lepidus Tertullus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul designatus per legionem III Augustam piam vindicem \n
EDCS-16201575.translation: For the safety and well-being of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, Gaius Julius Lepidus Tertullus, imperial legate with proconsular power, consul-designate, on behalf of the Third Augustan Legion, the Pious, the Victorious.
EDCS-16201575.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Gaius Julius Lepidus Tertullus, legate of the province of Numidia, likely in honor of Emperor Septimius Severus, possibly dating to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-16201647.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae sanctae Victoriae Herculi Augusto Genio loci dis deabusque omnibus Marcus Aurelius Decimus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae ex principe peregrinorum votum solvit\n
EDCS-16201647.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Juno Regina, Minerva the holy, Hercules Augustus, the Genius of the place, and all the gods and goddesses, Marcus Aurelius Decimus, a most distinguished man and governor of the province of Numidia, formerly chief centurion of the peregrine troops, paid his vow.
EDCS-16201647.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to the Capitoline Triad and other deities by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, governor of Numidia, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16201702.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto sacrum pro salute dddominorum nnnostrorum Augggustorum Caius Egrilius Fuscianus beneficiarius Subatiani Proculi legati Augggustorum pro praetore consulis designati adiutor principis praetori scribatu Horati Viatoris et Didi Aprilis Posita pridie Nonas Apriles Faustino et Rufino consulibus\n
EDCS-16201702.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus, for the safety of our lords Emperors Augustus, Gaius Egrilius Fuscinus, a *beneficiarius* of Subatianus Proculus, legate of the emperors with proconsular power, consul-designate, an aide to the *princeps* in the praetorian cohort, scribe of Horatius Viator and Didius Aprilis. Erected on the day before the Nones of April, when Faustinus and Rufinus were consuls.
EDCS-16201702.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Mars Augustus made by Gaius Egrilius Fuscinus, an official in the service of a legate, for the safety of the emperors, dating to 210 AD.

EDCS-16201720.text_edition: \n Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore filio stratores\n
EDCS-16201720.translation: For Marcus Aemilius Macro Dinarchus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macro, legate of the emperor with proconsular power, the *stratores*.
EDCS-16201720.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the *stratores* (orderlies or orderlies) for Marcus Aemilius Macro Dinarchus, son of a former imperial legate, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16201721.text_edition: \n Tiberio Iulio Pollieno Auspici legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro consuli Caius Publilius Septiminus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae Antoninianae candidatus eius\n
EDCS-16201721.translation: For Tiberius Julius Pollienus Auspex, clarissimus vir, legate of the emperor with proconsular power, consul, Gaius Publilius Septiminus, centurion of the Third Augustan Antoninian Legion, his candidate.
EDCS-16201721.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Gaius Publilius Septiminus, a centurion, for Tiberius Julius Pollienus Auspex, a former imperial legate and consul, likely dating to the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16201727.text_edition: \n Caio Pomponio Magno legato Augg(ustorum) pro praetore clarissimo viro consuli patrono inconparabili sic Publius Geminius Gallonianus beneficiarius domicurator promotus ab eo\n
EDCS-16201727.translation: For Gaius Pomponius Magnus, clarissimus vir, legate of the emperors with proconsular power, consul, our incomparable patron, thus Publius Geminius Gallonianus, a *beneficiarius* and *domicurator*, promoted by him.
EDCS-16201727.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Publius Geminius Gallonianus, an official serving under Gaius Pomponius Magnus, a former imperial legate and patron, likely dating to the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16202031.text_edition: \n Marco Aemilio Macro Dinarcho Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore filio stratores \n
EDCS-16202031.translation: For Marcus Aemilius Macro Dinarchus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macro, legate of the emperor with proconsular power, the *stratores*.
EDCS-16202031.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the *stratores* (orderlies) for Marcus Aemilius Macro Dinarchus, son of a former imperial legate, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16300322.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto sacrum pro salute et victoria Impperatorum Caessarum Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Pii et Marci Aureli Antonini Auggustorum et Iuliae Augustae matris Augusti Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro in honorem Publi Aeli Quinti fili Meonia Menecratiani Zita ex |(centurione) legionis honesta missione patris sui permittente eo Publius Aelius Menecrates Florus eques primus pilus flamen perpetuus patriae suae donum dedit numero III\n
EDCS-16300322.translation: Sacred to Hercules the Unconquered, for the safety and victory of the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor. To Quintus Anicius Faustus, clarissimus vir, legate of the emperors with proconsular power, in honor of Publius Aelius Quintus' son, Meonia Menecratiani Zita, formerly a centurion of the legion, with his father's honorable discharge, and with his permission, Publius Aelius Menecrates Florus, a Roman knight, chief centurion, perpetual priest of his fatherland, gave this gift, numbering three.
EDCS-16300322.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Hercules by Publius Aelius Menecrates Florus, a Roman knight and former centurion, for the safety and victory of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, and Empress Julia Domna, under the legateship of Quintus Anicius Faustus, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16400138.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Quinti Iuni Rustici praefecti urbis viri clarissimi\n
EDCS-16400138.translation: By the authority of Quintus Iunius Rusticus, clarissimus vir, prefect of the city.
EDCS-16400138.summary: This inscription records a decree or action taken by Quintus Iunius Rusticus, prefect of the city of Rome, likely dating to the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-16400403.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo votum rettuli Genio Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Claudi Invicti Pii Felicis Augusti Aelius Dubitatus miles cohortis VIIII praetoriae centuria Etrii annis VIIII gessi stationem Veneria Rusicade salvis et felicibus commanipulis faciendum curavi\n
EDCS-16400403.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, I have paid my vow. To the Genius of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, Aelius Dubitatus, a soldier of the 9th praetorian cohort, of the century of Etrusius, served on duty for 9 years. I have seen to its being made while my comrades are safe and well.
EDCS-16400403.summary: This inscription records a vow made by Aelius Dubitatus, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard, to Jupiter and the Genius of Emperor Claudius Gothicus, likely dating to his reign (268-270 AD).

EDCS-16400585.text_edition: \n Haec facilis patet aula sanctis ingrediens fabre factum parvis sumptibus videbis opus nam pater Secundus operam navavit si quis CTV facile putarit si potis est melius faxit\n
EDCS-16400585.translation: This hall stands accessible to the holy as they enter; you will see skillfully made work at little expense, for Father Secundus offered his service. If anyone thinks CTV easy, let him do better if he can.
EDCS-16400585.summary: A short, somewhat boastful epigram, likely inscribed on a building or a public work, praising the craftsmanship and inexpensive construction by Secundus.

EDCS-16400589.text_edition: \n Iovi omnipotenti sacrum Quintus Rufilius Quinti filius Arnensi Saturninus aedilis augur praefectus pro IIviris statuam quam ob honorem auguratus sui super legitimam ex SS V milibus nummum cum base promiserat ampliata pecunia posuit dedicavitque curantibus Rutilis Montano fratre et Ianuario et Saturnino filiis eius Mamertino et Rufo consulibus Kalendis Decembribus\n
EDCS-16400589.translation: Sacred to Almighty Jupiter. Quintus Rufilius Saturninus, son of Quintus, of the Arnensian tribe, aedile, augur, prefect, and acting quaestor, had promised a statue for his augurship, in addition to the legal requirement, costing 5,000 sesterces with its base. With augmented funds, he erected and dedicated it, with his brother Rutilius Montanus and his sons Rutilius Ianuarius and Rutilius Saturninus acting as supervisors, during the consulship of Mamertinus and Rufus, on the Kalends of December.
EDCS-16400589.summary: This is a dedication inscription by Quintus Rufilius Saturninus, a local magistrate and priest, recording the erection of a statue to Jupiter, funded by himself and his family, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16400629.text_edition: \n Pro felicitate et incolumitate saeculi dominorum nnn(ostrorum) Auggg(ustorum) Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Parthici Britannici Germanici maximi Auggustorum et Iuliae Augustae matris Augusti nostri castrorumque tubicines legionis III Augustae piae vindicis quorum nomina subiecta sunt Antonius Proculus optio Valerius Felix princeps Aemilius Crescens Flavius Felix Annaeus Priscus Tuccius Fortunatus Gellius Rogatus Iulius Messianus Flavius Iulianus ilius Primus ndicius Sallustianus Valerius Castus ius Speratus Manilius Saturninus ius Fortunatus lius Venustus Caius Iulius Victor Antistius Successus Quintus Granius Crispinus Quintus Fabius Donatus Caelius Faustus Postumius Urbanus Caius Annius Lucius Geminius Marcus Valerius Quintus Sulpicius Rutilius Caius Vereius I Caius Secundius Lucius Cassius Fortunatus Flavius Rogatus Iulius Datus Pompeius Datus Domitius Ci Servilius Aurelius D Gellius Rosinus Caius Domitius Valens Aurelius Bassus Scamnari nomine dabunt collegae qui facti fuerint |(denarios) DCCL anulari nomine instituimus veteranis missione accepta in singulos |(denarios) D vel si quis de collegis profecerit per ordinem accipiet |(denarios) D item si quis de collegis tramare profecerit cum praescripto accipiet viaticum pro merito |(denarios) CC eque arca D\n
EDCS-16400629.translation: For the prosperity and safety of the age of our lords, the Augusti. Tubicines (trumpeters) of the III Augusta Pia Vindex Legion, whose names are listed below, will contribute 775 denarii in total for the ring-bearer's office. We have established for veterans, upon acceptance of their discharge, 500 denarii each. And if any of the colleagues advances to a higher position, he will receive 500 denarii in order. Likewise, if any of the colleagues advances further by promotion, he will receive travel expenses according to his merit for 200 denarii, with a written order. And from the treasury, 500 denarii.
EDCS-16400629.summary: This inscription details a financial arrangement made by the trumpeters of the III Augusta Legion to provide retirement bonuses for veterans and stipends for those who advance in rank, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-16500235.summary: A very brief inscription recording the name and military affiliation of Donatus, likely a Christian soldier from the Roman army, probably dating to the 3rd or 4th century CE.

EDCS-16600326.text_edition: \n Deo Plutoni Augusto sacrum pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris [Publi Li]cini Egnati Gallieni Augusti Caius Valerius Valentinus templum modicum antiqua vetustate dilapsum ampliato spatio columnis et regiis duabus picturis ornatum pecunia sua ex HS LXVII milibus domini nostri a solo coeptum perfecit et deo victimis redditis et popularibus epulo exhibito statuto etiam perpetuo epulo annuo sacerdotibus dedicavit cura agentibus Caio Lollio Honorato et Quinto Septimio Vitale sacerdotibus faciendum curavit AC Dedicatum VI Kalendas Ianuarias Valeriano et Lucillo consulibus\n
EDCS-16600326.translation: Sacred to the god Pluto Augustus for the safety of our lord, the Emperor Caesar [Publius Licinius] Gallienus Augustus. Caius Valerius Valentinus, having expanded the space and adorned it with columns and two royal paintings, completed the modest temple, which had fallen into ruin through ancient decay, at his own expense, starting from the ground up with 67,000 sesterces. He dedicated it to the god, having provided sacrifices and a public feast, also establishing a perpetual annual feast for the priests. He saw to its construction under the care of the priests Caius Lollus Honoratus and Quintus Septimius Vitalis. Dedicated on the 6th day before the Kalends of January during the consulship of Valerian and Lucillus.
EDCS-16600326.summary: This is a dedication inscription by Caius Valerius Valentinus for a temple to Pluto Augustus, funded by himself, and likely dating to the reign of Gallienus (253-268 CE) as indicated by the consul names and emperor.

EDCS-16700102.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lila Mastucaranis Musulamius vixit annis LXXXX dedicantibus Iusto Iovino NABDADE\n
EDCS-16700102.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lila, daughter of Mastucaranis, the Musulamian, lived 90 years. Dedicated by Justus Iovinus.
EDCS-16700102.summary: A tomb inscription for Lila, a woman of likely North African origin (Musulamian), recording her name, parentage, ethnicity, and age, and the dedicator, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16700128.text_edition: \n Ex auctoritate Imperatoris Caesaris divi Nervae <f=I>(ilii) Nervae Traiani optimi Augusti Germanici Dacici Parthici fines adsignati genti Suburburum per Titum Sabinium Barbarum legatum Augusti pro praetore \n
EDCS-16700128.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Nerva, Nerva Trajan Optimus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus Parthicus, the boundaries have been assigned to the Suburburi tribe by Titus Sabinius Barbarus, legate of the Emperor with praetorian power.
EDCS-16700128.summary: An official inscription recording the formal assignment of boundaries to the Suburburi tribe by a Roman legate under Emperor Trajan, likely in the early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-16800207.text_edition: \n Imperatoribus Caessaribus Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Arabico Adiabenico et Marco Aurelio Antonino Britannico maximo Auggustis et Iuliae Augustae matri Augusti nostri et castrorum dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto consulari armorum custodes ob solemnitatem decreverunt ex arca sua veteranis qui de eodem collegio dimittentur anulari nummum singulis |(denarios) millenos et quingenos et qui ad uberiorem locum se transtulerint singulis |(denarios) millenos Pomponius Privatus Iulius Saturninus Flavius Donatus Claudius Secundus Sertorius Felicianus Sallustius Saturninus Mattius Castus Valerius Felix Antistius Saturus Umbrius Felix Tonneius Certus Valerius Honoratus Iulius Crescentianus Titienius Pius Caecilius Rogatus Baebius Speratus Maevius Candidus Coponius Crescens Tannonius Victor Caelius Fortunatus Papinius Salvius Marius Domitianus Claudius Rogatus Pescennius Primus nnius Thevestinus Figilius Felix Annius Victor Ammius Iucundus Iulius Quintianus Sittius Sittianus Iulius Asper Valerius Terentianus Fullonius Paulus Valerius Felix Areius Saturninus Iulius Victor Sallustius Victor Aelius Donatus Iulius Romanus Fabius Saturninus Publius Marcius Marcellus Blaesius Rufus Vatinius Fabianus Porcius Cirrenianus Aemilius Macrinus Sextilius Secundus Caesennius Alexander Percennius Donatus Iulius Vitalis Fabius Nampulus Granius Geminus Calicius Ianuarius Valerius Saturninus Pinarius Maximus Iulius Fundanius Egnatius Bonosus Iulius Andromachus Etereius Primus Cornelius Marinus Ancarius Vitalis Annius Martialis Lartidius Emeritus dedicatum Severo et Victorino\n
EDCS-16800207.translation: For the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Britannicus Maximus Augusti, and for Julia Augusta, mother of our Augustus and of the camps, the guardians of the arms (collegium armorum custodes) have decreed, with Faustus presiding, that veterans who are discharged from the same college shall receive 1,500 denarii each, and those who transfer to a more lucrative position shall receive 1,000 denarii each. Pomponius Privatus and many others are listed. Dedicated under Severus and Victorinus.
EDCS-16800207.summary: This inscription records a financial provision for veterans from the college of 'guardians of the arms', likely a military association, with a decree made under the Severan emperors, specifying retirement benefits.

EDCS-16800208.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aurelli sic Severi Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti et Iuliae Mamaeae matri Augusti et Iuliae Maesae avi[ae] eius armorum custodes legionis III Augustae Aemilius Honoratus Herennius Claudianus Iulius Agrippianus Etereius Victor Flavius Claudianus Iulius Saturninus Servilius Alexander Iulius Fabricianus Domitius Datus Terentius Saturninus Iulius Gratus Allius Iulianus Umbilius Dextrianus Scribonius Pla<t=I>o Sallustius Dexter Aurelius Gaudinus Iulius Vitalis Minucius Fortun Servilius Donatus Sallus<t=I>ius Securus Lucilius Saturninus Iulius Sulla Octavius Martialis Vettius Iustus Terentius Antonianus Areius Victor Iulius Celer Iulius Felicianus Antonius Silvanus Caius Sittius Avitus Manilius Victor Aurelius Saturus Iulius Victor Geminius Peregrinus Paccuvius Honoratus Cornelius Vincentius Iulius Maximus Iulius Victorinus Passenius Vitalis Iulius Saturninus Iulius Sedianus Bruttius Ianuarius Fiscilius Profuturus Sittius Victorinus Tettius Rufus Flavius Iustus Flavius Donatus Iulius Donatus Crepereius Silvanus Bassilius Lucianus Iulius Secundus Mattius Felix Sulpicius Valentinus Ge Ianuarius Alfius Datullus Tad Optatus Sallustius Victor Iulius Herennianus Numisius Pudens Arra Primus Nonius Buturarius Minucius Felix Cornelius Cumasius Volusius Optatus \n
EDCS-16800208.translation: To Minerva Augusta, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, and Julia Mamaea, mother of Augustus, and Julia MAesa, his grandmother. The guardians of the arms (collegium armorum custodes) of the III Augusta Legion, including Aemilius Honoratus and many others, are listed. Dedicated.
EDCS-16800208.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Minerva Augusta by the 'guardians of the arms' collegium of the III Augusta Legion, for the welfare of Emperor Severus Alexander and his mother and grandmother, likely dating to his reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-16800303.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-16800303.text_edition: \n Mensa Casti et Flori martures sic\n
EDCS-16800303.translation: A table of Castus and Florus, martyrs, as follows.
EDCS-16800303.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription referring to a 'table' (possibly a list or account) belonging to two martyrs named Castus and Florus, likely dating to the period of Christian persecution.

EDCS-16800380.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino legato Augusti pro praetore Titus Aemilius Capitolinus \n
EDCS-16800380.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Titus Aemilius Capitolinus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, [dedicated this].
EDCS-16800380.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Titus Aemilius Capitolinus, a legate, under the reign of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Caracalla), dated to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-17000022.text_edition: \n Divo Caro Genio tribuniciali Quintus Flavius Balbus tribunus laticlavius militum legionis III Augustae piae vindicis\n
EDCS-17000022.translation: Quintus Flavius Balbus, a broad-striped tribune of the soldiers of the Third Augustan Legion, piously victorious, [dedicated this] to the deified Caro, in his tribunician power.
EDCS-17000022.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the deified Emperor Carus by Quintus Flavius Balbus, a high-ranking officer (tribune) of the Third Augustan Legion, likely dating to the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-18300270.translation: Titus Flavius Bitus, an equestrian of the II Gemella Thracum Cohort, lived 55 years, served 27 years. Iulia Marcella, his sister, and Bitus, his son, made this.
EDCS-18300270.summary: This epitaph records the life and military service of Titus Flavius Bitus, an equestrian soldier in a Thracian cohort, who died at the age of 55 after 27 years of service; it was erected by his sister and son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100184.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate V consuli VII sic patri patriae Publius Metilius Secundus legatus Augusti pro praetore patronus coloniae dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-20100184.translation: Publius Metilius Secundus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, patron of the colony, dedicated this by decree of the decurions and public funds, to the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontiff, holder of tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 7th time, father of the country.
EDCS-20100184.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a public monument by Publius Metilius Secundus, a legate and patron of the colony, to Emperor Hadrian, dated to Hadrian's reign (117-138 CE) based on his consular and tribunician accreditations.

EDCS-20100218.text_edition: \n Publio Iulio Iuniano Martialiano clarissimo viro consuli legato Augustorum pro praetore provinciae Numidiae proconsuli provinciae Macedoniae praefecto aerari militaris curatori viae Clodiae praetoriae tribuno plebei sic quaestori provinciae Asiae patrono coloniae et municipi res publica coloniae Thamugadensium decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-20100218.translation: The republic of the colony of Thamugadi, by decree of the decurions, [dedicated this] to Publius Iulius Iunianus Martialis, most distinguished man, consul, legate of the emperors with praetorian power of the province of Numidia, proconsul of the province of Macedonia, prefect of the military treasury, curator of the Clodia and Praetorian roads, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Asia, patron of the colony and municipality.
EDCS-20100218.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the colony of Thamugadi to Publius Iulius Iunianus Martialis, honoring his distinguished career as a provincial governor and various high offices, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300015.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici fili divi Commodi fratris divi Antonini nepotis divi Hadriani pronepotis divi Traiani Parthici abnepotis divi Nervae adnepotis Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi fortissimique principis pontificis maximi tribuniciae potestatis VII imperatoris XI consulis II patris patriae proconsulis et Imperatoris Caesaris Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi felicissimique principis fili divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepotis divi Antonini pronepotis divi Hadriani abnepotis divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepotis [] tribuniciae potestatis bis proconsulis Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi fortissimi felicissimique principis iuventutis Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti [fratris Publi Septimi Getae nobilissimi Caesaris Augusti] dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augg[gustorum] pro praetore consule amplissimo patrono coloniae et Saevinio Proculo tribuno laticlavio curatore rei publicae decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-20300015.translation: For the health of Emperor Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici son of divi Commodi brother of divi Antonini grandson of divi Hadriani great-grandson of divi Traiani Parthici great-great-grandson of divi Nervae Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, most valiant prince, pontiff, holder of tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, proconsul; and of Emperor Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici son of divi Commodi brother of divi Antonini grandson of divi Hadriani great-grandson of divi Traiani Parthici and divi Nervae great-great-grandson Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, most fortunate prince, holder of tribunician power twice, proconsul Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus; and of Emperor Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici son of divi Commodi brother of divi Antonini grandson of divi Hadriani great-grandson of divi Traiani Parthici Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, most valiant and fortunate prince, Caesar, and of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, most valiant and fortunate prince, of the youth, Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus [brother of Publius Septimius Geta, most noble Caesar Augustus], dedicated by Quintus Anicius Faustus, most noble legate of the emperors, proconsul, patron of the colony, and by Saevinus Proculus, broad-striped tribune, curator of the state, by decree of the decurions and public funds.
EDCS-20300015.summary: This extensive dedication for the health of multiple emperors, including Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, was made by Quintus Anicius Faustus, a legate and patron, and Saevinus Proculus, a curator, likely during the co-rule of Septimius Severus' sons, early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300016.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pertinaci Augusto Pio Felici fortissimoque principi Arabico Adiabenico dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augusti pro praetore designato consule clarissimo viro possessori vici Lambafundensium faciendum curaverunt pecunia conlata quorum nomina at latus basis inscripta sunt Laterano et Rufino consulibus\n
EDCS-20300016.translation: Dedicated to Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Pius Felix, most valiant prince, Arabicus Adiabenicus, by Quintus Anicius Faustus, designated legate of the emperor, most distinguished man, owner of the vicus Lambafundensium. Laterano and Rufino being consuls, they took care to make this, with contributions from those whose names are inscribed on the side of the base.
EDCS-20300016.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Emperor Septimius Severus by Quintus Anicius Faustus, a legate and landowner, with contributions from local residents, dated by the consulship of Laterano and Rufino in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300044.text_edition: \n N Nonas Maias Maximo II et io Urbano consulibus milites numeri Palmyrenorum fecerunt Fausto tesserario leggionis! supra scriptae miles legionis supra scriptae scribsit! et sculpsit\n
EDCS-20300044.translation: On the 4th day before the Nones of May, during the second consulship of Maximo and Urbano. The soldiers of the Palmyrene cohort made this. Faustus, a tessararius of the aforementioned legion, a soldier of the aforementioned legion, wrote and carved [this].
EDCS-20300044.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by soldiers of the Palmyrene cohort on May 11th, during the consulship of Maximo II and Urbano, likely in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300045.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatorum Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Publi Septimi Getae totiusque domus divinae et victoria Quinti Anici Fausti legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati vexillatio legionis III Augustae piae vindicis morantes in procinctu sub cura Fontei Fortunati decurionis alae I nnoniorum Severo et Pompeiano consulibus Numisius Gemellus consulibus supra scriptis Caecilius Lucianus Flacco et Gallo consulibus Marcus An Saturninus Pisone et Iuliano consulibus Longinius Triton Pollione et Apro consulibus Iulius Verecundus Imperatore et Vero consulibus Valerius \n
EDCS-20300045.translation: For the health of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Publius Septimius Geta, and of the entire divine house, and for victory, the vexillation of the Third Augustan Legion, piously victorious, stationed in readiness, under the supervision of Fonteius Fortunatus, decurion of the First Ala of the Nonian cavalry. Numisius Gemellus and Caecilius Lucianus and Marcus An Saturninus and Longinius Triton and Iulius Verecundus and Valerius, being consuls [names repeated for different consul pairs and dates].
EDCS-20300045.summary: This complex inscription records a prayer for the health and victory of Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, by a detachment of the Third Augustan Legion under the command of Fonteius Fortunatus, with multiple consular dating formulas suggesting a period of several years in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300061.translation: For Victory, August, for the health of our lords Valerian and Gallienus, Augusti, Pii Felices, most fortunate, the soldiers of the Legion III Augusta Pia Vindex, restored from Raetia, having returned on the 11th day before the Kalends of November, Volusiano II and Maximo being consuls, paid their vow through Marcus Flavius Valens, centurion of the aforementioned legion. Lucius Volumnius Crescens, optio principis; Marcus Aurelius Licinius, optio; Caius Geminius Victor, optio. Donatus carved and wrote [this].
EDCS-20300061.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by soldiers of the Third Augustan Legion for the health of Emperors Valerian and Gallienus, upon their return from Raetia, dated to October 22nd, during the consulship of Volusianus II and Maximo, in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300067.text_edition: \n Imperatores Caesares Marcus Aurelius Antoninus et Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus Germanici Sarmatici fortissimi amphitheatrum vetustate corruptum a solo restituerunt per cohortem VI Commagenorum Aulo Iulio Pompilio Pisone Laevillo legato Augustorum pro praetore curante Aelio Sereno praefecto\n
EDCS-20300067.translation: Emperors Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Commodus Augustus, conquerors of the Germans and Sarmatians, restored the amphitheater, corrupted by age, from its foundations, through the VI Cohort of Commageni, while Aulus Julius Pompilius Piso Laevillus, Legate of the Augusti Pro Praetore, supervised, with Aelius Serenus as prefect.
EDCS-20300067.summary: This inscription records the restoration of an amphitheater by Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Commodus, overseen by their legate Piso Laevillus and prefect Aelius Serenus, likely in the late 2nd century CE.

EDCS-20300073.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Antonino Augusto burgum speculatorum Antoninianorum Marcus Valerius Senecio legatus eius pro praetore clarissimus vir fieri iussit curam agente Caio Iulio Aelurione |(centurione) legionis III Augustae Antoninianae praeposito numero Hemesenorum Antoniniano\n
EDCS-20300073.translation: Marcus Valerius Senecio, his legate, a man of the highest distinction, ordered the Antoninian fort of the spearmen to be built for Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Augustus, with Gaius Julius Aelurio, a centurion of Legio III Augusta Antoniniana, who was in charge of the Antoninian contingent of Hemesenorum, supervising.
EDCS-20300073.summary: This inscription records the construction of a fort for the spearmen by Marcus Valerius Senecio, legate of Emperor Caracalla, around 212-217 CE.

EDCS-20300074.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Commodo Antonino Pio Felice Augusto Germanico Sarmatico Britannico patre patriae tribunicia potestate XIII consule V burgum Commodianum speculatorium inter duas vias ad salutem commeantium nova tutela constitui iussit Tiberius Claudius Gordianus vir clarissimus legatus Augusti pro praetore cura agente \n
EDCS-20300074.translation: For Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, conqueror of the Germans, Sarmatians, and Britons, Father of the Country, in his 13th year of tribunician power and 5th consulship, Tiberius Claudius Gordianus, a man of the highest distinction, legate of the Augustus Pro Praetore, ordered the Commodian fort of the spearmen, situated between two roads, to be established as a new protection for the safety of travelers, while overseeing.
EDCS-20300074.summary: This inscription records the establishment of a fort for spearmen by Tiberius Claudius Gordianus, legate of Emperor Commodus, around 185-192 CE.

EDCS-20300078.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto sacrum Claudius Gordianus legatus Augusti pro praetore restituit\n
EDCS-20300078.translation: Claudius Gordianus, legate of the Augustus Pro Praetore, restored this [shrine] sacred to Silvanus Augustus.
EDCS-20300078.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a shrine to Silvanus Augustus by Claudius Gordianus, legate of the emperor, likely during the reign of Commodus.

EDCS-20300080.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXI imperatori II consuli IIII patri patriae Lucio Matuccio Fuscino legato Augusti pro praetore legio III Augustae\n
EDCS-20300080.translation: For Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, in his 21st year of tribunician power and 4th consulship, Father of the Country, Lucius Matuccio Fuscinus, legate of the Augustus Pro Praetore, [and] Legio III Augusta.
EDCS-20300080.summary: This inscription is dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius, listing his titles and powers, and mentions Legio III Augusta and its legate Lucius Matuccio Fuscinus, dating to 158 CE.

EDCS-20300108.text_edition: \n Genio legionis III Augustae piae vindicis pro salute Impperatorum Caessarum Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Felicis Parthici Britannici Germanici maximi Augusti et Iuliae Augustae matris Augusti nostri et castrorum dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro consule designato Titus Arranius Datus signifer ex HS III milibus nummum de suo posuit\n
EDCS-20300108.translation: For the Genius of Legio III Augusta, the pious avenger, for the safety of our Lords Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Felix, conqueror of Parthia, Britain, and Germany, and Julia Augusta, mother of our Lord and of the camp, Titus Arranius Datus, a standard-bearer, has set this up from his own funds of 3000 sesterces, with Quintus Anicius Faustus, Legate of the Augusti Pro Praetore, a man of the highest distinction, consul-designate, presiding.
EDCS-20300108.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Genius of Legio III Augusta for the safety of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla by a standard-bearer, under the supervision of legate Quintus Anicius Faustus, likely around 202-210 CE.

EDCS-20300109.text_edition: \n Genio Lambaesis pro salute Impperatorum Caessarum Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Augusti et Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Felicis Parthici Britannici Germanici maximi Augusti et Iuliae Augustae matri Augusti nostri et castrorum dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro consule designato Lucius Baebius Faustianus signifer legionis III Augustae piae vindicis Luci Baebi Felicis veterani ex signifero filius votum solvit\n
EDCS-20300109.translation: For the Genius of Lambaesis, for the safety of our Lords Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Felix, conqueror of Parthia, Britain, and Germany, and Julia Augusta, mother of our Lord and of the camp, Lucius Baebius Faustianus, a standard-bearer of Legio III Augusta, the pious avenger, son of Lucius Baebius Felix, a veteran standard-bearer, has fulfilled his vow, with Quintus Anicius Faustus, Legate of the Augusti Pro Praetore, a man of the highest distinction, consul-designate, presiding.
EDCS-20300109.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Lucius Baebius Faustianus, a standard-bearer of Legio III Augusta, for the safety of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla at Lambaesis, under the supervision of legate Quintus Anicius Faustus, likely around 202-210 CE.

EDCS-20300110.text_edition: \n Genio castrorum legionis III Augustae pro salute et incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Impperatorum Carini et Numeriani Marcus Aurelius Decimus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae ex principe peregrinorum votum solvit devotus [numini maies]tatique eius\n
EDCS-20300110.translation: For the Genius of the camp of Legio III Augusta, for the safety and well-being of our Lords Emperors Carinus and Numerian, Marcus Aurelius Decimus, a man of the highest attainment, governor of the province of Numidia, from the rank of Princeps Peregrinorum, has fulfilled his vow, devoted to his divine majesty and power.
EDCS-20300110.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, governor of Numidia, for the safety of Emperors Carinus and Numerian, likely in the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300111.text_edition: \n I patri pro salute adque incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Impperatorum Carini et Numeriani Marcus Aurelius Decimus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae ex principe peregrinorum votum solvit\n
EDCS-20300111.translation: For I(upiter) the Father, for the safety and well-being of our Lords Emperors Carinus and Numerian, Marcus Aurelius Decimus, a man of the highest attainment, governor of the province of Numidia, from the rank of Princeps Peregrinorum, has fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20300111.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, governor of Numidia, for the safety of Emperors Carinus and Numerian, likely in the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300115.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Traiano Hadriano Augusto fortissimo liberalissimoque dedicante Publio Cassio Secundo legato Augusti pro praetore veterani legionis III Augustae qui militare coeperunt\n
EDCS-20300115.translation: Dedicated to Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, the most valiant and generous, by Publius Cassius Secundus, legate of the Augustus Pro Praetore, [on behalf of] the veterans of Legio III Augusta who began their service.
EDCS-20300115.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Hadrian by veterans of Legio III Augusta, under the supervision of legate Publius Cassius Secundus, likely during Hadrian's reign (117-138 CE).

EDCS-20300117.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepote divi Nervae pro nepote Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio imperatore II pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate VIIII consule IIII patre patriae Caio Prastina Messalino legato Augusti pro praetore legionis III Augustae cohors I\n
EDCS-20300117.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, with his second consulship, Pontifex Maximus, in his ninth year of tribunician power, his fourth consul, Father of the Fatherland, Gaius Prastina Messalinus, his legate of the emperor with the rank of praetor, commanding the I Augusta legion, the first cohort.
EDCS-20300117.summary: This inscription records the official titles of Emperor Antoninus Pius and the participation of Gaius Prastina Messalinus, a legate, and the I Augusta legion, likely concerning a military or public works project.

EDCS-20300124.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XV consuli IV patri patriae dedicante Marco Valerio Etrusco legato Augusti pro praetore Lucius Sempronius Ingenuus primipilaris\n
EDCS-20300124.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, in his fifteenth year of tribunician power, fourth consul, Father of the Fatherland, dedicated by Marcus Valerius Etruscus, legate of the emperor with the rank of praetor, and Lucius Sempronius Ingenuus, a former centurion (primipilaris).
EDCS-20300124.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Marcus Valerius Etruscus, a legate, and Lucius Sempronius Ingenuus, a prominent former centurion, under Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-20300127.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Germanico patri patriae munimenta et turres a solo refectae per legionem III Augustam Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-20300127.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus, Father of the Fatherland, fortifications and towers rebuilt from the ground up by the III Augusta legion, under Marcus Aemilius Macro Saturninus, legate of the emperor with the rank of praetor.
EDCS-20300127.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of fortifications and towers by the III Augusta legion under the command of Marcus Aemilius Macro Saturninus, during the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-20300131.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Caesaris divi Marci Antonini Germanici Sarmatici filio divi Commodi fratri divi Pii Antonini nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis VI imperatori XI consuli II patri patriae proconsuli et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti nostri filio et fortissimo principi iuventutis Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti imperatoris nostri filio Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini fratri et Iuliae Domnae Augustae matri castrorum dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro consule designato equites legionis III Augustae piae vincicis\n
EDCS-20300131.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus, Pontifex Maximus, in his sixth year of tribunician power, eleventh imperial acclamation, second consul, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul, and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of our lord Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, and the most valiant Prince of Youth, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, our lord, brother of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, and to Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camp, dedicated by Quintus Anicius Faustus, clarissimus vir, legate of the Augusti with the rank of praetor, designated consul, by the equites of the III Augusta piae vincicis legion.
EDCS-20300131.summary: This is a complex dedication honoring multiple emperors (Septimius Severus, Caracalla, Geta) and Julia Domna, likely for military works or a public building, dedicated by Quintus Anicius Faustus and the cavalry of the III Augusta legion.

EDCS-20300132.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo et Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Augusti nostri filio Parthico Britannico Germanico maximo Augusti nostri Antonini Felicis filio domini nostri Severi et Iuliae Augustae matri Augusti nostri et castrorum dedicante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augggustorum pro praetore consule designato cornicularius et beneficiarii tribuni laticlavi milites legionis III Augustae piae vindicis ex arca sua fecerunt quorum nomina subiecta sunt Marcus Annius Honoratus cornicularius Caius Arrius Ingenuus Lucius Duronius Felix Caius Licinius Silvanus Caius Gabinius Vitalis Marcus Aemilius Rufinus Quintus Sutorius Secundus Geminius Ianuarius Lucius Cornelius Serapio Quintus Iulius Rufinus Caius Aelius Faustus Marcus Aemilius Catulus Sextus Caecilius Felix\n
EDCS-20300132.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of our lord, Parthicus Britannicus Germanicus Maximus, son of our lord Antoninus Felix, and to our lord Severus and Julia Augusta, mother of our lord and of the camp, dedicated by Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti with the rank of praetor, designated consul. The cornicularius and beneficiarii, tribunes with laticlavus, and soldiers of the III Augusta piae vindicis legion made this from their own treasury, whose names are listed below: Marcus Annius Honoratus, cornicularius; Caius Arrius Ingenuus; Lucius Duronius Felix; Caius Licinius Silvanus; Caius Gabinius Vitalis; Marcus Aemilius Rufinus; Quintus Sutorius Secundus; Geminius Ianuarius; Lucius Cornelius Serapio; Quintus Iulius Rufinus; Caius Aelius Faustus; Marcus Aemilius Catulus; Sextus Caecilius Felix.
EDCS-20300132.summary: This inscription records the construction of a building or project by soldiers of the III Augusta legion, funded from their treasury, and lists numerous individuals who contributed, under the legateship of Quintus Anicius Faustus.

EDCS-20600003.translation: For the prosperity and safety of the age of our lords the Augusti, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Parthicus Britannicus Germanicus Maximus Augustus, and Julia Augusta, mother of our lord and of the camp, and Fulvia Plautilla Augusta, and the Senate and the Fatherland, for Antoninus Augustus our invincible spouse. The cornicines of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion: Lucius Clodius Secundus, optio; Caius Julius Felix; Quintus Vibius Felix; Caius Julius Sterceius; Publius Vetulenius Rogatus; Caius Julius Africanus; Caius Julius Lucianus; Valerius Apollinaris; Lucius Antonius Maximus; Quintus Apronius Crescens; Nonius Faustinus; Titus Flavius Florus; Caius Julius Martialis; Aemilius Saturninus; Marcus Hortensius Primus; Caius Julius Donatus; Marcus Julius Donatus; Titus Claudius Catullinus. The scammers: Caius Malus Felix; Antonius Maximus; Caius Popilius Felix; Sextilius Datus; Caius Valerius Optatus; Valerius Gudullus; Caius Julius Septiminus; Julius Saturninus; Gargilius Masculus; Quintus Julius Saturninus; Sittius Crescentinus; Tadius Saturninus; Caecilius Secundus; Veturius Secundus; Aemilius Dexter; Caius Julius Paulinus; Caius Mallius Ianuarius; Julius Sigillanus. The stipends will be given by name by colleagues who will be appointed. If anyone from the collegia profits from the crossing with the prescribed amount, he will receive travel expenses of 200 denarii, and for a horse, 500 denarii. Likewise, to veterans in the name of the ring-bearers, 500 denarii. Likewise, if anyone from the collegia proceeds with a higher rank, he will receive 500 denarii. Likewise, if anyone yields to the call of nature (dies), his heir or procurator will receive 500 denarii. Likewise, which we detest, if anyone loses his place, he will receive 250 denarii. Those who are paid from the treasury and anyone from the recruits from this day forward makes the treasury secure will receive whatever is owed. The law was made on the 11th day before the Kalends of September, during the second consulship of Plautianus and the second of Geta, Geminus Antoninus Filinus Marcus.
EDCS-20600003.summary: This inscription details financial regulations and provisions for the cornicines and other members of the III Augusta legion, including stipends for death, loss of position, and retirement, enacted during the consulships of Plautianus and Geta in AD 203.

EDCS-20600026.translation: To Jupiter Valens, these temple buildings. To Aesculapius and Salus, Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus. To Silvanus, they made [these] through the III Augusta legion.
EDCS-20600026.summary: This inscription records the dedication of temple buildings to Jupiter Valens, Aesculapius, Salus, and Silvanus, by the emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, with construction carried out by the III Augusta legion.

EDCS-20600029.text_edition: \n Iovi Valenti Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus Lucius Stertinius Rufinus legatus Augustorum pro praetore consul designatus\n
EDCS-20600029.translation: To Jupiter Valens, Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus and Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, legates of the Augusti with the rank of praetor, designated consuls.
EDCS-20600029.summary: This inscription records the dedication of something to Jupiter Valens by Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus and Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, who were legates and designated consuls.

EDCS-20600030.text_edition: \n Silvano Pegasiano Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus Lucius Stertinius Rufinus legatus Augustorum pro praetore consul designatus\n
EDCS-20600030.translation: To Silvanus Pegasianus, Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus and Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, legates of the Augusti with the rank of praetor, designated consuls.
EDCS-20600030.summary: This inscription records the dedication of something to Silvanus Pegasianus by Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus and Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, who were legates and designated consuls.

EDCS-20600032.text_edition: \n Moenia qui Risinni Aeacia qui colis arcem Delmatiae sic nostri publice Lar populi sancte Medaure domi et sancte hic nam templa quoque ista vise precor parva magnus in effigia succussus laeva sonipes cui surgit in auras altera dum letum librat ab aure manus talem te consul iam designatus in ista sede locat venerans ille tuus [] notus Gmdivo belli vetus ac tibi Caesar Marce in primore clarus ubique acie adepto consulatu [] tibi respimntem faciem patrii numinis hastam eminus quae iaculat refreno ex equo tuus Medaure dedicat Medaurius\n
EDCS-20600032.translation: You walls, who inhabit the Aeacian fortress of Risinius, who guard the citadel of Dalmatia, so I, the Lar of the people, sacred Medaure, ask you to see these temples as well. I pray, large in their small effigy, to the one with the horse raising his head to the heavens on the left, who brandishes his hand from his ear while shaking his deadly weapon. Your Caesar Marcus, renowned everywhere in battle and by your side, having obtained the consulship, places you, venerating, in that seat, your aspect reflecting the ancestral divinity. The spear which he hurls from afar from his bridled horse, your Medaure dedicates you, Medaurius.
EDCS-20600032.summary: This poetic inscription, likely a dedication or commemoration, addresses the city walls and a figure named Medaure, possibly a local deity or hero, and mentions Caesar Marcus and a consul-designate, suggesting a context of military or civic importance in Dalmatia.

EDCS-20600039.text_edition: \n Hygiae Titus Caunius Priscus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul designatus cum Vera uxore et Firmino et Prisca filiis\n
EDCS-20600039.translation: To Hygeia, Titus Caunius Priscus, legate of the emperor with the power of a praetor, consul-designate, with his wife Vera and children Firminus and Prisca.
EDCS-20600039.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the goddess Hygeia by Titus Caunius Priscus, his family, and officials, likely during his tenure as legate.

EDCS-20600040.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus vir clarissimus legatus Auggustorum pro praetore consul designatus\n
EDCS-20600040.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygeia, Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, most distinguished man, legate of the emperors with the power of a praetor, consul-designate.
EDCS-20600040.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Aesculapius and Hygeia by Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, a high-ranking official and consul-designate.

EDCS-20600042.text_edition: \n Monitu Apollinis Publius Metilius Secundus legatus Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-20600042.translation: By the oracle of Apollo, Publius Metilius Secundus, legate of the emperor with the power of a praetor.
EDCS-20600042.summary: This inscription records a dedication or statement made by Publius Metilius Secundus, legate of the emperor, prompted by an oracle of Apollo.

EDCS-20600060.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo tempestatium divinarum potenti legio III Augusta dedicante Quinto Fabio Catullino legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-20600060.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, powerful in divine storms, by Legio III Augusta, dedicated by Quintus Fabius Catullinus, legate of the emperor with the power of a praetor.
EDCS-20600060.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter by Legio III Augusta, overseen by their legate Quintus Fabius Catullinus, likely related to protection from storms.

EDCS-20600061.text_edition: \n Ventis bonarum tempestatium potentibus legio III Augusta dedicante Quinto Fabio Catullino legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-20600061.translation: To the winds powerful in bringing good storms, by Legio III Augusta, dedicated by Quintus Fabius Catullinus, legate of the emperor with the power of a praetor.
EDCS-20600061.summary: This inscription records a dedication to favorable winds by Legio III Augusta, overseen by their legate Quintus Fabius Catullinus.

EDCS-20600066.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ceterisque diis deabusque immortalibus Caius Macrinius Decianus vir clarissimus legatus Auggustorum pro praetore provinciae Numidiae et Norici Bavaribus qui adunatis IIII regibus in provinciam Numidiam inruperant primum in regione Millevitana iterato in confinio Mauretaniae et Numidiae tertio Quinquegentaneis gentilibus Mauretaniae Caesariensis item gentilibus Fraxinensibus qui provinciam Numidiam vastabant capto famosissimo duce eorum caesis fugatisque\n
EDCS-20600066.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and all the immortal gods and goddesses, Caius Macrinius Decianus, most distinguished man, legate of the emperors with the power of a praetor of the province of Numidia and Noricum, [dedicated this] after capturing their most famous leader and defeating them, [to] the Bavari who had united with four kings and invaded the province of Numidia, first in the region of Millevitana, again on the border of Mauretania and Numidia, and a third time among the Quinquegentanei of Mauretania Caesariensis, and also among the Fraxinenses, who were ravaging the province of Numidia.
EDCS-20600066.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Caius Macrinius Decianus, legate of the emperors, to Jupiter and other gods after defeating barbarian tribes who had invaded Numidia.

EDCS-20600076.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Publius Aelius Claudia Col Iprimus pilus legionis Volubili III Augustae\n
EDCS-20600076.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, Publius Aelius Claudia, first spear-officer of Legio III Augusta Volubilis.
EDCS-20600076.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by Publius Aelius Claudia, a centurion of Legio III Augusta.

EDCS-20600081.text_edition: \n Isidi et Serapi Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus legatus Augusti pro praetore aedem cum Volteia Cornificia uxore et Matuccia Fuscina filia ab antecessoribus suis institutam exaltatam et adiecto pronao per legionem III Augustam columnis sua pecunia positis exornavit\n
EDCS-20600081.translation: To Isis and Serapis, Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, legate of the emperor with the power of a praetor, with his wife Volteia Cornificia and daughter Matuccia Fuscina, enlarged the temple founded by his predecessors and raised it up, and added a pronaos, using his own funds, with columns placed by Legio III Augusta.
EDCS-20600081.summary: This inscription records the completion and beautification of a temple to Isis and Serapis by Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, his family, and Legio III Augusta, continuing work by his predecessors.

EDCS-20600085.text_edition: \n Deo Marti militiae potenti statuam in honorem legionis III Augustae Valerianae Gallienae Valerianae Sattonius Iucundus primus pilus qui primus legione renovata aput sic aquilam vitem posuit votum dedit dedicante Veturio Veturiano viro clarissimo legato Auggustorum pro praetore\n
EDCS-20600085.translation: To the powerful god Mars of soldiery, Sattonius Iucundus, first spear-officer, who first placed a vine under the eagle after the legion was renewed, dedicated this statue in honor of Legio III Augusta Valeriana Galliena Valeriana, and made a vow, dedicated by Veturius Veturianus, most distinguished man, legate of the emperors with the power of a praetor.
EDCS-20600085.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue to the god Mars by a centurion in honor of Legio III Augusta, during the command of legate Veturius Veturianus.

EDCS-20600088.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Antonini Augusti Pii et senati sic populi Romani et Fuscini legati clarissimi viri et legionis III Augustae et auxilis eius Catius sacerdos Mauris de sua pecunia et locum instituit quos coli sub Statiano legato et Septiminus AV\n
EDCS-20600088.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius and the Senate and People of Rome, and of the most distinguished legates, the Fuscini, and of Legio III Augusta and its auxiliaries, Catius, a priest, established those who were to be worshipped under Statianus the legate and Septiminus AV, with his own money.
EDCS-20600088.summary: This inscription records the establishment of religious observances for the welfare of the emperor and Roman state by a priest Catius, involving Legio III Augusta and its auxiliaries, under the legates Statianus and Septiminus.

EDCS-20600089.translation: For the safety of our lord Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, Marcus Porcius Iasuctan, centurion of the 20th Legion Valeria Victrix Severiana, gladly and with a willing heart fulfilled his vow to the divine Moors.
EDCS-20600089.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the divine Moors by a centurion of the 20th Legion, likely during the reign of Severus Alexander.

EDCS-20600091.translation: Lucius Purtisius Firmus, a hastatus, and his wife Popilia Marciana gladly and with a willing heart made this sacred offering to the divine Moors.
EDCS-20600091.summary: A dedication to the divine Moors by Lucius Purtisius Firmus and his wife, made willingly.

EDCS-20600092.translation: Servilius Imperatus gladly and with a willing heart fulfilled his vow to the wild Moors, saying 'I came victorious, may you make me victorious'.
EDCS-20600092.summary: A personal votive inscription to the Moors by Servilius Imperatus, expressing a desire for continued victory.

EDCS-20600094.text_edition: \n Deo Mercurio sancto Augusto Marcus Aurelius Decimus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae votum solvit libens animo feliciter\n
EDCS-20600094.translation: Marcus Aurelius Decimus, a most perfect man and governor of the province of Numidia, happily and with a willing heart fulfilled his vow to the holy god Mercury Augustus.
EDCS-20600094.summary: A dedication to Mercury Augustus by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, governor of Numidia, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20600110.translation: The Emperors and Caesars Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian Pius Felix Invictus Augustus restored the aqueduct of Titula from its origin to the city, which had been corrupted by long age, through Aurelius Maximianus, a most perfect man and governor of the province of Numidia, to a better condition by adding channels, with Aemilius Lucinus, Augur and curator of the public affairs, and Iulius Aurelius, a centurion, overseeing.
EDCS-20600110.summary: This inscription records the restoration of the aqueduct of Titula by Emperors Diocletian and Maximian, overseen by the governor Aurelius Maximianus and local officials, likely in the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.
