EDCS-20600111.text_edition: \n Aquam Titulensem quam ante annos plurimos Lambaesitana civitas interverso ductu vi torrentis amiserat perforato monte instituto etiam a solo novo ductu Severinius Apronianus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae patronus coloniae restituit curante Aelio Rufo viro egregio flamine perpetuo curatore rei publicae\n
EDCS-20600111.translation: Severinius Apronianus, a most perfect man and governor of the province of Numidia, and patron of the colony, restored the water of Titula, which the city of Lambaesis had lost many years ago due to the course of a torrent through a changed channel, by piercing a mountain and also establishing a new channel from the ground, with Aelius Rufus, a distinguished man, perpetual flamen and curator of public affairs, overseeing.
EDCS-20600111.summary: This inscription details the restoration of the aqueduct to Titula by Severinius Apronianus, governor of Numidia, who pierced a mountain to create a new channel, likely in the 3rd or 4th century CE.

EDCS-20600112.translation: To the divine spirit of the Alexandrian water, I, Laetus, while holding the fasces for the fatherland, erected this altar to the Nymphs. However, the honor is more pleasing to me that in our year, blessed with this sacred name, the Nymph abundantly supplied Lambaesis with water.
EDCS-20600112.summary: An altar dedicated to the Nymphs of the Alexandrian water supply by Laetus, likely commemorating a successful water distribution during his magistracy in Lambaesis.

EDCS-20600163.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, and to Iulia Mamaea, Augusta, mother of our Augustus, and of the camps and senate and fatherland, and to the Curia Sabina Seniores, whose names are written below: Gaius Pomponius Felix, perpetual flamen; Gaius Valerius Clodianus, perpetual flamen; Lucius Postumus Honoratus, perpetual flamen; Lucius Licinius Felix, perpetual flamen; Gaius Sossius Pulcher, perpetual immunis; [3] Sittius Saturianus, perpetual immunis; [3]nus, perpetual immunis. Gaius Iulius Fortunatus [3]; Gaius Fabricius Im[3]; Marcus Asinius Sagu[3]; Gaius Pomponius Fe[3]; Gaius Valerius Sep[3]; Lucius Postumius Fi[3]; Lucius Licinius Felix.
EDCS-20600163.summary: This inscription records dedications to Emperor Severus Alexander, his mother Julia Mamaea, and local officials, including several perpetual flamines and immunes, from the Curia Sabina Seniores, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20600178.text_edition: \n Moenia quisque dolet nova condere successori inculto maneat lividus hospitio Acilius Clarus vir consularis praeses provinciae Numidiae sibi et successoribus fecit\n
EDCS-20600178.translation: Let him who laments building new walls for his successor remain in unwelcoming hospitality. Acilius Clarus, a consular man and governor of the province of Numidia, made this for himself and his successors.
EDCS-20600178.summary: Acilius Clarus, governor of Numidia, constructed walls for himself and his successors, ensuring continuity and perhaps expressing his authority.

EDCS-20600179.text_edition: \n Marco Aemilio Macro Dinarcho Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore consulis designati filio patrono Publius Iulius Cai filius Papiria Iunianus\n
EDCS-20600179.translation: Publius Iulius Cai filius Papiria Iunianus [dedicated this] to Marcus Aemilius Macro Dinarchus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macro, imperial legate pro praetore, and consul-designate, his patron.
EDCS-20600179.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Publius Iulius Iunianus to his patron, Marcus Aemilius Macro Dinarchus, who held significant military and political offices.

EDCS-20800596.text_edition: \n Marco Aemilio Macro Saturnino Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore filio Titus Valerius Rufinus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae\n
EDCS-20800596.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Macrus Saturninus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macrus, governor with the rank of propraetor, by Titus Valerius Rufinus, centurion of the Third Legion Augusta.
EDCS-20800596.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Aemilius Macrus Saturninus by a centurion of the Third Legion Augusta.

EDCS-20800597.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Cominio Cassiano clarissimo viro legato Auggustorum pro praetore omnibus virtutibus abundanti viro Memmius Valerianus a IIII militiis vir egregius\n
EDCS-20800597.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, governor with the rank of propraetor of the emperors, most illustrious man, by his beneficiarii, under the supervision of Publius Valerius Donatus, quaestor.
EDCS-20800597.summary: This records a dedication to Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, governor of the emperors, by his staff (beneficiarii).

EDCS-20800598.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Cominio Cassiano legato Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimo viro beneficiari eius curante Publio Valerio Donato quaestore\n
EDCS-20800598.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, clarissimus vir, imperial legate with the rank of proconsul, by his beneficiarii, supervised by Publius Valerius Donatus, quaestor.
EDCS-20800598.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the staff of the legate Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, overseen by the quaestor Publius Valerius Donatus.

EDCS-20800601.text_edition: \n Publius Caelius Optatus legatus Augusti pro praetore legionis III Augustae restitui iussit\n
EDCS-20800601.translation: Publius Caelius Optatus, governor with the rank of propraetor of the Third Legion Augusta, ordered this to be restored.
EDCS-20800601.summary: This inscription states that Publius Caelius Optatus, governor of the Third Legion Augusta, ordered a restoration.

EDCS-20800602.text_edition: \n Caio Fabio Fabiano Vetilio Luciliano legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro consuli designato praesidi iustissimo Aulus Tullius Iustianus primus pilus legionis III Augustae Severianae\n
EDCS-20800602.translation: To Gaius Fabius Fabianus Vetillius Lucilianus, governor with the rank of propraetor, most illustrious man, consul-designate, most just governor, by Aulus Tullius Iustianus, primus pilus of the Third Legion Augusta Severiana.
EDCS-20800602.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Fabius Fabianus Vetillius Lucilianus, a governor and consul-designate, by the primus pilus of the Third Legion Augusta Severiana.

EDCS-20800603.text_edition: \n Decimo Fonteio Frontiniano Lucio Stertinio Rufino legato Augustorum pro praetore consuli designato Sextus Terentius Saturninus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae\n
EDCS-20800603.translation: To Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, governor with the rank of propraetor of the emperors, consul-designate, by Sextus Terentius Saturninus, centurion of the Third Legion Augusta.
EDCS-20800603.summary: This records a dedication to Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus Lucius Stertinius Rufinus, a governor and consul-designate, by a centurion of the Third Legion Augusta.

EDCS-20800604.text_edition: \n Numisiae Publi filiae Celerinae coniugi Decimi Fontei Frontiniani legati Augustorum pro praetore consulis designati Magnia Procula mater Caecili Concessi corniculari\n
EDCS-20800604.translation: To Numisia Celerina, daughter of Publius, wife of Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, governor with the rank of propraetor of the emperors, consul-designate, by Magnia Procula, mother of Caecilius Concessus, cornicularius.
EDCS-20800604.summary: This is a dedication to Numisia Celerina, wife of the governor Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, by Magnia Procula.

EDCS-20800607.text_edition: \n Publio Iulio Iuniano Martialiano legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro consuli praesidi iustissimo et benignissimo Caius Calventius Ianuarius |(centurio) legionis III Augustae Alexandrianae\n
EDCS-20800607.translation: To Publius Iulius Iunianus Martialianus, governor with the rank of propraetor, most illustrious man, consul, most just and most benevolent governor, by Gaius Calventius Ianuarius, centurion of the Third Legion Augusta Alexandriana.
EDCS-20800607.summary: This inscription dedicates a monument to Publius Iulius Iunianus Martialianus, a governor and consul, by a centurion of the Third Legion Augusta Alexandriana.

EDCS-20800608.text_edition: \n Tiberio Iulio Pollieno Auspici consulari patrono Sinicii Rufus et Fortunatus fratres advocati\n
EDCS-20800608.translation: To Tiberius Iulius Pollienus Auspex, consular patron, by Sinicius Rufus and Fortunatus, brothers and advocates.
EDCS-20800608.summary: This records the dedication of advocates Sinicius Rufus and Fortunatus to their patron Tiberius Iulius Pollienus Auspex.

EDCS-20800610.text_edition: \n Statiae Agrippinae coniugis Modi Iusti legati Augusti pro praetore consulis speculatores et beneficiari\n
EDCS-20800610.translation: To Statia Agrippina, wife of Modius Iustus, governor with the rank of propraetor, consul, by the speculatores and beneficiarii.
EDCS-20800610.summary: This inscription is from the speculatores and beneficiarii to their patron's wife, Statia Agrippina, wife of Modius Iustus, governor and consul.

EDCS-20800611.text_edition: \n Lucio Novio Crispino Martiali Saturnino consuli designato legato Augusti pro praetore provinciae Africae proconsuli Galliae Narbonensis legato Augusti legionis I Italicae legato Augusti iuridico Astyriae et Gallaeciae praetori tribuno plebis quaestori pro praetore provinciae Macedoniae tribuno militum legionis VIIII Hispanae IIIIviro viarum curandarum seviro equitum Romanorum veterani legionis III Augustae qui militare coeperunt Glabrione et Torquato item Asiatico II et Aquilino consulibus\n
EDCS-20800611.translation: To Lucius Novius Crispinus Martialis Saturninus, consul-designate, governor of Africa, proconsul of Gallia Narbonensis, legate of Augustus of the I Italica legion, legal advisor of Augustus in Astyria and Gallaecia, praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor, governor of Macedonia, military tribune of the IX Hispana legion, commissioner of roads, decurion of Roman knights, veteran of the III Augusta legion, who began military service under consuls Glabrio and Torquatus, and also Asiaticus II and Aquilinus.
EDCS-20800611.summary: This inscription records the extensive career of Lucius Novius Crispinus Martialis Saturninus, likely a military diploma or career summary.

EDCS-20800612.text_edition: \n Aeliae Prosperae clarissimi feminae coniugi Cai Pomponi Magni legati Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimi viri praesidis stratores\n
EDCS-20800612.translation: To Aelia Prospera, a woman of the highest distinction, wife of Caius Pomponius Magnus, governor of Augustus with proconsular authority, distinguished president, from his orderlies.
EDCS-20800612.summary: This inscription honors Aelia Prospera, wife of the governor Caius Pomponius Magnus, likely a funerary dedication or memorial.

EDCS-20800613.text_edition: \n Marco Valerio Maximiano legato Augusti pro praetore legionis III Augustae consuli praesidi rarissimo Refrius Maximus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae strator eius\n
EDCS-20800613.translation: To Marcus Valerius Maximianus, governor of Augustus, consul, most rare president, by Refrius Maximus, centurion of the III Augusta legion, his orderly.
EDCS-20800613.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcus Valerius Maximianus, a governor, by his orderly and fellow centurion.

EDCS-20800617.text_edition: \n Marco Ulpio Maximo legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro praesidi benignissimo Aurelii Crescens et Gainas ||(centuriones) legionis III Augustae Severianae\n
EDCS-20800617.translation: To Marcus Ulpius Maximus, governor of Augustus, a most distinguished and most benevolent president, by Aurelius Crescens and Gainas, centurions of the III Augusta Severiana legion.
EDCS-20800617.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Ulpius Maximus, a governor, by two centurions of his legion.

EDCS-20800619.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Publius Aelius Crescentianus, son of Publius, a notary of the legate, in the office of Juvenalis, praetorian prefect, who died at the age of twenty-two and served for four years. Aelia Processa, his mother, for her innocent son.
EDCS-20800619.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Crescentianus, a young notary, set up by his mother.

EDCS-20800621.text_edition: \n Aelius Rufus Ianuarius vir egregius flamen perpetuus duumviralicius decreto ordinis rei publicae curator ad fisci advocationes ter numero promotus Thevestinam Hadrumetinam Thamugadensem ad annonam perpetuo Aemilius Victorinus\n
EDCS-20800621.translation: Aelius Rufus Ianuarius, an outstanding man, perpetual priest of the town, thrice promoted by decree of the city council to manage the state's treasury and to advocate for it perpetually for Theveste, Hadrumetum, and Thamagada. Aemilius Victorinus.
EDCS-20800621.summary: This inscription records the civic and financial achievements of Aelius Rufus Ianuarius, a prominent local official.

EDCS-20800627.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits, to eternal memory. For Claudius Maximus Sabinus, primipilaris of the III Augusta legion, who lived 76 years. Marcia Aurelia, his wife, and his freedmen Maximi Hermes Victor and Hermias, made this for their deserving patron.
EDCS-20800627.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Claudius Maximus Sabinus, a high-ranking centurion, set up by his wife and freedmen.

EDCS-20800628.translation: To Titus Flavius Maximus, prefect of the III Augusta legion. The heirs of Iulius Secundus, formerly a centurion of the aforementioned legion, to whom Maximus himself had bequeathed in his will the sum of 12,000 sesterces to have this monument constructed for him.
EDCS-20800628.summary: This inscription records a posthumous monument financed by Titus Flavius Maximus for a former centurion of his legion, as stipulated in the latter's will.

EDCS-20800630.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Publius Furius Rusticus, prefect of the I Cohort of Astures in the province of Britannia Inferior, military tribune of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion, who lived 40 years. Publius Furius Rusticus, his father, made this for his dearest son.
EDCS-20800630.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Furius Rusticus, a military officer, set up by his father.

EDCS-20800636.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Flavia Iuliose, wife, who lived 27 years. Marcus Servilius Fortunatus, from the soldiers, who brought back the remains of his wife from the province of Dacia, across seas and lands.
EDCS-20800636.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Iuliose, who died abroad, with her husband detailing the effort to bring her remains home.

EDCS-20800640.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sextus Verleblasius Victor, first duumvir of the municipality of Lambesis, lived 80 years. Sextus Verleblasius Victor, son of the prefect of knights, made this for his most exceptional father.
EDCS-20800640.summary: A funerary monument for Sextus Verleblasius Victor, a former duumvir of Lambesis, erected by his son of the same name who was a prefect of knights.

EDCS-20800641.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Vibius Romulus ad<ses>sor Maxi<mi>ani clarissimus vir vixit annos L\n
EDCS-20800641.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Vibius Romulus, esteemed colleague of the most distinguished Maximus, lived 50 years.
EDCS-20800641.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Vibius Romulus, recording his lifespan and his association with Maximus, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-20800661.text_edition: \n legato Augusti provinciae Numidiae et legionis III Augustae Gallienae Aemilius Florus beneficiarius domicurius eius patrono praestantissimo\n
EDCS-20800661.translation: To Aemilius Florus, beneficiarius and domicurius of the legate of the province of Numidia and of the III Augusta Galliena legion, for his most outstanding patron.
EDCS-20800661.summary: A dedication by Aemilius Florus, a beneficiarius and domicurius, to his patron who was the legate of the province of Numidia and commander of the III Augusta Galliena legion.

EDCS-20800672.translation: Gaius Antonius Maternus, from Clunia, centurion of the III Augusta legion, is buried here.
EDCS-20800672.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Antonius Maternus, a centurion of the III Augusta legion, originally from Clunia.

EDCS-20800677.translation: Manius Aquilius Proculus, son of Manius, of the Aniensis tribe, from Ariminum, a scribe, lived 37 years, is buried here.
EDCS-20800677.summary: A funerary inscription for Manius Aquilius Proculus, a scribe from Ariminum who lived 37 years.

EDCS-20800681.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Secunda lived 35 years. Aufidius Felix, a duplicarius of the legion, made this for his most beloved wife.
EDCS-20800681.summary: A funerary monument erected by Aufidius Felix, a duplicarius of a legion, for his wife Aelia Secunda.

EDCS-20800688.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Hermias, beneficiarius of the legion, lived 44 years. Flavia Nice, his wife and heir, made this for her incomparable husband, and dedicated it for 1500 sesterces.
EDCS-20800688.summary: A funerary dedication by Flavia Nice for her husband Aurelius Hermias, a beneficiarius of a legion, who lived 44 years, and includes a monetary sum for the dedication.

EDCS-20800691.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Aurelius Vibius, son of Titus, of the Aelia tribe, from Aquincium, a centurion of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion, lived 60 years. Aurelia [---] made this for her well-deserving husband. [---]
EDCS-20800691.summary: A funerary monument for Titus Aurelius Vibius, a centurion of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion, who lived 60 years, erected by his wife Aurelia.

EDCS-20800700.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Ulpia [---]e, who lived [---] years. Marcus Calvisius Felix, beneficiarius, made this for his most dutiful mother. [---]
EDCS-20800700.summary: A funerary dedication for Ulpia [---]e, mother of Marcus Calvisius Felix, a beneficiarius.

EDCS-20800701.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Calvisius Felix, a veteran, who lived 57 years. Marcus Calvisius Felix, beneficiarius, made this for his most dutiful father.
EDCS-20800701.summary: A funerary monument erected by Marcus Calvisius Felix, a beneficiarius, for his father Marcus Calvisius Felix, a veteran who lived 57 years.

EDCS-20800705.translation: Claudius Alexander, son of Tiberius, of the Colline tribe, veteran, formerly a guard of the armour of the Legio III Augusta, lived 56 years. He made this monument for himself.
EDCS-20800705.summary: This is a funerary monument for Claudius Alexander, a veteran of Legio III Augusta, who lived to be 56 years old.

EDCS-20800735.translation: Sacred to the shades of the departed. Publius Flavius Clemens, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, centurion of the Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex, lived 28 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-20800735.summary: This tombstone records the life and burial of Publius Flavius Clemens, a centurion of Legio III Augusta, who died at the age of 28.

EDCS-20800737.translation: Sacred to the shades of the departed. Titus Flavius Hercullanus, son of Titus, of the Ulpia tribe, a centurion of the Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex Antoniniana, lived 40 years. Cocceia Digna made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-20800737.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Titus Flavius Hercullanus, a centurion of Legio III Augusta, who died at 40, and was commemorated by his wife, Cocceia Digna.

EDCS-20800743.translation: Sacred to the shades of the departed. For Titus Flavius Virilis, formerly centurion of Legio II Augusta, centurion of Legio XX Valeria Victrix, centurion of Legio VI Victrix, centurion of Legio XX Valeria Victrix, centurion of Legio III Augusta, centurion of Legio III Parthica Severiana, in the post of the ninth hastatus posterior, lived 70 years, with 45 years of service. Lollia Bodicca his wife and his sons Flavi Victor and Victorinus, his heirs, had this monument made for 1200 sesterces.
EDCS-20800743.summary: This tombstone honors Titus Flavius Virilis, who held multiple centurion positions in various legions, lived to 70 with 45 years of service, and was commemorated by his wife and sons.

EDCS-20800744.translation: Sacred to the shades of the departed. For Marcus Furius Candidus, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, from Theveste, centurion of the Legio III Augusta Antoniniana, lived 55 years. Iulia Artimisia his wife and his children Furius Candidus and Artimisia and his heirs made and dedicated this for their most beloved father for 2000 sesterces.
EDCS-20800744.summary: This funerary monument honors Marcus Furius Candidus, a centurion of Legio III Augusta, who died at 55, and was commemorated by his wife, children, and heirs.

EDCS-20800753.translation: To Gaius Julius Fidus, a cavalryman of the Cohors VII Lusitanorum, lived 25 years. By Julius Castus, his brother.
EDCS-20800753.summary: This monument records the life of Gaius Julius Fidus, a cavalryman of Cohors VII Lusitanorum, who died at 25, commemorated by his brother.

EDCS-20800757.translation: Sacred to the shades of the departed. For Julius Bassus Sulpicianus, centurion of Legio II Traiana Fortis, also centurion of Legio XXII Primigenia Pia Fidelis, also centurion of Legio XIII Gemina, also centurion of Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex, also centurion of Legio III Parthica Severiana, lived 54 years, served 37 years. Iulia Saturnina, his wife, made this for her sweetest and deserving husband. Procured by Julius Bassus Donatus, procurator.
EDCS-20800757.summary: This tombstone honors Julius Bassus Sulpicianus, who served as a centurion in five different legions, lived to 54 with 37 years of service, and was commemorated by his wife, with a procurator overseeing the work.

EDCS-20800760.translation: Julius Donatus, an equestrian of Legio III Augusta, from the rank of optio, lived 55 years, and as a soldier for 25 years. Made for his son and deserving wife.
EDCS-20800760.summary: This monument records Julius Donatus, an equestrian and former optio of Legio III Augusta, who lived 55 years and served 25 years, and was commemorated by his son and wife.

EDCS-20800765.translation: Sacred to the shades of the departed. For Flavia Victoria, a most rare wife, lived 33 years. Julius Extricatus, an immunis (exempt from menial duties) of the Legio III Augusta Pia Vindex, piously made this for his parents, by order.
EDCS-20800765.summary: This funerary inscription is for Flavia Victoria, who died at 33, and was commemorated by Julius Extricatus, an immunis of Legio III Augusta, who also made it for his parents.

EDCS-20800766.translation: Vibia Optatina lived 41 years. Julius Felix, the scribe, made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-20800766.summary: This tombstone records the life of Vibia Optatina, who died at 41, and was commemorated by her husband, Julius Felix, a scribe.

EDCS-20900306.translation: In memory of Gaius Julius Nepos, from Ammedara, a soldier of the guard, who lived 18 years. Gaius Julius Verus, his kinsman and staff officer, made this for his most dutiful brother.
EDCS-20900306.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Julius Nepos, a soldier from Ammedara, set up by his brother Gaius Julius Verus.

EDCS-20900316.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Julius Rogatus, duplicarius of the Third Augustan Legion, Pia, Vindicia, lived 47 years. His sons and heirs, Julius Felix and Tertullus, made and dedicated this gift for their most innocent father.
EDCS-20900316.summary: This tombstone honors Gaius Julius Rogatus, a duplicarius in the Third Legion, erected by his sons.

EDCS-20900326.translation: Gaius Laetorius Pacatus, standard-bearer of the Third Augustan Legion, lived 50 years. Crescentianus and Har[...]
EDCS-20900326.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Laetorius Pacatus, a standard-bearer in the Third Legion, likely erected by his son Crescentianus.

EDCS-20900348.translation: Sacred to the eternal memory of Nonia Manliana, a virgin, who lived 12 years, 9 months, and 19 days. Gaius Nonius Manlianus, centurion of the Third Italic Legion, father, and Julia Clementina, mother, had this made for their sweetest daughter. For 2000 sesterces.
EDCS-20900348.summary: This tombstone honors the young Nonia Manliana, erected by her parents who were serving in the Third Legion in Italy.

EDCS-20900370.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Silius Faustus, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, a soldier from Ammedara, of the Third Augustan Legion, who died in Parthia, lived 41 years. For his most devoted brother, Lucius Silius Rufinus, standard-bearer of the Third Augustan Legion, and Silius Quietus, a soldier of the same legion.
EDCS-20900370.summary: This tombstone is for Marcus Silius Faustus, a soldier of the Third Legion who died in Parthia, set up by his brothers.

EDCS-21000668.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Valerio Constantino Invicto Pio Felici Augusto domino nostro Galerio Valerio Maximino Pio Felici Augusto domino nostro Valerio Liciniano Licinio Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-21000668.translation: To our Lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Invictus, Pious, Happy, Augustus; to our Lord Galerius Valerius Maximianus, Pious, Happy, Augustus; to our Lord Valerius Licinianus Licinius, Pious, Happy, Augustus.
EDCS-21000668.summary: This is an imperial acclamation honoring three Augusti: Constantine, Galerius Maximianus, and Licinius, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-21100001.translation: For Valerius Priscianus, who lived 5 years, 10 months, and 6 days. Valerius Priscinianus, a scout, made this for his dearest son. May the earth lie lightly upon you, Dalmatian.
EDCS-21100001.summary: This is a tombstone for a young boy named Valerius Priscianus, erected by his father who was a scout.

EDCS-21100003.translation: [Sacred to the spirits of the departed.] Lucius Vibius Felix, a veteran of the Third Augustan Legion, Pia, Vindicia, formerly a standard-bearer, made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-21100003.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Vibius Felix, a veteran and former standard-bearer of the Third Legion, who commissioned his own tomb.

EDCS-21100005.translation: [To Marcus Ulpius] Superatus, his freedman and foster-son, most beloved, who lived 9 years. Marcus Ulpius Optatus, centurion of the Third Augustan Legion, Pia, Vindicia, made this.
EDCS-21100005.summary: This tombstone is for the freedman and foster-son Marcus Ulpius Superatus, erected by his patron, a centurion in the Third Legion.

EDCS-21100007.translation: Sacred to the eternal memory. Lucius Volumnius Crescens, optio of the Third Augustan Legion, lived 30 years. Furia Emerita, his wife and heir, made this for him as one who deserved well.
EDCS-21100007.summary: This tombstone honors Lucius Volumnius Crescens, an optio in the Third Legion, erected by his wife and heir.

EDCS-21100052.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Aulius Apollinaris, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, from the Third Gallic, lived 47 years, served 25 years. Julia Lucilla, his dearest mother, [erected this].
EDCS-21100052.summary: A tombstone commemorating Publius Aulius Apollinaris, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, erected by his mother, Julia Lucilla.

EDCS-21100069.translation: To Gaius Caecilius Rufus, veteran of the Third Adjutant Legion. Grania Silvana, his wife, placed [this] for her husband.
EDCS-21100069.summary: A funerary monument dedicated to Gaius Caecilius Rufus, a veteran of the Third Adjutant Legion, by his wife Grania Silvana.

EDCS-21100092.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Maturia Rogata lived 70 years. Lucius Cornelius Flaccus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Pious Victorious Legion, made [this] for his most loving mother.
EDCS-21100092.summary: A tombstone for Maturia Rogata, erected by her son Lucius Cornelius Flaccus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion.

EDCS-21100114.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Flavius Siginus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, from the Third Gallic, lived 40 years.
EDCS-21100114.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Flavius Siginus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, recording his age at death.

EDCS-21100156.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. May the earth be light for you. Gaius Julius Nestor Junior lived 7 years and 9 months. Gaius Julius Nestor, veteran, [a father] contributing from the Third Gallic Legion to the Third Augustan Legion, and Agria Fabiana, his mother, made [this] for their dearest son.
EDCS-21100156.summary: A tombstone for the young Gaius Julius Nestor Junior, erected by his veteran father and mother; it also mentions the father's service and transfer between legions.

EDCS-21100163.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Julius Saturninus, cavalryman of the Third Augustan Antoninian Legion, lived 40 years. Propertia Quinta, his wife and heir, placed [this] here.
EDCS-21100163.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Julius Saturninus, a cavalryman of the Third Augustan Legion, erected by his wife and heir, Propertia Quinta.

EDCS-21100180.text_edition: \n Lucius Lucretius Sergia Robustus Scallabi miles legionis VII geminae felicis vixit annos XXX S Victor frater posuit hic est situs\n
EDCS-21100180.translation: Lucius Lucretius Sergia Robustus Scallabi, soldier of the happy Second Twin Legion, lived 30 years. Victor, his brother, placed [this] here.
EDCS-21100180.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Lucretius Sergia Robustus Scallabi, a soldier of the Second Twin Legion, erected by his brother Victor.

EDCS-21100184.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. To the memory of Publius Magnus Ianuarius, son of Publius, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion. Publius Magnius Abagalianus, his father, made and dedicated [this].
EDCS-21100184.summary: A funerary monument for Publius Magnus Ianuarius, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, erected by his father, Publius Magnius Abagalianus.

EDCS-21100207.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Octavius Barbarus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Pious Victorious Legion, lived 25 years.
EDCS-21100207.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Octavius Barbarus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, recording his age at death.

EDCS-21100222.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Pontius Felix, a soldier of the Third Augustan Pious Victorious Legion, lived 25 years. Iulia Mapalica, while alive, made [this] for herself and her son.
EDCS-21100222.summary: A tombstone for Pontius Felix, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, and his mother Iulia Mapalica, who also made provision for herself and her son.

EDCS-21100228.translation: To the departed spirits. Perelia Ispeni lived 46 years. Sallustius and Fortunatus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, made this for their deserving mother.
EDCS-21100228.summary: This funerary inscription records the age and parents of Perelia, likely a woman, and her son who was a soldier.

EDCS-21100230.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Salustius Datus, a soldier of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion, lived 50 years. Maximia Clara, his wife and heir, with her sons, made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-21100230.summary: This funerary inscription records the age and military service of Gaius Salustius Datus and was erected by his wife and sons.

EDCS-21100240.translation: Gaius Stabilius Maternus of the Pomptina tribe, a soldier of the VII Gemina Felix legion, in the century of Aprinus, aged 30, with 13 years of service. Stabilius Valerius Flavius Valerianus made this for his brother.
EDCS-21100240.summary: This funerary inscription records the age, military service, and legion of Gaius Stabilius Maternus and was erected by his brother.

EDCS-21100245.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Pomponia Victoria lived 80 years. Quintus Titius Sallustianus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, made this for his most pious grandmother.
EDCS-21100245.summary: This funerary inscription records the age of Pomponia Victoria and was erected by her grandson who was a soldier in the III Augusta legion.

EDCS-21100252.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Valerius Felix, a veteran, lived 45 years. Iulia made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-21100252.summary: This funerary inscription records the age of Valerius Felix, a veteran, and was erected by his wife.

EDCS-21100269.translation: To the departed spirits. For Vibius Felix, a soldier of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion, who died fighting in an engagement with the enemy. He lived 21 years, 10 months, and 15 days. He served 1 year, 1 month, and 2 days. Lucius Vibius Felix, father, made this for his most loving son, on his own property.
EDCS-21100269.summary: This funerary inscription records the death in battle and lifespan of Vibius Felix, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, and was erected by his father.

EDCS-21100276.translation: To the departed spirits. Of Quintus Volusius Rogatus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion.
EDCS-21100276.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to Quintus Volusius Rogatus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion.

EDCS-21100284.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits and to good rest. For Aurelia Severa, who lived 22 years, 11 months, and 10 days. Heliodorus, freedman of the emperors, from the archives, made this for his most pious and obedient wife.
EDCS-21100284.summary: This funerary inscription records the age of Aurelia Severa and was erected by her husband, Heliodorus, an imperial freedman.

EDCS-21100327.summary: This brief inscription records the lifespan of Aelius Iamlicus.

EDCS-21100330.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aemilius Arisus lived 50 years. His sons made this.
EDCS-21100330.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Aemilius Arisus and was erected by his sons.

EDCS-21100338.translation: To the divine shades of Aelia Benigna, who...
EDCS-21100338.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to Aelia Benigna, likely a tombstone with missing information about her life and death.

EDCS-21100339.translation: To the divine shades. Aelia Castula lived for 50 years. Her husband made this for his wife.
EDCS-21100339.summary: A funerary inscription set up by a husband for his wife, Aelia Castula, who lived to be 50 years old.

EDCS-21100341.translation: To the divine shades. Aelia Fortunata lived for 40 years. Nampamo made this for his wife.
EDCS-21100341.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Nampamo for his wife, Aelia Fortunata, who lived to be 40 years old.

EDCS-21100359.translation: To Aemilia Fortunata, who lived 25 years. Aemilius Flaccus made this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-21100359.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life and death of Aemilia Fortunata, dedicated by her husband.

EDCS-21300144.translation: Bibenia Aventina lived 25 years. Pompeius Extricatus made this for his wife. May you rest well here.
EDCS-21300144.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Bibenia Aventina, dedicated by her husband, with a wish for her peaceful rest.

EDCS-21300149.translation: To Burrenia Fortunata, most devoted wife, who lived 22 years.
EDCS-21300149.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Burrenia Fortunata, dedicated to her as a most devoted wife.

EDCS-21300208.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Clodia Maxima lived 40 years. Arruntius Donatus, her husband, made this.
EDCS-21300208.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Clodia Maxima, dedicated by her husband.

EDCS-21600156.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae sacrum Lucius Cestius Successus filius et heres Luci Cesti Galli fideiussoris Flavi Natalis pollicitatoris huius statuae iussus ex decreto Fontei Frontiniani legati Augusti pro praetore clarissimi viri adiectis ad HS IIItria milia nummum quanti tunc hanc statuam idem Flavius Natalis rei publicae positurum se pollicitus erat HS IIIXXXXtria milia quadraginta nummum ex HS VIXXXXsex milibus quadraginta nummum posuit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-21600156.translation: Sacred to Victory Augusta. Lucius Cestius Successus, son and heir of Lucius Cestius Gallus, a guarantor, and Flavius Natalis, proposer, of this statue, by order of Fonteius Frontinianus, clarissimus vir, legate of the emperor with proconsular power, having added to the 3,000,000 sesterces for which the same Flavius Natalis had promised to set up this statue for the state, placed and dedicated it at a cost of 3,040,000 sesterces, from a total of 6,040,000 sesterces.
EDCS-21600156.summary: This inscription records the commissioning and funding of a statue of Victory Augusta, detailing the financial contributions and legal authority involved, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-21600171.translation: Domitius Crispinus lived for 42 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-21600171.summary: A funerary inscription for Domitius Crispinus, who lived 42 years and is buried here.

EDCS-21600177.translation: To the divine shades. Lucius Domitius Secundus established an eternal home for the remains of his body during his lifetime.
EDCS-21600177.summary: A funerary inscription where Lucius Domitius Secundus made arrangements for his burial during his lifetime.

EDCS-21600204.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Felicius lived for 5 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-21600204.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Felicius, a child who died at age 5, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21600288.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed, to the memory and sanctity of Horatia Maxima, who lived for 80 years. Quintus Horatius P[---] made this for his patroness, his freedman.
EDCS-21600288.summary: A tombstone set up by a freedman for his deceased patroness, Horatia Maxima, who lived to 80, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21600311.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Julius Felix lived for 60 years. Victoria made this for her husband.
EDCS-21600311.summary: A tombstone set up by Victoria for her husband, Gaius Julius Felix, who lived to 60, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-21600322.translation: Gaius Julius Neon lived for 8 years. Gaius Julius Annianus lived for 4 years. Julia Ianua lived for 5 years.
EDCS-21600322.summary: A tombstone recording the deaths of three children, Gaius Julius Neon (8), Gaius Julius Annianus (4), and Julia Ianua (5), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22000446.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Donata lived for 35 years. Her husband made this for his well-deserving wife for eternal security.
EDCS-22000446.summary: A tombstone erected by a husband for his wife, Iulia Donata, who died at 35, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22000482.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed, to the ashes of Iulia Pannonia. Nona Primitiva made this for her most worthy patroness.
EDCS-22000482.summary: A tombstone dedicated by Nona Primitiva to her patroness, Iulia Pannonia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22000484.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed, to the memory of Iulia Prima, mother. Aurelius Felix, her son.
EDCS-22000484.summary: A tombstone honoring the memory of Iulia Prima, dedicated by her son Aurelius Felix, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22000549.translation: Luria Felica lived for 16 years. Lurius Secundus, her brother.
EDCS-22000549.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Luria Felica at 16, erected by her brother Lurius Secundus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22000555.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Magnius Vitalis lived for 92 years. His dutiful sons made this for his perpetual security.
EDCS-22000555.summary: A tombstone set up by sons for their father, Publius Magnius Vitalis, who died at 92, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22000598.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Mocimus, son of Sumon, a Palmurene, lived for 30 years. His heir placed this.
EDCS-22000598.summary: A tombstone for Mocimus, son of Sumon, a native of Palmyra, who died at 30, erected by his heir, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22200732.translation: Publia Victoria lived 35 years. Caecilius Felix made this for his wife.
EDCS-22200732.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of the deceased, Publia Victoria, and dedication by her husband, Caecilius Felix.

EDCS-22700053.translation: Quintus Sutrius Rogatus lived 50 years. Iulia Silvana made this for her deserved husband from the ground up.
EDCS-22700053.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the age of the deceased, Quintus Sutrius Rogatus, and dedication by his wife, Iulia Silvana.

EDCS-22700074.translation: Tullius Hermes made this for his daughter Tullia Elaina, with her husband Iunius Pompeianus supervising.
EDCS-22700074.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tullia Elaina, dedicated by her father Tullius Hermes and overseen by her husband Iunius Pompeianus.

EDCS-22700099.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Varenius Montanus vixit annis LXVII Iulia Licinia marito et sibi fecit\n
EDCS-22700099.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Varenius Montanus lived 67 years. Iulia Licinia made this for her husband and herself.
EDCS-22700099.summary: A funerary epitaph for Varenius Montanus, recording his age and indicating that the monument was made by his wife, Iulia Licinia, for both of them.

EDCS-22700122.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Vibullia Tertulla lived 38 years and 1 month. Lucius Vibius Felix made this for his most rare and well-deserving wife.
EDCS-22700122.summary: A funerary epitaph for Vibullia Tertulla, recording her age and dedication by her husband, Lucius Vibius Felix, who praises her qualities.

EDCS-22700129.translation: Lucius Vitruvius, son of Sextus, of the Pollia tribe, Verecun[---]ius, pious, lived 7 years.
EDCS-22700129.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Vitruvius, a young boy, recording his name, tribe, a partial epithet, and his age at death.

EDCS-22700140.translation: To the spirits of the departed. The remains of Lucius Umbrius Felix are placed here.
EDCS-22700140.summary: A funerary inscription stating that the remains of Lucius Umbrius Felix are interred at this location.

EDCS-23100031.text_edition: \n Genio patriae Augustae dedicante Iulio Lepido Tertullo legato Augusti pro praetore quod Lucius Pompeius Rogatus testamento suo ex HS XX (viginti milibus) nummum fieri iussit ordo Verecundensium faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-23100031.translation: Dedicated to the Genius of the Augustan homeland, with Iulius Lepidus Tertullus, legatus Augusti pro praetore, presiding, because Lucius Pompeius Rogatus ordered in his will that it be made from 20,000 sesterces; the council of Verecundenses took care of its construction.
EDCS-23100031.summary: A dedication of a public work to the Genius of the homeland, funded by a bequest from Lucius Pompeius Rogatus and overseen by the local council and the governor Iulius Lepidus Tertullus.

EDCS-23100038.text_edition: \n Numini domus Augustae et Imperatori Caesari divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepoti Tito Aelio Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate dedicante Lucio Novio Crispino legato Augusti pro praetore consule designato possessores vici Verecundensis\n
EDCS-23100038.translation: The proprietors of the district of Verecundensis dedicated this to the divine spirit of the Augustan household and to the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul designate, presiding, with Lucius Novius Crispinus, legatus Augusti pro praetore, as consul designate.
EDCS-23100038.summary: A dedication by local landowners to the imperial family, specifically Emperor Antoninus Pius, during the consulship of Lucius Novius Crispinus.

EDCS-23100042.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XXIII imperator II consul IIII pater patriae per legionem III Augustam Decimo Fonteio Frontiniano legato Augusti pro praetore dedicante\n
EDCS-23100042.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 23rd time, imperator for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland, dedicated this through the III Augusta legion, with Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, legatus Augusti pro praetore, presiding.
EDCS-23100042.summary: An imperial dedication of a public work, likely constructed by the III Augusta legion under the command of legate Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, listing the extensive titles of Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-23100044.text_edition: \n Divo Antonino Augusto ex cuius indulgentia aqua vico Augustorum Verecundensium perducta est dedicante Decimo Fonteio Frontiniano legato Augusti pro praetore decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-23100044.translation: To the deified Antoninus Augustus, thanks to whose generosity water was brought to the Augustan quarter of the Verecundenses, dedicated by Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, imperial legate with praetorian power, by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-23100044.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a water supply to the deified Emperor Antoninus Augustus, funded by public money and dedicated by the imperial legate Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus.

EDCS-23100067.text_edition: \n Marco Aemilio Dinarcho Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore clarissimi viri filio decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-23100067.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Dinarchus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macer, the clarissimus vir, imperial legate with praetorian power, by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-23100067.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcus Aemilius Dinarchus, son of a former imperial legate, funded by public money and passed by a decurial decree.

EDCS-23100068.text_edition: \n Marco Aemilio Saturnino Marci Aemili Macri legati Augusti pro praetore clarissimi viri filio decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-23100068.translation: To Marcus Aemilius Saturninus, son of Marcus Aemilius Macer, the clarissimus vir, imperial legate with praetorian power, by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-23100068.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcus Aemilius Saturninus, son of a former imperial legate, funded by public money and passed by a decurial decree.

EDCS-23100069.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Gordiano legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro patrono decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-23100069.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Gordianus, the clarissimus vir, patron, imperial legate with praetorian power, by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-23100069.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Tiberius Claudius Gordianus, an imperial legate and patron, funded by public money and passed by a decurial decree.

EDCS-23100070.text_edition: \n Numisiae Publi filiae Celerinae coniugi Decimi Fontei Frontiniani consularis patroni decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-23100070.translation: To Numisia Celerina, daughter of Publius, wife of Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, the consular patron, by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-23100070.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Numisia Celerina, wife of a former consul and patron, funded by public money and passed by a decurial decree.

EDCS-23100157.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imppperatorum Caesssarum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini et Parthico sic maximi Augggustorum et Iuliae Augustae matri sic castrorum et Auggustorum totiusque domus divinae dedicante Subatiano Proculo legato Augggustorum sub cura Cai Iuli Paulini |(centurionis) cohortis II Maurorum vexillatio nummo collato numero CXXVI Kalendis Augustis domino nostro Antonino III et Geta II consulibus votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-23100157.translation: For the safety of the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, Parthicus Maximus Augustus, and Julia Augusta, mother of the camp and of all Augusti, and of the whole divine family, dedicated by Subatianus Proculus, legate of the Augusti, under the care of Gaius Julius Paulinus, centurion of the II Cohort of Moors, a detachment having contributed 126 coins, on the Kalends of August, to our lord Antoninus (Caracalla) and Geta, in their third and second consulships respectively, a vow was willingly fulfilled.
EDCS-23100157.summary: This inscription records a military dedication for the welfare of the imperial family during the consulship of Antoninus (Caracalla) III and Geta II, dedicated by a military detachment under the command of their legate.

EDCS-23100163.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Julius Victor, veteran and former tesserarius, born under consuls Tertullus and Sacerdos, lived 80 years. He made this for himself and his wife while alive.
EDCS-23100163.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Victor, a veteran, who erected the monument for himself and his wife during his lifetime, dating to the consulship of Tertullus and Sacerdos.

EDCS-23100213.translation: Gaius Antonius Fortunatus, son of Gaius, veteran, from Lamigg[---]. He made this for his most devoted wife and for Antonius Verus, his most innocent son, and for Antonia Maxima, his foster-child. He lived [---] years.
EDCS-23100213.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Antonius Fortunatus, a veteran, who erected the monument for his wife, son, and foster-child.

EDCS-23100409.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae sanctae Soli Mithrae Herculi Marti Mercurio Genio loci diis deabusque omnibus Marcus Aurelius Decimus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae ex principe peregrinorum votum solvit\n
EDCS-23100409.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, holy Minerva, Sol Mithras, Hercules, Mars, Mercury, the Genius of the place, and all the gods and goddesses, Marcus Aurelius Decimus, a most accomplished man and governor of the province of Numidia, formerly a prefect of peregrines, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-23100409.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, the governor of Numidia, to a wide array of deities.

EDCS-23100413.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustorum sacrum dedicante Decimo Fonteio Frontiniano legato Augustorum pro praetore ex testamento Marci Cossini Secundi flaminis perpetui ex HS IIII milibus nummum Lucius Sutorius Luci filius Papiria Felix flamen perpetuus heres eius posuit\n
EDCS-23100413.translation: Sacred to Victoria Augusta. Dedicated by Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, legate of the Augusti with praetorian power, according to the will of Marcus Cossinius Secundus, perpetual flamen. Lucius Sutorius Felix, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, the perpetual flamen, his heir, placed this, at a cost of 4000 sesterces.
EDCS-23100413.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a shrine to Victoria Augusta, funded by an inheritance and dedicated by Lucius Sutorius Felix, heir of Marcus Cossinius Secundus, a perpetual flamen.

EDCS-23100414.translation: To the Parthian Victory. For the Emperors, Caesars, [Lucius] Septimius Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti [[[and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar]]]. Titus Aurelius Fortis, aedile and duumvir, having promised 4,000 sesterces for the office, gave and dedicated this with an increased sum in the second year of his duumvirate. He also dedicated it on the Ides of May in the consulship of Saturninus and Gallus.
EDCS-23100414.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar by Titus Aurelius Fortis, who served as aedile and duumvir, to commemorate the Parthian victories of Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-23100420.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto tribunicia potestate II consuli II divi Antonini filio divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani Parthici pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti Decimo Fonteio Frontiniano legato Augusti pro praetore consule designato patrono municipi decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-23100420.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva. Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, imperial legate with proconsular authority, consul-designate, patron of the municipality, dedicated this by decree of the decurions with public funds.
EDCS-23100420.summary: This is a dedication of a monument by Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, imperial legate and patron of the municipality, to Emperor Lucius Aurelius Verus, likely dating to the period of Verus's co-rule with Marcus Aurelius (161-169 CE).

EDCS-23100430.text_edition: \n Decimo Fonteio Frontiniano Lucio Stertinio Rufino legato Augusti pro praetore consuli patrono municipi ordo Dianensium aere conlato\n
EDCS-23100430.translation: For Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, imperial legate with proconsular authority, consul, patron of the municipality. The order of the people of Dianium, with collected funds, dedicated this.
EDCS-23100430.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the local council of Dianium to Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, their patron and imperial legate, likely in the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-23100431.text_edition: \n Marco Valerio Maximiano Marci Valeri Maximiani quinquennalis sacerdotalis filio pontifici coloniae Poetovionensium legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro consuli designato nnissimo praesidi patrono Aquilius Restitutus flamen perpetuus aedilis IIvirum sic IIvir quinquennalis praefectus iure dicundo pro IIviris et Marcianus augur aedilis IIvirum sic sua pecunia fecerunt idemque dedicaverunt\n
EDCS-23100431.translation: Marcus Valerius Maximianus, son of Marcus Valerius Maximianus, quinquennial official, priest of the colony of Poetovio, imperial legate with proconsular authority, a most distinguished man, consul-designate, our revered president and patron. Aquilius Restitutus, perpetual priest, aedile, duumvir, quinquennial duumvir, prefect ruling by law in place of the duumvirs, and Marcianus, augur, aedile, duumvir, made this with their own money and dedicated it themselves.
EDCS-23100431.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a building or monument by several prominent local officials, including Marcus Valerius Maximianus and Aquilius Restitutus, in the colony of Poetovio, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-23200371.translation: Titus Arranius Peregrinus, son of Titus, of the Arnensis tribe, a soldier of the Legion III Augusta, served for 12 years, lived for 30 years. He lies here.
EDCS-23200371.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Titus Arranius Peregrinus, a soldier of the Legion III Augusta, who served for 12 years and died at the age of 30, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200374.translation: Aelia Fortunata, daughter of Publius, lived for 40 years. She lies here.
EDCS-23200374.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Aelia Fortunata, who lived to be 40 years old, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200386.translation: Iulia Fortunata, dutiful, lived for 40 years. She lies here.
EDCS-23200386.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Iulia Fortunata, noted for her piety, who lived to be 40 years old, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200390.translation: Marcia Maritima, dutiful, lived for 80 years. She lies here.
EDCS-23200390.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcia Maritima, noted for her piety, who lived to be 80 years old, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200391.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Pompeius Euglycus, who died at the age of 3, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200396.translation: Gaius Rubellius Niger lived for 75 years. He lies here.
EDCS-23200396.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Gaius Rubellius Niger, who lived to be 75 years old, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23200399.translation: Gaius Veturrius Felix lived 25 years [restored name and duration].
EDCS-23200399.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Gaius Veturrius Felix, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-23600009.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pertinace sic Augusto Parthico Adiabenico Iuliae Augustae matri castrorum Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Augusti nostri filio Lucio Septimio Getae Caesari Augusto Augusti nostri filio principi iuventutis procurante Rossio Vitulo egregio viro Pastor vilicus Cuiculi et Milei et Adauctus contrascriptor\n
EDCS-23600009.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius Augustus, Parthicus Adiabenicus; to Julia Augusta, mother of the camps; to the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of our Augustus; to Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar Augustus, son of our Augustus, prince of the youth; under the administration of Rossius Vitulus, an outstanding man, Pastor, vilicus of Cuicul, and Milei and Adauctus, contrascriptores.
EDCS-23600009.summary: This monument honors the emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, and records the involvement of imperial officials and local administrators in its erection.

EDCS-23600032.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis sexs<i=T>es sic imperatori quater patri patriae consuli II proconsuli nepoti divorum Gordianorum res publica Cuiculitanorum devota numini maiestatique eius decreto decurionum pecunia publica \n
EDCS-23600032.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, unconquered, pious, fortunate Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the sixth time, imperator four times, father of the country, consul twice, proconsul; the city of Cuicul, devoted to his divine numen and majesty, [erected this] by decree of the decurions with public funds.
EDCS-23600032.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Emperor Gordian III by the city of Cuicul, highlighting his titles and honors.

EDCS-23600059.text_edition: \n Clementissimis temporibus dddominorum nnnostrorum Diocletiani et Maximiani Auggustorum et Constanti et [Maximiani] nobbilissimorum Caessarum Valerius Concordius vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Numidiae aquam ex fontibus vetustate conlabsis sic ad novam faciem restituit curante opus omne Marco Rutilio Felice Fel no equite Romano pontifice curatore rei publicae\n
EDCS-23600059.translation: In the most clement times of our lords Diocletian and Maximian, Augusti, and Constantius and [Maximian], nobilissimi Caesares, Valerius Concordius, vir perfectissimus, governor of the province of Numidia, restored the water from dilapidated springs to a new form, with all the work supervised by Marcus Rutilius Felix, an equestrian of Rome, pontiff and curator of the state.
EDCS-23600059.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a water supply by Valerius Concordius, governor of Numidia, during the reign of Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius.

EDCS-23600104.text_edition: \n Iuliae Titi filiae Chilonidi clarissimae feminae coniugi Tiberi Claudi Gordiani legati Augusti pro praetore clarissimi viri patroni coloniae decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-23600104.translation: To Julia Chilonis, daughter of Titus, a most distinguished woman, wife of Tiberius Claudius Gordianus, legate of the emperor pro praetore, a most distinguished man, patron of the colony, by decree of the decurions with public funds.
EDCS-23600104.summary: This inscription honors Julia Chilonis, wife of Tiberius Claudius Gordianus, a high-ranking official, and records the dedication by the local council.

EDCS-23600107.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Petronio Probato Iuniori Iusto legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro praesidi exempli rarissimi\n
EDCS-23600107.translation: To Gnaeus Petronius Probus Iunior Iustus, legate of the emperor pro praetore, a most distinguished man, governor, of most rare example.
EDCS-23600107.summary: This inscription honors Gnaeus Petronius Probus Iunior Iustus, a distinguished imperial legate and governor, notable for his exemplary conduct.

EDCS-23600555.text_edition: \n Telluri Genetrici res publica Cuiculitanorum templum fecit Caius Iulius Lepidus Tertullus legatus Augusti pro praetore dedicavit simulacrum deae acrolithum Tiberius Iulius Honoratus pontifex flamen perpetuus dono dedit\n
EDCS-23600555.translation: To Tellus Mater, the city of Cuicul erected a temple. Gaius Julius Lepidus Tertullus, legate of the emperor pro praetore, dedicated the statue of the goddess, acrolithic. Tiberius Julius Honoratus, pontifex and perpetual flamen, gave it as a gift.
EDCS-23600555.summary: This inscription records the erection and dedication of a temple and statue to Tellus Mater by the city of Cuicul, involving various officials and priests.

EDCS-23600585.translation: By decree of the decurions with public funds to Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius Augustus, father of the country, holding tribunician power and consul for the third time.
EDCS-23600585.summary: This inscription, likely from a public monument, honors Emperor Antoninus Pius with his titles and decrees its erection by the local council with public funds.

EDCS-23600591.translation: By decree of the decurions with public funds to the deified Antoninus, father of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus.
EDCS-23600591.summary: This inscription honors the deified Antoninus Pius, father of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, and indicates its erection by the local council with public funds.

EDCS-23600598.translation: By decree of the decurions with public funds to Concordia, [associated with] Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus Parthicus.
EDCS-23600598.summary: This inscription records the dedication to Concordia, likely a personification of harmony, associated with Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, and its erection by the local council with public funds.

EDCS-23600606.text_edition: \n Genio populi Cuiculitanorum Lucius Claudius Tiberi filius Quirina Honoratus tribunus militum legionis II adiutricis praefectus cohortis I Augustae Pannoniorum equo publico exornatus ab Imperatore Antonino Augusto Pio in quinque decurias adlectus coloniae Cirtensis decurio et aedilis coloniae Cuiculitanae decurio et augur exedram cum statua et columnis marmoreis quam Claudius Modestus pater suus ob honorem pontificatus sine taxatione promiserat ex decreto Fontei Frontiniani legati Augusti pro praetore clarissimi viri ampliata pecunia sua fecit dedicavitque\n
EDCS-23600606.translation: To the genius of the people of Cuiculitan. Lucius Claudius Honoratus, son of Tiberius, of the Quirina tribe, tribune of soldiers of the II Adiutrix legion, prefect of the I Cohort of Pannonians, honored with the equestrian order, admitted by the Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius into the quinquennial council of the colony of Cirta, and a decurion and augur of the colony of Cuiculitan, made and dedicated the exedra with a statue and marble columns, which his father Claudius Modestus had promised without charge on the occasion of his pontificate, enlarged with his own funds, by decree of Fonteius Frontinianus, clarissimus vir, legate of the emperor with praetorian power.
EDCS-23600606.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a public building by Lucius Claudius Honoratus in the colony of Cuiculitan, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-23600667.translation: To Flavia Rufina, who lived 11 years and 3 months, for whom Valerius Narseus, her husband, and Romanus, her dearest son, made this.
EDCS-23600667.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Rufina, who died at a young age, dedicated by her husband and son, probably in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-23700012.text_edition: \n Nundinae habentur hic in castello Mastarensi die III Kalendarum Septembrium primarum et die III Iduum Septembrium subsequentium et deinceps suo quoque mense ex permissu Marci Aureli Comini Cassiani legati Auggustorum pro praetore clarissimi viri\n
EDCS-23700012.translation: Markets are held here in the fortress of Mastar on the 3rd day before the Kalends of September and on the 3rd day before the Ides of September which follows, and thereafter each month, by permission of Marcus Aurelius Cominus Cassianus, clarissimus vir, legate of the Emperors with proconsular power.
EDCS-23700012.summary: This inscription records market days in the fortress of Mastar, approved by the imperial legate Marcus Aurelius Cominus Cassianus, likely in the late 2nd century CE.

EDCS-23700207.text_edition: \n Genio balineo castelli MASOM a solo quartum Idibus Decembribus Modesto et Probo consulibus\n
EDCS-23700207.translation: To the genius of the bathhouse of the fortress of Maso, from the ground up, on the 4th day before the Ides of December, in the consulship of Modestus and Probus.
EDCS-23700207.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a bathhouse in the fortress of Maso, dating to 236 CE based on the consuls.

EDCS-23701274.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto sacrum conservatori domini nostri [Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli] [Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti] Marcus Clodius Luci filius Quirina Fidus magister quaestura functus amator patriae statuam cum base quam die III Nonarum Ianuariarum sua liberalitate pollicitus est inlatis praeterea rei publicae honorariis summis decurionatus et magistratus et ob eius dedicationem edito die ludorum scaenicorum sua pecunia fecit idemque dedicavit locus datus decreto decurionum Promissa III Nonas Ianuarias Grato [et] [Seleuco] consulibus dedicata [Antonino] [IIII et Alexandro consulibus]\n
EDCS-23701274.translation: Sacred to Hercules the Unconquered, protector of our lord [Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius] Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus. Marcus Clodius Fidus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, having served as quaestor, a lover of his country, made the statue with its base, which he promised with his generosity on the 3rd day of the Nones of January, by contributing moreover sums of money to the state as honoraria for his decurionate and magistracy, and for its dedication, on a day appointed with scenic games, he made it at his own expense and dedicated it himself. The site was granted by decree of the decurions. Promised on the 3rd day of the Nones of January, in the consulship of Gratus and Seleucus, dedicated in the consulship of Antoninus (for the 4th time) and Alexander.
EDCS-23701274.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue to Hercules by Marcus Clodius Fidus in honor of his public service and imperial patronage, likely in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-23701921.translation: Sacred to the Holy Great Mother of the Gods of Ida, made for the safety of [Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius] Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus [[[and Julia Mamaea]]] Augusta, and their divine household. Claudii Basilicus Amphio Mnesius made the criobolium and undertook it themselves, through Caius Aemilius Saturninus, the priest, according to the oracle of the archigallus. The site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-23701921.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a sacred object to the Great Mother of the Gods for the well-being of the imperial family, likely in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24100260.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Sittius Mustius, son of Lucius, lived 71 years.
EDCS-24100260.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Sittius Mustius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24100263.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Manius [name illegible], veteran, lived 85 years.
EDCS-24100263.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Manius, a veteran, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24100274.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Arria Apollinia lived 105 years, she lies here.
EDCS-24100274.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Arria Apollinia, who lived to an exceptionally old age, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24100276.translation: Quintus Cecilius [name illegible] lived 50 years, he lies here.
EDCS-24100276.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Cecilius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200382.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iulia Rustica vixit annos \n
EDCS-24200382.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iulia Rustica lived for [X] years.
EDCS-24200382.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Iulia Rustica.

EDCS-24300271.translation: To Mother Earth, to Cure(s), Mother of the Great Gods, Ideae, Popilia, daughter of Marcus Maxima, erected, moved, and made this altar for the taurobolium.
EDCS-24300271.summary: An altar dedicated to various mother goddesses by Popilia Maxima, likely for a taurobolium ritual, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24400486.text_edition: \n Lunae Augustae sacrum Tuccius Flavius Flavi filius RNI Fortunatus atur\n
EDCS-24400486.translation: Sacred to Luna Augusta. Tuccius Flavius Fortunatus, son of Flavius, [erected this].
EDCS-24400486.summary: A dedication to the goddess Luna Augusta by Tuccius Flavius Fortunatus.

EDCS-24400857.text_edition: \n Termini positi inter Igilgilitanos in quorum finibus kastellum Victoriae positum est et Zimizes ut sciant Zimizes non plus in usum se habere ex auctoritate Marci Vetti Latronis procuratoris Augusti qua in circuitu a muro kastelli passus D provinciae LXXXIX Torquato et Libone consulibus\n
EDCS-24400857.translation: Boundary stones placed between the Igilgilitani, in whose territory the fort of Victoria was established, and the Zimizes, so that the Zimizes know they have no more use from the authority of Marcus Vettius Latro, procurator of the Emperor, than 89 paces from the wall of the fort, under the consulship of Torquatus and Libo.
EDCS-24400857.summary: A boundary marker inscription from 153 AD, defining territory between two groups and referencing the procurator of the Emperor and specific consuls.

EDCS-24500177.text_edition: \n Nymphis Caius Prastina Messalinus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul designatus \n
EDCS-24500177.translation: To the Nymphs. Caius Prastina Messalinus, legate of the Emperor with the power of a proconsul, consul-designate.
EDCS-24500177.summary: An inscription dedicating something to the Nymphs by Caius Prastina Messalinus, an imperial legate and consul-designate.

EDCS-24500180.text_edition: \n Pro salute et victoria domini nostri Imperatoris Luci Septimi Severi Pertenacis sic Augusti e[t] Clodi Albini Caesaris Aesculapem sic et Hygiam sic dedicante Lepido Tertullo legato Augusti pro praetore clarissimo viro Marcus Oppius Antiochianus |(centurio) legionis III Augustae piae v[indicis] posuit et votum solvit\n
EDCS-24500180.translation: For the health and victory of our lord the Emperor Lucius Septimius Pertinax Augustus and Caesar Albinus Caesar, Aesculapius and Hygia [were honored]. Dedicated by Lepidus Tertullus, legate of the Emperor with the power of a proconsul, a most distinguished man. Marcus Oppius Antiochianus, centurion of the III Augusta legion, the pious, the victorious, placed [this] and fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-24500180.summary: A dedication to Aesculapius and Hygia for the health and victory of Emperors Septimius Severus and Clodius Albinus, set up by Marcus Oppius Antiochianus, a centurion, and dedicated by Lepidus Tertullus, a legate.

EDCS-24500268.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani nepote pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate VI consule III patre patriae plateam stratam Caius Prastina Messallinus legatus Augusti pro praetore legionis III Augustae patronus coloniae dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-24500268.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, chief priest, in his sixth year of tribunician power, consul for the third time, father of the fatherland, Caius Prastina Messallinus, legate of the Emperor with the power of a proconsul, patron of the colony, dedicated the paved square by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-24500268.summary: Caius Prastina Messallinus, imperial legate and patron of the colony, dedicated a paved square under Antoninus Pius, funded by the local council.

EDCS-24500271.text_edition: \n Imperatore! Caesare sic divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepote sic divi Nervae pronepote sic Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifice sic maximo tribunicia potestate XV imperatore sic II consule! IIII patre sic patriae Marcus Valerius Etruscus legatus Augusti pro praetore patronus coloniae curiam renovatam et exornatam dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-24500271.translation: Under Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan the Parthian, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, in his fifteenth year of tribunician power, emperor for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland, Marcus Valerius Etruscus, legate of the Emperor with the power of a proconsul, patron of the colony, dedicated the curia, renovated and adorned, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-24500271.summary: Marcus Valerius Etruscus, imperial legate and patron of the colony, dedicated a renovated and adorned curia under Antoninus Pius, funded by the local council.

EDCS-24500272.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepoti divi Nervae pronepoti Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XV imperatori II consuli IIII patri patriae Marcus Valerius Etruscus legatus Augusti pro praetore patronus coloniae dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-24500272.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan the Parthian, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, in his fifteenth year of tribunician power, emperor for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland, Marcus Valerius Etruscus, legate of the Emperor with the power of a proconsul, patron of the colony, dedicated [this] by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-24500272.summary: Marcus Valerius Etruscus, imperial legate and patron of the colony, dedicated something under Antoninus Pius, funded by the local council.

EDCS-24500273.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus legatus consul designatus dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-24500273.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, legate and consul-designate, dedicated [this] by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-24500273.summary: Lucius Matuccius Fuscinus, imperial legate and consul-designate, dedicated something under Antoninus Pius, funded by the local council.

EDCS-24500301.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Armeniaco Parthico Medico Germanico Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXVIII imperatori VII consuli III patri patriae proconsuli divi Antonini Pii filio divi Veri Parthici maximi fratri divi Hadriani nepos sic divi Traiani pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti aquamnetensem Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul patronus coloniae dedicavit decreto decurionum pecunia publica\n
EDCS-24500301.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, the Armenian, Parthian, Median, German, Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 28th time, imperator for the 7th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, son of the deified Antoninus Pius, brother of the deified Verus, conqueror of Parthia, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Marcus Aemilius Macer Saturninus, imperial legate with praetorian authority, consul, and patron of the colony, dedicated this aqueduct from public funds by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-24500301.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an aqueduct to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus by the legate M. Aemilius Macer Saturninus, funded by the colony, likely during Marcus Aurelius' reign (AD 161-180).

EDCS-24500302.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Antonino Caesari imperatori destinato Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici vindicis et conditoris Romanae disciplinae filio divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti decreto decurionum pecunia publica Quintus Anicius Faustus legatus Augustorum pro praetore patronus coloniae dedicavit\n
EDCS-24500302.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, designated emperor, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus, the defender and founder of Roman discipline, grandson of the deified Marcus Aurelius Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Quintus Anicius Faustus, imperial legate with praetorian authority for the Augusti, and patron of the colony, dedicated this by decree of the decurions from public funds.
EDCS-24500302.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to the future Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (later Caracalla) by the legate Q. Anicius Faustus, likely during the reign of Septimius Severus (AD 193-211).

EDCS-24500320.text_edition: \n Sexto Anicio Fausto Anici consularis filio Pauliniano clarissimo puero Quintus Tegonius Latinus Quinti Tegoni Saturnini flaminis perpetui filius patrono\n
EDCS-24500320.translation: To Sextus Anicius Faustus, son of Anicius, a consular man, a most distinguished boy; Quintus Tegonius Latinus, son of Quintus Tegonius Saturninus, perpetual flamen, dedicated this to his patron.
EDCS-24500320.summary: This inscription records a dedication to a young boy, Sextus Anicius Faustus, by Quintus Tegonius Latinus, who was a perpetual flamen and considered Faustus his patron, likely in the 2nd century AD.
