EDCS-13000875.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Fufius Rogatus lived 30 years.
EDCS-13000875.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Fufius Rogatus at the age of 30.

EDCS-13000876.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iunius Felix lived 20 years.
EDCS-13000876.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Iunius Felix at the age of 20.

EDCS-13000877.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iunia Rustica lived 55 years. Her husband made this.
EDCS-13000877.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Iunia Rustica at the age of 55, set up by her husband.

EDCS-13000878.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Lurius Maritimus.
EDCS-13000878.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lurius Maritimus.

EDCS-13000879.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Luri Maritimi lived 18 years. His son made this.
EDCS-13000879.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Luri Maritimi at the age of 18, set up by his son.

EDCS-13000880.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Luria Galla lived 70 years. Pomponii Phosphorus and Saturninus made this for their mother.
EDCS-13000880.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Luria Galla at the age of 70, set up by her sons Pomponii Phosphorus and Saturninus.

EDCS-13000882.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Vergilia Rogata lived 101 years. Marcus Iulius Maximus made this.
EDCS-13000882.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Vergilia Rogata at the age of 101, set up by Marcus Iulius Maximus.

EDCS-13000884.summary: An inscription identifying Plautonius as a priest.

EDCS-13000885.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Flavius Donatus the priest lived 90 years.
EDCS-13000885.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Flavius Donatus, a priest, at the age of 90.

EDCS-13000886.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caius Iulius Expeditus Marax, priest of Saturn, made this in his own lifetime, lived 75 years.
EDCS-13000886.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Caius Iulius Expeditus Marax, a priest of Saturn, during his lifetime, recording his death at the age of 75.

EDCS-13000887.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Arruntius Augustalis lived 120 years and 10 days. His son made this.
EDCS-13000887.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Arruntius Augustalis, commemorating his age and noting that his son erected it.

EDCS-13000888.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Caecilia Ianuaria lived 75 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-13000888.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caecilia Ianuaria, stating her age and burial location.

EDCS-13000889.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Celsa Macri lived 65 years. Her father made this from his own resources for his daughter.
EDCS-13000889.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Celsa Macri, recording her age and indicating it was funded by her father.

EDCS-13000890.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Cornelius Crescens lived 32 years. His parents made this.
EDCS-13000890.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cornelius Crescens, stating his age and that it was made by his parents.

EDCS-13000891.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Fabius Nabor lived 12 years.
EDCS-13000891.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Fabius Nabor, recording his young age.

EDCS-13000892.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Fabia Conducta lived 71 years.
EDCS-13000892.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Fabia Conducta, stating her age.

EDCS-13000893.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Fabia Donata lived 70 years.
EDCS-13000893.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Fabia Donata, recording her age.

EDCS-13000894.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Flavia Maioriana lived 30 years. Her husband made this.
EDCS-13000894.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Maioriana, noting her age and that her husband erected the monument.

EDCS-13000895.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gargilius [---]
EDCS-13000895.summary: This incomplete funerary inscription is dedicated to Gargilius.

EDCS-13000896.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gargilia Donata.
EDCS-13000896.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Gargilia Donata.

EDCS-13000897.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Gargilius Gers.
EDCS-13000897.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Gargilius Gers, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-13000899.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulius Fuscus, aged 40. His heir made this.
EDCS-13000899.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Iulius Fuscus at the age of 40, with his heir setting up the monument.

EDCS-13000900.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulius Maximus lived 63 years.
EDCS-13000900.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Iulius Maximus, who lived to be 63 years old.

EDCS-13000901.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulius Menio lived 30 years.
EDCS-13000901.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Iulius Menio, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-13000902.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulius Maxutus lived 80 years. His heir made this. He is buried here.
EDCS-13000902.summary: This funerary inscription states that Iulius Maxutus lived to 80, and his heir erected the monument and burial.

EDCS-13000903.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulia Venusta lived 77 years. Pulex made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-13000903.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Iulia Venusta at age 77, erected by her husband Pulex.

EDCS-13000904.translation: Macrinius Donatus lived [unknown age]. Secunda lived 66 years.
EDCS-13000904.summary: This inscription records the lifespan of Secunda as 66 years, possibly associated with Macrinius Donatus.

EDCS-13000905.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Mucius Victor, aged [unknown]. He made this for one deserving well.
EDCS-13000905.summary: This funerary inscription honors Mucius Victor, dedicated by an unknown person who considered him deserving.

EDCS-13000907.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Servilia Ingenua lived 53 years.
EDCS-13000907.summary: This funerary inscription records that Servilia Ingenua lived for 53 years.

EDCS-13000925.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Avianius Lucceius, son of Lucius Avianius Lucceius, lived 31 years.
EDCS-13000925.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Lucius Avianius Lucceius, son of Lucius Avianius Lucceius, at the age of 31.

EDCS-13000926.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Curtius Masus lived 54 years and 10 months. Iulia Buturaria made this for her dearest husband.
EDCS-13000926.summary: A funerary epitaph for Curtius Masus, set up by his wife Iulia Buturaria.

EDCS-13000927.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Herennius Iullus lived 55 years; he is buried here. His wife and sons made this.
EDCS-13000927.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Herennius Iullus, set up by his wife and sons.

EDCS-13000928.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Iulia Victorina lived 30 years. Avianius Ianuarius [made this].
EDCS-13000928.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Victorina, likely set up by Avianius Ianuarius.

EDCS-13000929.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Ulpia Salvia lived 100 years.
EDCS-13000929.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ulpia Salvia, recording her long lifespan.

EDCS-13000930.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Sextia Ingenua lived 95 years. She made this herself.
EDCS-13000930.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sextia Ingenua, who lived to 95 and likely erected it herself.

EDCS-13000932.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Hostilius Quintus lived 49 years; he is buried here. A good man.
EDCS-13000932.summary: A funerary epitaph for Hostilius Quintus, noting his age and character.

EDCS-13000951.translation: To Saturn, Augustus. Avianius Narnitas willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13000951.summary: A votive inscription to Saturn, dedicated by Avianius Narnitas.

EDCS-13001383.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Protector and to Juno Augusta Queen, Marcus Gargilius Syrus, an outstanding man and perpetual priest, and his wife Iulia Victoria, have erected this monument with their own liberality and money.
EDCS-13001383.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and Juno by Marcus Gargilius Syrus and his wife Iulia Victoria, who served as perpetual priest.

EDCS-13001387.translation: To Marcus Amullius, son of Marcus, Papirius Optatus Clementianus, Roman knight of singular loyalty, goodness, and generosity, the most distinguished council of the Thagastinians, with money collected by them, [erected this] in honor of whom, at its dedication, he donated 100,000 sesterces for the work of his generosity to his fatherland, and to the curiae, besides feasts and wine and games, 500 denarii each.
EDCS-13001387.summary: An honorary inscription honoring Marcus Amullius Optatus Clementianus, a Roman knight, detailing his generous donations to his fatherland and local communities.

EDCS-13001388.translation: Marcus Amullius r [because of his merit] from the building even if he had seen, for the spectacle, a portico, with an additional 200,000 sesterces to his fatherland, from 300,000 sesterces.
EDCS-13001388.summary: A record of Marcus Amullius's contribution of 300,000 sesterces towards a public building project, including a portico.

EDCS-13001389.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Claudia Rufina, the high priestess, devout and pious, lived 103 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001389.summary: A tombstone for Claudia Rufina, a high priestess who lived 103 years.

EDCS-13001390.translation: To Gaius Flavius Hilarus Felix, son of Gaius Flavius, of the Papiria tribe, a Roman knight. When the most distinguished order of his town, the Thagastenses, with their highest consent, decided to erect a statue in honor of his father and of himself, and in consideration of the public good, on a granted site next to his parents, he arranged for its construction, returning the money which was being offered.
EDCS-13001390.summary: A dedication of a statue by the town council of Thagastenses in honor of Gaius Flavius Hilarus Felix and his father.

EDCS-13001396.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Novia Dativa, a woman of good omen, devout and pious, who lived 35 years; she is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. To Caelius Victoria, a midwife, most rare, devout and pious, who lived 26 years; she is buried here. Caelius Noricus dedicated this to his dearest wife and sister.
EDCS-13001396.summary: A tombstone for Novia Dativa and Caelius Victoria, a midwife, erected by Caelius Noricus.

EDCS-13001397.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aebutia Festa, devout and pious, lived 60 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001397.summary: A tombstone for Aebutia Festa, who lived 60 years.

EDCS-13001398.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Publius Haelvius Octavius Sedatus, devout and pious, lived 17 years.
EDCS-13001398.summary: A tombstone for Publius Haelvius Octavius Sedatus, who lived 17 years.

EDCS-13001399.translation: Marcus Aemilius Thagasius erected and dedicated this gift for eternity to my memory and that of Pollentia Victoria, my dearest wife.
EDCS-13001399.summary: A memorial inscription by Marcus Aemilius Thagasius for himself and his wife Pollentia Victoria.

EDCS-13001401.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Apronius Donatus, devout and pious, lived 33 years; he is buried here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Apronius Apronianus, devout and pious, lived 30 years; he is buried here. Apronius Victor erected this for his dearest brothers.
EDCS-13001401.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Apronius Donatus and Marcus Apronius Apronianus, erected by their brother Apronius Victor.

EDCS-13001402.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Apronia Laeta, devout and pious, lived 60 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001402.summary: A tombstone for Apronia Laeta, who lived 60 years.

EDCS-13001403.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Sextus Aufidius Donatus, devout and pious, lived 35 years.
EDCS-13001403.summary: A tombstone for Sextus Aufidius Donatus, who lived 35 years.

EDCS-13001404.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. B. Privatus lived 80 years. B. Ianuaria made this for her father at her own expense; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001404.summary: A tombstone for B. Privatus, who lived 80 years, erected by his daughter B. Ianuaria.

EDCS-13001405.translation: Gaius Caecilius Pythagora lived piously for 65 years.
EDCS-13001405.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Gaius Caecilius Pythagora.

EDCS-13001407.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Ingeniosa lived for 4 years and is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Dioscorus lived for 53 years and is buried here.
EDCS-13001407.summary: This is a tombstone recording the deaths of Ingeniosa and Dioscorus, likely family members, with accompanying epithets.

EDCS-13001408.translation: Tullius Martialis made this for his parents.
EDCS-13001408.summary: This inscription records Tullius Martialis dedicating a monument to his parents.

EDCS-13001409.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Iulia Prima lived piously for 45 years. Gaius Caecilius Pythagora erected this for his devoted wife.
EDCS-13001409.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Iulia Prima, erected by her husband Gaius Caecilius Pythagora.

EDCS-13001410.summary: This is a simple epitaph identifying Nampame as the daughter of Felicis.

EDCS-13001411.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Quintus Praecilius Genialis lived piously for 81 years and is buried here. Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Caecilia Libosa lived piously for 75 years and is buried here. Praecilius Buturus for his parents.
EDCS-13001411.summary: This is a tombstone recording the deaths of Quintus Praecilius Genialis and Caecilia Libosa, likely family members, and a dedication by Praecilius Buturus.

EDCS-13001412.translation: SA tius Sedatus lived piously for 71 years and is buried here.
EDCS-13001412.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan and burial of SA tius Sedatus.

EDCS-13001413.translation: Sedeni, daughter of Musti, lived piously for 57 years and is buried here.
EDCS-13001413.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan and burial of Sedeni, daughter of Musti.

EDCS-13001414.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Statia Paulina lived piously for 67 years and is buried here.
EDCS-13001414.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan and burial of Statia Paulina.

EDCS-13001415.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Vettius Maximus lived piously for 75 years and is buried here.
EDCS-13001415.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan and burial of Lucius Vettius Maximus.

EDCS-13001417.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Apronia Vindemia lived 45 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001417.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Apronia Vindemia, recording her age at death and burial.

EDCS-13001437.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Amaia lived 59 years.
EDCS-13001437.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Amaia, recording her age at death.

EDCS-13001441.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Aug Sadavis, son of Numida, of Misictri, dutiful, lived 60 years.
EDCS-13001441.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aug Sadavis, son of Numida, son of Misictri, recording his age at death and mentioning his filial piety.

EDCS-13001442.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Quintus Antonius Gemellus lived 55 years. Maxima is buried here with her husband.
EDCS-13001442.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Antonius Gemellus, recording his age, and also indicating that his wife Maxima is buried with him.

EDCS-13001717.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Aufidia Euthicia lived 40 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001717.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aufidia Euthicia, recording her age at death and burial.

EDCS-13001718.translation: Copronia Praeiecta lived 17 years; Titus Copronius is buried here.
EDCS-13001718.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Copronia Praeiecta, recording her age and mentioning that Titus Copronius is buried there, likely her father or a related family member.

EDCS-13001719.translation: Gaius Cornelius Quirina Catanus, having completed his life, received a seat as a reward for the service of a good father. Cornelia Spes gave [this]. Let her be crowned in this way, with her full name and designation, as she deserved, so that by the will of the goddess Fortuna, she might be transformed into a powerful daughter. Farewell.
EDCS-13001719.summary: This is an epitaph for Gaius Cornelius Quirina Catanus, dedicated by Cornelia Spes, who rewarded his virtues and hopes for his apotheosis, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-13001720.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. To Tito Pomponio, son of Lade, of the Quirina tribe.
EDCS-13001720.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Tito Pomponio, son of Lade, of the Quirina tribe.

EDCS-13001721.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Lucius Corvinius, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, Flaminalis, lived 71 years. Sacred to the Divine Manes. Agrinia Victoria, daughter of Agrini Donati, lived 47 years. We were joined as equals, separated, and I never lived a good life with my husband. After my husband died, it seemed right that this place be his; here he is buried.
EDCS-13001721.summary: This is a double funerary epitaph for Lucius Corvinius and Agrinia Victoria, recording their ages, tribal affiliations, and Agrinia's personal reflections on her life with her deceased husband.

EDCS-13001724.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Annio Primo Iuniori, Roman knight of the Quirina tribe, consecrated for his integrity, gravity of morals, and Roman simplicity. He lived 26 years and 1 month. [---]atus Ianuarius, son of Saxentius.
EDCS-13001724.summary: This funerary inscription honors Annio Primo Iuniori, a Roman knight, who died at the age of 26.

EDCS-13001725.translation: Iulius Selicius, son of Clarus, lived 55 years.
EDCS-13001725.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Iulius Selicius.

EDCS-13001727.translation: In the consulship of Laetus and Cerealis, the day before the Kalends of April. Sacred to Bacacus Augustus. Lucius Cessius Rogatianus and Lucius Sittius Libosus, magistrates of the Thibilitani.
EDCS-13001727.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Bacacus Augustus made by the magistrates of the Thibilitani during the consulship of Laetus and Cerealis.

EDCS-13001728.translation: Sacred to Bacacus Augustus. In the consulship of Fuscus II and Dexter, the day before the Kalends of April. Marcus Popilius Vitalis and Lucius Pomponius Thigianus, magistrates of the Thibilitani.
EDCS-13001728.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Bacacus Augustus made by the magistrates of the Thibilitani during the consulship of Fuscus II and Dexter.

EDCS-13001729.translation: In the consulship of Severus and Quintianus, the day before the Kalends of April. Sacred to Bacacus Augustus. Publius Aufidius Buturicus and Caecilianus, magistrates of the Thibilitani.
EDCS-13001729.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Bacacus Augustus made by the magistrates of the Thibilitani during the consulship of Severus and Quintianus.

EDCS-13001730.translation: In the consulship of Sabinus and Venustus, the day before the Kalends of April. Sacred to Bacacus Augustus. Lucius Herennius Verulus and Iulius Severianus, magistrates of the Thibilitani.
EDCS-13001730.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Bacacus Augustus made by the magistrates of the Thibilitani during the consulship of Sabinus and Venustus.

EDCS-13001731.translation: Sacred to Bacacus Augustus. In the consulship of Sabinianus and Praetextatus, on the Kalends of May. Publius Manilius Victor, perpetual flamen, magistrate of the Dothensium.
EDCS-13001731.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Bacacus Augustus made by Publius Manilius Victor, perpetual flamen and magistrate of the Dothensium, during the consulship of Sabinianus and Praetextatus.

EDCS-13001732.translation: Sacred to Bacacus Augustus. In the consulship of Arrianus and Papus, on the Kalends of May. Quintus Iulius Honoratus, magistrates of the Dothensium and Thibilitanorum.
EDCS-13001732.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Bacacus Augustus made by Quintus Iulius Honoratus, magistrate of the Dothensium and Thibilitanorum, during the consulship of Arrianus and Papus.

EDCS-13001733.translation: Sacred to Bacacus Augustus. In the consulship of Praesens and Albinus, on the Kalends of May. [---]ius Ianuarius, magistrate of the Dothensium.
EDCS-13001733.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Bacacus Augustus made by [---]ius Ianuarius, magistrate of the Dothensium, during the consulship of Praesens and Albinus.

EDCS-13001735.translation: Sacred to Bacacus Augustus. In the consulship of Marinianus and Paternus. Gennius Marc and Flavius Marsa II, of the Thibilitanorum, fulfilled their vow with a willing heart.
EDCS-13001735.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Bacacus Augustus by Gennius Marc and Flavius Marsa II, members of the Thibilitani, during the consulship of Marinianus and Paternus.

EDCS-13001736.translation: Sacred to the deified Augustus. In the consulship of Paterno and Mariniano, Cornelius Donatus gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13001736.summary: This is a dedication to the deified Augustus by Cornelius Donatus, dated by consuls.

EDCS-13001737.translation: In the consulship of Aurelianus III and Marcellinus, Hirrius Vitalis, as duovir, with his colleague Aemilius.
EDCS-13001737.summary: This inscription records the tenure of office of Hirrius Vitalis as duovir alongside his colleague Aemilius, dated by consuls.

EDCS-13001738.translation: In the consulship of Probus III and Paterno II, on the third day before the Kalends of March. Eopraxius and Claudius Ceriolus Titus Iulius.
EDCS-13001738.summary: This inscription lists names and a date, possibly related to a public office or transaction, dated by consuls.

EDCS-13001739.translation: Sacred to the deified Augustus. In the consulship of the Emperor IV and Balbinus, on the day before the Kalends of April. Quintus Sergius Honoratus and Lucius Bruttius Felicissimus, masters of the Thibilitani.
EDCS-13001739.summary: This is a dedication to the deified Augustus by the magistri of the Thibilitani, dated by consuls.

EDCS-13001740.translation: Sacred to Augustus. Marcus Turulius Honoratus and Sitius Paratus, masters of the Thibilitani.
EDCS-13001740.summary: This is a dedication to Augustus by the magistri of the Thibilitani.

EDCS-13001741.translation: In the consulship of Marcus Claudius Tacitus, on the Kalends of April. Seius Victor, perpetual flamen, master of the Dothensium, gladly fulfilled his vow according to merit.
EDCS-13001741.summary: This is a dedication by Seius Victor, perpetual flamen and master of the Dothensium, dated by consuls.

EDCS-13001743.translation: Sacred to the August Goddess Bellona. Fortis Caesaris gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13001743.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Bellona by Fortis Caesaris.

EDCS-13001745.translation: From the property of Venustus. Lucius Laelius Maximus, Caius Memius Apronianus, Sextus Lollius Rogatus, Caius Iulius Faustianus, Papius Pullus Arator, Dabaris, Valerius Martialis, Urbanillus, Gutruris, Sittius Matellio, Decimius Proculus, Barneus Crescens.
EDCS-13001745.summary: This inscription lists several names, possibly landowners or members of a collegium, with a reference to Venustus.

EDCS-13001746.translation: To Marcus Lollius Senecio, father; to Grania Honorata, mother; to Lucius Lollius Senio, brother; to Marcus Lollius Honoratus, brother; to Publius Granius Paulus, great-uncle. Quintus Lollius Urbicus, prefect of the city. [Repeated names and titles follow].
EDCS-13001746.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication to various family members by Quintus Lollius Urbicus, prefect of the city, with repeated entries.

EDCS-13001748.translation: Sittius Marinus of the Silian cavalry troop lived 35 years.
EDCS-13001748.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittius Marinus, a soldier in the Silian cavalry, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-13001749.translation: Rupilia Marcella, daughter of Lucius, priestess of Ceres.
EDCS-13001749.summary: This is a dedication by Rupilia Marcella, priestess of Ceres.

EDCS-13001750.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sittius Ianuarius, priest of Ceres, lived 100 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-13001750.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sittius Ianuarius, priest of Ceres, who died at the age of 100.

EDCS-13001751.translation: To Quinto Sittio Fausto, son of Quinto, of the Quirina tribe, who held the office of III vir, prefect with judicial powers in the colony of Veneria Rusicade, and in the colony of Sarnia Mileu, and in the colony of Minerva Chullu, and aedile. Erected by the townspeople, by decree of the decurions, for his merits, with a communal contribution.
EDCS-13001751.summary: This is a public monument honoring Quinto Sittio Fausto, a local official, erected by his community.

EDCS-13001752.translation: To Apronia Fida, daughter of Sextus, wife of Quintus Sittius Faustus Probatus, who was appointed to the decuries by the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, and also by the divine Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius as a perpetual flamen; who served as III vir quinquennalis, as prefect with judicial powers in the colony of Veneria Rusicade, as prefect with judicial powers in the colony of Sarnia Mileu, and as prefect with judicial powers in the colony of Minerva Chullu; and as aedile. Her husband's friends, by decree of the decurions, with a communal contribution, granted this place because of his merits towards them.
EDCS-13001752.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Apronia Fida, wife of Quintus Sittius Faustus Probatus, honoring her husband's extensive civic career and his merits.

EDCS-13001753.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Sittius Urbanus, son of Caius, of the Quirina tribe, aedile with quaestorian power, quaestor, lived 92 years. He is buried here.

EDCS-13001882.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Fillecius.

EDCS-13001884.translation: To the Divine Shades, Lania CTI.
EDCS-13001884.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the spirits of the departed for Lania CTI.

EDCS-13001886.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of lius Quetus.

EDCS-13001887.translation: L OPI lived 70 years; may your bones rest well.
EDCS-13001887.summary: This is a funerary epitaph with a hopeful wish for the deceased L OPI.

EDCS-13001888.translation: L M L Marcellus lived 35 years.
EDCS-13001888.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of L M L Marcellus.

EDCS-13001889.translation: Mienisius lived 14 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001889.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Mienisius.

EDCS-13001890.translation: To the Divine Shades, NE R IA V A NEP lived 80 years.
EDCS-13001890.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the spirits of the departed for NE R IA V A NEP, recording her age at death.

EDCS-13001891.translation: Mustio lived 105 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001891.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of Mustio.

EDCS-13001893.translation: Sacred to the Genius of Caldis, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-13001893.summary: This is a public dedication to the Genius of Caldis, funded by the local council.

EDCS-13001894.translation: AIOAIIASIVIIIA, daughter of Rogatus, lived [---] years.
EDCS-13001894.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age at death of AIOAIIASIVIIIA, daughter of Rogatus.

EDCS-13001895.translation: Aelia Casta lived 30 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001895.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aelia Casta, who lived 30 years.

EDCS-13001896.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Aemilius Martialis, who lived 18 years.

EDCS-13001897.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aemilius Sattarus, who lived 55 years.

EDCS-13001898.translation: Aemilius Severus lived 25 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001898.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aemilius Severus, who lived 25 years.

EDCS-13001899.translation: Quinta Aemilia lived 35 years; may her bones be well.
EDCS-13001899.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quinta Aemilia, who lived 35 years.

EDCS-13001900.translation: To the Divine Spirits, Aemilia Secunda.
EDCS-13001900.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the Divine Spirits for Aemilia Secunda.

EDCS-13001901.translation: Aemilia Extricata lived 95 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001901.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aemilia Extricata, who lived 95 years.

EDCS-13001902.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aurelius Mazambra Fittavis lived 27 years; Nedifa made this for her dearest brother.
EDCS-13001902.summary: This is a funerary epitaph and dedication, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, where Nedifa set up the monument for her brother Aurelius Mazambra Fittavis.

EDCS-13001903.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Basilius Ugusalis, who lived 14 years.

EDCS-13001904.translation: To the Divine Spirits, Caecilia Fecialis lived 20 years.
EDCS-13001904.summary: This is a funerary epitaph and dedication for Caecilia Fecialis, who lived 20 years.

EDCS-13001905.translation: To the Divine Spirits, Caelius Ianuarius lived 51 years.
EDCS-13001905.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Caelius Ianuarius.

EDCS-13001906.translation: Quintus Carnius Rufus lived 35 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001906.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial place of Quintus Carnius Rufus.

EDCS-13001907.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Carnia Pocosa.

EDCS-13001909.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Cilius Honoratus.

EDCS-13001910.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Cilia.

EDCS-13001911.translation: To the Divine Spirits, Quintus Cladius Rusticus lived 40 years; he is buried here. May his bones rest well.
EDCS-13001911.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial place of Quintus Cladius Rusticus, with a wish for his repose.

EDCS-13001912.translation: To the Divine Spirits, Cornelius Compes lived 8 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001912.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial place of Cornelius Compes, a child.

EDCS-13001913.translation: Clara lived 70 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001913.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial place of Clara.

EDCS-13001914.translation: To the Divine Spirits, Cornelius Cornelianus lived 75 years.
EDCS-13001914.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Cornelius Cornelianus.

EDCS-13001915.translation: Flavia lived 55 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001915.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial place of Flavia.

EDCS-13001916.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaetulicus lived 30 years, he is buried here.
EDCS-13001916.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, age at death, and burial of Gaetulicus.

EDCS-13001917.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gellius Gemelus lived 80 years.
EDCS-13001917.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name and age at death of Gellius Gemelus.

EDCS-13001918.summary: A sepulchral inscription giving the name and filiation of Aulus Gennius.

EDCS-13001920.translation: Quintus Iulius, son of Quintus, of the Voting Tribe Quirina.
EDCS-13001920.summary: An inscription recording the name, filiation, and tribal affiliation of Quintus Iulius.

EDCS-13001921.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Sextus Iulius Aelius lived 35 years, he is buried here.
EDCS-13001921.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, age at death, and burial of Sextus Iulius Aelius.

EDCS-13001922.translation: Caius Iulius Balbus lived 100 years, he is buried.
EDCS-13001922.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, age at death, and burial of Caius Iulius Balbus.

EDCS-13001923.translation: Caius Iulius Consors lived 35 years, he is buried here. May his bones rest well.
EDCS-13001923.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, age at death, burial, and a wish for the deceased.

EDCS-13001924.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Iulius Marcianus lived 85 years.
EDCS-13001924.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name and age at death of Marcus Iulius Marcianus.

EDCS-13001925.translation: Lucius Iulius Severus lived 25 years, he is buried here.
EDCS-13001925.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name, age at death, and burial of Lucius Iulius Severus.

EDCS-13001926.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Iulius Urbanus lived 51 years.
EDCS-13001926.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name and age at death of Marcus Iulius Urbanus.

EDCS-13001927.translation: Iulia Biricusa, daughter of Quintus, aged 33, is laid here.
EDCS-13001927.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Biricusa, who died at the age of 33.

EDCS-13001928.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Flavia, daughter of Lucius.

EDCS-13001929.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Honorata, who lived for 35 years.

EDCS-13001930.translation: Iulia Maxima, daughter of Publius, lived 63 years, is laid here.
EDCS-13001930.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Maxima, daughter of Publius, who died at the age of 63.

EDCS-13001931.summary: This is a tombstone for Iulia Rogata, who lived for 30 years.

EDCS-13001932.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Iulia Saturna lived 45 years.
EDCS-13001932.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated to the spirits of the underworld for Iulia Saturna, who lived for 45 years.

EDCS-13001933.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Iulia Severa lived 81 years, is laid here.
EDCS-13001933.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated to the spirits of the underworld for Iulia Severa, who died at the age of 81.

EDCS-13001934.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Licinia Mustia lived 55 years.
EDCS-13001934.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated to the spirits of the underworld for Licinia Mustia, who lived for 55 years.

EDCS-13001935.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Mustia lived 55 years, is laid here.
EDCS-13001935.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated to the spirits of the underworld for Mustia, who died at the age of 55.

EDCS-13001936.summary: This is a tombstone for Lollia Matutina, who lived for 77 years.

EDCS-13001937.translation: Lucius Quintus lived 50 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001937.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Quintus, recording his name and lifespan.

EDCS-13001938.translation: M. Gargilia lived 90 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001938.summary: This is a funerary inscription for M. Gargilia, recording her name and lifespan.

EDCS-13001939.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Nasidia Rogatilla lived 10 years.
EDCS-13001939.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Nasidia Rogatilla, recording her name and lifespan.

EDCS-13001940.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Vitusnus Natalis, recording his name and lifespan.

EDCS-13001941.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Natilla Marcela, recording her name and lifespan.

EDCS-13001942.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Paconius DI.
EDCS-13001942.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Paconius, possibly with an incomplete name or title.

EDCS-13001943.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Paconius Seneca, recording his name and lifespan.

EDCS-13001944.translation: Quintus Percenius Secundus lived 17 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001944.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Percenius Secundus, recording his name and lifespan.

EDCS-13001945.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Pescennius Victor lived [---] years.
EDCS-13001945.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Pescennius Victor, whose lifespan is incomplete.

EDCS-13001946.translation: Publius Pomponius Sodalis lived 30 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001946.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Pomponius Sodalis, recording his name and lifespan.

EDCS-13001947.translation: Rennia Claudia lived 31 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001947.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial of Rennia Claudia.

EDCS-13001948.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Quintus Scribonius Optatus lived 42 years.
EDCS-13001948.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed, recording the lifespan of Quintus Scribonius Optatus.

EDCS-13001949.translation: Lucius Sempronius Severus lived 9 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001949.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial of Lucius Sempronius Severus.

EDCS-13001950.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Sempronia Rogata lived 25 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001950.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed, recording the lifespan and burial of Sempronia Rogata.

EDCS-13001951.translation: Daughter of Severus lived 35 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001951.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial of a woman identified only as the daughter of Severus.

EDCS-13001952.translation: Lucius Sittius Extricatus, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, lived 81 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001952.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan and burial of Lucius Sittius Extricatus, noting his father and tribal affiliation.

EDCS-13001953.summary: An incomplete funerary epitaph recording the beginning of the name Quintus Sittius Martialis and his lifespan, which is cut off.

EDCS-13001954.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Lucius Sittius Nampulus lived 25 years.
EDCS-13001954.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed, recording the lifespan of Lucius Sittius Nampulus.

EDCS-13001955.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Sittius Severus lived 82 years.
EDCS-13001955.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to the departed, recording the lifespan of Sittius Severus.

EDCS-13001956.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Titius Martialis.

EDCS-13001957.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Titia Pruptiva lived for 30 years.
EDCS-13001957.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span of Titia Pruptiva.

EDCS-13001958.translation: Titinia Rufina, daughter of Publius, lived for 27 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001958.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titinia Rufina, daughter of Publius, indicating her burial place.

EDCS-13001959.translation: Quintus Iullius, son of Caius, lived for 10 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001959.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Iullius, son of Caius, indicating his burial place and age.

EDCS-13001960.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Marcus Valerius Musolus lived for 51 years.
EDCS-13001960.summary: A funerary epitaph commemorating the life of Marcus Valerius Musolus.

EDCS-13001961.translation: Aulus Virius Victor lived for 51 years.
EDCS-13001961.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span of Aulus Virius Victor.

EDCS-13001962.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Volummia Urbana lived for 80 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-13001962.summary: A funerary epitaph for Volummia Urbana, stating her age and burial location.

EDCS-13001963.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Totabisila lived for 101 years.
EDCS-13001963.summary: A funerary epitaph noting the exceptional longevity of Totabisila.

EDCS-13001964.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Quintus Preconius lived for 51 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001964.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Preconius, specifying his age and burial site.

EDCS-13001965.translation: To the spirits of the departed, ANTIATOI lived for 55 years.
EDCS-13001965.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life span of someone named ANTIATOI.

EDCS-13001966.translation: Hadrianus lived for 70 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-13001966.summary: A funerary epitaph for Hadrianus, indicating his age and burial place.

EDCS-13001967.translation: To the Divine Manes. Lucius Memius Novelus lived 31 years.
EDCS-13001967.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Memius Novelus, who died at the age of 31.

EDCS-13001968.translation: To the Divine Manes. Varilia Fausta lived 101 years.
EDCS-13001968.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Varilia Fausta, who died at the age of 101.

EDCS-13001969.translation: Sacred to Caelestis Augusta. Titus Horatius Martialis, in fulfillment of a vision concerning his head, offered this gift on the Nones of May.
EDCS-13001969.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Caelestis Augusta by Titus Horatius Martialis.

EDCS-13001970.translation: Sacred to Castor Augustus. Lucius Calpurnius Successianus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, curator of the dendrophori, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13001970.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Castor Augustus by Lucius Calpurnius Successianus, curator of the dendrophori.

EDCS-13001971.translation: Sacred to Pollux Augustus. Lucius Calpurnius Successianus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, curator of the dendrophori, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13001971.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Pollux Augustus by Lucius Calpurnius Successianus, curator of the dendrophori.

EDCS-13001972.translation: Sacred to Concordia of the Cirtensian colonies. Caius Iulius Barbarus, son of Caius, of the Quirina tribe, quaestor and aedile, erected this statue at his own expense, which he had promised for the honor of his aedileship; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13001972.summary: This is a public dedication by Caius Iulius Barbarus, aedile and former quaestor, of a statue to Concordia.

EDCS-13001973.translation: Sacred to Fortuna Caelestis. Publius Paconius Cerialis fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13001973.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Fortuna Caelestis by Publius Paconius Cerialis.

EDCS-13001974.translation: Sacred to Fortuna Redux Augusta, for the safety and most fortunate return of our Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, most brave and most fortunate prince, and of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, and of Lucius Septimius Geta Nobilissimus Caesar, most dutiful sons of our Augustus, and of Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor and Caesar and the camp and of all their divine house. Caius Sittius Flavianus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, aedile for the third time, prefect of the colonies, for the honor of his third term as official, dedicated this, having also presented at the appropriate times the sums of twenty thousand sesterces for each of the highest public honors, and for the dedication of so great a deity, he also provided stage plays for the people at his own expense, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13001974.summary: This is a comprehensive public dedication by Caius Sittius Flavianus, aedile and prefect, to Fortuna Redux, for the safety of the imperial family, including a donation and games.

EDCS-13001975.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the Augustan household. Telesphorus willingly dedicated a small altar.
EDCS-13001975.summary: This is a private dedication of a small altar to the Genius of the Augustan household by Telesphorus.

EDCS-13001976.translation: Public vows for the Genius of our Lord Julianus Augustus.
EDCS-13001976.summary: This inscription records public vows made for the Genius of Julianus Augustus.

EDCS-13001977.translation: To the Genius of the People, Gaius Pontius Saturninus, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe, erected at his own expense the statue which he had promised in honor of his aedileship; at its dedication, he also presented stage games with gladiatorial combats, a site having been granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13001977.summary: This inscription records a statue erected by Gaius Pontius Saturninus in honor of his aedileship, with games provided at its dedication, funded by himself and approved by the local council.

EDCS-13001978.translation: To the Genius of the People, Marcus Roccius Felix, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, holding public equestrian rank, III vir, priest of the city, and flamen of the deified Marcus Antoninus, erected at his own expense the statue which he had promised in honor of his III virate, costing 6,000 sesterces; at its dedication, he also gave single denarii as gifts to the citizens according to the public register and likewise presented stage games with gladiatorial combats.
EDCS-13001978.summary: This inscription records a statue erected by Marcus Roccius Felix, a prominent local official and priest, in honor of his term as III vir, including a significant financial contribution and public distributions.

EDCS-13001981.translation: Sacred to the Honor and August Power. Quintus Domitius Primianus and his wife Iulia Fortunula, his candidate, having been warned by a divinity, offered this votive gift.
EDCS-13001981.summary: This inscription records a sacred offering dedicated to Honor and August Power by Quintus Domitius Primianus and his wife, likely after a divine omen.

EDCS-13001982.translation: A willing vow, deservedly offered. Hieron.
EDCS-13001982.summary: This is a simple votive inscription by Hieron, indicating a freely given vow.

EDCS-13001983.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Augustus. The altar, which C...
EDCS-13001983.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Augustus, recording an altar.

EDCS-13001984.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Quintus Sittius Victor, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, the perpetual flamen, erected this.
EDCS-13001984.summary: This inscription records an altar or monument dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Quintus Sittius Victor, a perpetual flamen.

EDCS-13001985.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, and to the gods and goddesses, and to the Great Mother of the Gods, the Idaean Mother, and to Apollo. Marcus Cocceius Anicius Faustus Flavianus, a man of consular rank, and [---]
EDCS-13001985.summary: This inscription is a dedication to multiple deities, including Jupiter, various goddesses, the Great Mother of the Gods, and Apollo, by Marcus Cocceius Anicius Faustus Flavianus.

EDCS-13001986.translation: Sacred to Neptune Augustus. Marcus Licinius Ianuarius, curator of the elones, gave this altar as a gift in his year, along with gifts.
EDCS-13001986.summary: This inscription records an altar donated to Neptune Augustus by Marcus Licinius Ianuarius, a curator, during his term.

EDCS-13001991.translation: Saturninus Domitius Primianus and his family made this for the ministers of the god Saturn.
EDCS-13001991.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Saturninus Domitius Primianus and his family for the servants of the god Saturn.

EDCS-13001993.translation: Sacred to the August Power of Silvanus. Iulius Honoratus made this at his own expense with a willing spirit for 140 sesterces, by decree of the decurions, and he himself dedicated it.
EDCS-13001993.summary: This inscription records a sacred offering to the August Power of Silvanus, funded by Iulius Honoratus and approved by the decurions.

EDCS-13001995.translation: To Venus Augusta, Lucius Julius Martialius, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, former III vir of the aedilician and quaestorian rank, gave a bronze statue of Venus with her temple and Cupids, out of the generosity of Lucius Julius Martialius, his father, in addition to another gift for Rome Eternal which he had dedicated in the name of his brother Victor, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13001995.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a bronze statue and temple to Venus Augusta by Lucius Julius Martialius, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE, based on the mention of senatorial career ranks.

EDCS-13001996.summary: This is a simple maker's inscription by Lucius Julius Martialius, likely related to the previous inscription.

EDCS-13001998.translation: To the Unconquered Augustus, Gaius Amullius Pultarius fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13001998.summary: This inscription records Gaius Amullius Pultarius fulfilling a vow to the Unconquered Augustus, likely dating to the period of imperial cult prominence.

EDCS-13001999.translation: Sacred to Imperator Caesar, son of the divine Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the divine Commodus, grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, father of the country, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 12th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 3rd time, propagator of the empire, the most brave and most fortunate of princes; and to Imperator Caesar, son of the divine Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus and the divine Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, consul designate for the 2nd time, proconsul, and to Lucius Septimius Geta nobilissimus Caesar, prince of the youth, son and brother of our lords Severus and Antoninus, the most sacred, most brave, most fortunate, and most indulgent emperors.
EDCS-13001999.summary: This lengthy inscription records the imperial titles and lineage of Septimius Severus and Caracalla, likely dating to Severus' 11th acclamation as imperator and 12th tribunician power, placing it around 204 CE.

EDCS-13002002.translation: Publicius Callidromus, freedman, dedicated a window as a vow.
EDCS-13002002.summary: This inscription records a freedman, Publicius Callidromus, dedicating a window as a votive offering.

EDCS-13002003.translation: Publicius Renatus, a home-born slave, gave this gift to the Genius of the place.
EDCS-13002003.summary: This inscription records Publicius Renatus, a slave, making a dedication to the Genius of the place.

EDCS-13002004.translation: Publilius Ceionius Caecina Albinus [dedicated] a cave with statues and decorations.
EDCS-13002004.summary: This inscription records Publilius Ceionius Caecina Albinus dedicating a decorated cave, likely a grotto or nymphaeum.

EDCS-13002005.translation: Quintus Seius Eutices, a forester, gladly dedicated his vow.
EDCS-13002005.summary: This inscription records Quintus Seius Eutices, identified as a forester, dedicating a vow with willing spirit.

EDCS-13002008.translation: The temple was dedicated in the consulship of Lucius Venuleius Apronianus II and Lucius Sergius Paullus II, on the 21st day before the Kalends of March, which day was the 18th day before the Kalends of March.
EDCS-13002008.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple on a specific date, noted by its consuls Lucius Venuleius Apronianus and Lucius Sergius Paullus, and provides a precise calendar reference.

EDCS-13002009.translation: To the powers of Providence and Didia Luc[ia?].
EDCS-13002009.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the deities Providentia and Didia Lucia, likely a personal devotion.

EDCS-13002013.translation: Pollicitus dedicated on the 19th day before the Kalends of February, in the consulship of Senecio and Aemilianus, on the 3rd day before the Nones of January, in the same consulship.
EDCS-13002013.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by someone named Pollicitus, with specific dates and consular years noted, likely related to a religious or public act.

EDCS-13002016.translation: To the Emperor Nerva Trajan Caesar Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, father of the fatherland, chief priest.
EDCS-13002016.summary: This is a dedication or an honorary title inscribed for Emperor Trajan, highlighting his achievements and titles, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-13002024.translation: To the deified Commodus, son of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius, victor of Germany and Sarmatia, brother of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus, propagator of the empire, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 5th time, Imperator for the 10th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, uncle of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, designated Emperor: Marcus Verus erected the statue which he had promised in his aedileship with the presentation of games, along with Lucius Julius Martialis, his nephew, and Marcus Sempronius Risticinus, his heirs; the location was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13002024.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue promised by Marcus Verus during his aedileship, funded by his heirs, in honor of Emperor Commodus and then subsequently in relation to Emperor Septimius Severus and his designated heir, with the site granted by local authorities.

EDCS-13002030.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, chief priest, father of the fatherland, most valiant prince, and the Emperor Caesar Publius Septimius Geta Pius Augustus Britannicus, most valiant prince, most fortunate and most indulgent of all princes, son of the deified Severus Pius Felix Augustus: Marcus Seius Maximus, son of Gnaeus, of the Quirina tribe, honored with the public horse, for the honor of his office as aedile, erected a statue with a tetrastylo and theatrical games with gifts, which he presented in honor when he dedicated it, in addition to 20,000 sesterces, which he contributed to the state for the honor of his aedileship, and dedicated it; the location was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13002030.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue, a tetrastyle, and public games by Marcus Seius Maximus, an official, for the safety of Emperors Caracalla and Geta, and also mentions significant financial contributions made by him during his aedileship, with the site approved by the local council.

EDCS-13002034.translation: To our lord Lucius Domitius Alexander Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, restorer of public liberty and propagator of the entire human race and the Roman name: Scironius Pasicrates, a most perfect man, governor of the provinces of Numidia, and Valerius Antoninus, a most perfect man, governor of the provinces of Numidia and Mauretania.
EDCS-13002034.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Lucius Domitius Alexander as a restorer of public liberty and propagator of the Roman name, with dedications by two provincial governors, Scironius Pasicrates and Valerius Antoninus.

EDCS-13002035.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantine Pius Felix Invictus and always Augustus, author of perpetual security and liberty: Iallius Antiochus, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-13002035.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great by Iallius Antiochus, a provincial governor of Numidia, praising him as the author of perpetual security and liberty.

EDCS-13002036.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantine Maximus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, triumphant over all nations and conqueror of all factions, who illuminated with his victorious and new light the liberty oppressed by the darkness of servitude: Valerius Paulus, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, dedicated with devoted mind to his divine majesty.
EDCS-13002036.summary: This inscription dedicates a monument to Emperor Constantine the Great by Valerius Paulus, governor of Numidia, celebrating his victories and role in restoring liberty.

EDCS-13002039.translation: To the always Augustus: Vettius Florentinus, a most perfect man, rationalis of Numidia and Mauretania, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-13002039.summary: This inscription is a devotion to an unnamed 'always Augustus', likely Emperor Constantine, by Vettius Florentinus, who held the position of rationalis in Numidia and Mauretania.

EDCS-13002040.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantine, most victorious and greatest Augustus, restorer of liberty and preserver of the entire world: Julius Juvenalis, a most perfect man, rationalis of Numidia and Mauretania, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-13002040.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantine the Great as a restorer of liberty and preserver of the world, dedicated by Julius Juvenalis, the rationalis of Numidia and Mauretania.

EDCS-13002041.translation: To the perpetual victory of our lords the Emperors Constantine Maximus, the triumphant always Augustus, and Constantine, and Constantius, and Constantis, most noble and flourishing Caesars: Clodius Celsinus, a most distinguished man, consular of the province of Numidia, devoted always to their divine majesty.
EDCS-13002041.summary: This inscription is dedicated to the perpetual victory of Emperors Constantine the Great and his sons Constantine, Constantius, and Constantis, by Clodius Celsinus, the consular governor of Numidia.

EDCS-13002042.translation: For his merits towards them and the province, namely outstanding continence, patience, fortitude, and exceptional love for all, the council and people of the fortunate colony of Constantina and of the province of Numidia erected this statue to Ceionius Italicus, a most distinguished and consular man, their patron, in recognition of the generosity of our perpetual Lords, the August Emperors Constans and Constantius.
EDCS-13002042.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue by the council and people of Constantina and Numidia to Ceionius Italicus, honoring his merits as their patron. It dates to the period of the emperors Constans and Constantius (mid-4th century CE).

EDCS-13002043.translation: By order of our venerable Lords, the Emperors Constans and Constantius, the council and people of the colony of Milevum erected this bronze statue to Ceionius Italicus, a most distinguished and consular man, honored for his singular continence, integrity, patience, fairness, and distinguished service to him and the province. It was placed in the forum of the city of Constantina, where they judged it most fitting to erect it, to their patron.
EDCS-13002043.summary: This inscription records the erection of a bronze statue by the council and people of Milevum to Ceionius Italicus, honoring his virtues and service as their patron. It dates to the period of the emperors Constans and Constantius (mid-4th century CE).

EDCS-13002045.translation: In the golden age of our Invincible Emperors Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, Annius Mianus, a most distinguished consular man and treasurer of the province of Numidia Constantina, dedicated this portico, begun and constructed from its foundations, and named it in honor of our Lord Gratian, the Emperor, with Nevius Numidianus acting as supervisor and completing it with his own funds, and he dedicated it.
EDCS-13002045.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a portico by Annius Mianus, treasurer of Numidia Constantina, during the reign of Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian (late 4th century CE), with completion funded by Nevius Numidianus.

EDCS-13002053.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to record the name of an emperor, likely Antoninus Pius or one of the Caracalla/Elagabalus emperors, as part of a dedication or title.

EDCS-13002060.translation: Caius Iulius Libo, triarch of the new Libyan fleet, a patron, gave and dedicated this to Marcianus Antoninianus, curator and guardian, first appointed curator of the Nolani, brother, Arval augur, iuridicus of the Transpadane region, curator of the Ariminenses, curator of the cities in Emilia, curule aedile, official of the acts of the Senate, sevire of the Roman knights, urban quaestor, tribune of the IIII Scythicae legion, and quattuorvir for maintaining roads, and to the patron of four colonies. (He gave it) for the appointment of curators and guardians. He gave and dedicated it. Son of Iulius, I (give).
EDCS-13002060.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Caius Iulius Libo to Marcianus Antoninianus, a distinguished official holding numerous positions, likely for his service in appointing curators and guardians. It dates to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13002061.translation: Caius Arrius Pacatus, the Pacatian bathhouse, for himself, 14 months.
EDCS-13002061.summary: This inscription records the construction of a bathhouse, named the Pacatian bathhouse, by Caius Arrius Pacatus for his own use over a period of 14 months.

EDCS-13002062.translation: Lucius Antonius Cassianus, freedman, to his well-deserving patroness Antonia Saturnina, daughter of Lucius Antoninus, wife of Caius Arrius Pacatus, aunt of the most distinguished men Arriorum Antoninus Maximus and Pacatus, erected this with a willing heart at his own expense, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13002062.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Lucius Antonius Cassianus, a freedman, to his patroness Antonia Saturnina, wife of Caius Arrius Pacatus, by order of the local council.

EDCS-13002064.translation: For the prosperity which the glorious colony of Constantina required in these times, with our Pious, Fortunate, Victorious, and Triumphant Lords always August Emperors presiding, Caecina Decius Albinus the Younger, a most distinguished consular man and treasurer of the province of Numidia Constantina, constructed, completed, and dedicated the aqueduct which, from a celestial element, provides a supply of water, the substance of life and use for the people throughout the entire year, with Ecdicius acting as priest.
EDCS-13002064.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of an aqueduct by Caecina Decius Albinus the Younger, treasurer of Numidia Constantina, to ensure the water supply for the colony during the reign of certain August Emperors (likely 4th century CE).

EDCS-13002067.translation: Claudius Avitianus, count of the first rank, acting on behalf of the prefects, took care to construct from the ground up and complete the Constantinian basilica with its porticoes and tetrapylon.
EDCS-13002067.summary: This inscription records the construction and completion of a basilica, porticoes, and a tetrapylon in Constantina by Claudius Avitianus, acting on behalf of the prefects, likely during the late Roman period.

EDCS-13002068.translation: To Marcus Claudius Restitutus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, procurator of the August, of the diocese of the Hadrumetine and Thevestine regions, and of the Matutine school, and responsible for auditing the accounts of the Syrian cities, tribune of the VII Gemina legion, prefect of the I Cohort of Gaetulians. Alexander, freedman of the August, the archivist, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13002068.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Claudius Restitutus, a high-ranking imperial official with extensive responsibilities in North Africa and Syria, honoring his various military and administrative roles, erected by Alexander, an imperial freedman and archivist, by local decree.

EDCS-13002069.translation: To Marcus Cocceius Anicius Faustus Flavianus, a former consul and a man of all virtues.
EDCS-13002069.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Marcus Cocceius Anicius Faustus Flavianus, a prominent figure.

EDCS-13002070.translation: To Marcus Coculnius Quintillianus, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, a *latoclavus*, honored by the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Pius Parthicus Arabicus Parthicus Adiabenicus, when he was quaestor designate, after he had performed all the flamonic duties and honors in his fatherland, the colony Iulia Iuvenalis Honoris et Virtutis Cirta; Florus, son of Labaeus, a chief and *undecimprimum* of the gentile association of the Saboids, erected this at his own expense for a deserving friend and dedicated it; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13002070.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Florus for Marcus Coculnius Quintillianus, a distinguished local official, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-13002071.translation: To Marcus Coculnius Quintillianus, son of Sextus, of the Quirina tribe, a *latoclavus*, honored by the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Pius.
EDCS-13002071.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Coculnius Quintillianus, honoring his distinction bestowed by Emperor Septimius Severus.

EDCS-13002072.translation: To Fabia Victoria Iovina, wife of Lucius Lucceius Hadrianus, a most accomplished *rationalis*, for the republic of the Cirtensians, patrons.
EDCS-13002072.summary: This is a dedication by the city of Cirta to Fabia Victoria Iovina, wife of a high-ranking official.

EDCS-13002073.translation: To Marcus Flavius Postumus, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe, *praefectus aerarii militaris*, appointed in Gaul as *legatus* of the VI Ferrata legion with the *quinque fasces*, *praetor*, promoted to the *tribunician* rank by Emperor Antoninus Augustus, curator of the colony of Ardea, quaestor and patron of four colonies; Marcus Paccius Rufinus, Quintus Aemilius Pontianus, Publius Nonius Silvanus, Aulus Publicius Pontianus, and Caius Julius Gargilianus erected this at their own expense for their most excellent patron.
EDCS-13002073.summary: This is a dedication by several individuals to their patron, Marcus Flavius Postumus, a high-ranking official with a distinguished career, likely dating to the Antonine period.

EDCS-13002074.translation: For the victory statue of Flavius Titianus, a most accomplished man, a *dux* granted by the council and people of S IV.
EDCS-13002074.summary: This inscription records the commissioning of a victory statue for Flavius Titianus, a military commander.

EDCS-13002082.translation: To Lucius Julius Victor Modianus, an outstanding man, procurator of our Emperors *Augusti nostri* throughout Numidia, acting as deputy for the procurator of the Thevestine district; Fortunatus Vindex and Diotimus, freedmen of the Emperors *Augustorum liberti*, assistants in the record office, always protected by his love and consideration.
EDCS-13002082.summary: This is a dedication to Lucius Julius Victor Modianus, a procurator in Numidia, by his freedmen assistants.

EDCS-13002083.translation: To Seia Gaetula, daughter of Marcus, wife of Naevius Censit us, mother of Naevia Marciana and Naevilla, women of most illustrious memory, married to Fulvius Faustinus, a man of praetorian rank, and grandmother of Sabinia Celsina, a woman of most illustrious memory, married to Geminius Modestus, a man of praetorian rank; the same Gaetula erected this at her own expense by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13002083.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Seia Gaetula for her deceased mother and grandmother, honoring their distinguished marriages.

EDCS-13002084.translation: To Marcus Naevius Seianus, son of Marcus, brother of Naevia Marciana and Naevilla, women of most illustrious memory, married to Fulvius Faustinus, a man of praetorian rank, and granduncle of Sabinia Celsina, a woman of most illustrious memory; Seia Gaetula, his mother, erected this at her own expense by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13002084.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Seia Gaetula for her son Marcus Naevius Seianus, honoring his familial connections.

EDCS-13002085.translation: To Naevia Naevilla, a woman of most illustrious memory, daughter of Naevius Censit us, married to Fulvius Faustinus, a man of praetorian rank; Seia Gaetula, her mother, erected this at her own expense by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13002085.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Seia Gaetula for her daughter Naevia Naevilla, honoring her marriage to a man of praetorian rank.

EDCS-13002088.translation: To Publius Pactumeius Clement, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, decemvir for judging legal cases, quaestor, legate of his father-in-law Rosianus Geminius, proconsul in Achaia, tribune of the plebs, fetial, legate of the deified Hadrian in Athens, Thespiae, and Plataeae, also in Thessaly, urban praetor, legate of the deified Hadrian for assessing the finances of the cities of Syria, legate of the same in Cilicia, consul, legate in Cilicia of Emperor Antoninus Augustus, legate of Rosianus Geminius, proconsul, in Africa, a legal expert, patron of four colonies, by decree of the local councillors, at public expense.
EDCS-13002088.summary: This is a career-spanning inscription (cursus honorum) of Publius Pactumeius Clement, detailing his various magistracies and appointments, likely dating to the Antonine period, set up by decree of the local council.

EDCS-13002092.translation: Lucius Maecilius Nepos, perpetual flamen, with his sons Proculus and Martialis, restored this statue of Porcia Maxima Optata, daughter of Publius, a most distinguished girl, daughter of Porcius Optatus, a most distinguished man, which had been stolen; he added money from his own funds.
EDCS-13002092.summary: This inscription records Lucius Maecilius Nepos's restoration of a stolen statue of a young girl, Porcia Maxima Optata, with his sons, likely a private dedication.

EDCS-13002093.translation: Of the same Porcius Optatus, a most distinguished man, as governor with the rank of propraetor of the province of Raetia.
EDCS-13002093.summary: This inscription likely refers to Porcius Optatus, previously mentioned, in his capacity as governor of the province of Raetia, possibly a fragment of a larger monument.

EDCS-13002094.translation: Oblicia Basilla, daughter of Lucius, and Torquata, daughter of Caius, [dedicated this to] Caius Iulius Bassus.
EDCS-13002094.summary: This is a dedication inscription by two women, Oblicia Basilla and Torquata, to Caius Iulius Bassus, possibly a funerary or dedicatory monument.

EDCS-13002095.translation: Because of the statues publicly offered to her parent Sosius Priscus, a consul, and his own Falconilla, daughter of Quintus Pompeius Priscus, consul, granddaughter of Quintus Pompeius Falco, consul, great-granddaughter of Quintus Sosius Senecio, consul twice, and great-great-granddaughter of Sextus Julius Frontinus, consul three times, having remitted all the cost and expense for the rest of them, herself having received V nummi together with the statues.
EDCS-13002095.summary: This inscription honors Sosius Priscus and his daughter Falconilla, detailing Falconilla's renunciation of public expenses for statues in favor of a private sum, likely a civic honor from around the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-13002102.translation: To the memory of Helvetia, who was enrolled among the knights at public expense; her heir from her will lived.
EDCS-13002102.summary: This is a memorial inscription for Helvetia, who was admitted to the equestrian order at public expense, with her heir noted, likely a funerary monument from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13002103.translation: To the memory of Marcus Antonius Ianuarius, freedman of Augustus, an assistant scribe.
EDCS-13002103.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Marcus Antonius Ianuarius, a freedman who served as an assistant scribe (adiutor tabularii), likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-13002108.translation: To Veratia Frontonilla, his most devoted wife, priestess of four Cirtensian colonies, Publius Iulius Theodorus, recently discharged from the legions with an honorable discharge as a centurion, by decree of the local councillors.
EDCS-13002108.summary: This inscription honors Veratia Frontonilla, wife of Publius Iulius Theodorus, a former centurion, and priestess of four colonies, likely a funerary monument set up by decree of the local council.

EDCS-13002111.translation: Of Iae, daughter of V F, daughter of Fabr C, to Caesar.
EDCS-13002111.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or memorial, mentioning a female name and referring to Caesar, making its precise meaning and date difficult to ascertain.

EDCS-13002116.translation: Marcus Aemilius, priest of the city, dedicated.
EDCS-13002116.summary: This is a short dedicatory inscription by Marcus Aemilius, identifying him as a priest of the city, likely erected by him for a public building or monument.

EDCS-13002117.translation: He found and erected. Aufidius Aequitus (dedicated?) OVIMONAS.
EDCS-13002117.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Aufidius Aequitus, possibly related to a monument or object named OVIMONAS.

EDCS-13002120.translation: Marcus Caecilius Natalis, son of Quintus, of the voting tribe Quirina, aedile, member of the board of three for public works, quaestor, quinquennial official, prefect of the colonies of Milev, Rusicada, and Chullu, in addition to 60,000 denarii which he contributed to the state for the honor of his aedileship, membership on the board of three, and quinquennial office, and a bronze statue of the Imperial Security and a four-columned shrine with a bronze statue of Imperial Indulgence, which he erected in honor of his aedileship and membership on the board of three, and theatrical performances for seven days, which he presented with gladiatorial games in the four colonies, and a triumphal arch with a bronze statue of the Imperial Virtue of our lord Antoninus Augustus, which he promised for the quinquennial office and constructed in the same year at his own expense.
EDCS-13002120.summary: This extensive inscription details the considerable financial contributions and public works undertaken by Marcus Caecilius Natalis, a prominent magistrate, likely in the mid-2nd century CE, across several Roman colonies.

EDCS-13002122.translation: Marcus Caecilius Natalis, son of Quintus, of the voting tribe Quirina, aedile, member of the board of three for public works, quaestor, quinquennial official, prefect of the colonies of Milev, Rusicada, and Chullu, in addition to 60,000 denarii which he contributed to the state for the honor of his aedileship, membership on the board of three, and quinquennial office, and a bronze statue of the Imperial Security and a four-columned shrine with a bronze statue of Imperial Indulgence, which he erected in honor of his aedileship and membership on the board of three, and theatrical performances for seven days, which he presented with gladiatorial games in the four colonies, and a triumphal arch with a bronze statue of the Imperial Virtue of our lord Antoninus Augustus, which he promised for the quinquennial office and constructed in the same year at his own expense.
EDCS-13002122.summary: This extensive inscription details the considerable financial contributions and public works undertaken by Marcus Caecilius Natalis, a prominent magistrate, likely in the mid-2nd century CE, across several Roman colonies.

EDCS-13002123.translation: Marcus Caecilius Natalis, son of Quintus, of the voting tribe Quirina, aedile, member of the board of three for public works, quaestor, quinquennial official, prefect of the colonies of Milev, Rusicada, and Chullu, in addition to 60,000 denarii which he contributed to the state for the honor of his aedileship, membership on the board of three, and quinquennial office, and a bronze statue of the Imperial Security and a four-columned shrine with a bronze statue of Imperial Indulgence, which he erected in honor of his aedileship and membership on the board of three, and theatrical performances for seven days, which he presented with gladiatorial games in the four colonies, and a triumphal arch with a bronze statue of the Imperial Virtue of our lord Antoninus Augustus, which he promised for the quinquennial office and constructed in the same year at his own expense.
EDCS-13002123.summary: This extensive inscription details the considerable financial contributions and public works undertaken by Marcus Caecilius Natalis, a prominent magistrate, likely in the mid-2nd century CE, across several Roman colonies.

EDCS-13002124.translation: Under the supervision of Lucius Sattius, son of Lucius, Tiron, augur, duovir, by decree of the council of twenty, for the sake of honor. Under the supervision of Lucius Sattius.
EDCS-13002124.summary: This inscription records the supervision of a project by Lucius Sattius, who held the important magistracies of augur and duovir, likely during the early imperial period.

EDCS-13002125.summary: This brief inscription simply records the name and office of Marcus Dupidius, a quinquennial official, possibly in a municipium.

EDCS-13002131.translation: Gaius Grattius Victor the younger gave and dedicated (this) to his most excellent patron.
EDCS-13002131.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Gaius Grattius Victor the younger to his patron, likely a private monument.

EDCS-13002132.translation: Quintus Iulius Potitus, son of Quintus, of the voting tribe Quirina, gave a tetrastyle and a tholos.
EDCS-13002132.summary: This inscription records Quintus Iulius Potitus's dedication of two architectural structures, a tetrastyle and a tholos, likely in his home town.

EDCS-13002136.translation: Publius Paconius Cerialis erected (this) at his own expense for his excellent and deserving friend Lucius Maecilius, son of Publius, grandson of Quintus, perpetual priest, honored with the public equestrian rank, who had performed all offices in the four colonies; the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13002136.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Publius Paconius Cerialis for his friend Lucius Maecilius, who had a distinguished career in public service across several colonies, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-13002140.translation: Quintus Rupilius Licinianus, in addition to his father's generosity, from his own resources.
EDCS-13002140.summary: This inscription indicates that Quintus Rupilius Licinianus made a contribution or erected something using his own funds, in addition to funds provided by his father, likely for a public benefit.

EDCS-13002143.translation: Sittia Extricata, daughter of Gaius, of the Calpurnian tribe, perpetual priestess, with the consent of the people, because of her generosity, since the requested contributions had been waived, erected this at her own expense by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13002143.summary: This is a dedication by Sittia Extricata, a perpetual priestess, for her own generosity in paying for a project herself after contributions were waived, erected by decree of the town council, likely in the late Roman period.

EDCS-13002144.translation: Her family erected this for Sittia Extricata, daughter of Gaius Sitius Quadratus, perpetual priestess.
