EDCS-15300220.translation: In memory of Cornelia Urbanilla. Euterpia: 'I was not, I was born, I am not, I do not care.'
EDCS-15300220.summary: This funerary epigram commemorates Cornelia Urbanilla, with a philosophical quote from Euterpia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15300380.translation: The crown of martyrs, who are Ianuaria and companions, on the 4th day before the Ides of May.
EDCS-15300380.summary: This inscription records the martyrdom of Ianuaria and eleven companions, likely from the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-15300384.translation: Oh, the memory of Saturninus's birthday.
EDCS-15300384.summary: This inscription is a memorial to Saturninus's birthday.

EDCS-15300385.translation: In this place are the remains of the saints Fortunatianus and Silvanus.
EDCS-15300385.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of the saints Fortunatianus and Silvanus.

EDCS-15300387.translation: Maxima, wife, lived 75 years. To the Divine Manes. Memius, priest of Saturn, lived 90 years.
EDCS-15300387.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespans of Maxima and Memius, a priest of Saturn.

EDCS-15300388.translation: The eighth day before the Kalends of March was the birthday of Tertullus and Donatus, the day they were buried in their place.
EDCS-15300388.summary: This inscription commemorates the birthday of Tertullus and Donatus, which was also the day of their burial, noting the date as the eighth day before the Kalends of March.

EDCS-15300533.translation: Sacred to Frugiferus the Saint. Gaius Popilius Felix, priest, with his sons Popilius Seleucus and Feliciano supervising, paid his vow with his own money and dedicated it.
EDCS-15300533.summary: This inscription records a vow paid and dedication made by the priest Gaius Popilius Felix and his sons to the deity Frugiferus.

EDCS-15300534.translation: Sacred to Frugiferus Augustus. For the concord of the Iulii, Fructuosus and Ionica, freedmen, through their father Ionicus, placed this.
EDCS-15300534.summary: This inscription, dedicated to Frugiferus Augustus, records a dedication made by freedmen Fructuosus and Ionica for the concord of the Iulii.

EDCS-15300535.translation: Lucius Domitius Suriacus, priest, with his father and his wife Claudia Monnica supervising, willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-15300535.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by the priest Lucius Domitius Suriacus, with his father and wife involved.

EDCS-15300536.translation: Caecilia Maximina and Caecilia Veprionauta made the portico with columns with their own money, and dedicated it, with their husband Felix supervising.
EDCS-15300536.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a portico with columns by Caecilia Maximina and Caecilia Veprionauta, funded by themselves and supervised by Felix.

EDCS-15300538.translation: Ulpius Egnatius Faventinus, a most distinguished man, consular governor of the province of Numidia, took care to have a statue of Victoria made and placed, which was lacking for the grandeur or adornment of the basilica of the Tisediacorum tribunals.
EDCS-15300538.summary: This inscription states that Ulpius Egnatius Faventinus, a consular governor of Numidia, commissioned and installed a statue of Victoria to enhance the basilica of the Tisediacorum tribunals.

EDCS-15300539.translation: To our August Emperors Victoria. Fabentinus, a most distinguished man, consular governor of the province of Numidia.
EDCS-15300539.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors and likely records the actions of Fabentinus, a consular governor of Numidia.

EDCS-15300541.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription by Mucius Messor, possibly indicating his profession or a dedication of lots.

EDCS-15300544.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Valerius Vincentius, biarchus (a military officer) from the troop of scutarii (shield-bearers).
EDCS-15300544.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the Divine Spirits by Valerius Vincentius, identified by his military rank and unit.

EDCS-15300546.translation: In memory of Flavius Ursacius, from the troop of propugnantes (defenders) from the school of gladiators.
EDCS-15300546.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Flavius Ursacius, who was a member of the propugnantes and belonged to a gladiatorial school.

EDCS-15300547.translation: To Considius Felicula. Marcus Pompeius Quintianus, Roman knight and perpetual priest, granted a place to his most dutiful mother by decree of the decurions of Thuriacum.
EDCS-15300547.summary: This funerary inscription records the granting of a burial plot by Marcus Pompeius Quintianus to his mother, approved by the town council of Thuriacum.

EDCS-15300548.translation: To Marcus Pompeius Pudentianus, veteran and perpetual priest. Marcus Pompeius Quintianus, Roman knight and perpetual priest, granted a place to his most dutiful father by decree of the decurions of Optantia.
EDCS-15300548.summary: This funerary inscription records the granting of a burial plot by Marcus Pompeius Quintianus to his father, approved by the town council of Optantia.

EDCS-15300549.translation: During the reign of our emperor and lord Licinianus Licinius Augustus, the state of the colony of Thmuis, and Julia Augusta, mother of the emperor and of the camp and of the senate and of the fatherland, the state of the colony of I...
EDCS-15300549.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription detailing the dedication of public works or honorifics during the joint reign of Emperors Licinius I and Licinius II, mentioning the colony of Thmuis and imperial family members.

EDCS-15300550.translation: Sacred to the Momentary Gods and Fortune. Lucius Vettius Felix fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-15300550.summary: This votive inscription records Lucius Vettius Felix fulfilling a vow to the gods Momentis and Fortuna.

EDCS-15300552.translation: Sacred to Neptune, the Nymphs, and the Genius of this place, the August Gods. Publius Aelius Silvanus, a perpetual priest from three military commands, fulfilled his vow with his sons Silvanus and Urbanus.
EDCS-15300552.summary: This votive inscription is dedicated to Neptune, Nymphs, and the local Genius by Publius Aelius Silvanus, a perpetual priest, along with his sons.

EDCS-15300553.translation: Sacred to Neptune Augustus. Publius Clodius Felix fulfilled his vow with a willing heart.
EDCS-15300553.summary: This votive inscription records Publius Clodius Felix willingly fulfilling a vow to Neptune Augustus.

EDCS-15300554.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Munatius Baliaton fulfilled his vow with a willing heart.
EDCS-15300554.summary: This votive inscription records Munatius Baliaton willingly fulfilling a vow to Saturn Augustus.

EDCS-15300621.translation: A vow from Felicis, a soldier, from the workshop of Parapatosus.
EDCS-15300621.summary: This is a dedication from a soldier, likely an ex-voto, mentioning his workshop and origin.

EDCS-15300622.translation: To the honor of the martyrs, Publius, a martyr, happily in peace.
EDCS-15300622.summary: This is a funerary or devotional inscription honoring martyrs and a person named Publius.

EDCS-15400817.translation: To our lord Flavius Valentinianus Augustus.
EDCS-15400817.summary: This inscription honors the emperor Flavius Valentinianus Augustus, likely as part of a public building or monument.

EDCS-15400820.translation: He who knew how to strike bulls and strike with strong arms was Sabinus, whom a bull, once it had gored him, with its tail gored again, and sent the unhappy youth, honored with the final rite, to the Stygian shades; Tigimma bore you, Thigibba holds you buried.
EDCS-15400820.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young man named Sabinus, describing his death in combat with a bull and naming his mother and grandmother.

EDCS-15500070.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. I, Masties, commander, 67 years old, and imperator, 10 years, who never swore falsely, nor broke faith, neither with Romans nor with Moors, and obeyed in war and in peace, and thus God dealt well with me according to my deeds. I, Vartaia, with my brothers, built this structure, for which I spent one hundred silicas.
EDCS-15500070.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Masties, a commander, recounting his life and oaths, and also by Vartaia, who built a structure with her brothers using 100 silicas.

EDCS-15600002.translation: Lucius Apronius Pius, imperial legate with praetorian power, consul-designate, fulfilled the vow which he had promised for the begun work of the Alexandrian aqueduct; Clodius Septiminus, a student of surveyors, made this after the work was completed.
EDCS-15600002.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Lucius Apronius Pius for an aqueduct, with the work credited to Clodius Septiminus.

EDCS-15600003.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Salutaris. Aelius Crispinus, from the Flavian district of Solva, procurator of the emperors of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, procurator of inheritances, procurator of the 20% inheritance tax, procurator of Mauretania Tingitana, procurator of Hispania Tarraconensis, first spearman twice, a vow.
EDCS-15600003.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by Aelius Crispinus, listing his numerous procuratorial and military offices in various Roman provinces.

EDCS-15600005.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Gaius Julius Saturninus, son of Bellicus, a veteran, lived 62 years.
EDCS-15600005.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Saturninus, identifying his father and stating his age and veteran status.

EDCS-15600006.translation: and to Julius Abdinnus, brother, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, lived 24 years.
EDCS-15600006.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julius Abdinnus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, stating his age and relationship.

EDCS-15600007.translation: Sacred to Caelestis Augusta. For the well-being of their patrons, Secundius, a freedman, restored the temple to Caelestis.
EDCS-15600007.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to Caelestis Augusta by Secundius, a freedman, for the welfare of his patrons.

EDCS-15600010.translation: To the Divine Shades. Tiberius Claudius Petronius Lusitanicus, a primus pilus, prefect of the III Augusta legion, dedicated this to his most excellent brother, Tiberius Claudius Celer, a primus pilus, tribune of the VI Praetorian cohort.
EDCS-15600010.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a military officer to his deceased brother, who also served in the military, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15600012.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Publius Aelius Rufus, prefect of the III Augusta legion.
EDCS-15600012.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus by a military officer, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15600052.summary: This is a brief dedication to the god Saturnus Augustus, the precise context and date are unknown.

EDCS-15600053.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the worshippers of the Xth Hall of Caelestina.
EDCS-15600053.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by members of a specific hall or collegium to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from a Roman colony.

EDCS-15700069.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Augustus Invictus Pius Felix, from the people of Gurza.
EDCS-15700069.summary: This is an acclamation of the emperor Philippus I (reigned 244-249 CE) by the people of Gurza.

EDCS-15700076.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Severus, freedman of Augustus, lived 75 years; he is covered by the stones of this mountain. You, traveler, pass safely on your night journey; Iter Severianus, freedman of Augustus, placed this for his dearest father and mother.
EDCS-15700076.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a freedman and his parents, including a poem and references to Roman emperors and an imperial road, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15700077.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Nardina, my dear mother, most chaste, you lived your 60 years with good character; now worthy of such a tomb, you will remain in everlasting fame through your inscriptions.
EDCS-15700077.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a mother named Nardina, praising her character and her resting place, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15700079.translation: To Victoria Victrix, of our most holy and most brave Lords Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Geta, 'Greatest Conqueror of Parthia', 'Greatest Conqueror of Britain', 'Greatest Conqueror of Germany', 'Chief Priest', Augusti, and to Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperors and of the camps. Lucius Junius Vibianus, because of the honor of his augurship, having paid to the state the legal sum of 6,000 sesterces and the statue which he had promised for 3,000 sesterces, added 1,800 sesterces with a base and dedicated it during the theatrical games he presented.
EDCS-15700079.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Lucius Junius Vibianus to the imperial family, commemorating his augurship and a public games festival, likely during the reign of Septimius Severus and his sons (c. 200-211 CE).

EDCS-15700080.translation: To the Genius of the colony of Thamugadi. Marcus Pompeius Pudentianus, a veteran and perpetual priest, dedicated this. Because of the honor of the priesthood, having paid the legal amount to the state, he promised a statue of Mars near the Pantheon arch and also statues of our Lords and of Julia Augusta in the theatre, for 40,000 sesterces; with the money increased by 10,000 sesterces, for a total of 50,000 sesterces, he placed them and because of the dedication he gave a feast and a gymnasium for the people and presented theatrical games.
EDCS-15700080.summary: This inscription details public benefactions by Marcus Pompeius Pudentianus, a veteran and priest in Thamugadi, including statues, a feast, and games, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15700081.translation: With God favoring, in the 13th year of the most prosperous times of our Lords Justinian and Theodora, perpetual Augusti, the city of Thamugadi was built from the foundations by the foresight of the most excellent Solomon, master of the soldiers, ex-consul and patrician, and overseer of all Africa.
EDCS-15700081.summary: This inscription records the founding of the city of Thamugadi under the rule of Emperors Justinian and Theodora (Byzantine period, likely 6th century CE), initiated by Solomon, a high-ranking military official.

EDCS-15700083.translation: With God's favor, in the 13th year of the most prosperous reign of our lords Justinian and Theodora, perpetual Augusti, the city of Thamugadi was built from its foundations by the foresight of the most excellent Solomon, master of soldiers, former consul and patrician, and prefect of all Africa.
EDCS-15700083.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of the city of Thamugadi under the reign of Justinian and Theodora, highlighting the role of Solomon as a high-ranking official.

EDCS-15800400.translation: In memory of Paternus the bishop.
EDCS-15800400.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Paternus, a bishop.

EDCS-15800402.translation: By the authority of Publius Cassius Secundus, legate of the emperor, concerning the public land of the Siguintani.
EDCS-15800402.summary: This inscription records an official decree by a Roman legate regarding public land.

EDCS-15800404.translation: Sergius Marianus, the high priest, enlarged and restored the temple, which had fallen into ruin through age, to the Holy God Silvanus, the genius of the praetorium, and gave and dedicated candelabra.
EDCS-15800404.summary: This inscription details the restoration and expansion of a temple dedicated to the god Silvanus by Sergius Marianus, its high priest.

EDCS-15800405.translation: I completed this in my youth, by Optatus, priest of the god.
EDCS-15800405.summary: This inscription records the completion of a task or building by Optatus, a priest, during his youth.

EDCS-15800407.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gargilius Silvanus, a cavalryman of the Flavian ala, lived 45 years. Aelia Damulna to her deserving husband.
EDCS-15800407.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gargilius Silvanus, a cavalryman, set up by his wife Aelia Damulna.

EDCS-15800408.translation: Sacred to Neptune. Lucius Volcacius, a cavalryman of the Flavian ala.
EDCS-15800408.summary: This is a dedication to Neptune by Lucius Volcacius, a cavalryman.

EDCS-15900104.translation: To Publius Julius Proximus, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, aedile three times, quinquennial official, prefect for administering justice, flamen of the deified Claudius, augur, and master of augurs. Quintus Seius Novellus, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, and his son Quintus Seius Novellus.
EDCS-15900104.summary: This inscription honors Publius Julius Proximus with a lengthy list of his civic and religious offices in the colony of Rusicade, and is dedicated by two individuals named Quintus Seius.

EDCS-15900405.translation: May you live well among good people, Optatus, with all your clergy.
EDCS-15900405.summary: This is a dedicatory or congratulatory inscription addressed to Optatus and his clergy.

EDCS-15900406.translation: The basilica was built at the initiative of Optatus the presbyter. Glory to God, happily.
EDCS-15900406.summary: This inscription records the construction of a basilica initiated by Optatus, a presbyter, and concludes with praise to God.

EDCS-15900407.translation: The memory of Saint Julian was deposited here by Saint Bishop Aemilianus. Floridus, a priest in need, fulfills his vow to the Lord God, with His help.
EDCS-15900407.summary: A Christian inscription recording the burial of Saint Julian and a vow made by a priest named Floridus, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-15900408.translation: The devoted council of the Verecundenses erected this in honor of our lord Flavius Claudius Julianus, the Invincible Augustus, propagator of the Roman name and restorer of liberty.
EDCS-15900408.summary: An honorific inscription dedicated to the emperor Julian by the town council of Verecundenses, likely in the 4th century CE.

EDCS-15900409.translation: Publius Geminius Laetus lived for XX years; he made this for himself and his family while still alive on his own estate.
EDCS-15900409.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Publius Geminius Laetus and the construction of his monument during his lifetime.

EDCS-15900410.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Flavius Forus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, lived for 30 years.
EDCS-15900410.summary: A funerary inscription for Flavius Forus, a soldier of the Legio III Augusta, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900411.translation: A splendid and beautiful house of God and Christ, our Lord and Savior, under the supervision of Felix, our priest and father.
EDCS-15900411.summary: An inscription dedicating a building, likely a church, to Christ and mentioning a priest named Felix, probably from the late Roman or early Byzantine period.

EDCS-15900414.translation: Two miles from the camp, for the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, consul, father of the fatherland, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, descendant of the deified Severus.
EDCS-15900414.summary: A milestone inscription indicating the distance to the camp, referring to Emperor Severus Alexander and his imperial lineage, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15900415.translation: May Peter and Lazarus rejoice in goodness. I beg you, Lord, help me, Christ, you alone are the physician for the holy and penitent, with your loving hands and feet.
EDCS-15900415.summary: A personal devotional inscription expressing a plea for help and referencing saints Peter and Lazarus, likely from a late Roman Christian context.

EDCS-15900417.translation: For the safety of our lord the Emperor, the worshippers of the god Genii Vaieni made this at their own expense, through the masters Primitius and Gallus.
EDCS-15900417.summary: A votive inscription erected for the emperor's safety by a group identified as 'cultores dei Genii Vaieni', likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15900418.summary: A very brief inscription recording the memorial of an individual named Marchulus, possibly late Roman or early medieval.

EDCS-16000046.translation: To the holy god Mercury, Lucius Petronius made his vow.
EDCS-16000046.summary: A votive inscription to the god Mercury by Lucius Petronius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16000047.translation: To the Emperors Caesars Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, twice consul, proconsul, and Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus.
EDCS-16000047.summary: This inscription names two co-emperors, Trebonianus Gallus and his son, likely indicating a dedication during their reign in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-16000050.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Iereheus Zabde of Palmyra, who lived 60 years. Vulcacius Saturninus, a veteran of the Third Legion Augusta Pia Vindex, made this in honor of his goodness.
EDCS-16000050.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a Palmyrene man named Iereheus Zabde, made by a veteran of the III Augusta legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16000051.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Ulcacius Saturninus, a veteran of the Third Legion Augusta Pia Vindex, made this for myself and for those deserving. I gave it to Iunctus. He lived 75 years.
EDCS-16000051.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Ulcacius Saturninus, a veteran of the III Augusta legion, dedicates a tomb for himself and others, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16000328.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Posphorus, who lived 9 years. The association of Caelestinus took care to have this made for the deserving. May the earth be light on you.
EDCS-16000328.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a young boy named Posphorus, made by an association, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16000329.translation: Biricbal. Here lies buried. Priest of Ceres. He lived 58 years.
EDCS-16000329.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for a man named Biricbal who was a priest of Ceres, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16000562.translation: To Gaius Julius Lepidus Tertullus, clarissimus vir, imperial legate with proconsular power; Marcus Aemilius Felix Iunior, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, aedile of quaestorian rank, prefect for administering justice, his most excellent patron, placed this at his own expense and also dedicated it.
EDCS-16000562.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Julius Lepidus Tertullus, a prominent imperial legate, by Marcus Aemilius Felix Iunior, an official in the province, likely dating to the late 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-16000565.translation: To Gaius Aemilius Serenus, veteran of the Seventh Gemina Felix Legion, a Spaniard. Galeria Clunia and Sabinus and Focaria [dedicated this].
EDCS-16000565.summary: This is a dedication to Gaius Aemilius Serenus, a veteran of the VII Gemina legion, made by his family or associates, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16000566.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Felix Augustus. Marcus Valerius Maximianus, clarissimus vir, consul, imperial legate with proconsular power, took care to have the work of collecting and containing the Paludian aqueduct begun and carried out, and he himself dedicated it by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-16000566.summary: This inscription records the completion and dedication of a water infrastructure project (aqueduct) under the supervision of the legate Marcus Valerius Maximianus, likely during the reign of Commodus in the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-16100224.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Gaius V F for the safety of our Lords the Emperors Severus and Antoninus Augusti.
EDCS-16100224.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the safety of the emperors Severus and Antoninus, likely dating to their joint rule (198-211 AD).

EDCS-16100227.translation: Sacred to Mercury Augustus. Quintus Valerius Ianuarius gladly paid his vow.
EDCS-16100227.summary: This is a votive inscription to Mercury Augustus, dedicated by Quintus Valerius Ianuarius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100228.translation: Marcus Herennius Urbanus, a decurion, made this in memory of his father, Idio, son of Nurbelo.
EDCS-16100228.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Marcus Herennius Urbanus in memory of his father.

EDCS-16100229.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Ierehes lived 45 years.
EDCS-16100229.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Ierehes.

EDCS-16100230.translation: He lived... Nabuzabatus made this for his son and grandson.
EDCS-16100230.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records a lifespan and was made by Nabuzabatus for his relatives.

EDCS-16100231.translation: Mucianus Malchus and Lisinius Mucianus, the magistrates, fulfilled their vow to the Holy God Malagbel.
EDCS-16100231.summary: This is a votive inscription from two magistrates to the god Malagbel.

EDCS-16100232.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Heranus of Palmyra, son of Herani, lived 55 years and served as a soldier for 17 years. Malchus the physician made this for his deserving friend.
EDCS-16100232.summary: This funerary inscription honors Malchus the physician's friend, a former soldier.

EDCS-16100233.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Potsilus, son of Themarsa, lived 48 years and served for 26.
EDCS-16100233.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan and military service of Potsilus.

EDCS-16100234.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To the dearest parents. Iacubus, son of Mocimulus, lived 46 years.
EDCS-16100234.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to parents, likely by their son Iacubus.

EDCS-16100282.translation: For Julia Mamaea Augusta, mother of our lord the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, the state of the Taguatenses, by decree of the decurions, at its own expense.
EDCS-16100282.summary: This is a public dedication by the city of Tagaste to Julia Mamaea Augusta, mother of Emperor Severus Alexander.

EDCS-16100283.translation: In the 16th year of our lord Emperor Justinian, Laudeti was assessed in the lord's portion as a single oil merchant.
EDCS-16100283.summary: This inscription records an assessment from the reign of Emperor Justinian concerning an oil merchant in Laudeti.

EDCS-16100284.translation: Ierhoboles, son of Ieddei, a Palmyrene soldier, lived 45 years. Son of A G A, 2002 V.
EDCS-16100284.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan and origin of Ierhoboles, a Palmyrene soldier.

EDCS-16100285.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, the most holy and noble prince. Publius Aelius Silvanus, son of Publius, of the Papiria tribe, quaestor, aedile, with quaestorian power, duovir for the office of duovir, gave and dedicated [this statue] from his own funds of 4300 sesterces, in addition to another statue, according to his promise.
EDCS-16100285.summary: This inscription records a public benefaction by Publius Aelius Silvanus, a local magistrate, to Emperor Septimius Severus, likely dating to the period of Severus's reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-16100286.translation: To the genius of the most fortunate age. Publius Iulius Longinus, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, aedile, gave [this statue] as duovir for the office of augur.
EDCS-16100286.summary: This inscription records a public benefaction by Publius Iulius Longinus, a local magistrate, possibly dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16100287.translation: To Numisia Celerina, wife of Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, consul-designate, patron of the municipality, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-16100287.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Numisia Celerina, wife of a governor, funded by the local council, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-16100288.translation: To Ulpia Aristonica, the most distinguished lady, wife of Marcus Valerius Maximianus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, clarissimus vir, consul-designate. Aquilius Restutus, perpetual flamen, aedile, duovir, quinquennial duovir, prefect with power of justice in place of the duovirs, and Marcianus, augur, aedile, duovir, made [this monument] with their own funds and dedicated it.
EDCS-16100288.summary: This inscription records the joint construction and dedication of a monument by two local officials, Aquilius Restutus and Marcianus, to Ulpia Aristonica, wife of a governor, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16100442.translation: Sacred to Faunus Augustus. Marcus Claudius Faustinus erected [this] and joyfully fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16100442.summary: This is a votive dedication to the god Faunus Augustus, erected by Marcus Claudius Faustinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16100443.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, dedicated by Titus Caesernius Statianus Quintius Statianus Memmius Macrinus, legate with praetorian power, consul-designate, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-16100443.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius, funded by the local council and approved by the governor Titus Caesernius Statianus Quintius Statianus Memmius Macrinus, likely dating to Antoninus Pius's reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-16100445.translation: Iora, son of Lisri, an equestrian of the I Pannonian cavalry wing, a Golailan by nationality, aged 22 years and 22 years of service, lies here.
EDCS-16100445.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Iora, an equestrian soldier from the I Pannonian cavalry wing, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200073.translation: To the happy genius of the Thacaratenses. Caius Iulius Faustus made [this].
EDCS-16200073.summary: This is a dedication to the local genius by Caius Iulius Faustus, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200075.translation: Optatus, son of Sadecis, decurion of the Lusitanian cohort, lived 55 years. A Sardinian.
EDCS-16200075.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Optatus, a decurion in a Lusitanian cohort, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200713.summary: This is a maker's mark from the workshop of Donatus, likely indicating the origin of a manufactured object, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16200811.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus, from Caius Rubrius, son of Caius Rubrius Fortunatus, with his sister Rubria, from his sister Sextum Papinium P, with the exercitus, had it made.
EDCS-16200811.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus, likely by a family member and soldiers, possibly related to military service.

EDCS-16200895.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Quintus Numidicius Amator, buried in the earth, who, completing his ninth lustrum (45 years), served the laws, defended justice, and was skilled in their practice, erected this tomb when the fated death arrived.
EDCS-16200895.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Numidicius Amator, a jurist and public servant, commemorating his life and death around the age of 45.

EDCS-16200896.translation: To the Genius of Ausum. Valerius Crescens, ordinary leader of the detachment, and Manilius Felix, together with their fellow soldiers.
EDCS-16200896.summary: A dedication to the Genius of Ausum by a military officer and his fellow soldiers, likely honouring their unit or a specific location.

EDCS-16200897.translation: Arruntius Martialis and Seianus made it at their own expense.
EDCS-16200897.summary: A record of construction or a project funded by Arruntius Martialis and Seianus.

EDCS-16200899.translation: The temple was enlarged out of reverence for Caelestis and the beneficence of the divinity, by the founder Emperor Flavius Valens, triumphant, ever Augustus. Publilius Ceionius Caecina Albinus, most illustrious, consular.
EDCS-16200899.summary: This inscription records the enlargement of a temple dedicated to Caelestis, funded by Emperor Valens and overseen by the prominent official Publilius Ceionius Caecina Albinus.

EDCS-16200901.translation: To Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus, son of the Augusti, by decree of the decurions, with public funds.
EDCS-16200901.summary: This is a public dedication to Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus, likely a young prince, funded by the local town council.

EDCS-16200902.translation: To Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus, the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the country.
EDCS-16200902.summary: A titulary inscription listing the full imperial titles of Emperor Valerian.

EDCS-16200903.translation: To the August God Pluto, Maria Monnosa and Flavius Marianus and Flavius Ianuarius the younger fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-16200903.summary: A votive inscription to the god Pluto, indicating a vow fulfilled by three individuals.

EDCS-16200904.translation: Publius with his wife and sons happily fulfilled his vow to the August God Pluto.
EDCS-16200904.summary: A votive inscription to the god Pluto, fulfilled by a family.

EDCS-16200905.translation: Sacred to the August Diana. Aemilius Donatus, prompted by a dream, along with his sons, erected and dedicated this altar at his own expense.
EDCS-16200905.summary: A votive altar dedicated to Diana Augusta by Aemilius Donatus and his sons, inspired by a dream.

EDCS-16200972.translation: Sacred to the Divine Sun Augustus. Aufustius Adiutor, a staff officer of the governor, fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-16200972.summary: A private dedication to Sol Augustus by a beneficiarius consularis, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16200974.translation: Sacred to Augusta Venus. Marcellus, freedman of our emperor, a copyist, and Chrestus, a house-born slave of our emperor, the overseer of the four public officials of Africa, erected this.
EDCS-16200974.summary: A dedication to Venus Augusta by freedmen and slaves involved in public administration, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16201050.translation: To Augusta Annona. Titus Flavius Nepos Marcianus, son of Titus, of the Papiria tribe, a priest.
EDCS-16201050.summary: An inscription recording the name and titles of Titus Flavius Nepos Marcianus, likely a dedication to Annona Augusta, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16201185.summary: A brief dedication to Hercules Augustus, potentially from any period but commonly found in the Imperial era.

EDCS-16201186.translation: Caius Iulius Herennianus and Quintus Lisinius Lisinianus, magistrates of the Medianorum, made the public grain measures at Rusicade Lambiridi at their own expense.
EDCS-16201186.summary: An official record of public works (grain measures) funded by magistrates in Rusicade, dating likely to the Imperial period.

EDCS-16201230.summary: A short dedication to Augusta Genitrix, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-16201235.translation: Live in envy and see, so that you may see more.
EDCS-16201235.summary: A witty and somewhat cynical epigram, possibly a tomb inscription or a private marker, dating from antiquity.

EDCS-16201551.translation: The public water jug and the public pitcher were made by Caius Silicius Campus and Marcus Ulpius Mustus, magistrates of the Medianorum.
EDCS-16201551.summary: A record of public works (water jug and pitcher) commissioned by magistrates of the Medianorum, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-16201554.translation: The bath of Terentius Donatus was restored through the efforts of Frumetus Longinianus.
EDCS-16201554.summary: A record of the restoration of a private bathhouse, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16201569.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Publius Iulius Iunianus Martialianus, governor's legate of the Third Augustan Legion Alexandriana, consul-designate, gave a vow with his son, the most distinguished boy, Publius Iulius Iunianus Tironillianus.
EDCS-16201569.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a high-ranking military official and his son, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16201574.translation: To the most dutiful and victorious Iovius Valerius Diocletianus, Invictus, Dutiful, Fortunate Augustus, the council of the colony of Lambaesis, devoted to his divine will and majesty.
EDCS-16201574.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the council of Lambaesis to Emperor Diocletian, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-16201576.translation: To the god Saturninus, cultivator of the god Torchobolis, having earned the priesthood for the second time from the god, Marcus Aemilius Iulianus, priest.
EDCS-16201576.summary: This text records a priest, Marcus Aemilius Iulianus, dedicating to the god Saturninus, likely a local deity, and possibly to Torchobolis as well.

EDCS-16201577.translation: Sacred to the Invictus: Aelius Aurelius Iulianus, son of Iulius Piso, legate of the III Augusta legion, proconsul designate, gave this from his own means; [---] tribunus militum of the III Augusta legion.
EDCS-16201577.summary: This inscription is a dedication to an Invictus deity, possibly Sol Invictus, by an Aelius Aurelius Iulianus, who held several high military and administrative posts in North Africa.

EDCS-16201578.translation: To Apollo the Healer, by his command, Publius Metilius Secundus, imperial legate proconsul.
EDCS-16201578.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Apollo Salutifer (the Healer) by Publius Metilius Secundus, a Roman governor of a province, acting on the god's instruction.

EDCS-16201579.translation: To the Companions of the Moon.
EDCS-16201579.summary: This very short inscription is a dedication to the 'Comitibus Lunae,' likely referring to deities associated with the moon, possibly the Dea Luna.

EDCS-16201646.translation: To the Good Gods, to Mars Gradivus the father, and to the Holy Victory, Marcus Aurelius Decimus, most distinguished man, president of the province of Numidia, willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-16201646.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, the governor of Numidia, to the Good Gods, Mars Gradivus, and Victory.

EDCS-16201648.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Faltonia Monnica made this for her husband, Iulius Martialis, quaestor.
EDCS-16201648.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Faltonia Monnica for her deceased husband, Iulius Martialis, who held the office of quaestor.

EDCS-16201649.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Claudius Restitus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, lived 19 years. Claudius Domnion made this for his deserving son.
EDCS-16201649.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Claudius Restitus, a 19-year-old soldier of the III Augusta legion, set up by his father.

EDCS-16201698.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Felix lived 75 years. Tsedden lived 73 years. Vibius Cirrenius made this testimony from his own means.
EDCS-16201698.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages of two individuals, Felix and Tsedden, with Vibius Cirrenius providing the monument.

EDCS-16201699.translation: Tomb of the Pontii. Lucius Pontius Martis, also called Pattiso, lived 80 years, whose day of burial is the 15th day before the Kalends of October. Nimasen, his wife, lived 82 years, the day of her burial is the Ides of January. [---] the day of burial is the Kalends of December.
EDCS-16201699.summary: This inscription records the death dates and ages of Lucius Pontius Martis and his wife Nimasen, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, marking their burials.

EDCS-16201707.translation: Sacred to Mercury Augustus. Lucius Cosinius Primus, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe, perpetual priest, erected this.
EDCS-16201707.summary: This is a dedication to Mercury Augustus by Lucius Cosinius Primus, a perpetual priest.

EDCS-16201709.translation: Gaius Cosinius Maximus, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe, having been aedile, quaestor, prefect for dispensing justice, duovir for five years, pontiff, and appointed to five decuries, remitted the cost of a bronze chariot, which the most respected order of the Cuiculitans had decreed should be erected for him upon his request by the people, content with the inscription, and erected it at his own expense, and dedicated it by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-16201709.summary: This inscription records the erection of a bronze chariot by Gaius Cosinius Maximus, detailing his various public offices and his decision to fund the monument himself.

EDCS-16201710.translation: Gaius Cosinius Maximus, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe, having been aedile, quaestor, prefect for dispensing justice, duovir for five years, pontiff, and appointed to five decuries, remitted the cost of a statue, which the most respected order of the Cuiculitans had decreed should be erected for him upon his request by the people, content with the inscription, and erected it at his own expense, and dedicated it by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-16201710.summary: This inscription details the erection of a statue by Gaius Cosinius Maximus, listing his extensive public career and his personal funding of the monument.

EDCS-16201712.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, Imperator for the 4th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, the best and most valiant prince. The public community of the Cuiculitans, by decree of the decurions, dedicated this with public funds, with Gaius Julius Lepidus Tertullus, legate of the emperor with the power of a proconsul, a most distinguished man, patron of the colony, overseeing the dedication.
EDCS-16201712.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus by the community of Cuiculita, overseen by the governor Gaius Julius Lepidus Tertullus.

EDCS-16201713.translation: Martinianus lived for 33 years and served for X years as an equestrian; he died at Herculea Idicra, born in the region of Issesa, in the village of Baniolu. His father, the quartermaster S., made this in memory.
EDCS-16201713.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Martinianus, a veteran and equestrian, detailing his age at death, place of birth, and who erected the monument.

EDCS-16201714.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, proconsul, son of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 7th time, Imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, propagator of the empire, father of the country, the most valiant and fortunate prince; son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus; grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius; great-grandson of the deified Hadrian; great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan and the deified Nerva. The public community of the Gentis Suburburum Colonorum, with Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti with the power of a proconsul, a most distinguished man, consul, overseeing the dedication.
EDCS-16201714.summary: This inscription is a lengthy dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), listing his titles and imperial lineage, erected by the community of Suburburum Colonorum.

EDCS-16201717.translation: Sacred to Victoria Augusta. Marcus Virrius Diadumenus, of the Hadrianic Senate, a veteran of Legio III Augusta, made this at his own expense for the honor of the perpetual priesthood, which they conferred upon him in his absence, and dedicated it.
EDCS-16201717.summary: This is a dedication to Victoria Augusta by Marcus Virrius Diadumenus, a veteran, to honor his appointment as perpetual priest.

EDCS-16201722.translation: To Lucius Julius Apronius Maenius Pius Salamallianus, a senatorial tribune of Legio X Gemina, appointed among the quaestorians, prefect of the Senate's records, curule aedile, legate of the emperor with the power of a proconsul for the province of Belgica, legate of Legio I Adiutrix and legate of the emperor with the power of a proconsul for the province of Galatia, legate of Legio III Augusta Severiana and the province of Numidia. Marcus Aurelius Crescens, primus pilus of the same legion, as protector, a most rare individual.
EDCS-16201722.summary: This inscription honors Lucius Julius Apronius Maenius Pius Salamallianus, listing his extensive military and administrative career, with a commendation from Marcus Aurelius Crescens.

EDCS-16201723.translation: ...filled with honors, dedicated by Publilius Ceionius Caecina Albinus, a most distinguished man, consular governor of Numidia for the sixth fiscal year, under the supervision of Valerius Ianus, duovir.
EDCS-16201723.summary: This fragment records the dedication of something by the consular governor of Numidia, Publilius Ceionius Caecina Albinus, during the tenure of duovir Valerius Ianus.

EDCS-16201724.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Cominianus Cassianus, legate of the Augusti with the power of a proconsul, a most distinguished man, a most benevolent governor. Lucius Herennius Sacutianus and Caecilius Victor, cornicularii.
EDCS-16201724.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the governor Marcus Aurelius Cominianus Cassianus by two officers, Lucius Herennius Sacutianus and Caecilius Victor.

EDCS-16201725.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, most distinguished man, imperial legate with pro-praetorian power, his subordinates, under the care of Caecilius Bellicianus, quaestor, and Licinius Fortunatus, a beneficiary, [honoring him] for his outstanding patience, admirable integrity, and highest virtues.
EDCS-16201725.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, an imperial legate, with the care of his subordinates.

EDCS-16201726.translation: To Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, a most distinguished man, imperial legate with pro-praetorian power, and patron; Lucius Valerius Optatianus, an equestrian of Rome, an advocate, [erected this] because of his outstanding kindness towards him.
EDCS-16201726.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Lucius Valerius Optatianus to his patron, Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, a former imperial legate.

EDCS-16201728.translation: To Marcus Valerius Senecio, imperial legate with pro-praetorian power, governor of the province of Lower Germania, a most distinguished man; Lucius Fabius Silvanus, his clerk, [dedicated this] to his outstanding governor.
EDCS-16201728.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Valerius Senecio, governor of Lower Germania, by his clerk Lucius Fabius Silvanus.

EDCS-16201729.translation: To Tiberius Iulius Pollieno Auspicus, imperial legate with pro-praetorian power, a most distinguished man, consul; his attendants, [dedicated this] to their most innocent governor.
EDCS-16201729.summary: This inscription honors Tiberius Iulius Pollieno Auspicus, an imperial legate and consul, by his attendants.

EDCS-16201730.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, most distinguished man, imperial legate with pro-praetorian power; Caius Iulius Rogatianus, a decurion of the Flavian cavalry unit, who was promoted by him from clerk, [dedicated this].
EDCS-16201730.summary: This inscription honors Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, an imperial legate, by Caius Iulius Rogatianus, who was promoted by him.

EDCS-16201731.translation: To Consulius Lucius Titinius Clodianus, a man of many two-hundred-thousand sesterces [inheritances], now a two-hundred-thousand sesterces man of the great training school; Caius Vibius Maximus, an equestrian of Rome, perpetual priest of the treasury, an advocate, his candidate, [dedicated this] to his most rare patron.
EDCS-16201731.summary: This inscription honors Consulius Lucius Titinius Clodianus, a wealthy official and patron, by Caius Vibius Maximus, an equestrian and advocate.

EDCS-16201732.translation: To Vergilia Flavia, a most distinguished woman, wife of Iulius Fortunatianus, a most distinguished consular man; Aemilius Florus, a beneficiary and administrator, [dedicated this] to his patron.
EDCS-16201732.summary: This inscription honors Vergilia Flavia, wife of a consular man, by Aemilius Florus, her husband's beneficiary and administrator.

EDCS-16201734.translation: During the consulship of the Emperor Macrinus and Adventus, on the day before the Kalends of April, sacred to Bacchus Augustus; Marcus Manilius Augustalis and Iulius Rusticinus, masters, of Thibilis.
EDCS-16201734.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Bacchus Augustus made by the masters of Thibilis during the consulship of Macrinus and Adventus in 218 AD.

EDCS-16201789.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the Senate of Cuiculitanus; Lucius Flavius Celsus, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, aedile, prefect acting as a duovir, prefect acting as quinquennial, augur, master of augurs, because of the honor of the augurship, from three statues which he promised for 20,000 sesterces beyond the legal requirement, with additional money he enlarged [the provision] and placed it with added bases, and he himself dedicated it.
EDCS-16201789.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Lucius Flavius Celsus, an official in Cuiculitanus, related to the funding and erection of statues and bases for the honor of his augurship.

EDCS-16201790.translation: To the Piety of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, holding tribunician power for the 20th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-16201790.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius, funded by public money and decreed by the local council, likely commemorating his titles and virtues.

EDCS-16201792.translation: To Iulia Celsina, a most distinguished lady, wife of Aulus Julius Pompilius Piso, imperial legate with praetorian power, consul-designate, patron of the colony; erected by decree of the decurions with public funds.
EDCS-16201792.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument for Iulia Celsina, erected by her husband and the town council.

EDCS-16201796.translation: To Fortuna Redux of the Augusti, Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus, father of the fatherland, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul; and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix, with tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul; and of the most noble Caesar Publius Septimius Geta Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, father of the fatherland; erected by decree of the decurions with public funds.
EDCS-16201796.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Fortuna Redux by the town council on behalf of the emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, likely erected with public funds.

EDCS-16201797.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the people of Cuicul; Lucius Flavius Celsus, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, as aedile, prefect acting as a duovir, duovir, prefect acting as quinquennial, augur, chief of the augurs, for the honor of his augurship, from three statues which he promised beyond the legal requirement for 21,000 sesterces, with increased funds, made and placed the bases, and dedicated them himself.
EDCS-16201797.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Lucius Flavius Celsus, a local magistrate and priest, to the Genius of the people of Cuicul, detailing his public works and financial contributions.

EDCS-16201798.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for [our] health.
EDCS-16201798.summary: This is a short votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus for health.

EDCS-16202027.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time; the state of the people of Hippo.
EDCS-16202027.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Gordian III by the city of Hippo, likely on the occasion of his third tribunician power grant.

EDCS-16202028.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus and the Genius of the colony; Titus Flavius Breucus, of the Quirina tribe, a veteran, served in the 1st Pannonian cavalry ala, decurion and princeps, for 26 years, perpetual priest of the colony, placed this at his own expense.
EDCS-16202028.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Mars Augustus and the Genius of the colony by Titus Flavius Breucus, a veteran and local priest, who served in the army and held a perpetual priesthood.

EDCS-16202029.translation: To Tiberius Julius Pollienus Auspex, clarissimus vir, imperial legate with praetorian power, consul, most rare governor; Quintus Ancharius Felix, from the military services.
EDCS-16202029.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Tiberius Julius Pollienus Auspex, a high-ranking imperial official, made by Quintus Ancharius Felix.

EDCS-16202032.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered God Mithras; Marcus Valerius Maximianus, imperial legate with praetorian power.
EDCS-16202032.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the god Mithras by Marcus Valerius Maximianus, an imperial legate.

EDCS-16202033.translation: To the Holy Goddess Fortuna Redux; Marcus Aurelius Decimus, vir perfectissimus, governor of the province of Numidia.
EDCS-16202033.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Fortuna Redux by Marcus Aurelius Decimus, the governor of Numidia.

EDCS-16202034.translation: Sacred to the Salutary Gods Aesculapius and Hygieia, by whose aid adverse illnesses are repelled; Domitius Zenofilus, vir clarissimus, consularis and sexfascalis of the province of Numidia, gave this sacred offering of his devotion, as an indication next to them; by the Curetii.
EDCS-16202034.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Aesculapius and Hygieia by Domitius Zenofilus, a high-ranking official in Numidia, for health and well-being.

EDCS-16300168.translation: Sacred to the spirit of the people of Cuiculitan. Lucius Lurius Felix, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, quaestor, prefect in place of a duovir, pontifex, twice master of the pontiffs, erected and dedicated the statue which he promised for the honor of the pontificate, beyond the legal amount, for 6,000 sesterces.
EDCS-16300168.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue by Lucius Lurius Felix, who held various magistracies in Cuiculitan, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300170.translation: Flavius Uranius, a most distinguished man, tribune, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16300170.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Flavius Uranius, a distinguished tribune, likely from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-16300171.translation: Flavius Rusticianus, a most distinguished man, tribune, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16300171.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Flavius Rusticianus, a distinguished tribune, likely from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-16300172.translation: To Public Faith. Lucius Flavius Honoratus, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, aedile, quaestor, duovir, pontifex, master of the pontiffs, quinquennial censor, erected and dedicated the statue which he promised for the honor of the pontificate, beyond the legal amount, for 3,000 sesterces.
EDCS-16300172.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue to Public Faith by Lucius Flavius Honoratus, a prominent magistrate in his community, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300175.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Caius Geminius Maximus, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, pontifex, master of the pontiffs, prefect in place of a duovir, erected and dedicated the statue with its base, which he promised for the honor of his pontificate, beyond the legal amount, for 4,400 sesterces, with increased funds.
EDCS-16300175.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue to Hercules Augustus by Caius Geminius Maximus, a magistrate and priest, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300176.translation: Sacred to Mercury Augustus. Caius Cassius Fortunatus, son of Caius, of the Quirina tribe, quaestor, aedile, duovir, prefect in place of a duovir, augur, with his own money, erected and dedicated it, beyond the legal amount, for the sum of 4,000 sesterces, which he contributed to the state, for the honor of the duovirate, having promised 2,000 sesterces in a gift, with increased funds from 3,000 sesterces, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-16300176.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to Mercury Augustus by Caius Cassius Fortunatus, a magistrate, with funds he contributed to the state, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300179.translation: To Julia Augusta, mother of the Augusti and of the camps, of the household administration of the camp.
EDCS-16300179.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Julia Augusta, likely a member of the imperial family, related to military administration, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300181.translation: Publius Geminius Rogatianus, perpetual priest, formerly a decurion of the Flavian cavalry ala, contributed 6,000 sesterces, also adding a legacy of 11,000 sesterces, and 10,000 sesterces for the work on the curia, likewise he provided golden statues for Marsyas and the perpetual priests and twice the amount for the condecurions, and also gifts for the curial officials, and erected [them].
EDCS-16300181.summary: This inscription details contributions made by Publius Geminius Rogatianus, a perpetual priest and former decurion, for various public works and honors, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300183.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, Minerva Augusta, and to the other immortal gods and goddesses. Marcus Veturius Veturianus, a most distinguished man, imperial legate of consular rank, proconsul of Sicily, for his successful administration in the province of Numidia.
EDCS-16300183.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Capitoline Triad and other deities by Marcus Veturius Veturianus, a high-ranking imperial official, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300315.translation: The birthday of Claudius, on the Kalends of October.
EDCS-16300315.summary: This inscription records a date, the birthday of someone named Claudius, falling on October 1st, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-16300316.translation: On the birthday of the Lord, on the day of Pascentis, the Kalends of January.
EDCS-16300316.summary: This inscription records a date related to a religious festival.

EDCS-16300319.translation: Marcus Claudius Syriacus, his son, with his own money, erected this monument, which the most holy Senate had decreed should be erected with gathered bronze and at the people's request, to Tiberius Claudius, son of Tiberius, Papiria, Cicero, who held the offices of quaestor, aedile, prefect, deputy duovir for the second time, duovir, youth prefect, Augustan flamen, and quinquennial duovir, and augur, as he was entitled to a 'biga'.
EDCS-16300319.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a son for his father, Tiberius Claudius Cicero, who had a distinguished public career, likely in the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-16300321.translation: With Quintus Anicius Faustus, consular patron, officiating, Marcus Sedius Rufus, advocate, perpetual flamen, having completed his Commodian and Herculian flaminate and quinquennality and duovirate and quaestorship, promised and gave this gift in his own name and in the name of his wife, Gargilia Nucerina.
EDCS-16300321.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Marcus Sedius Rufus, an advocate and local official, likely during the reign of Commodus or shortly after, honoring Hercules and possibly the emperor.

EDCS-16300323.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered Hercules for the safety and victory of the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor, in honor of Publius Maevius Saturninus Honoratianus, procurator of the Augusti, a man of the highest distinction, Publius Aelius Menecrates gave this gift to his fatherland, number III.
EDCS-16300323.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Hercules for the safety and victory of the Severan emperors, made by Publius Aelius Menecrates in honor of his father-in-law, Publius Maevius Saturninus Honoratianus, a procurator, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300324.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered Hercules for the safety and victory of the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor, in honor of Publius Maevius Saturninus Honoratianus, a broad-striped tribune of the soldiers of the XI Claudian legion, a most distinguished youth, his sister's son, Publius Aelius Menecrates gave this gift to his fatherland, number III.
EDCS-16300324.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Hercules for the safety and victory of the Severan emperors, made by Publius Aelius Menecrates in honor of his nephew, Publius Maevius Saturninus Honoratianus, a young officer, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300325.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered Hercules for the safety and victory of the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor, in honor of Decimus Aelius Menecrates, from the military service, his brother, Publius Aelius Menecrates gave this gift to his fatherland, number III.
EDCS-16300325.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Hercules for the safety and victory of the Severan emperors, made by Publius Aelius Menecrates in honor of his brother, Decimus Aelius Menecrates, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16300326.translation: To the Genius of Lambaesis, from the will of Titus Flavius Vettianus, beneficiarius of the legate, under the supervision of Gaius Iulius Marcianus, guardian.
EDCS-16300326.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Genius of Lambaesis, made according to the will of Titus Flavius Vettianus, likely in the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-16300327.translation: Sacred to the Perpetual Divine Power of the Augustan Virtue, sacred to the Eternal Mother, the Phrygian Divine Apollo Attis, from Phrygia.
EDCS-16300327.summary: This inscription records a complex religious dedication to various deities, including the Augustan Virtue and Apollo Attis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16300329.translation: Sacred to Serapis Augustus for [his] safety.
EDCS-16300329.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Serapis, likely made for the well-being of the emperor and the Roman state, probably during the Imperial period.

EDCS-16300330.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the People Augustus.
EDCS-16300330.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Genius of the People, invoking Augustus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16300582.translation: Sacred to the genius of the senate of the people of Cuicul (or of Cuiculitanus). Lucius Pompeius Novellus, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, twice aedile, twice duovir, augur, and master of augurs, set up this statue, one of two statues which he promised in addition to the legally required ones, in honor of his augurship, paying double the customary fee. He also dedicated it.
EDCS-16300582.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue by Lucius Pompeius Novellus in honor of his augurship in Cuicul, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-16300584.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus and the genius of the colony. Quintus Gargilius Quietus, son of Quintus, of the Papiria tribe, who had held all offices and was perpetual flamen, had ordered by his will that this statue be set up by his wife, Domitia Honorata, perpetual flaminica. Their sons, Quintus Gargilius Honoratus, perpetual flamen, and Lucius Gargilius Severus, Roman knights, erected it.
EDCS-16300584.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue to Mars Augustus and the genius of the colony by the sons of Quintus Gargilius Quietus, fulfilling his testamentary wish, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16400070.translation: Sacred to the Great Mother of the Gods Augusta. Caecilius Paulinus, master of the dendrophori and annual flamen, with his son Gaius Caecilius Rufillus acting as curator, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16400070.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the Great Mother of the Gods Augusta by Caecilius Paulinus, master of the dendrophori, likely in a North African province during the Roman Empire.

EDCS-16400093.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the divine Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Augustus, and for Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the Caesar, the senate, and the fatherland, daughter of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus and the divine Nerva, who is Parthicus Maximus, who holds tribunician power for the 3rd time, is emperor for the 3rd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. The republic of the people of Cuicul restored the roads, exhausted by torrents, and widened them with new fortifications.
EDCS-16400093.summary: This inscription records the restoration and widening of roads in the colony of Cuicul by the municipal authorities, dedicated to the emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla (or Elagabalus) and empress Julia Domna, likely in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16400094.translation: Sacred to Ceres Augusta. Longinus Sula willingly paid his vow with his own money in the name of Flavia Mazica, his wife.
EDCS-16400094.summary: This inscription records the payment of a vow to Ceres Augusta by Longinus Sula on behalf of his wife, likely from Roman North Africa.

EDCS-16400095.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aemilius Aemilianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontiff, who holds tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, and for Caia Cornelia Supera Augusta, his wife. The republic of the colony of Cuicul, devoted to their divine majesty, restored the roads, exhausted by torrents.
EDCS-16400095.summary: This inscription records the restoration of roads in the colony of Cuicul by the municipal authorities, dedicated to the emperor Aemilianus and his wife Cornelia Supera, dating to Aemilianus' brief reign in 253 CE.

EDCS-16400096.translation: Sacred to Victoria Augusta. Lucius Claudius Brutus, son of Titus, of the Papiria tribe, aedile, prefect acting as duovir, augur, set up this statue, with its bases, and also dedicated it, with increased funds, because of the honor of his augurship, from two statues of Victoria and Mercury, which he had promised in addition to the legally required ones, for 10,000 sesterces.
EDCS-16400096.summary: This inscription records the dedication of statues to Victoria Augusta and Mercury by Lucius Claudius Brutus in honor of his augurship in Cuicul, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16400099.translation: For Matidia Augusta, daughter of the emperor, sister of the emperor, by decree of the decurions, with public money.
EDCS-16400099.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Matidia Augusta, daughter and sister of an emperor, funded by public money and decreed by the local council, likely in Roman North Africa.

EDCS-16400139.translation: All that the Nile irrigates all around, up to Euphrates, beyond the boundary of Valentinian.
EDCS-16400139.summary: This inscription describes a geographical or administrative boundary related to the Nile and Euphrates rivers, possibly referencing a territorial claim or division during the late Roman period, associated with Valentinian.

EDCS-16400459.translation: SL SI (or 'Soldiers of...') for the rebellious Bavarians, both in the previous command and afterwards in the duchy. Cornelius Octavianus, prefect of the praetorian fleet, acting (as leader).
EDCS-16400459.summary: This inscription records a military action or dedication by Cornelius Octavianus, prefect of the praetorian fleet, against rebellious Bavarians during the late Roman period.

EDCS-16400466.translation: Sacred to Janus the August Father, for the health of our lords Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, our August son, Parhician, Britannicus, Germanicus Maximus, and Julia Domna, mother of the camps. Gaius Julius Victor, armatura quaestor of the armatura, gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16400466.summary: A private dedication to Janus by Gaius Julius Victor, an armatura quaestor, for the health of the emperors and empress, likely dating to the reign of Septimius Severus and Caracalla.

EDCS-16400467.translation: To the deified Antoninus. Gaius Satrius Crescens, son of Gaius, of the Fabia tribe, from Rome, who was a member of the equestrian order and a first-rank centurion of Legio III Augusta, dedicated this, with Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, legatus Augustorum pro praetore, presiding.
EDCS-16400467.summary: A dedication to the deified Emperor Antoninus Pius by Gaius Satrius Crescens, a high-ranking military officer, likely during the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16400469.translation: From the generosity of Marcus Julius Quintianus Flavius Rogatianus, a man of most distinguished memory, which he bequeathed to the public of the colony of Thamugadi, his fatherland, the work of the library, completed with 400,000 sesterces, was carried out under the administration of the state.
EDCS-16400469.summary: This inscription records the completion of a library in the colony of Thamugadi, funded by a bequest from Marcus Julius Quintianus Flavius Rogatianus, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16400470.summary: A brief dedication to the genius of a local deity or emperor named Montius Augustus by Crementius Mespofylus, likely a private dedication.

EDCS-16400584.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Aulus Larcius Priscus, son of Aulus, of the Quirina tribe, former decemvir for judging lawsuits, quaestor of the province of Asia, legate of the Augusti of Legio IV Scythica, propraetorian legate of the consular province of Syria, tribune of the plebs, praetor of the province of Hispania Baetica, prefect of the grain supply, legate of the Augusti of Legio II Augusta, and propraetorian legate of the army of Africa, gladly fulfilled his vow. Valens, centurion of Legio III Augusta, [made it] out of [his own funds].
EDCS-16400584.summary: A lengthy dedication to Jupiter by Aulus Larcius Priscus, detailing his extensive senatorial and military career, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16400587.translation: To Flavius Aelius Victorinus, most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, our patron, by decree of the decurions, from the splendid town council of the colony of Cuiculitanus.
EDCS-16400587.summary: The town council of Cuiculitanus honors their patron, Flavius Aelius Victorinus, a Roman governor, by decree, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16400588.translation: To the Genius of the Senate of Cuiculitanus. Lucius Flavius Augustianus, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, aedile, prefect, acting as a duovir, augur, and duovir, placed this statue, which he had promised because of the honor of his aedileship, with its base, using 2,000 sesterces, doubling the money, and he himself dedicated it.
EDCS-16400588.summary: A dedication by Lucius Flavius Augustianus, an official in the colony of Cuiculitanus, honoring the Genius of the Senate and dedicating a statue he promised during his aedileship, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-16400593.translation: For the road, Sextus Asicius, a shopkeeper, dedicated [this] as a vow.
EDCS-16400593.summary: A simple votive dedication for a road by Sextus Asicius, a shopkeeper, likely a private offering.

EDCS-16400628.translation: To the Genius of Subtabartus, for the health of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, and Publius Septimius Geta nobilissimus Caesar, and Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the emperors and of the camps, the buyers of the toll stations of Iumiana, Sapona, Pontensis, and Cammensis, for the well-being of the age, made [this dedication] while [it was] being overseen by.
EDCS-16400628.summary: A dedication to the Genius of Subtabartus by the toll collectors of several locations, for the health and well-being of the ruling imperial family, likely dating to the reign of Septimius Severus and his sons.

EDCS-16500003.translation: To the Great Eternal God. Flavius Donatus and Julia Tertulla, his wife, fulfilled the vow which they had promised with a willing mind, as they were returning, He heard [them] with Fu sariu.
EDCS-16500003.summary: A votive dedication to a 'Great Eternal God' by Flavius Donatus and his wife Julia Tertulla, likely from the later Roman Empire, possibly influenced by mystery cults or Christianity.

EDCS-16500004.translation: By the authority of Axius Aelianus, a most distinguished man, procurator of the Emperor, this old boundary stone was placed according to the agreements between the Gurolensian fort, Medianum Matidianum, and the Alexandrian fort of Tilirna.
EDCS-16500004.summary: This inscription records the placement of a boundary stone between three forts, authorized by the procurator Axius Aelianus.

EDCS-16500137.translation: To the divine Holy Fortune and the genius of the place, Caius Iunianus Adiutor, centurion of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious and Victorious, paid his vow willingly, happily, and deservedly.
EDCS-16500137.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to Fortuna and the genius loci, dedicated by Caius Iunianus Adiutor, a centurion.

EDCS-16500138.translation: On these estates of Marcus Rutilius Castrensis, a hostel is maintained.
EDCS-16500138.summary: This inscription states that a hostel is maintained on the estates of Marcus Rutilius Castrensis.

EDCS-16500139.translation: During the reign of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus, Medicus, Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 23rd time, consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland, son of the divine Antoninus Pius, grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-grandson of the divine Nerva.
EDCS-16500139.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Marcus Aurelius, listing his numerous titles and honors, likely part of a larger dedication.

EDCS-16500202.translation: To this house of the god, the tribunal of the chaste, holy, and revered lady (goddess/martyr), Sabinianus, together with his wife and sons, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16500202.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to a female deity or martyr, fulfilled by Sabinianus and his family.

EDCS-16500203.translation: The vow is completed; let us give thanks to God from the workshop of Fortunus and his sons Victor.
EDCS-16500203.summary: This inscription marks the completion of a vow and gives thanks to God, originating from the workshop of Fortunus and Victor.

EDCS-16500204.translation: Deogratias fulfilled the vow of the armor-bearers.
EDCS-16500204.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Deogratias on behalf of the armor-bearers.

EDCS-16500206.translation: To Pluto Augustus, Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Medicus Germanicus Maximus, with tribunician power, Father of the Fatherland, he restored this temple to Pluto Augustus, which had fallen into ruin through age, from its foundations, enlarged it through his care, at his own expense, and he himself dedicated it.
EDCS-16500206.summary: This inscription records the restoration and dedication of a temple to Pluto Augustus by Emperor Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-16500222.translation: For Valubia Virria Flavia Severiana, daughter of Flavia Petroniana and Marcus Virrius Flavius Iugurtha, an equestrian of Rome and perpetual flamen, Pompeius Fuscus and Felix, showing the fidelity of paternal friendship through the permanence of this memorial, have granted this place by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-16500222.summary: This inscription records the granting of a place by decree of the decurions to commemorate Valubia Virria Flavia Severiana, honoring her father Marcus Virrius Flavius Iugurtha.

EDCS-16500234.summary: This very short inscription is likely a dedication or memorial for two individuals named Donatus and Crescentiana.

EDCS-16500250.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the deified emperor Aurelian.

EDCS-16500252.translation: To our lord Gratian, the Pious, Fortunate, Victorious, and triumphant, ever Augustus.
EDCS-16500252.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperor Gratian, likely from the late 4th century CE.

EDCS-16500253.translation: To the most sacred and unconquered Caesars, Flavius Valerius Constantius Junior, and Herculius, and Valerius Maximianus Iovius, Caesars.
EDCS-16500253.summary: This inscription honors multiple Caesars, including Constantius II and Maximian, likely dating to the Tetrarchic period or shortly after.

EDCS-16500254.translation: To the most sacred and unconquered Caesars, Valerius Constantius Junior, and Herculius, and Gaius Valerius Maximianus Iovius, Caesars.
EDCS-16500254.summary: This inscription honors multiple Caesars, including Constantius II and Maximian, likely dating to the Tetrarchic period or shortly after.

EDCS-16500257.translation: To Flavius Valerius Constantinus, son of the unconquered Emperor, Augustus.
EDCS-16500257.summary: This inscription honors Flavius Valerius Constantinus, son of an emperor, likely Constantine the Great.

EDCS-16600200.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Protector of our August Emperors, Lucius Maius Severus, military tribune of the Third Augustan Legion, has fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16600200.summary: This inscription records a military tribune of the Third Augustan Legion fulfilling a vow to Jupiter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16600209.translation: Celerinus, the aedile, established standardized measures at his own expense for his fellow citizens.
