EDCS-16600209.summary: This inscription records an aedile named Celerinus who provided standardized weights and measures for his community, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16600251.translation: Sacred to the Good August Goddess.
EDCS-16600251.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to the Bona Dea, a Roman goddess.

EDCS-16600252.translation: Sittia Urbana of Cirta lived 70 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-16600252.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittia Urbana of Cirta, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16600253.translation: Iulia Nanphama, daughter of [---], lived 45 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-16600253.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulia Nanphama, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16600254.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Teuda rests here. She lived for 43 years.
EDCS-16600254.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Teuda, who lived 43 years.

EDCS-16600384.translation: To Iilsar Es, most excellent patron, the town council of Lemellefa by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-16600384.summary: This is a dedication by the municipal council of Lemellefa to their patron, Iilsar Es.

EDCS-16700015.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, having inspected the exercises of his Legion III Augusta and spoken to them the words written below, on the Kalends of July in the consulship of Torquatus II and Libo, to the infantry.
EDCS-16700015.summary: This inscription records an address by Emperor Hadrian to the infantry of Legion III Augusta in July, during his consulship.

EDCS-16700016.translation: Here the memory of Saint Julian was deposited on the 3rd Ides of September. Floridus M T prescribed.
EDCS-16700016.summary: This inscription marks the deposition of the relics of Saint Julian, possibly related to a memorial or tomb.

EDCS-16700017.translation: Here the memory of Saint Julian was deposited on the 11th day of the 7th month in the 17th year of Justinian.
EDCS-16700017.summary: This inscription records the deposition of the relics of Saint Julian during the reign of Emperor Justinian.

EDCS-16700018.translation: Floridus the presbyter rendered his vow.
EDCS-16700018.summary: This is a votive inscription by Floridus, a presbyter, fulfilling a promise.

EDCS-16700019.translation: To Pluto Augustus. A. Datas fulfilled his vow upon returning.
EDCS-16700019.summary: This is a votive inscription by A. Datas to Pluto Augustus, made upon his return.

EDCS-16700033.translation: Mensa Quintais lived for 14 years. Donatus Posorica and Crescens of the province and VI made it.
EDCS-16700033.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Mensa Quintais, who died at age 14, made by Donatus Posorica and Crescens.

EDCS-16700034.translation: Atinius Augustalis, duplicarius of the elite troop, lived for 30 years.
EDCS-16700034.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Atinius Augustalis, a soldier in an elite unit, who died at age 30.

EDCS-16700096.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the other gods and goddesses. Tiberius Claudius Vitalis, primus pilus, from Interamna Praetuttis.
EDCS-16700096.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and other deities by Tiberius Claudius Vitalis, a senior centurion from Interamna Praetuttis.

EDCS-16700098.translation: The surveyors Abbonius Mucrubius, Sallustius Ianuarius, Arruntius Maximus, Afranius Lucius, Cossutius Gududus, Octavius Saturninus, Iulius Bassinus, Iulius Fortunatus, Iulius Emeritus, Aurelius Rusticus, Octavius Felix, Aurelius Rogatianus, Aelius Victor, Successus Successianus, Iulius Castricus, Iulius Guzabius.
EDCS-16700098.summary: This inscription lists the names of several men identified as 'surveyors', likely a professional collegium, and may represent a dedication or list of members.

EDCS-16700122.translation: To the magister of the Lares and to Bennius Sabinus the priest.
EDCS-16700122.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a magister of the Lares and to a priest named Bennius Sabinus.

EDCS-16700123.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Gudud Unabis lived for 61 years; he set this up for himself.
EDCS-16700123.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gudud Unabis, who set up the monument for himself and recorded his age at death.

EDCS-16700125.translation: Here is the memorial of the holy Antionianus.
EDCS-16700125.summary: This inscription marks the memorial of a holy person named Antionianus.

EDCS-16700126.translation: Here is the memorial of the holy Guresi.
EDCS-16700126.summary: This inscription marks the memorial of a holy person named Guresi.

EDCS-16700236.translation: In the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, the memorials of the holy Iulianus and Laurentius with their companions were placed by the hands of blessed Columba, bishop of the holy church of Nicaea, of this parish, at the instigation of Donatus the priest, during the reign of Tiberius, in the 5th year and 14th indiction, on the day before the Nones of October.
EDCS-16700236.summary: This inscription records the dedication of memorials for several saints by a bishop and priest, with a precise date during the reign of an unspecified Tiberius and a specific indiction and day in October, likely from the early medieval period.

EDCS-16700302.translation: By rescript of the god Probus, at the request of Munatius Flavianus, the market days of Emadaucapensis, free from taxes on the Kalends of March and the Ides of March, are to be celebrated; the most excellent Aurelius Diogenes, governor of Numidia, deigned to grant this favor.
EDCS-16700302.summary: This inscription, likely from the late Roman period, records a rescript from a divine figure named Probus, confirmed by the governor of Numidia, Aurelius Diogenes, granting tax immunity for market days in Emadaucapensis to Munatius Flavianus.

EDCS-16700339.translation: Here is the holy Pastor's memorial. Here the memorial of the holy Pastor was laid in peace.
EDCS-16700339.summary: This inscription marks the burial place or memorial of a holy person named Pastor.

EDCS-16700340.translation: Here is the memorial of the holy Laurentius. In the name of God, Floridus the priest rendered his vow in Christ.
EDCS-16700340.summary: This inscription records a memorial for the holy Laurentius and a vow fulfilled by Floridus the priest.

EDCS-16700341.translation: Here is the memorial of the holy Felix.
EDCS-16700341.summary: This inscription marks the memorial of a holy person named Felix.

EDCS-16800298.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the Divine Ancestors; Caius Iulius Martialis, Vovrea, magistrate, [did this] for the republic of the Phuensians.
EDCS-16800298.summary: This is a dedication to the Genius of the Divine Ancestors by a magistrate of the republic of Phuensians.

EDCS-16800302.translation: Sacred to Mercury Augustus; Lucius Grassidius, the priest, built the temple with his own money and dedicated it.
EDCS-16800302.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of a temple to Mercury Augustus by the priest Lucius Grassidius at his own expense.

EDCS-16800324.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus and for the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, greatest and most powerful prince of our Augusti, son of our Augustus, and for Julia Domna Augusta, mother of our Augusti and of the camps, [dedicated by] the curia of Victoria Antonini.
EDCS-16800324.summary: This is a dedication to the imperial family, including Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Julia Domna, likely by a local curia.

EDCS-16800370.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, chief priest, prince of youth, son of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Parthicus Arabicus Adiabenicus, chief priest; by decree of the decurions and at public expense, the pagus Trisipensis [dedicated this].
EDCS-16800370.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcus Aurelius Antoninus by the pagus Trisipensis, funded by public money and approved by the decurions.

EDCS-16800381.translation: Sacred to the god Malagbel; Iulius Faustus, with all his family, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-16800381.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Malagbel by Iulius Faustus and his family.

EDCS-16800425.translation: Caius Papirius Fortunatus, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, duumvir, aedile, prefect for administering justice, promised a statue, which together with another statue of Fortuna Redux of our Augusti, for the honor of his duumvirate, beyond the legal obligation or promise of 4000 sesterces brought to the republic, he set up for 2000 sesterces, and dedicated it, with a feast given to the curiae and stage plays performed.
EDCS-16800425.summary: This inscription details the commissioning and dedication of two statues by Caius Papirius Fortunatus, a local magistrate, for the city, with significant personal expenditure beyond his legal obligations.

EDCS-16800427.translation: To the Divine Shades; Caius Helvidius Diogenes, the father, prefect of cavalry, [erected this] for his son Caius Helvidius Diogenes.
EDCS-16800427.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by a father, Caius Helvidius Diogenes, to his son of the same name, who was a prefect of cavalry.

EDCS-16800429.translation: For Annio Armenio Donato, a most distinguished boy; Caius Vibius Marinus, a most distinguished man, friend and fellow citizen, [dedicated this] in place of his father.
EDCS-16800429.summary: This is a dedication to a young boy named Annio Armenio Donato by his father's friend and fellow citizen, Caius Vibius Marinus.

EDCS-16900098.translation: Iulia Fida, my wife, my equal in age, my joy; what your last wish commanded to be done, I, your husband, Quintus Herennius, surnamed Felix, have carried out according to your command, so that you may always rest in quiet in your own place, which will be common [to us] when the hour of death comes.
EDCS-16900098.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Quintus Herennius Felix for his deceased wife, Iulia Fida, fulfilling her last wishes.

EDCS-16900115.translation: By the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, in his second consulship, with tribunician power for the 14th time, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva; Marcus Valerius Etruscus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, patron of the colony, by decree of the decurions and at public expense, dedicated the paved street.
EDCS-16900115.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a paved street by Marcus Valerius Etruscus, a legate and patron of the colony, during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-16900133.translation: The officials of Aelius Saturninus, prefect of the Third Augustan, Pious, Victorious Legion, Marcus Baebius Speratus, cornicularius, and the scribes whose names are listed below, funded the school with sacred images from their very ample stipends and donations. On account of this ceremony, they decreed that the annulators should pay to their veterans and to any who advance, each, 4000 sesterces without delay, and to the scribes.
EDCS-16900133.summary: This inscription records the funding and construction of a legionary school with sacred images by military officers and scribes of the Third Augustan Legion.

EDCS-16900146.translation: Officials in Carthage, quartermaster in the camp; trumpeter in the camp; Flavia Apus of Anaunia from Hadrumetum; quartermaster in the camp; quartermaster in the camp; from Theveste; in Carthage N. Flavia Setulius Aemilianus, Lucius Quintius Silo, Lucius Valerius Longus, Marcus Iulius Victor, Caius Iulius Primus, Quintus Postumius Ap, Marcus Iuvenilius, Lucius Reius, Caius Valerius, Caius Iulius, Caius Iulius.
EDCS-16900146.summary: This inscription lists names and titles of military personnel, likely associated with various locations in Roman Africa, with a focus on administrative and musical roles.

EDCS-16900147.translation: From Cirta, Crescens from Theveste, Rogatianus from Zmares, nlius Saturninus from Bagai, Felix the adjutant from the camp, ninus T.
EDCS-16900147.summary: This inscription lists names and places of origin or service for individuals, likely military or administrative personnel, in Roman North Africa.

EDCS-17000071.translation: The record office of the legion with images of the divine house, from their very ample stipends and donations, was constructed by Lucius Aemilius Cattianus, cornicularius, and Titus Flavius Surus, actarius, and the scribes and tax collectors of the Third Augustan, Pious, Victorious Legion, whose names are listed below. On account of this ceremony, it was decreed that if anyone is substituted in place of a cornicularius or actarius of the legion who has been honorably discharged, he shall give to the one whose place he has taken 1000 denarii in the name of the annulator; likewise, if anyone is substituted in place of a scribe, he shall give 1000 denarii in the name of the scamnarii to the colleges; and if anyone from the same college has been honorably discharged, he shall receive 800 denarii in the name of the annulator; and if anyone has advanced from the colleges, he shall receive 200 denarii. Tax collectors: Caius Aponius Vitalis, Domitius Paulinus, Abinneus Victorinus, Aurelius Optatus, Caius Iulius Aurelianus, Florius Celsus, Lucius Plotius Vitalis, Lucius Aemilius Quadratus, Caius Iulius Crescentianus, Decimus Valerius Quintianus, Quintus Vereius Vereianus, Marcus Valerius Proculus, Marcus Strobulius Marcianus, Marcus Cornelius Augurianus, Aemilius Clarus, Clodius Victor, Marcus Castricius Frugi, Aelius Nummenius, Iunius Saturninus, Lucius Munatius Felix, Lucius Tonneius Martialis, the brass-worker.
EDCS-17000071.summary: This inscription details the construction of a legionary record office and establishes regulations and payments for officers and members of collegia within the Third Augustan Legion.

EDCS-17100045.translation: Memorial for Felicianus, of the publicani of Africa, IIII. Iulia SVESE.
EDCS-17100045.summary: This is a memorial inscription for Felicianus, associated with public tax collectors in Africa, with a possible place name or family name.

EDCS-17100190.translation: The Venusianenses initiated the new basilica; the Mucrionenses gave five columns; the Cuzabetenses gave six columns; all paved the apses; the Cuzabetenses decorated further. Rogatus the priest and Aemilius the deacon built.
EDCS-17100190.summary: This inscription records the contributions of various communities (Venusianenses, Mucrionenses, Cuzabetenses) towards the construction and decoration of a basilica, with a priest and deacon credited for building.

EDCS-17100198.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-nephew of the deified Hadrian, great-great-nephew of the deified Trajanus Parthicus, descendant of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul; and to the Emperor Caesar, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, nephew of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-nephew of the deified Hadrian, descendant of the deified Trajanus Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, prince of the youth, most valiant, most fortunate, most indulgent; the expanded baths were dedicated by decree of the decurions, from public funds, by the legate Quintus Anicius Faustus, Augustorum pro praetore, patron of the colony.
EDCS-17100198.summary: This inscription dedicates expanded public baths in a colony to Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, detailing their full titulatures and the authority under which the dedication was made.

EDCS-17100200.translation: In these private estates of Publius Iulius Iunianus Martialis, clarissimus vir, the revenues are let to Publius Iulius Iunianus Tironianus, his son, and the Leontii.
EDCS-17100200.summary: This inscription records the leasing of revenues from the private estates of Publius Iulius Iunianus Martialis to his son and the Leontii family.

EDCS-17100201.translation: To Liber Pater Augustus, sacred. Lucius Calpurnius Fortunatus, cistifer and attendant, and Iulia Pattara, his wife and attendant, and Calpurnius Restutus, cistifer and attendant, and Calpurnia Fortunata, his daughter and attendant, and the sons of Calpurnius Fortunatus, cistiferi and attendants, fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-17100201.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the god Liber Pater by members of a family, identified by their roles as 'cistifer' and 'pedisequarius' (attendants).

EDCS-17100203.translation: Lucius Calidius Bassus, prefect of the Second Cohort of Thracian Gemella.
EDCS-17100203.summary: This is a military inscription identifying Lucius Calidius Bassus as the prefect of a specific cohort.

EDCS-17100217.translation: The inhabitants of the estate of Thavagel made this for Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Augustus Pius Felix Britannicus Maximus, the pacifier.
EDCS-17100217.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the colonists of the fundus Thavagel to the emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Caracalla) around the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-17200015.translation: Publilius Ceionius Caecina Albinus, a most illustrious man, consular, sexfascalis of the province of Numidia, set this up for the concord of our lords Valentinian and Valens, perpetual Augusti, with Aelius Iulianus, perpetual flamen and curator of the res publica, overseeing it.
EDCS-17200015.summary: This inscription records a dedication set up by Publilius Ceionius Caecina Albinus for the emperors Valentinian and Valens, likely in the mid-4th century CE.

EDCS-17300076.translation: Quintus Publicius Crescens, magister of the pagus and centuria of the Thigillavenses, set this up with his own money for the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva.
EDCS-17300076.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Quintus Publicius Crescens for the emperor Hadrian, likely in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-17500066.translation: Benenatus fashioned a worthy crown for his worthy father Arcentius.
EDCS-17500066.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument, likely a tombstone, set up by Benenatus for his father Arcentius, date unknown.

EDCS-18100237.translation: In memory of my patrons and lords, Quintus Bullatius Sabinus the father, his sons Quintus Bullatius Sabinus and Donatus, and his grandsons Bullatius Sabinus and Honorata, Quintus Bullatius Sabinus, tribune of the I Cohort of Syrians.
EDCS-18100237.summary: This inscription records a memorial set up by Quintus Bullatius Sabinus, a military tribune, to honor his patrons and family members, date unknown.

EDCS-18300232.translation: The lake of lions Cerbus am
EDCS-18300232.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragment of a larger text, possibly describing a feature or a mythological scene, date unknown.

EDCS-18300236.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Caius Iulius Metthun lived 20 years. His father made this.
EDCS-18300236.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Iulius Metthun, who died at the age of 20, set up by his father, date unknown.

EDCS-18300239.translation: Here is the house of our God Christ, here the dwelling of the Holy Paraclete Spirit, here the memory of the blessed martyr of God, Emeritus, consulted by God; here shall be heard everyone who invokes the name of the Lord Almighty God; why do you wonder, man? with God's help, you will see better things. In the provincial year 340.
EDCS-18300239.summary: This inscription is from a Christian site, likely a memoria or church, referring to Christ, the Holy Spirit, and a martyr, dated to the provincial year 340 (likely 340 CE).

EDCS-18300240.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Caius Considius Dexter lived 105 years. Good, best.
EDCS-18300240.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Considius Dexter, who lived to 105 years old, date unknown.

EDCS-18300243.translation: Here remains the memory of a most pious wife, for whom Caius Iunius Victor, centurion of the III Augusta Legion, who died while alive, with his children, made this for the deceased for 63,000 sesterces.
EDCS-18300243.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument for a pious wife, funded by her husband Caius Iunius Victor, a centurion, and his children, likely in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-18300244.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Quintus Calpurnius Ianuarius lived 40 years. Iulia Maior made this for her dearest husband.
EDCS-18300244.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Calpurnius Ianuarius, set up by his wife Iulia Maior.

EDCS-18300245.translation: With the help of the gods Caelestis, Saturnus, Mercurius, and Fortuna, Lucius Octavius Felix, a decurion of the cohort of Spaniards, having been a beneficiary of the legate, paid his vow at that time.
EDCS-18300245.summary: A military dedication by Lucius Octavius Felix, a decurion, to several gods, likely related to his service.

EDCS-18300247.translation: To Cautopates. Eutyces, a very fortunate servant of our Emperors, paid his vow and dedicated this from his own funds as a vicarius of the tax collector.
EDCS-18300247.summary: A dedication to the god Cautopates by Eutyces, a tax collector's assistant, for his good fortune.

EDCS-18300248.translation: Quintus Minucius Saturninus, Quintus Sallustius Felix, a veteran, paid his vow willingly, with Cornelianus as father.
EDCS-18300248.summary: A vow paid by two veterans, Quintus Minucius Saturninus and Quintus Sallustius Felix, possibly indicating a shared vow or a family connection.

EDCS-18300249.translation: Sacred to the August Mother of the Gods. Gaius Sittius Ianuarius, a priest, gave this offering for the safety of our Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti, and of Publius Septimius Geta Caesar, and of Iulia Augusta, and of their entire divine family.
EDCS-18300249.summary: A religious dedication by the priest Gaius Sittius Ianuarius to the Mother of the Gods for the imperial family's safety during the Severan dynasty.

EDCS-18300250.translation: To Pluto Augustus. Aelius Cirrus willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-18300250.summary: A dedication to the god Pluto by Aelius Cirrus, who willingly paid a vow.

EDCS-18300251.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Adiectus, a farm manager, paid his vow willingly for his livestock.
EDCS-18300251.summary: A dedication to Saturn Augustus by Adiectus, a farm manager, for his livestock.

EDCS-18300252.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Lucius Aelius.
EDCS-18300252.summary: A dedication to Saturn Augustus by Lucius Aelius, likely a brief record of ownership or presence.

EDCS-18300253.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Lollius Rogatus, a priest, paid his vow willingly for his livestock.
EDCS-18300253.summary: A dedication to Saturn Augustus by Lollius Rogatus, a priest, for his livestock.

EDCS-18300254.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Narcius Rusticus, from the livestock, paid his vow willingly.
EDCS-18300254.summary: A dedication to Saturn Augustus by Narcius Rusticus, who paid a vow for his livestock.

EDCS-18300255.translation: Sacred to Imperial Saturn. Publius Pomponius Ianuarius, priest of the flocks, fulfilled his vow willingly with all his heart.
EDCS-18300255.summary: A votive inscription to Saturn, dedicated by a priest of the flocks, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18300256.translation: Sacred to Imperial Saturn. Publius Pullaienus Montanus, priest, fulfilled his vow willingly with all his heart.
EDCS-18300256.summary: A votive inscription to Saturn, dedicated by a priest, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-18300264.translation: Thomas, the prefect, also constructed these walls, but adding to them another decoration of better strength, he called the city Tiberias in honour of Caesar, and with the Lord Christ helping, and with the Emperor our Lord most Invincible Tiberius Augustus confirming, and Gennadius the master of soldiers and patrician, Victor the tribune, he established his own people.
EDCS-18300264.summary: An inscription recording the construction of city walls, possibly in honour of Emperor Tiberius, by a prefect named Thomas, with religious and military officials mentioned, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-18300266.translation: They placed (this) for Victor, son of Illorn, most fortunate.
EDCS-18300266.summary: A short dedication, possibly for a tomb or a monument, honoring a person named Victor.

EDCS-18300267.translation: Sacred to the Dis Manibus. Julius Marci filius Nundinarius, aedile, twice duovir for five years, of equestrian rank, (set this up) for Lucius Iulius Crispinus of equestrian family, who died in Hispania, by Juliae Honoratae, daughter of Diani, from military service, and for Quintus Iulius Rogatus, son (of Diani), in memory of them, and of his very rare wife Demetria Hiea; he made (this) for himself and his surviving sons Iulius Demetrianus and Iulius Sallustianus, and for the Dis Manibus.
EDCS-18300267.summary: A funerary monument set up by Julius Marci filius Nundinarius for his deceased relative Lucius Iulius Crispinus, his wife Demetria Hiea, and for himself and his surviving sons, indicating his own family history and public career.

EDCS-18300268.translation: Of CBACAI NTI I NIN S, centurion of the eleventh legion Claudiae Augustae.
EDCS-18300268.summary: An inscription identifying a centurion of the Legio III Augustae Undecima Claudia.

EDCS-18300269.summary: A tombstone inscription for a soldier named Cornelius Valens.

EDCS-18300271.translation: Sacred to the Dis Manibus. Caecilia Bona lived 21 years. Her mother made (this) for her deserving daughter.
EDCS-18300271.summary: A tombstone inscription set up by a mother for her deceased daughter, Caecilia Bona.

EDCS-18300272.summary: A tombstone inscription for Claudia Helpis.

EDCS-18300274.translation: Sacred to the Dis Manibus. Eumusus lived 30 years. Epictesis made (this) for her deserving fellow slave.
EDCS-18300274.summary: A tombstone inscription set up by Epictesis for her fellow slave Eumusus.

EDCS-18300275.summary: This inscription is a simple naming of Fabia Aemiliana, likely a funerary dedication.

EDCS-18300276.translation: Flavidianus lived 40 years with all his family, he is buried here. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-18300276.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Flavidianus and stating that he is buried here.

EDCS-18300277.translation: He lived 23 years. Flavius Menodo made this for her.
EDCS-18300277.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of the deceased and stating that Flavius Menodo made it, likely as a husband.

EDCS-18300278.translation: To Titus Flavius Valerianus, son of Titus Flavius Sosander, of the Quirina tribe, brother of T. F. Sosander, he lived 3 months and 4 days.
EDCS-18300278.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Valerianus, detailing his parentage, tribal affiliation, and age at death.

EDCS-18300279.translation: Annia Fortunata, wife of SNV Tichadas, lived 96 years.
EDCS-18300279.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name, marital status, and age at death of Annia Fortunata.

EDCS-18300280.translation: To the departed spirits. Gargilia Castula lived 21 years. Statia Ianuaria, her devoted mother, made this.
EDCS-18300280.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gargilia Castula, stating her age at death and that her devoted mother, Statia Ianuaria, commissioned the monument.

EDCS-18300281.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Geminia Quinta lived 55 years. Flavius Atus made this for the deserving one.
EDCS-18300281.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Geminia Quinta, recording her age at death and that Flavius Atus commissioned the monument.

EDCS-18300282.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aulius Felix and Marcus Aulius Felix made this monument, father and mother; he lived 50 years and the mother 100 years.
EDCS-18300282.summary: This funerary epitaph records a monument made by parents for their children (implied), specifying the lifespans of the father (50 years) and mother (100 years).

EDCS-18300283.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulia Peregrina, dutiful, lived 60 years. Cannius Crescens made and dedicated this for his most dutiful wife.
EDCS-18300283.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulia Peregrina, recording her age at death and that her husband, Cannius Crescens, commissioned and dedicated the monument.

EDCS-18300284.translation: Iulia Redita lived 6 years. Caius Iulius Messius, father, for his dearest, sweetest daughter, beloved by all.
EDCS-18300284.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulia Redita, stating her age at death and that her father, Caius Iulius Messius, commissioned it out of love.

EDCS-18300286.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Titus Maecilius Elpidinius.

EDCS-18300287.translation: Pontia made this for her dearest wife, having been deceived by a bull, lived 35 years.
EDCS-18300287.summary: This epitaph records the death of a woman, likely killed by a bull, and was erected by her wife. The cause of death is unusual.

EDCS-18300288.translation: His wife made this monument for Sapidus; he lived 51 years.
EDCS-18300288.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Sapidus and was erected by his wife.

EDCS-18300289.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Valeria Saturnina lived 60 years.
EDCS-18300289.summary: This is an epitaph dedicated to the Manes (spirits of the dead) for Valeria Saturnina.

EDCS-18300291.translation: Flabius Abus, a domestic official, completed what he promised in the name of the father and son of his lord Muntanus.
EDCS-18300291.summary: This is a dedication by Flabius Abus, a domestic official, fulfilling a vow to his lord Muntanus.

EDCS-18300294.translation: By decree of the decurions, at public expense, for the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-18300294.summary: This inscription records a public dedication funded by the local council to Emperor Lucius Aurelius Verus, likely commemorating a visit or benefaction.

EDCS-18300295.translation: Publius Antonius Cassianus, procurator of the Emperor, a distinguished man, dedicated this to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, conqueror of Armenia, Parthia, Media, conqueror of Germany, Sarmatia, Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 29th time, imperator for the 8th time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-18300295.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Publius Antonius Cassianus, an imperial procurator, to Emperor Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-18300296.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Augustus, the Pious, Victorious in Parthia, Victorious in Britain, Great Pontiff, Victorious in Germany, Father of the Fatherland, holding tribunician power, emperor, consul for the fourth time.
EDCS-18300296.summary: This inscription is a titulature of Emperor Caracalla, listing his various titles and honors.

EDCS-18300297.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Quintus Valerius Ianuarius, a soldier of the 9th Praetorian Cohort, and Martialis of the 3rd Augustan Legion, made this for their deserving father.
EDCS-18300297.summary: This epitaph was erected by two soldiers, one from the Praetorian Guard and one from a legion, for their father.

EDCS-18300298.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Acia Marcia lived [XX] years.
EDCS-18300298.summary: This is an epitaph dedicated to the Manes (spirits of the dead) for Acia Marcia.

EDCS-18300299.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Aemilius Satullus lived 110 years. Quintus Aemilius (made this).
EDCS-18300299.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Aemilius Satullus, recording his age and presumably made by a relative of the same name.

EDCS-18300300.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Felix lived 10 years. Annia V A made and dedicated this.
EDCS-18300300.summary: This funerary epitaph records the short life of Felix and was made by Annia V A.

EDCS-18300819.translation: Of Aemilius Faustus, 40 [years]. Of Aemilius Faustus, 45 [years].
EDCS-18300819.summary: This inscription, likely from a tombstone, records the ages of two individuals named Aemilius Faustus.

EDCS-20100111.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caius Aterius Pius lived about 70 years. Septimia Sal, his wife, made this.
EDCS-20100111.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Aterius Pius, set up by his wife.

EDCS-20100112.translation: To the memory of Calpurnia Proculina, who lived 32 years.
EDCS-20100112.summary: This inscription records the age of Calpurnia Proculina, likely on a tomb.

EDCS-20100115.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Titus Flavius Germanus lived 29 years. Titus Flavius Germanus made this for his dutiful son; he is buried here.
EDCS-20100115.summary: This funerary epitaph records the age of Titus Flavius Germanus and was made by his father of the same name.

EDCS-20100116.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. P. Faustina lived 32 years.
EDCS-20100116.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of P. Faustina.

EDCS-20100117.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Servilius Florus lived [age unspecified]. His sons and heirs, Servilius Fortunatus and Gaetulicus, made this for him who deserved well.
EDCS-20100117.summary: This funerary epitaph for Lucius Servilius Florus was erected by his sons and heirs.

EDCS-20100118.translation: MV IDNICEES in his own name from the R BQVISTVARVI sites TVE Brica
EDCS-20100118.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to record land ownership or a dedication, with personal and possibly place names.

EDCS-20100120.translation: X memorial stone V H I for Bishop Ciprianus of Bagaliensis, 220 years P D C
EDCS-20100120.summary: This inscription appears to be a memorial, possibly religious, for Bishop Ciprianus of Bagaliensis, with dates that are difficult to interpret fully.

EDCS-20100124.translation: To the divine shades. Anila Domitia Gemina, daughter of Natalis, and priestess of the god Hercules Primus, erected this temple at her own expense.
EDCS-20100124.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple to Hercules Primus by Anila Domitia Gemina, who was also a priestess, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100125.translation: Sacred to the divine shades. Florus, son of Titus Flavius Minianus, a most distinguished youth, his devoted slave, lived 35 years. Fortunata, his fellow slave, made this monument for her devoted husband.
EDCS-20100125.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life of Florus, a freedman, who lived 35 years, and was commemorated by his fellow slave, Fortunata, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100127.translation: Sacred to the divine shades. Amphurus, devoted, lived 110 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-20100127.summary: This epitaph records the remarkable age of 110 years attained by Amphurus, who is buried here, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100128.translation: Sacred to the divine shades. Evocata, devoted mother, lived [X] years. Her devoted father placed [this].
EDCS-20100128.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Evocata, a mother, who lived for an unspecified number of years, and was commemorated by her father, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100129.translation: Sacred to the divine shades. Iulia Rogata lived 65 years. Iulius Ianuarius, her son-in-law, and Iulia Berregt, his most devoted daughter, made [this]. She is buried here.
EDCS-20100129.summary: This epitaph records the death of Iulia Rogata at age 65, commemorated by her son-in-law Iulius Ianuarius and daughter Iulia Berregt, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100130.translation: Sacred to the divine shades. Lucius [N]ius Natalis lived 81 years.
EDCS-20100130.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Lucius Nius Natalis, who lived 81 years, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100131.translation: Sacred to the divine shades. Tiparena, devoted, lived 5 years. Sacred to the divine shades. Fortunata, devoted, lived 45 years. Sacred to the divine shades. Primitiva, devoted, lived 5 years.
EDCS-20100131.summary: This inscription lists three deceased individuals, Tiparena, Fortunata, and Primitiva, noting their devotion and ages (5, 45, and 5 years respectively), likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100132.translation: To the shades. Vitalis lived 40 years.
EDCS-20100132.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Vitalis, who lived 40 years, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100133.translation: [...] OISMEVS V QENI V M LIX pious V
EDCS-20100133.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the name and age (59 years) of a devoted individual, possibly a soldier or veteran, with a partial restoration of names, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100135.translation: Sacred to the divine shades. Mechiet lived 80 years; likewise his wife SAHNAMT Musavis lived 65 years.
EDCS-20100135.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates a married couple, Mechiet and Sahnamt Musavis, recording their ages as 80 and 65 years respectively, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100136.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aelia Romana lived for 70 years.
EDCS-20100136.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Romana, recording her age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100137.summary: This is a short religious expression of praise to God, possibly from a Christian context.

EDCS-20100138.translation: In the name of the Father, the Lord God, who is the word, Donatus and Navigius, sinners from Cedie, made this.
EDCS-20100138.summary: This inscription, possibly Christian, records Donatus and Navigius from Cedie as the makers of this monument.

EDCS-20100139.translation: Quintus Minucius Saturninus, priest, and his wife Iulia Veneria, priestess of Juno, fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-20100139.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by a priest and his wife, who was a priestess of Juno.

EDCS-20100141.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulia Faustina lived for 65 years.
EDCS-20100141.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Faustina, recording her age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100142.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Pulit Mari, son of Quintulus, lived for 70 years.
EDCS-20100142.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Pulit Mari, son of Quintulus, recording his age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20100143.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Assinis lived. His wish.
EDCS-20100143.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Assinis, possibly recording a personal wish related to their burial.

EDCS-20100145.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Ilius Octavius lived for 75 years. His [relatives] Octavius Octavius made [this]. He is buried here.
EDCS-20100145.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ilius Octavius, recording his age at death and identifying relatives who erected the monument.

EDCS-20100146.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To the pious Hermetus.
EDCS-20100146.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Hermetus, described as pious.

EDCS-20100147.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Aelius Ianvarius lived for 10 years. [Son of] Faustus.
EDCS-20100147.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Aelius Ianvarius, recording his age at death and his father, Faustus.

EDCS-20100148.translation: Clodius Felix lived 42 years. Clodius Felix [erected this] at his own expense.
EDCS-20100148.summary: A funerary epitaph for Clodius Felix, who lived to be 42 years old, erected by himself.

EDCS-20100149.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Caius Flavius Diofotus lived 27 years.
EDCS-20100149.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Flavius Diofotus, who lived to be 27 years old.

EDCS-20100151.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Titus Calius Fuscinus lived 50 years. His heirs dedicated [this monument] to their father.
EDCS-20100151.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Calius Fuscinus, who lived to be 50 years old, dedicated by his heirs.

EDCS-20100152.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Villius Sulla lived 90 years. His heir made and dedicated [this monument].
EDCS-20100152.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Villius Sulla, who lived to be 90 years old, made and dedicated by his heir.

EDCS-20100153.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Antonius Secundus lived 40 years. He lies here.
EDCS-20100153.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Antonius Secundus, who lived to be 40 years old.

EDCS-20100154.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Ianuaria Namgedenia lived 85 years. Caius M. Felix made [this monument] from his own peculium.
EDCS-20100154.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ianuaria Namgedenia, who lived to be 85 years old, made by Caius M. Felix.

EDCS-20100155.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lailius Rufus lived 75 years. His heirs made [this monument].
EDCS-20100155.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lailius Rufus, who lived to be 75 years old, made by his heirs.

EDCS-20100156.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Octavia Lailim lived 65 years. Marcus Larius Quintulus, her husband, made [this monument].
EDCS-20100156.summary: A funerary epitaph for Octavia Lailim, who lived to be 65 years old, made by her husband Marcus Larius Quintulus.

EDCS-20100157.translation: Menu lived 14 years. Pious [son/daughter?], he/she gave the family [this monument?].
EDCS-20100157.summary: A funerary epitaph for Menu, who lived to be 14 years old, likely erected by family members.

EDCS-20100158.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. L. Caelanus Faustina lived 106 years.
EDCS-20100158.summary: A funerary epitaph for L. Caelanus Faustina, who lived to be 106 years old.

EDCS-20100159.translation: To the Divine Shades. Nigra lived piously for 3 years; she is laid here. To the Divine Shades of Claudius Callistus. 20
EDCS-20100159.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Nigra, who lived for 3 years, and mentions Claudius Callistus, likely a family member.

EDCS-20100160.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Sallustia Fabia lived for 33 years.
EDCS-20100160.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Sallustia Fabia.

EDCS-20100161.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Sallustia Urbana lived for 14 years.
EDCS-20100161.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Sallustia Urbana.

EDCS-20100163.translation: Publius Petronius Tunninus promised and fulfilled their vow to their god and Christ, with God's favor, Gadiniana flore.
EDCS-20100163.summary: This inscription records a vow made and fulfilled by Publius Petronius Tunninus to a deity and Christ, mentioning Gadiniana.

EDCS-20100164.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Valeria Successa made this in her lifetime; she lived for 60 years.
EDCS-20100164.summary: This funerary inscription records that Valeria Successa dedicated this monument during her lifetime and lived for 60 years.

EDCS-20100165.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Domitia Fortunata lived piously for 85 years.
EDCS-20100165.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Domitia Fortunata.

EDCS-20100166.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Domitius Frauctus made this for himself and his family while alive.
EDCS-20100166.summary: This funerary inscription records that Domitius Frauctus dedicated this monument for himself and his family during his lifetime.

EDCS-20100167.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, Tribune of the Plebs for the 10th time, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Fatherland. Titus Aelius Datus, freedman of Augustus, made this at his own expense and dedicated it.
EDCS-20100167.summary: This is a dedication by Titus Aelius Datus, a freedman of Emperor Antoninus Pius, for the emperor, likely commemorating public works or honors.

EDCS-20100168.translation: Lucius Acilius Granianus gave this as a gift to Aesculapius for his father-in-law, Lucius Julius Ianuarius, to adorn the baths.
EDCS-20100168.summary: This inscription records a gift of a statue or object to the god Aesculapius by Lucius Acilius Granianus for his father-in-law, Lucius Iulius Ianuarius, intended for the decoration of the baths.

EDCS-20100169.translation: Sacred... Caius Publicius Celer, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, twice chief magistrate designated, having contributed the legal sum of 4000 sesterces to the public treasury from 4000 sesterces, placed this, and dedicated it by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-20100169.summary: This inscription records the public works or monument funded by Caius Publicius Celer, who contributed 4000 sesterces and was designated as a quinquennial duovir, and was dedicated by local council decree.

EDCS-20100171.translation: To Diana Augusta, Publius Iulius Liberalis, priest of the province of Africa, twice duovir and quinquennial magistrate, prefect for administering justice in the colonia of Thysdrus, and perpetual priest, gave this in the name of his daughters Iulia and dedicated it by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-20100171.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Diana Augusta by Publius Iulius Liberalis, a local magistrate in the colonia of Thysdrus, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20100172.translation: To Fortuna Redux of Augustus, Caius Annius Victor, son of Caius, of the Papiria tribe, perpetual priest and aedile, placed this statue, which he pledged in addition to his official duties for the honor of his aedileship, for 16,000 sesterces, and dedicated it with public games.
EDCS-20100172.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Fortuna Redux by Caius Annius Victor, a local magistrate, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20100173.translation: To the Genius of virtues, to Mars Augustus the Preserver, Valerius Florus, a most accomplished man and governor of the province of Numidia Militiana, dedicated this to the divine majesty of Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble and most brave Caesar, having been devoted to them, under the supervision of Iulius Lambesius, curator of the public affairs.
EDCS-20100173.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Genius of virtues and Mars Augustus by Valerius Florus, governor of Numidia Militiana, likely during the reign of Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus (late 3rd/early 4th century CE).

EDCS-20100174.translation: To Hercules Augustus the Preserver of our lords the Emperors Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Invictus and always Fortunate Augustus, Valerius Florus, a most accomplished man and governor of the province of Numidia Militiana, dedicated this to their divine majesty, having been devoted to them, under the supervision of Iulius Lambesius, curator of the public affairs.
EDCS-20100174.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Hercules Augustus by Valerius Florus, governor of Numidia Militiana, likely during the reign of Emperor Maximian (late 3rd/early 4th century CE).

EDCS-20100175.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus the Preserver of our lord the Emperor Caius Valerius Diocletian Invictus and always Fortunate Augustus, Valerius Florus, a most accomplished man and governor of the province of Numidia Militiana, dedicated this to their divine majesty, having been devoted to them, under the supervision of Iulius Lambesius, curator of the public affairs.
EDCS-20100175.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Valerius Florus, governor of Numidia Militiana, likely during the reign of Emperor Diocletian (late 3rd/early 4th century CE).

EDCS-20100177.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Caius Nonius Donatus, priest, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20100177.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Saturn Augustus by Caius Nonius Donatus, a priest, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20100178.translation: Sacred to Sol Augustus. Valerius Carpus, an Augustalis, with the honor of the decurionate conferred upon him by the most sacred order, placed this statue, which he promised for 4,000 sesterces on the occasion of his magistracy, for 5,000 sesterces and also gave and dedicated the gift.
EDCS-20100178.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue to Sol Augustus by Valerius Carpus, an Augustalis and decurion, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20100180.translation: Sacred to Victoria. Lucius Cestius Successus placed this at his own expense.
EDCS-20100180.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Victoria by Lucius Cestius Successus, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20100181.translation: Sacred to Victoria Parthica Augusta. From the will of Marcus Annius Martialis, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, a soldier of Legio III Augusta, a duplicarius of the Pannonian ala, a decurion of the same ala, and a centurion of Legio III Augusta et XXX Ulpia Victrix, who was honorably discharged by the Emperor Trajan Optimus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus Parthicus, Protus Hilarus and Eros, freedmen of Annius Marcius, at an additional cost of 3,000 sesterces each, saw to the erection of statues costing 8,000 sesterces and 20 (sestertii) more than the twentieth part of the inheritance, and they also dedicated the gift to Victoria Parthica Augusta.
EDCS-20100181.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a will for a dedication to Victoria Parthica Augusta by the freedmen of Marcus Annius Martialis, a decorated soldier of Emperor Trajan, likely in the early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-20100185.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontiff, with tribunician power for the 10th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, by decree of the decurions, with public funds, Varius, legate of Augustus, pro praetore, patron of the colonia, dedicated this.
EDCS-20100185.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Varius, a legate of Augustus and patron of the colonia, during the reign of Emperor Hadrian (mid-2nd century CE).

EDCS-20100189.translation: Marcus Caelius Saturninus, son of Marcus, of the Horatia tribe, for the honor of the quinquennial office, having presented 5,000,000 sesterces to the state, erected and dedicated [this monument] by decree of the decurions, to Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius Augustus, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-20100189.summary: This inscription records a public benefaction by Marcus Caelius Saturninus for the quinquennial office, dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-20100190.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Caesar, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-20100190.summary: This inscription records a public dedication to Marcus Aurelius Caesar, funded by the state.

EDCS-20100199.translation: Marcus Pompeius Pudentianus, a veteran and perpetual flamen, erected and dedicated [this monument] to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, for the honor of the flamenship, beyond the legal requirement, and a statue of Mars at the altar, with a Pantheon, at his own expense.
EDCS-20100199.summary: This inscription records a private dedication by Marcus Pompeius Pudentianus, a veteran and flamen, to Emperor Marcus Aurelius, including a statue and Pantheon.

EDCS-20100205.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.
EDCS-20100205.summary: This is a short dedication to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.

EDCS-20100206.translation: The state of the colony of Thamugadi, devoted to the divine power and majesty of Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, Germanicus maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-20100206.summary: This inscription records the devotion of the colony of Thamugadi to Emperor Valerian, likely from his 4th tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-20100207.translation: The state of the colony of Thamugadi, devoted to the divine power and majesty of Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, Germanicus maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-20100207.summary: This inscription records the devotion of the colony of Thamugadi to Emperor Gallienus, likely from his 3rd tribunician power and 2nd consulship.

EDCS-20100208.translation: The state of the colony of Thamugadi, devoted to the divine power and majesty of Publius Cornelius Licinius Valerianus, most noble Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-20100208.summary: This inscription records the devotion of the colony of Thamugadi to the Caesar, Publius Cornelius Licinius Valerianus (likely an early inscription for Valerian).

EDCS-20100209.translation: The state of the colony of Thamugadi, devoted to the divine power and majesty of Publius Licinius Cornelius Valerianus, most noble Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-20100209.summary: This inscription records the devotion of the colony of Thamugadi to the Caesar, Publius Licinius Cornelius Valerianus (likely an early inscription for Valerian).

EDCS-20100212.translation: The state of the colony of Thamugad, [dedicated to] our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Augustus, victorious in valor, outstanding in piety, always and everywhere victorious.
EDCS-20100212.summary: This inscription records the devotion of the colony of Thamugadi to Emperor Constantine the Great.

EDCS-20100213.translation: The state and the council of the colony of Thamugad erected and dedicated [this monument] to our lord Flavius Claudius Julianus Invictus Pius Felix semper Augustus, conqueror of enemies, invincible emperor, most indulgent prince, under the supervision of Flavius Aquilinus, perpetual flamen and curator of the state.
EDCS-20100213.summary: This inscription records the erection and dedication of a monument by the colony of Thamugad to Emperor Julian the Apostate, overseen by Flavius Aquilinus.

EDCS-20100214.translation: For the magnificence of the age of our lords Valentinian and Valens, perpetual Augusti, Publilius Ceionius Caecina Albinus, a most distinguished consular, dedicated four porticoes of the Capitol, worn away by age and collapsed to their very foundations, having been completed and adorned with new work, with Aelius Iulianus, twice curator of the state, Flavius Aquilinus, perpetual priest, and Antonius Ianuarianus, perpetual priest, and Notionus, curator of the state, overseeing.
EDCS-20100214.summary: This inscription records the dedication of four rebuilt porticoes of the Capitol in Rome, commissioned by the distinguished official Publilius Ceionius Caecina Albinus during the reign of Valentinian and Valens (364-375 CE).

EDCS-20100215.translation: In the time of the Emperor Constantine, the patrician Bellicius Gregorius, and John, Duke of Tigisi, offer a house. A native of Armenia.
EDCS-20100215.summary: This inscription records a house being offered by John, Duke of Tigisi, to the patrician Bellicius Gregorius during the reign of Emperor Constantine (306-337 CE).

EDCS-20100217.translation: To Vocontius Publius Flavius Pudentianus Pomponianus, a most distinguished man, a generous supporter of citizens and fatherland, skilled in military exercises, surpassing in eloquence, equalling Roman dignity, the council of the inhabitants of Fons patron, to our other abundant and flowing source.
EDCS-20100217.summary: This is a dedication to Vocontius Publius Flavius Pudentianus Pomponianus, praising his virtues and public service by the council of Fons, likely in the 3rd or 4th century CE.

EDCS-20100219.translation: To Antacius, a man of all virtues, Titus Julius Tertullus Antiochus. Lucius Valerius Optatianus, an equestrian, his client Panacrius, in gratitude for his signal kindness to him.
EDCS-20100219.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Titus Julius Tertullus Antiochus, praised for his virtues, by his client Lucius Valerius Optatianus, an equestrian.

EDCS-20100220.translation: To Sertius Marcus Plotius Faustus, Roman equestrian, prefect of the III Cohort of Ituraeans, tribune of the I Flavian Cohort of Canathenians, prefect of the I Flavian Wing of Gauls of Tauriana, perpetual priest, priest of the city. Plotius Thallus, his freedman, to his most benevolent patron.
EDCS-20100220.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Plotius Thallus to his patron, Sertius Marcus Plotius Faustus, who held various military and religious offices, likely in the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20100221.translation: To Sertius Marcus Plotius Faustus, Roman equestrian, prefect of the III Cohort of Ituraeans, tribune of the I Flavian Cohort of Canathenians, Pias Fidelis, prefect of the I Flavian Wing of Gauls of Tauriana, perpetual priest, priest of the city. Plotius Thallus and Plotia Faustiana, his daughter, his freedmen, to their most outstanding patron.
EDCS-20100221.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Plotius Thallus and his daughter Plotia Faustiana to their patron, Sertius Marcus Plotius Faustus, detailing his extensive military and religious career, likely in the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20100222.translation: To Sertia Cornelia Valentina Tucciana, perpetual priestess, wife of Marcus Plotius Faustus, of honorable memory in military service, a woman of virtue. Plotia Faustiana and her father Plotius Thallus, her freedmen, to their most benevolent patroness.
EDCS-20100222.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Plotia Faustiana and her father Plotius Thallus to Sertia Cornelia Valentina Tucciana, wife of Marcus Plotius Faustus and a woman of virtue, likely in the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20100223.translation: To Sertia Cornelia Valentina Tucciana, perpetual priestess. Marcus Plotius Faustus, perpetual priest, to his most missed wife.
EDCS-20100223.summary: This inscription is a memorial from Marcus Plotius Faustus to his deceased wife, Sertia Cornelia Valentina Tucciana, a perpetual priestess.

EDCS-20100224.translation: To Cornelia Valentina Tucciana, perpetual priestess, a woman of good memory. Faustus, her husband, placed this for the adornment of the market building which they made for their fatherland.
EDCS-20100224.summary: This inscription records Faustus, husband of Cornelia Valentina Tucciana, a perpetual priestess, setting up this monument for the adornment of a market building they constructed for their community.

EDCS-20100225.translation: Marcus Plotius Faustus, Roman equestrian, thrice honored in military service, perpetual priest, priest of the city, placed this for himself for the adornment of the market building which he made for his fatherland with Valentina, his wife.
EDCS-20100225.summary: This inscription records Marcus Plotius Faustus, a distinguished equestrian and priest, dedicating a market building to his fatherland, built with his wife Valentina.

EDCS-20100226.translation: To Quintus Hammonius Donatianus, quaestor, praefect, and one who administers justice, our patron, a place was granted by decree of the decurions, son of Quintus Hammonius Donatianus, quaestor, praefect, and one who administers justice, patron, to Potamios, son of Gaius Pontius Ulpius Verus Nianus Victor, a most brilliant boy, and son of Gaius Pontius Victor Verianus, a distinguished man, perpetual flamen, and beloved of citizens.
EDCS-20100226.summary: This inscription records a grant of a place by the decurions to Quintus Hammonius Donatianus, likely a local magistrate and patron, honoring his father and son.

EDCS-20100227.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Aelius Extricatus lies here, who after so much burden and many frequent labors, now lies silent and rests content in a silent seat. Gaius Aelius Tertiolus, beneficiarius of the consular governor of the VI Victrix Legion, made this for his most devoted father.
EDCS-20100227.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Aelius Extricatus, set up by his son, Gaius Aelius Tertiolus, a soldier serving under the provincial governor.

EDCS-20100228.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Senius Flaccus, having departed this life, lived 30 years in Rome. Lucius Senius Felix made this for his dearest brother, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-20100228.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Senius Flaccus, erected by his brother Lucius Senius Felix, and was set up by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-20100229.translation: The list of the council of the colony of Thamugadi, of most distinguished men: Vulcacius Rufinus, patron; Marius Decianus, patron; Insteius Lampadius, patron; Pompeius Deuterius, patron; Cornelius Valentinus, patron; Valerius Herennianus; Sessius Pulverius; Valerius Porphyrius; Cessius Trigetius; Cessius Andanius; Plotius Florentinus, a most accomplished man, perpetual flamen; Aelius Ampelius, a most accomplished man, priest; Iulius Paulus Trigetius, patron; Antonius Victor, perpetual flamen, curator; Octavius Sosinianus, perpetual flamen, duoviri; Sessius Cresconius, augur; Papirius Vitalis, perpetual flamen; Corfidius Valentinianus, perpetual flamen; Grasidius Victorinus, perpetual flamen; Antonius Vindicianus, perpetual flamen; Grasidius Saduntius, perpetual flamen; Claudius Licentius, perpetual flamen; Sentius Victor, perpetual flamen; Aufidius Optatus, perpetual flamen; Sessius Iulianus, perpetual flamen; Egnatius Florentius, perpetual flamen; Plotius Crescentilianus, perpetual flamen; Claudius Saturus, perpetual flamen, tax collector; Aurelius Maximus, perpetual flamen, tax collector; Cincius Porphyrius, perpetual flamen; Aelius Iulianus, perpetual flamen; Flavius Palminus, perpetual flamen; Flavius Vincentius, perpetual flamen; Sulpicius Ingennuus, perpetual flamen; Plotius Praetextatus, perpetual flamen; Agrius Praetextatus, perpetual flamen; Cincius Innocentius, perpetual flamen; Iulius Gubernius, perpetual flamen; Vallius Candidus, perpetual flamen; Flavius Aquilinus, perpetual flamen; Flavius Faustinianus, perpetual flamen; Virius Manilianus, perpetual flamen; Flavius Donatianus, perpetual flamen; Octavius Falacer, perpetual flamen; Antonius Petronianus, perpetual flamen; Annius Verissimus, perpetual flamen; Acilius Concessanus, perpetual flamen; Gargilius Calventianus, perpetual flamen; Sessius Ianuarianus, perpetual flamen; pontiffs: Plotius Romulus, Ulpius Purpurius, Horatius Maximus, Aelius Bibianus; augurs: Iulius Victorinianus, Flavius Pullentius, Plotius Paulinianus; aediles: Aurelius Rufinus, Iulius Valerinus; quaestors: Vetilius Saturninus; duoviral officials: Flavidius Sudianus, Vaterius Sapidus, Flavidius Procilianus, Pompeius Rufinianus, Acilius Valerianus, Iulius Faustus, Vaterius Donatus, Laetorius Laertius, Vallius Hospes, Ulpius Isthefanus, Faustinianus Citheri, Varius Victor, Aelius Victor; duoviral official: Sextilius Praetextatus; duoviral official: Plotius Valerianus; aediles not excused: Claudius Firminus the younger, Vetilius Crescens, Claudius Ticerius the elder, Sextus Simplicius, Annius Urc, Claudius Ticerius the younger, Claudius Firminus the elder, Donatus S., Victor S., Matius S., Silvanus S., Quintilianus; quaestors not excused: ?rius Liberalis, Camius C., ?us; those who have not performed magistracies, excused: IV. ?osanus Metianus, Ceri Fabricius, Lactantius, Publicius Victorinianus, Caecilius Silvius, Pompeius Severianus, Faustinus, Pompeius Flavianus the younger, Annius Cubernius, Vallius Hospes, Vallius Aemilianus, Vallius Protasius, Virius Adelfius, Sessius Ampelius, Sessius Petronianus, Aelius Donatianus, Annius Flavianus, Cincius Avicius, ?dius Secundianus, ?us, ?us, Dubitatus, ?us, Victorinianus, ?us, Leontius, Faustinianus, Alfius, ?us, Gubernius, ?us, Dulcitius, ?anus; those who have not performed magistracies, not excused: Censitus Minentius, ?us, Domitianus, Ianuarius, ?us, Flavianus the younger, ?ianus, ?us, ?tius Calvin, Iulius Agrobius, Papirius Alfius, Tintirius Fortunatianus, Tintirius Saturus, Helvius A., ?ius, Helvius ?, ?ulus, Granius Mucro, Marcius Munatianus, Caecilius Gangalius, Pompeius Rufinianus, Gregori, Octavius Cresconius, Flavius Primianus, Granius Optantius, Plotius Senecio, Innocentius, Abassi, Varius Ianuarius, Pullaenius Victor, Vetilius Gaianus, Iulius Victorinianus, Pomponius Eucromius, Antonius Salonius, Flavius Ianuarius, Gaiulus, Datulli Vitalis, Datulli, Iulius Leporius, Antonius Datianus, Sessius Pulverius, Faustinianus Palminus, Papirius Felix, Sertorius Cromatius, Iulius Eustratius, Iullus E., Sertorius R. A., In., In., An., Satu, Iulius Anto, Victo, Messi, Aurelius Caelius, Secundianus, Aemilius Chorentus, Caelius Matutinus, Brutius Viventius, Dubidius Victorinus, Sessius Cresconius, Ulpius Victor; clerks who serve in the office of the deputy governor: Virius Agrobius, Iulius Zuccarius, Octavius Gallus, Sallustius Victorinus, Aurelius Crescens, ?asegmei, Iulius Baric, Sempronius Donatus, Sempronius Germanus, Sempronius Germanus, Fabricius Apuleus; soldiers who serve in the office of the consular governor: Virius Artemius, Caecilius Calcedonius, Donatus son of Innocens, Abassi, Virius Artemiolus, Leontius son of Cerealis, Cerealis son of Cerealis, Sessius Innocentius, Letorius Potentius, Flavidius Sudianus, Pomponius Cresconius, Pomponius Maurentius, Pompeius Simplicius, Pomponius Uranius, Flavidius Iulianus, Acilius Acilianus, Sertorius Chromatius, Dubidius Iulianus, Iulius Protasius the younger, Granius Caesonianus, Claudius Discolus, Domitius Laurentius, Pompeius Eucarpius, Avinius Bassus, Pompeius Cresconius, Pompeius Adelfius, Eucarpi, Lucceius Luccei, Dubizius, Acilius Speratianus, Plotius Macrinus, Virius Curtiolus, Antonius Botellicus, Dubizius Celerinus who is also Quintianus, Corfizius Crementius, Antonius Florus, Caecilius Restutus, Dubizius Crescentianus, Granius Amantius; in the office of the prefect of the grain supply: Avianius Donatus, Avianius Cresconius, Avianius Concilianus, Sempronius son of Secundus, Caecilius Fabbianus, Pompeius Pascentius, Possidi, Sessius Cresconius, Clodius Atianus, Claudius Felix, Sallustius Germanus, Iulius Zuccarius, Avianius Faustinianus, Annius Cresconius, Flavius Crescentianus, Pompeius Adelfius, Possidius, Pompeius Simplicius, Gregorius, Nasidius, Nasidius, Sempronius Melior, Sempronius Optatianus, Clodius Restitutus, Papirius Aurelianus, Flavius Repentinus; those who serve in the office of the rationalis: Rogatianus son of Paulinus Arissani, Palminus son of Palmini Arissani, Annius Gallus son of Vincentius, Iulius Placuntius, Felix son of Palmini Arissani.
EDCS-20100229.summary: This is a comprehensive list of officials, patrons, soldiers, clerks, and citizens of the colony of Thamugadi, detailing their various roles and statuses, likely for administrative or public record purposes.

EDCS-20100230.translation: Her freedmen erected this for Marcia Caellia Proocilla, daughter of Marcus Caelius Saturninus, perpetual flamen, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-20100230.summary: This inscription records that the freedmen of Marcia Caellia Proocilla erected a monument for her, by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-20100231.translation: The curiales of the Commodan district erected and dedicated this at their own expense to their patron Iulius Papirius Comicianus Martialianus Eucarpus.
EDCS-20100231.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the curiales (local councillors) of the Commodan district to their patron, Iulius Papirius Comicianus Martialianus Eucarpus.

EDCS-20100233.translation: To Quintus Sulpicius Licinius, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, Roman knight, perpetual flamen in the most distinguished cities of both the colony of Thamugadenses and the municipium of Lambaesitani, and in the colony of Thamugadenses also having held all offices, our patron.
EDCS-20100233.summary: This is a dedication honoring Quintus Sulpicius Licinius, a Roman knight and perpetual flamen, who had also held all the highest offices in the colonies of Thamugad and Lambaesis.

EDCS-20100234.translation: To Quintianus, Roman knight, perpetual flamen, a friend. Gaius Papirius Fortunatus granted this place to his most simple friend by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-20100234.summary: This inscription records the grant of a place by Gaius Papirius Fortunatus to his friend Quintianus, a Roman knight and perpetual flamen, by decree of the decurions.

EDCS-20100235.translation: The most distinguished council of the colony of Thamugad gave and ... [dedicates] to Marcus Virius Flavius Iugurtha, son of Marcus, of the Papiria tribe, Roman knight, perpetual flamen, decurion of the most distinguished colony of Carthaginienses, curator of public affairs, as eloquent as he was good.
EDCS-20100235.summary: This inscription records the bestowal of honors by the council of the colony of Thamugad upon Marcus Virius Flavius Iugurtha, a distinguished Roman knight, perpetual flamen, and public official.

EDCS-20100241.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Augustalis lived 20 years. Natalis made this for his fellow comrade.
EDCS-20100241.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Augustalis, who lived 20 years, set up by his friend Natalis.

EDCS-20100242.translation: To the divine spirits. Lucius Caecilius Dexter lived 10 years, is buried here.
EDCS-20100242.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the short life and burial of Lucius Caecilius Dexter.

EDCS-20100243.translation: To the divine spirits. Ottacilia Geminia made this for her most cherished mother, Gaeliae Proculinae, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-20100243.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Ottacilia Geminia for her mother, Gaeliae Proculinae.

EDCS-20100244.translation: To the divine spirits. For Cottia, daughter of Marci, a most rare wife.
EDCS-20100244.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Cottia, wife of Marci, highlighting her rarity.

EDCS-20100245.translation: Crescens made this for [her] most dutiful and deserving daughter, she is buried here.
EDCS-20100245.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Crescens for her deserving daughter.

EDCS-20100246.translation: To the divine spirits. Lucius Curtilius Ianuarius, dutiful, lived 70 years, is buried here.
EDCS-20100246.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life and burial of Lucius Curtilius Ianuarius.

EDCS-20100247.translation: To the divine spirits. Decimus Equitius Orestinus, A.
EDCS-20100247.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription dedicated to Decimus Equitius Orestinus.

EDCS-20100248.translation: To the divine spirits. Eufra, daughter of Primitivus, a dutiful freedwoman, lived [age missing] years, is buried here.
EDCS-20100248.summary: A funerary epitaph for Eufra, a dutiful freedwoman, with a missing age and burial information.

EDCS-20100250.translation: To the divine spirits. Titus Flavius Vitalis lived 18 years, is buried here.
EDCS-20100250.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the young age and burial of Titus Flavius Vitalis.

EDCS-20100251.translation: To the divine spirits. Victor placed this for his dutiful comrade, Marcus Gargilius Ingenuus, who lived 40 years.
EDCS-20100251.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Victor for his deceased friend Marcus Gargilius Ingenuus.

EDCS-20300004.translation: To the divine spirits. Iulius Victor lived 75 years.
EDCS-20300004.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the life of Iulius Victor, with an incomplete burial statement.

EDCS-20300005.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Valeriosa lived 60 years. Her daughter Venusta made this for her dearest mother.
EDCS-20300005.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Iulia Valeriosa and made by her daughter.

EDCS-20300006.translation: To the deities [???] Iunianus Martialianus Leontius, to his most worthy master.
EDCS-20300006.summary: A dedication, possibly funerary, to a master, likely from freedmen.

EDCS-20300007.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Laelia Tertia lived 50 years. She rests here.
EDCS-20300007.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Laelia Tertia.

EDCS-20300008.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Peneleius Maximus made this; he rests here with his wife.
EDCS-20300008.summary: A funerary epitaph recording Peneleius Maximus making a tomb for himself and his wife.

EDCS-20300009.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Pompeia Demetria, my most rare wife, who lived 40 years. Vettius Africanus, her husband.
EDCS-20300009.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Vettius Africanus to his deceased wife, Pompeia Demetria.

EDCS-20300010.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Sallustius Victor, my son, who lived 21 years.
EDCS-20300010.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated to Lucius Sallustius Victor by a parent.

EDCS-20300011.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Servilius Proculus lived 65 years. Iulia Victoria, his wife, made this for her dearest husband. He rests here.
EDCS-20300011.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Quintus Servilius Proculus, made by his wife Iulia Victoria.

EDCS-20300012.translation: To Marcus Valerius, son of Marcus.
EDCS-20300012.summary: A dedication or epitaph for Marcus Valerius, son of Marcus.

EDCS-20300013.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Pupa lived 4 years, 56 days, 1 hour. Her dearest daughter made this for her.
EDCS-20300013.summary: A funerary epitaph for a child named Pupa, made by her daughter (likely a typo and meant to be mother).

EDCS-20300017.translation: Rogatus, freedman of Titus Flavius Respectus, freedman of the Emperor, and his mother Secuntilla, freedwoman of the Emperor, made this.
EDCS-20300017.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Rogatus and his mother Secuntilla, both freedpersons of imperial freedmen.

EDCS-20300019.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Iunius Hilarus made this for his wife, Ulpia Castula, with whom he lived happily for 42 years.
EDCS-20300019.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his wife, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20300020.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sulpicia Rogata lived 105 years.
EDCS-20300020.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name and extreme age of Sulpicia Rogata, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20300021.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Marius Fortunatus.
EDCS-20300021.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name of Gaius Marius Fortunatus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20300022.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Apronius Ianuarius, veteran of the Praetorian Guard, set this up for himself while alive and for his most rare wife, Naevia Ianuaria, having lived [some number of] years.
EDCS-20300022.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a Praetorian Guard veteran for himself and his wife, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20300023.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Primula Anderica lived 35 years. Primulus Marcellinus made this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-20300023.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a husband for his wife, recording her name, age, and his devotion, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20300027.translation: Curtianus Erotus lived 88 years. Curtianus [set this up for his?] most devoted.
EDCS-20300027.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name and age of Curtianus Erotus and a partial dedication, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20300028.translation: To our lord the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, imperator for the fifth time. Gaius Servilius Macedo, decurion of the municipality of Gemellenses, made and dedicated this statue in honor of the perpetual priesthood conferred upon him by the people.
EDCS-20300028.summary: An inscription dedicating a statue to Emperor Septimius Severus by a local magistrate, likely dating to Severus's reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-20300029.translation: His sons and heirs made this mausoleum for 12,000 sesterces for Pinarius Processianus, father, who held the offices of aedile, duovir, and augur, decurion of the municipality of Badiensis, who lived 40 years, and for Aelia Marcellina, mother, who lived the same number of years, and especially for two outstanding Pinarii, Titianus, the brother, and Apronianus, the son of the brother, noted for their virtue and chastity.
EDCS-20300029.summary: A funerary monument inscription detailing the cost and the family members honored, set up by sons for their father and mother, and specific brothers, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20300030.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulius Victor Amizza lived piously for 67 years. His heirs made and dedicated this monument for their pious brother.
EDCS-20300030.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by heirs for their pious brother, recording his name and age, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20300032.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Millus Verecundus lived 55 years.
EDCS-20300032.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the name and age of Millus Verecundus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20300033.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Panala Victoris lived 25 years. Her brother made and dedicated this.
EDCS-20300033.summary: A funerary inscription for Panala Victoris, set up by her brother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20300034.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Sillia Monna lived 75 years. Her sons made this for their worthy and deserving mother.
EDCS-20300034.summary: A funerary inscription for Sillia Monna, set up by her sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20300035.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Asculus Amizza lived [---] years.
EDCS-20300035.summary: A funerary inscription for Asculus Amizza, with the age at death missing, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20300036.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Clodia Aegyptia lived 28 years. Her husband made and dedicated this for his worthy wife.
EDCS-20300036.summary: A funerary inscription for Clodia Aegyptia, set up by her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20300037.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iulia Epre lived 20 years. Iulius Felix [made this] for his wife.
EDCS-20300037.summary: A funerary inscription for Iulia Epre, set up by her husband Iulius Felix, dating to the Roman Imperial period.
