EDCS-20300038.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Simplex Victoris son lived 17 years.
EDCS-20300038.summary: A funerary inscription for Simplex Victoris, likely set up by his parents or family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20300049.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Iulia Laevera lived 15 years. Caius Iulius Quintanus [made this].
EDCS-20300049.summary: A funerary inscription for Iulia Laevera, set up by Caius Iulius Quintanus, likely a relative, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20300050.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Iulia Ianuaria lived 15 years. Caius Iulius Cren Tam [made this].
EDCS-20300050.summary: A funerary inscription for Iulia Ianuaria, set up by Caius Iulius Cren Tam, likely a relative, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20300051.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Publius Ovinius Viator lived 53 years, 3 months, 10 days. Publius Ovinius Exsus made this for his pious father.
EDCS-20300051.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Ovinius Viator, set up by his son Publius Ovinius Exsus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20300052.translation: Secundus [made this] for Iunia Victorina, his sister. She lived 35 years.
EDCS-20300052.summary: A funerary inscription for Iunia Victorina, set up by her brother Secundus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20300053.translation: To Iulius Victor, my most deserving husband, Iulia [erected this].
EDCS-20300053.summary: A funerary dedication by Iulia to her husband, Iulius Victor.

EDCS-20300054.translation: Publius I... of Macedon lived 30 years; the cupola was made by his brothers.
EDCS-20300054.summary: A funerary inscription recording the age of Publius I... and mentioning the construction of a cupola by his brothers.

EDCS-20300055.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Cartilius Optatus [erected this] to Cartilius Secundus, his dutiful son, who lived 30 years, deserving [of this monument]. [---] from Aurasi.
EDCS-20300055.summary: A funerary dedication by Publius Cartilius Optatus to his son Cartilius Secundus, who died at age 30.

EDCS-20300056.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Cartilius Optatus made this for his deserving wife, Iulia Matronica, who lived 26 years, with his own money.
EDCS-20300056.summary: A funerary dedication by Publius Cartilius Optatus to his wife, Iulia Matronica, who died at age 26.

EDCS-20300062.translation: To the Unconquered God. Marcus Messius Messor, prefect of the cohort, has re-established [this] for his own health and that of his family.
EDCS-20300062.summary: A dedication to the god Sol Invictus by Marcus Messius Messor, a cohort prefect, for his and his family's well-being.

EDCS-20300063.summary: A fragmentary inscription identifying the dedicator as a prefect of a cohort.

EDCS-20300064.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Homulia Ravilina lived 25 years. For the sake of her name/memory.
EDCS-20300064.summary: A funerary dedication for Homulia Ravilina, who died at age 25.

EDCS-20300065.translation: To Mercurius Augustus, sacred. For the health of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius, Marcus Annius Valens, centurion of the III Augusta legion, in command of the Palmyrene unit, has willingly fulfilled his vow, with a glad heart, for his own health and that of his family.
EDCS-20300065.summary: A military dedication by Marcus Annius Valens, a centurion and commander, to Mercurius Augustus for the health of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and his own family.

EDCS-20300066.translation: To the spirits of the departed. I made this for Cluvius Optatus, my dutiful father, who lived 60 years. Buried here.
EDCS-20300066.summary: A funerary dedication by an unnamed person to their father, Cluvius Optatus, who died at age 60.

EDCS-20300068.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Publicius Rogatianus made this for Flavia Processa, his most exceptional virgin wife, deserving [of this monument], who lived 47 years, and also for himself and his family.
EDCS-20300068.summary: A funerary dedication by Quintus Publicius Rogatianus to his wife Flavia Processa, who died at age 47, and for himself and his family.

EDCS-20300070.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. He/She angered four grandmothers.
EDCS-20300070.summary: This is a funerary dedication of uncertain date, recording a person who displeased four of their grandmothers.

EDCS-20300072.translation: Trebius Rogatus lived 90 years. Ncia, his wife, aged 70.
EDCS-20300072.summary: A funerary epitaph from an uncertain date recording the lifespans of a husband and wife.

EDCS-20300076.translation: Sacred to Malagbelus Augustus Sanctus. Titus Flavius Mansuetus, centurion of the III Augusta legion, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20300076.summary: A votive inscription by Titus Flavius Mansuetus, a centurion of the III Augusta legion, dating possibly to the 2nd or 3rd century CE, dedicating something to the god Malagbelus Augustus Sanctus.

EDCS-20300077.translation: Sacred to Mercury, Hercules, and Mars. Titus Iulius Rufus, centurion of the III Augusta legion, made this happily.
EDCS-20300077.summary: A votive inscription by Titus Iulius Rufus, a centurion of the III Augusta legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, dedicating this to Mercury, Hercules, and Mars.

EDCS-20300082.translation: Marcus Cornelius Faustus, centurion of the III Augusta legion.
EDCS-20300082.summary: An inscription, likely funerary or dedicatory, identifying Marcus Cornelius Faustus as a centurion of the III Augusta legion, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20300084.translation: Maximus, a veteran of Hadrino the Palmyrene, formerly of the Palmyrene cohort, lived 75 years. Valeria, his most beloved wife and heir, in addition to what was provided in the will, made this from her own generosity for her dearest husband.
EDCS-20300084.summary: A funerary monument from the 2nd or 3rd century CE, erected by Valeria for her husband Maximus, a Palmyrene veteran, who lived to be 75.

EDCS-20300085.translation: To eternal sleep, Aufidia Achi, wife of Aris.
EDCS-20300085.summary: A funerary inscription from an uncertain date, dedicated to Aufidia Achi, identified as the wife of Aris, for eternal sleep.

EDCS-20300086.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Attia lived 7 years.
EDCS-20300086.summary: A funerary epitaph from an uncertain date for Flavia Attia, who died at the age of seven.

EDCS-20300087.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Inventa lived 70 years.
EDCS-20300087.summary: A funerary epitaph from an uncertain date for Iulia Inventa, who died at the age of seventy.

EDCS-20300088.summary: A funerary epitaph from an uncertain date for Iulia Palmyra, who died at the age of thirty-five.

EDCS-20300089.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Herennia Hariana, his wife, who lived 65 years. Hobolus made this for her, as she deserved.
EDCS-20300089.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Herennia Hariana and was erected by her husband, Hobolus.

EDCS-20300090.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Herennia Rufilla, his mother, who lived 60 years. Harianus Themarsa, his elder son, made this for her, as she deserved.
EDCS-20300090.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Herennia Rufilla and was erected by her elder son, Harianus Themarsa.

EDCS-20300091.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Themarsa Hariani, his father, who lived 80 years. Harianus Themarsa, his son, made this for him, as he deserved.
EDCS-20300091.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Themarsa Hariani and was erected by his son, Harianus Themarsa.

EDCS-20300094.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Suricus, a Palmyrene, archer in the century of Maximus, who lived 45 years and served 14 years.
EDCS-20300094.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Suricus, an archer, and mentions his military service and age at death.

EDCS-20300095.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Publius Cornelius Castus, his most excellent husband.
EDCS-20300095.summary: This funerary epitaph, dedicated to Publius Cornelius Castus, was likely erected by his wife.

EDCS-20300100.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For T. Saturnina, who lived 30 years and 10 months. Her husband made this for her, as she deserved.
EDCS-20300100.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of T. Saturnina and was erected by her husband.

EDCS-20300102.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Caius Primus lived 90 years and 4 months. His heirs made this for their father, as he deserved.
EDCS-20300102.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Caius Primus and was erected by his heirs.

EDCS-20300105.translation: Most Christian Emperor Maurice [built] the gates.
EDCS-20300105.summary: This inscription likely records the construction of gates by the Emperor Maurice, probably in the late 6th or early 7th century.

EDCS-20300106.translation: Sextus Lartidius Verus, [son] of Sextus, [and] Cornelia [daughter] of Mustius, [died aged] 19 years. Sextus Lartidius Firmanus, father, made this for his most dutiful son.
EDCS-20300106.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Sextus Lartidius Verus at the age of 19 and was erected by his father.

EDCS-20300107.translation: To Fortuna Redux, of the Augusti, in fulfillment of a vow for the restoration of the 3rd Augustan Legion Valeriana Galliena Valeriana. The state of the municipium of Gemellenses dedicated this, under the presidency of Veturius Veturianus, most distinguished man, legate of the Augusti pro praetore.
EDCS-20300107.summary: This dedication by the community of Gemellenses to Fortuna Redux and the 3rd Augustan Legion was made under the legateship of Veturius Veturianus, likely during the reign of Valerian and Gallienus.

EDCS-20300112.translation: Sacred to the genius of the century. Caius Servilius Rogatus, optio, having been dismissed, gladly paid his vow.
EDCS-20300112.summary: A freedman and former optio, Caius Servilius Rogatus, dedicates an offering to the genius of his century.

EDCS-20300126.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, conqueror of Armenia, of Media, of Parthia, of Germania.
EDCS-20300126.summary: An honorific inscription dedicated to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, listing his numerous titles and victories.

EDCS-20300135.translation: For the health of the Augusti, the optio's school, along with statues and images of the divine house, and their Conservator deities, made from generous contributions and gifts which they confer upon them, under the administration of Lucius Egnatius Myro, quaestor. For this ceremony, they decreed that a departing colleague should receive 8,000 sesterces to confirm his hopes, and veterans should also receive 6,000 sesterces each on the Kalends of January. The quaestor ensured that these sums were paid without delay. [List of names follows].
EDCS-20300135.summary: A lengthy inscription detailing the funding and construction of a school with statues for the optio's collegium, including provisions for departing members and veterans, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20600001.translation: The emperor's record office, with images of the divine house, was restored by Ulpius Antoninus, princeps, and the optio's of the first cohort and their assistants, from their own funds. Marcus Aurelius Aurelianus, primipilaris, [and] the optio's of the first cohort, Quintus Sempronius Felix, primipilaris, Publius Aelius Macrinus, princeps, Lucius Valerius Ianuarius, posterior hastatus, Caius Iulius Longinianus, posterior princeps, Caius Antonius Silvanus, posterior hastatus, Marcus Aurelius Terentius, prior princeps, Caius Manilius Donatus, prior hastatus, Quintus Aebutius Saturninus, posterior princeps, Marcus Aurelius Licinius, posterior hastatus, Caius Iulius Saturninus, Caius Iulius Numidius, scribes of the princeps, made from their own funds.
EDCS-20600001.summary: This inscription records the restoration of an imperial record office with religious imagery, undertaken by various military officials including optio's and princeps, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20600002.translation: Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, Maximus, and [---] Valer[---] who are inscribed below, each to receive 2,000 sesterces when dismissed from the same college. [List of names follows].
EDCS-20600002.summary: An inscription that records a payment of 2,000 sesterces to be given to members of a college upon their dismissal, with a list of names, likely from the Severan period.

EDCS-20600004.translation: To the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, and Geta 'Pius Felix Britannicus Germanicus Maximus Augustus', and Julia Augusta, mother of the camps, dedicated by Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti, pro praetore, consul-designate. The college of immunis soldiers and beneficiarii, whose names are written below. [List of names follows].
EDCS-20600004.summary: A dedication to the Severan emperors and Julia Domna, mother of the camps, by Quintus Anicius Faustus, a legate, likely marking the establishment or dedication of a collegium of soldiers.

EDCS-20600005.translation: To Julia Augusta, mother of the camps.
EDCS-20600005.summary: A short dedicatory inscription to Julia Domna, mother of the camps.

EDCS-20600006.translation: Scribes: Cerellius Don, Volussenius Iu estius Ian, Acilius cinius Gabinius, Sextus Pompeius S Sextus Caecilius V, Caius Marius Ian, Caius Iulius Marcus Avitius, Quintus Flaminius Claudius, Titus Flavius Lucius Cornelius, Quintus Vibius Sextus Cassius A, Marcus Caelius Ad, Marcus Sentius Su, Quintus Iulius Rog, Lucius Iulius Manlianus, Caius Iulius Pudentianus.
EDCS-20600006.summary: A list of names identified as scribes, likely part of a military or administrative unit.

EDCS-20600007.translation: The entire rank of signiferi: O Lollius Rogatianus, Geminius Felix, Annius Pacatianus, Apuleius Crescens, Magnius Celestinus, Mussius Felix, Aurelius Probianus, Aurelius Tiberinus, Sossibius, Pullaenius Iulius Iulianus, Iulius Emeritus, Iulius Donatianus, Iulius Faustus, Aurelius Victor, Iulius Castus, Alveius Fortunatus, Aurelius Eutichianus, Gabinius Caecilianus, Iulius Rogatianus rius ius Honoratus ius tino TIIIVS vinius Victor ius.
EDCS-20600007.summary: A list of individuals identified as signiferi (standard-bearers), possibly from a military cohort.

EDCS-20600008.translation: Pompeius Rufus, Fonteius Fortunatus, ius Saturninus, vexillarius. Pinarius Primus, vexillarius. Flavius Paulinus, tesserarius. Aulus Geminius Extricatus, magister kampi. Marcus Terentius Saturninus, hastatus. Quintus Iulius Barbarus, Caius Iulius Septiminus, Marcus Iulius Maximus, Marcus Iulius Processus, Caius Valerius Silvanus, curator scholae. Seius Primicianus, Caius Lollius Martialis, Caius Valerius Vitalis, Caius Cestius Felix, Caius Iulius Optatus, Marcus Fundanius Rogatus, Pontius Minervalis, Servius Victor, Marcus Stertinius Trebonianus, Lucius Claudius Antonianus, Caius Antonius Bassus, Aelius Rufus, Lucius Atilius Emeritus, Iulius Victor, Lucius Cornelius Vitalis, Caius Valerius Saturninus, Caius Iulius Urbanus, Lucius Fulvius Saturninus, Marcus Servilius Privatus, Bugius Saturninus, Caius Annius Saturninus, Marcus Egnatius Secundus, Lucius Memmius Maximus, Marcus Octvaius Germanus, Titus Iulius Rogatianus, Numisius Optatus, Caius Iulius Fuscinus, Iulius Seranus, Iulius Priscus, Caius Helvius Rogatus, Caius Iulius Optatus, Publius Aelius Victorinus, Caecilius Festus, Iulius Primus, Caius Caecilius Rogatus, Caius Annius Festus, Quintus Tuccius Felix, Quintus Caecilius Quintianus, Fufius Florus, Publius Aelius Ptolomeus, Quintus Aurelius Saturninus.
EDCS-20600008.summary: A comprehensive list of military personnel, including vexillarii, a tesserarius, a magister kampi, hastati, a curator scholae, and numerous others, likely associated with a specific military unit or collegium.

EDCS-20600009.translation: Lucius Caecilius Urbanus, optio of the infirmary, curator of the armory works, dedicated this to the divine house of the Emperors.
EDCS-20600009.summary: This is an inscription by a military officer dedicating part of an armory to the imperial family.

EDCS-20600011.translation: Lucius Publicius Felix, Caius Raecius Rogatianus, Caius Fufius Egnatianus, Publius Omidius Reginalis, Tu Publius Aelius Emeritus from the camp, Caius Aufidius Ianuarius from Carthage, duplicarius, Quintus Laelius Victor, C. Caius Cornelius Felix, TI. Lucius Evatius Datus from the camp, Caius Gargilius Felix from the camp, Caius Blosius Ianuarius, Titus Aebutius Rusticus from Utica, Lucius Servilius Gemellus from Ammaedara, Lucius Propertius Victor, C. Lucius Licinius Honoratus, C. Marcus Aurelius Myro from Ammaedara, Marcus Aurelius Sarapion, son of Gargilius Quinti, from the Arnensis tribe, Fatalis from Sicca, Cornelius Caius, son of Pollia, Antulus from the camp,lius Victor, optio, from Hadrumetum, Caecilius Donatus, L.lius Martialis from Carthage, Antistius Victor from the camp, equestrian, s. Magnius from Hadrumetum, custos armorum, alvidius Celer from the camp, Cornelius Saturninus, custos armorum, lius Primus, custos armorum from Carthage, Calidius Commodus from Pollia, from the camp, ius Martialis from Papiria, Thelepte, strator, Egrilius Fuscus from Carthage.
EDCS-20600011.summary: This inscription lists a large number of individuals, many identified by their origin and role, likely associated with a military unit or administrative body.

EDCS-20600012.translation: Domitius Valens from the camp, Caius Iulius Dexter from Cirta, Lucius Licinius Ianuarius from Ammaedara, Caius Homullius Sedatus from Thamugadi, Caius Iulius Hadrias from Theveste, Lucius Publicius Rufus from the camp, Caius Caecilius Donatus from the camp, Marcus Aurelus Quintus, Clodius Marcus Ulpius Sa, Marcus Celleius, C. Caius Cornelius, Marcus Numerius, Titus Aelius So.
EDCS-20600012.summary: This inscription lists numerous names, many associated with specific locations or military roles, possibly related to a military unit.

EDCS-20600013.translation: Felix from Carthage, Pompeius Natalis from Sicca, Asinius Iulianus from Thamugadi, Pomponius Calamus from Sicca, Sextus Pullaenius Prior from Sicca, Iulius Canetus from Carthage, Lucius Aemilius Rufinus from the camp, Caius Plotius Maximus from the camp, Lucius Sittius Felicianus from Milevo, Titus Flavius Saturninus from Thamugadi, Caius Licinius Primus from the camp, Marcus Sempronius Crescens from Carthage, Marcus Annius Victor from the camp, Pompeius Felix from the camp, Caius Nonius Bellicus from the camp, Marcus Memmius Crescens from the camp, Caius Sulpicius Clementinus from Sicca, Caius Marius Decianus, beneficiarius consularis, Caius Cassius Marcianus from Oea, Helvius Calvus from the camp, haruspex, Lucius Aelius Rufus from the camp, Caius Antistius Saturninus from Thamugadi, Caius Iulius Victor from Utica, Lucius Aelius Charitonianus from the camp, Titus Aelius Vitalis from the camp, cohors II, Caecilius Quietus from Thamugadi, Petronius Motthun from Sufetula, Rutilius Rutillianus from Simitthu, Caius Helvidius Datus from Thamugadi, Caius Aelius Vitalis from the camp, strator, Caius Octavius Rusticus from the camp, Quintus Cornelius Vitalis from the camp, equestrian, Caius Iulius Saturninus from Cirta, custos armorum, Aulus Geminius Bellicus from Sicca, Quintus Crepereius Quadratus from Theveste, Caius Iulius Quintianus from Maxula, Caius Aelius Crispus from the camp, Setius Secundianus from Carthage, Quintus Munius Victor from Thuburbo, Caius Iulius Vetustianus from Milevo, Caius Antonius Victor from Theveste, Quintus Nonius Felix from the camp, Caius Clodius Felix from the camp, Caius Hortensius Felix from Simitthu, Caius Geminius Crescens from Cirta, Quintus Modius Urbanus from Cirta, beneficiarius tribuni, cohors III, Caius Memmius Verus from the camp, tesserarius, Caius Gargilius Festus from Simitthu, Caius Homullius Fortunatus from Carthage, Caius Aponius Vitalis from Tipasa, exactus, Caius Iulius Pequarius from the camp, Tiberius Claudius Secundus from Thysdro, custos armorum, Caius Iulius Bassus from the camp, Marcus Amilius Crescens from Cuiculo, Titus SPE NT Mercurialis, Caius Annius Felix from Simitthu, Caius Iulius Ianuarius, Marcus Antonius Saturninus, Marcus Antonius Ap, Caius Annius Maximus, Caius Caecilius Saturninus, Caius Iulius Ianuarius, cohors IIII, Caius Pomponius Pri, Caius Iunius Emeritus, Lucius Fundanius L, Caius Helvacius Ex, Caius Mundicius, Caius Annius Saturninus, Lucius Caesius Ianuarius, Lucius Coridius C, Titus Flavius Marcus, Marius Quintus Papirius Victor, Caius Iulius Secundus, Caius Iulius Sci, Caius Antonius Auc, Marcus Staberius sg sn sc ai h tre s, Tert onius, Caius Autronius, Caius Antonius Sa, Lucius Gargilius Donatus, Caius Aponius F, Maeviu Caius, Caius Caius Iulius Quintus, Fabius Caius Iulius, Caius Flavius, Lucius Cannius, Lucius Egnatius, Marcus Cornelius, Caius Cornelius Rogatus, Titus Sextilius Pudens, Quintus Caecilius Felix from Cirta, cohors VI, Caius Iulius Messor from the camp, Lucius Lucceius Crispus from the camp, Lucius MO EIVS from the camp.
EDCS-20600013.summary: This extensive inscription lists a vast number of individuals, many with their place of origin and military roles, likely representing members of several cohorts or a large military contingent.

EDCS-20600016.translation: Titus Rausius Ro, Caius Iulius Felix, Publius Tannonius Saturninus, Lucius Domitius Eufratianus, Lucius Valerius Rogatianus, Mucius Laetus, Iulius Rufinus, Aelius Felix, Marcus Seius Torquatus, Geminius Geminianus, Lucius Geminius Honoratus, Lucius Iulius Titianus.
EDCS-20600016.summary: This inscription consists of a list of names, possibly commemorating individuals associated with a particular unit or event.

EDCS-20600019.translation: The aqueduct of Legio III Augusta, having been dilapidated through the neglect of many, and neglected through a long series of years, was restored to its entirety for the better by the most invincible restorers and preservers of their world, Diocletian and Maximian Augusti, under the supervision of Aurelius Maximianus, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, and Clodius Honoratus, an excellent man, prefect of the same legion.
EDCS-20600019.summary: This inscription records the restoration of an aqueduct by the emperors Diocletian and Maximian, supervised by the governor of Numidia and the prefect of the legion.

EDCS-20600020.translation: To our most pious and bravest prince, Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian, Marcus Aurelius Diogenes, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-20600020.summary: This is a dedication by Aurelius Diogenes, governor of Numidia, to the emperor Maximian.

EDCS-20600021.translation: To the most pious Emperor Maximian, the unconquered Augustus, and the bravest prince beyond all past rulers, Aurelius Diogenes, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, devoted to his divinity.
EDCS-20600021.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Aurelius Diogenes, governor of Numidia, to the emperor Maximian.

EDCS-20600022.translation: To the most pious Emperor Diocletian, the unconquered Augustus, and the bravest prince beyond all past rulers, Aurelius Diogenes, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Numidia, devoted to his divinity.
EDCS-20600022.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Aurelius Diogenes, governor of Numidia, to the emperor Diocletian.

EDCS-20600035.translation: May you be good, may you be better when you leave.
EDCS-20600035.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a personal motto or wish.

EDCS-20600038.translation: To the Holy Aesculapius, Marcus Porcius Iustus, prefect of the camp of the III Augusta Legion, gave this gift. Dedicated on the Ides of November, during the consulship of Emperor Commodus III and Burrus.
EDCS-20600038.summary: A military official dedicates a gift to Aesculapius in the presence of Emperor Commodus, likely during the late 2nd century AD.

EDCS-20600041.translation: Sacred to Aesculapius and Hygieia, good gods, from Pelusium.
EDCS-20600041.summary: A dedication to the gods Aesculapius and Hygieia from the city of Pelusium, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20600044.translation: Aelius Severus, a cavalryman of the III Augusta Legion, from the century of Iulius Candidus, having been discharged, restored the altar he had vowed to Fortuna Augusta, with a willing heart, and dedicated it.
EDCS-20600044.summary: A discharged cavalryman of the III Augusta Legion dedicates a restored altar to Fortuna Augusta, likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20600047.translation: Publius Maccaeus Silvanus, a veteran, made this for Julia Felix Curia, using his own money, because of the honor of the flamenship conferred upon him.
EDCS-20600047.summary: A veteran dedicates a building or space to Julia Felix Curia in recognition of a priesthood, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20600048.translation: Sextus Pomponius Ceacilianus, a veteran and former groom, restored this altar to the genius of his home.
EDCS-20600048.summary: A veteran and former groom restores an altar dedicated to the genius of his household, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20600049.translation: To the Genius of Lambaesis, Lucius Aemilius Marcellinus, primus pilus, [made this] with his own money, with his son Apollonius overseeing.
EDCS-20600049.summary: A senior centurion dedicates something to the Genius of Lambaesis, funded by himself and overseen by his son, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20600052.translation: Lucius Iulius Crescentianus, quaestor, donated small altars with statuettes to the college for the genius of the school.
EDCS-20600052.summary: A quaestor donates religious items for the genius of a collegium or school, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20600053.translation: Lucius Iulius Crescentianus, quaestor, donated small altars with statuettes to the college for the genius of the school.
EDCS-20600053.summary: A quaestor donates religious items for the genius of a collegium or school, likely Roman Imperial period. This is a duplicate of EDCS-20600052.

EDCS-20600054.translation: Publius Aurelius Felix, a speculator from the III Augusta Legion, originally from Thamugadi, gave this gift to the genius of his own neighborhood.
EDCS-20600054.summary: A soldier from the III Augusta Legion, originating from Thamugadi, dedicates a gift to the genius of his local community, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20600055.translation: Caius Valerius Crispus made this, with a willing heart, for the genius of the neighborhood, [including] his sons, daughters-in-law, and granddaughters.
EDCS-20600055.summary: Caius Valerius Crispus makes a dedication to the genius of his neighborhood, including his family members, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-20600057.translation: To the genius of the avena [a type of grain or fodder]
EDCS-20600057.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the genius of the avena, likely a farmer's offering.

EDCS-20600058.translation: To the genius of Caius Iulius F autor
EDCS-20600058.summary: This inscription records a dedication to a genius, likely personal, by Caius Iulius F autor.

EDCS-20600059.translation: To Janus Pater, Lucius Bennius Primus, the priest, with his son Bennius Fortunatus, gladly paid his vow with a willing heart.
EDCS-20600059.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to Janus Pater by a priest and his son, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-20600065.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Veturius Veturinus, a most distinguished man.
EDCS-20600065.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Veturius Veturinus, a prominent individual, likely from the senatorial or equestrian class.

EDCS-20600067.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Caius L. Servilius Rogatus, centurion of the III Augusta legion, gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-20600067.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a centurion of the III Augusta legion, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-20600069.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, while our most pious Emperors, brothers, and the entire divine house, consuls, Marcus Acutius Ingenus from the honorable cohort, released from service, veteran, Caius Iulius Honoratus, former standard-bearer, Rutilius Iulianus, former exempt, chaplains to Anullinus and Fronto, Caius Cassius Quadratus, from the camp in Pollia, Lucius Licinius Ianuarius, former exempt, from Tubursico of the Numidians, to Severus and Victor, Publius Pompeius Caprarius, from the pollio of Cirta, to Caelius Rufinus, from the armatura, from the camp, Lucius Ianuarius Rogatianus, candidate, from Thysdrus, to Lateranus and Rufinus, Titus Senius Saturninus, former candidate, from the camp, Marcus Pontennius Victor, from the Mars, Caius Iulius Firmus, from the camp in Pollia, Marcus Aurelius Felix, from Thamugadi, who began military service in the year when Emperor Commodus and Glabrio were consuls, Marcus Areus Vicore, Publius Tamudius Venustus, a student of the armatura of Arnensis, from Carthage, Quintus Geminius Rogatianus, former candidate, from the camp, to Tertullus and Clemens, Iulius Secundus, from Hadrumetum, to Dextrus and Priscus, Lucius Tannonius Crescens, from Quirina in Cirta, Valerius For, formerly exempt, having been made from M. factus amacio, Valerianus, Iulius Agriaus, former standard-bearer, Aem. IE II O. Egrilius Felix, from him, Tertu. e. C. D. P. C. P. C.
EDCS-20600069.summary: This extensive inscription records a collective dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by numerous veterans and serving soldiers, including various ranks and origins, during the consulship of Emperor Commodus and Glabrio, dating it to 186 CE.

EDCS-20600071.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator of Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, and of Julia Mamaea, mother of our lord Augustus and the entire divine house, Lucius Marius Crescentianus, quaestor, aedile of the II viralis, devoted to their divine will, placed the altar which he vowed with his own money.
EDCS-20600071.summary: This inscription records a dedication of an altar to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator by Lucius Marius Crescentianus, a local magistrate, in honor of Emperor Severus Alexander and his mother Julia Mamaea, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20600072.translation: To Jupiter the Repeller, and the genius of the place, Marcus Valerius Maximianus, imperial legate of the III Augusta legion, proconsul, and Ulpia Aristonice.
EDCS-20600072.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter the Repeller and the genius of the place by Marcus Valerius Maximianus, a high-ranking imperial legate, and his wife Ulpia Aristonice, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20600073.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus Augustus, Lucius Septimius Maximus Fabius Maximus, tribune of the III Augusta legion, in his own name and that of his father Lucius Septimius Marcellus, tribune of the same legion, with his wife Lurcia Puteolana and his son Lucius Septimius Fabius Magnus, gladly and deservedly paid his vow with a joyful heart.
EDCS-20600073.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to Jupiter Dolichenus by Lucius Septimius Maximus Fabius Maximus, a tribune of the III Augusta legion, along with his family, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20600074.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Tiberius Memmius Ulpianus, son of Titus, of the Palatina tribe, from Rome, prefect of the II Cohort of Roman Citizens, Equitata, Pious and Faithful, tribune of the III Augusta legion, with his wife Veratia Athenaide and his daughter Memmia Macrina, gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-20600074.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to Jupiter Dolichenus by Tiberius Memmius Ulpianus, a prefect and tribune of the III Augusta legion, along with his family, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-20600075.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, to Aesculapius and Hygieia, and to the other immortal gods, Titus Flavius Maximus, formerly a centurion of the praetorian guard, chief centurion, and prefect of the III Augusta Severiana legion, together with his wife Antonia Antonina, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20600075.summary: This inscription records a fulfillment of a vow by a high-ranking military officer and his wife to several deities.

EDCS-20600077.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Fulgur, for the safety of our lord Aurelian Augustus, [this dedication is made by] Lurius Fortunatus, formerly a scribe; Aufidius Processus, formerly a scribe; Stenius Flavianus, formerly a scribe of the legate; Aurelius Lisimacus, formerly a scribe; Aurelius Felix, formerly a scribe; Aurelius Marinus, formerly a scribe; Granius Felix, formerly a duplicarius of the grain supply; Servilius Victor senior, formerly a scribe; Aurelius Malchus, formerly a scribe; Camerius Victorius, formerly a scribe; Bibius Ianuarius, formerly a dispensator; Logius Magnius, formerly a scribe; Iulius Aurassus, formerly a scribe of a broad-striped senator; Pompeius Pontius, formerly a scribe; Mesius Saturninus, formerly a scribe of the fiscus; Aelius Liberalis, formerly a scribe; Aelius Crescens, formerly a scribe; Iulius Saturninus, formerly a scribe; Calpurnius Proculus, formerly a scribe; Varius Felix, formerly a scribe of the legate; Caecilius Donatus, formerly a scribe; Petronius Saturninus, formerly a scribe; Sallus Modius, formerly a scribe; Aurelius Viator, formerly a scribe of a tribune; Aurelius Claudianus, formerly a scribe; Calpurnius Fortunatus, formerly a scribe; Dumi Atticus, formerly a scribe; Aufidius Victor, formerly a scribe of the legate; Valerius Marcianus, formerly a scribe; Antonius Flavianus, formerly a scribe; Dubidius Crescens, formerly a scribe; Petronius Novellinus, formerly a scribe; Flavius Maximus, formerly a scribe; Aurelius Florentinus, formerly a scribe of an evocatus; Valerius Victor, formerly a scribe of an evocatus arcarius; Perelius Donatus, formerly a scribe; Servilius Diocianus Faustinus, formerly a scribe; Didius Vincentius, formerly a scribe; Petronius, formerly a scribe; Iulius F[---], formerly a scribe of a cohort; Caecilius Victorinus, formerly a scribe; Iulius Fortunatus, formerly a scribe; Domitius Ingenuus, formerly a scribe; Vasidius Festus, formerly a scribe; Iulius Victor, formerly a scribe; Aurelius Euticianus, formerly a scribe; Sextilius Vitalis, formerly a standard-bearer.
EDCS-20600077.summary: This extensive inscription lists numerous individuals, primarily scribes and other administrative personnel, who dedicate an offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Fulgur for the emperor's safety.

EDCS-20600078.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus, Gaius Iulius Valerianus, centurion of the III Augusta XVI Flavia Firma, bis IIII Scythica, bis, for the safety of himself and Licinia Aquilina his wife, and of Iulius Proculus, centurion of the V Macedonica legion and III Gallica and XXII Primigenia, his brother, and of Varia Aquilina his wife, and of Iulia Aquilina their daughter, placed [this monument].
EDCS-20600078.summary: A centurion dedicated this monument to Jupiter Heliopolitanus for the well-being of his extended family, including his brother and their wives and daughter.

EDCS-20600079.translation: To the most holy Jupiter Optimus Maximus Heliopolitanus, Publius Seius Rufus, son of Publius, of the Arnensi tribe, from Teate in the territory of the Marrucini, prefect of the III Augusta legion, [dedicated this].
EDCS-20600079.summary: A prefect of the III Augusta legion dedicated this sacred offering to Jupiter Heliopolitanus.

EDCS-20600080.summary: This is a simple titular inscription identifying an individual as a priest of Jupiter Pluton Serapis.

EDCS-20600082.translation: Lucius Figilius Secundus and Flavius Crispinus, as aediles, took care that the lake, which had been dry for four years, should flow.
EDCS-20600082.summary: Two aediles are credited with restoring a water supply from a lake that had been dry for four years.

EDCS-20600083.translation: To Alfeno Fortunato, who in a dream seemed to say, 'Born of Jupiter's thunderbolt, a two-year-old', I dedicated this basis for the renewal of the household god, to be consecrated; as prefect of the camp yourself, attend therefore to this our gift, making our children see Rome, honored and crowned, our lords safe, with their mother.
EDCS-20600083.summary: This inscription is a complex votive dedication to Alfeno Fortunato, referencing a dream and a divine birth, possibly related to military service and the well-being of superiors and family.

EDCS-20600086.translation: To Mars Augustus and the Campestrian gods, Lucius Aurelius Maximus [dedicated this].
EDCS-20600086.summary: Lucius Aurelius Maximus dedicated this inscription to Mars Augustus and the Campestrian gods.

EDCS-20600087.translation: Sacred to Mars and Minerva Augustorum, Aurelius Gaius, evocatus of his cohort, fulfilled his vow with a willing heart.
EDCS-20600087.summary: Aurelius Gaius, a soldier enlisted as an evocatus in his cohort, willingly fulfilled a vow to Mars and Minerva Augustorum.

EDCS-20600090.translation: To the August Mauri gods, Lucius Flavius Geminus, son of Marcius, of the Quirina tribe, from Kalama, served in the III Augusta legion, and fulfilled his vow with a willing heart.
EDCS-20600090.summary: Lucius Flavius Geminus, a soldier from Kalama who served in the III Augusta legion, fulfilled a vow to the Mauri gods.

EDCS-20600093.summary: This is a dedication to Augustus from Medaura, likely an imperial cult inscription.

EDCS-20600096.translation: Quintus Octavius Getulicus willingly paid his vow to Mercury Augustus.
EDCS-20600096.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Quintus Octavius Getulicus to Mercury Augustus.

EDCS-20600097.translation: A small image of Mercury Silvanus.
EDCS-20600097.summary: This inscription likely labels a statue or image of Mercury Silvanus.

EDCS-20600098.translation: Marcus Ulpius Quintianus, a centurion of the III Augusta legion, and his wife Ulpia Ingenua restored the temple and the statue of the divinity of Minerva Augusta, and they dedicated them.
EDCS-20600098.summary: This inscription records the restoration and dedication of a temple and statue to Minerva Augusta by a centurion of the III Augusta legion and his wife.

EDCS-20600101.translation: Quintus Manlius Victor, a centurion of the III Augusta legion, willingly paid his vow to Motmanius and Mercury.
EDCS-20600101.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Quintus Manlius Victor to Motmanius and Mercury.

EDCS-20600105.translation: The gardens were established during the consulship of Plautianus II and Septimius Geta II.
EDCS-20600105.summary: This inscription records the establishment of gardens during the consulship of Plautianus and Septimius Geta, likely in the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-20600115.translation: Marcus Aurelius Fortunatus, an outstanding man and prefect of the III Augusta Aureliana legion, and Aelia Optata, a most distinguished woman, his wife, willingly paid their vow for the safety of our lord, the Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Emperor Lucius Domitius Aurelianus.
EDCS-20600115.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by a prefect of the III Augusta Aureliana legion and his wife for the health of Emperor Aurelian.

EDCS-20600116.translation: Sacred to the fruitful Augustus Saturn. Tiberius Teltonius Marcellus, prefect of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion, willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-20600116.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Tiberius Teltonius Marcellus, prefect of the III Augusta legion, to Saturn Augustus.

EDCS-20600117.translation: Publius Antonius Donatus, the priest, restored from the ground up, together with his son Antonius Donatus, willingly.
EDCS-20600117.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a structure, possibly a sanctuary, by a priest and his son to the Unconquered God Saturn.

EDCS-20600118.translation: Calventius paid his vow willingly with his family for the priesthood to Augustus Saturn.
EDCS-20600118.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Calventius for the priesthood to Saturn Augustus.

EDCS-20600119.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Marcus Silius Felix, priest.
EDCS-20600119.summary: An inscription dedicating a sanctuary to Saturn Augustus, made by the priest Marcus Silius Felix.

EDCS-20600120.translation: For the health of Emperor Antoninus and Julia Domna, their priests made this sacred temple, altar, and portico to Lord Saturn and Queen Ops; Lucius Veturius Felix and Lucia Quieta happily.
EDCS-20600120.summary: A dedication to Saturn and Ops for the health of Emperor Antoninus and Julia Domna, by their priests Lucius Veturius Felix and Lucia Quieta.

EDCS-20600121.translation: For the health of the Emperors, Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius Felix and Publius Septimius Geta Nobilissimus Caesar and Julia Augusta, mother of the Augusti and of the camp and of the entire divine family, the Third Augustan Legion, pious, restored this temple to Silvanus, which had collapsed from age.
EDCS-20600121.summary: The Third Augustan Legion restored a temple to Silvanus, which had collapsed due to age, for the health of the Severan imperial family, likely during their reign (c. 198-211 CE).

EDCS-20600122.translation: Sacred to the most holy Silvanus.
EDCS-20600122.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-20600123.translation: Sacred to the most holy Silvanus.
EDCS-20600123.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-20600125.translation: To the Unconquered Sun-god Mithras. Marcus Aurelius Sabinus, son of Marcus, of the Sergia tribe, from Carnuntum, prefect of the Third Augustan Legion, Pious, Victorious, Maximian, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20600125.summary: A vow fulfilled by Marcus Aurelius Sabinus, prefect of the Third Augustan Legion, to the god Mithras.

EDCS-20600126.translation: For the health of our lord Aurelianus Invictus Augustus. Aurelius Longinianus, centurion of the Third Augustan Legion, first-rank soldier of the aforementioned legion, built the temple of the Invictus from his own money from the ground up.
EDCS-20600126.summary: Aurelius Longinianus, a centurion of the Third Augustan Legion, built a temple to the Unconquered god at his own expense for the health of Emperor Aurelian.

EDCS-20600127.translation: To the Victory of the Augusti. Caius Gargilius Felix, quinquennial aedile, duumviral quinquennial, the site was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-20600127.summary: A site granted by local council for a dedication to the Victory of the Augusti, initiated by Caius Gargilius Felix.

EDCS-20600132.translation: Lucius Aemilius Marcellinus, former first spear officer, built the shrine with porticoes at his own expense and furthermore, to adorn it, he placed and dedicated six ornate columns according to the wish of Lucius Aemilius Salvianus, his brother of excellent memory, out of love for the citizens.
EDCS-20600132.summary: Lucius Aemilius Marcellinus funded the construction of a shrine with porticoes and decorative columns, dedicated to honor his deceased brother, Lucius Aemilius Salvianus.

EDCS-20600133.translation: Decimus Iulius Liberalis made the altar at his own expense from his choice.
EDCS-20600133.summary: An altar dedicated by Decimus Iulius Liberalis, funded at his own expense.

EDCS-20600134.translation: Decimus Iulius Liberalis made this at his own expense from his position as optio.
EDCS-20600134.summary: This inscription records a private dedication made by Decimus Iulius Liberalis, likely a soldier holding the rank of optio.

EDCS-20600135.translation: Ulpius Iulius, an outstanding man and prefect of the III Augusta legion, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-20600135.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Ulpius Iulius, a high-ranking military officer (legionary prefect).

EDCS-20600137.translation: Of Caius Julius Magnus, the priest.
EDCS-20600137.summary: This inscription is a dedication or a marker associated with Caius Julius Magnus, who held the priesthood.

EDCS-20600138.translation: Quintus Castricius Clarus fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20600138.summary: This inscription records Quintus Castricius Clarus fulfilling a votive promise.

EDCS-20600139.translation: Durius Plotinus gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-20600139.summary: This inscription records Durius Plotinus gladly fulfilling a votive promise.

EDCS-20600140.translation: The masters of the craft guild of the Pannonians, R. s., and Maximius Commodianus fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-20600140.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by the guild masters of the Pannonian craftsmen.

EDCS-20600145.translation: The city of Lambaesis, by decree of the decurions and at public expense, [dedicated this] to the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, conqueror of Armenia and Parthia, holder of tribunician power, pontifex maximus.
EDCS-20600145.summary: This inscription is a public dedication by the city of Lambaesis to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, likely related to his military victories.

EDCS-20600149.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus, Caius Pomponius Maximus, formerly a centurion of the III Augusta legion and now a decurion of the colony of Thamugadi.
EDCS-20600149.summary: This inscription is a dedication, likely in honor of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Commodus, by Caius Pomponius Maximus, a former centurion and current decurion.

EDCS-20600151.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, conqueror of Arabia, Adiabene, and Parthia, greatest father of the country, in his eleventh year of tribunician power, third consulship, proconsul, [dedicated by] the staff of the military treasury.
EDCS-20600151.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the staff of the military treasury to Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus, listing his titles and honors.

EDCS-20600152.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, conqueror of Arabia, Adiabene, and Parthia, greatest father of the country, in his eleventh year of tribunician power, third consulship, proconsul, [dedicated by] the staff of the military treasury.
EDCS-20600152.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the staff of the military treasury to Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus, listing his titles and honors.

EDCS-20600154.translation: For the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, most excellent and bravest leaders, preservers of the empire, the Third Augusta Legion made the Septimian Road.
EDCS-20600154.summary: This inscription records the construction of the Septimian Road by the Third Augusta Legion during the reign of Septimius Severus and Caracalla.

EDCS-20600155.translation: For the safety of the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius Felix, greatest and bravest princes and leader of youth, and Julia Augusta, mother of the camps, the Third Augusta Legion rebuilt a bathhouse which had collapsed from age.
EDCS-20600155.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a bathhouse by the Third Augusta Legion for the imperial family, likely during the reign of Septimius Severus and Caracalla.

EDCS-20600157.translation: For Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, Father of the Fatherland, son of the deified Severus Pius Augustus, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor and of the camps and of the Senate and of the Fatherland.
EDCS-20600157.summary: This inscription lists honorific titles for Severus Pius Augustus and Julia Augusta, likely part of a larger monument.

EDCS-20600160.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 11th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, son of our Emperor Severus Augustus, Sextilius Saturninus, perpetual flamen, having promised for the honor of the perpetual flaminate conferred upon him, he placed and dedicated [it] with an increased sum of 5,000 sesterces, in addition to 12,000 sesterces for honoraria, and an additional 8,000 sesterces for the public treasury, by bringing in and giving double portions to his fellow curial officials who had performed their duties.
EDCS-20600160.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Sextilius Saturninus, a perpetual flamen, to Emperor Caracalla, detailing financial contributions for public honors and expenses.

EDCS-20600161.translation: For the safety and well-being of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor and of the camps and of the Senate and of the Fatherland, the Curia Papiria.
EDCS-20600161.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the Curia Papiria for the safety and well-being of Emperor Caracalla and Julia Domna.

EDCS-20600165.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, a vexillation of Moorish soldiers from Caesariana, devoted to his divine will and majesty.
EDCS-20600165.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a vexillation of Moorish soldiers from Caesariana to Emperor Gordian III.

EDCS-20600168.translation: To the blessed ones and to Maximian.
EDCS-20600168.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription mentioning 'Maximian', likely a dedication or part of an epitaph.

EDCS-20600169.translation: For our lord Flavius Constantius, most noble and most flourishing Caesar, the order of the colonia of Lambaesis made [this] through the benevolence of our divine lords and Emperors Augustus.
EDCS-20600169.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the city council of Lambaesis to Caesar Constantius.

EDCS-20600170.translation: For our lord, the perpetual Emperor Flavius Valerius Constantinus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, triumphant, who restored liberty to the world, the republic of the colonia of Lambaesis made [this].
EDCS-20600170.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the city of Lambaesis to Emperor Constantine I, celebrating his victories and restoration of liberty.

EDCS-20600171.translation: By the indulgence of our divine lords and Emperors Augustus, Valentinian and Valens, conquerors of the whole world, a market hall, with the temple of Calenus, which had completely collapsed and was ruined, was rebuilt from the foundation.
EDCS-20600171.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a market hall and temple by decree of Emperors Valentinian and Valens.

EDCS-20600172.translation: To adorn the square on the festive occasion of the tenth anniversary of the emperor, an arch was constructed under the supervision of Silicius Silicianus, perpetual priest and curator of public affairs.
EDCS-20600172.summary: This inscription records the construction of an arch to celebrate an imperial anniversary, commissioned by a local official.

EDCS-20600173.translation: By order of the pious and fortunate Emperor, pontifex maximus, in his tribunician power, at the expense of Fabius Felix.
EDCS-20600173.summary: This inscription records an expense incurred by Fabius Felix, likely for a public work or event, during the reign of an emperor.

EDCS-20600177.translation: Patience, Virtue, Hope. Varius Clemens, along with the most splendid city of Saldus and myself with the people of Saldus, implore you, my lord, to encourage Nonius Datus, a veteran of Legio III Augusta and a surveyor, to come to Saldus to complete the work he left unfinished. I set out and suffered greatly from bandits on the road, but escaped naked and wounded with my companions. I arrived at Saldus and met Clemens, the procurator, who brought me to the mountain where they were struggling with a tunnel of difficult construction, as if it were to be abandoned because the perforation of the tunnel was longer than the mountain's span appeared. It became clear that the ditches had deviated from the intended line, such that the upper ditch veered to the right towards the south, and the lower similarly veered to the right towards the north. Therefore, two-thirds of the project, leaving the intended line, were errant, while the intended line was shifted from east to west above the mountain. So that no error may occur for the reader regarding the ditches, let us understand that the upper is the part where the tunnel receives water, and the lower is where it emits it. When I designated the work, so that they might know the measure of their own perforation, I organized a competition of work among the classic soldiers and Gaesates, and thus they converged at the completion of the mountain. Therefore, I, who had first made the plan and assigned the route, and had initiated its construction according to the plan I gave to Petronius Celer, the procurator, dedicated the completed work, with water flowing. Varius Clemens, the procurator, [dedicated it]. To make my work clearer regarding this Saldian water channel, I have attached some letters. To Porcius Vetustinus, to Crispinus: You have acted most kindly, my lord, and in addition to your other humanity and benevolence, you sent for Nonius Datus to be summoned so that I might discuss with him the works he has undertaken. Therefore, although I was pressed for time and hastened to Caesarea, I nevertheless rushed to Saldus and inspected the well-begun but massive water channel, which cannot be completed without the care of Nonius Datus, who managed it simultaneously diligently and faithfully. And therefore, I was going to ask if you would allow us to remain for some months to attend to this matter, unless I had fallen ill from the strain of the work.
EDCS-20600177.summary: This lengthy inscription details a complex dispute and resolution concerning the construction of a water channel (aquaeductus) near Saldus, involving various officials, workers, and a veteran surveyor named Nonius Datus, and includes correspondence related to the project.

EDCS-20600209.translation: Catullinus, legate, [commander] of the OII [unit?], where a horse...
EDCS-20600209.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning a legate named Catullinus and a horse, likely related to a military or equestrian context.

EDCS-20800606.translation: Dedicated by Claudius Gallus, clarissimum virum, legate Augustorum pro praetore, of the sacred temple, Publius Aelius Procles Menecratianus, Florius Fortunatus, and Publius Maevius Saturninus Honoratianus, tribunes of the soldiers of Legio XI Claudia, a most distinguished young man, and Decimus Aelius Menecratianus, tribune of the soldiers of Legio I Adiutrix, prefect of Cohors VI Brittonum equitata, through Publius Aelius Menecrates Florianus, of equestrian rank, perpetual flamen of the municipality of Lambaesis, and duumvir, and Iulia Procula.
EDCS-20800606.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a sacred temple or shrine by various military officers and local officials, including a legate, tribunes, a prefect, and a perpetual flamen, in Lambaesis.

EDCS-20800615.translation: By Marcus Valerius Senecio, legate of the emperor pro praetore, governor of the province of Germania Inferior, consul, the speculatores, beneficiarii, and quaestionarii, under the supervision of Agilius Felix, quaestor.
EDCS-20800615.summary: This inscription records a dedication or dedication-related activity supervised by Agilius Felix, quaestor, on behalf of military personnel serving under the legate Marcus Valerius Senecio in Germania Inferior.

EDCS-20800620.translation: What were the testimonies of past life are now declared by this final writing; these are indeed the consolations of death, wherein is contained eternal memory of name or lineage. Ennia Fructuosa, dearest wife, most chaste and praiseworthy matron for her good obedience, lies here. She accepted the name of wife in the 15th year, in which she could not live more than 13 years. She did not receive the lot of death as she deserved, but lay fixed in time, mute, as if her spirit were extorted by force, to be returned to nature, whose transgressors, whether Manes or celestial gods, will be the avengers of the crime. Aelius Proculinus, husband himself, tribune of the great Legio III Augusta, erected this.
EDCS-20800620.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Ennia Fructuosa, the wife of Aelius Proculinus, a tribune of Legio III Augusta, praising her virtue and lamenting her untimely death.

EDCS-20800622.translation: Lucius Aemilius Marcellinus, primus pilus, placed this for Lucius Aemilius Salvianus, a man of excellent memory, his brother, according to the terms of his will, under the care of his freedmen, the three Aemilii, Festus, Curio, and Chrestus.
EDCS-20800622.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Lucius Aemilius Salvianus by his brother, Lucius Aemilius Marcellinus, a primus pilus, according to Salvianus's will.

EDCS-20800623.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Aufidius Felix erected this for his parents, from his own means: for Aufidius Felix, primus pilus, who lived 75 years, and for Prima, his mother, who deserved well, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-20800623.summary: This funerary inscription records Publius Aufidius Felix dedicating a monument from his own funds for his father, Aufidius Felix (a primus pilus), and his mother, Prima.

EDCS-20800625.translation: To Aurelia Clementina, from Aurelius Reginus, prefect of Legio III Augusta.
EDCS-20800625.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Aurelius Reginus, prefect of Legio III Augusta, to Aurelia Clementina.

EDCS-20800626.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Romanus, son of Marcus, a military tribune of the III Augusta Legion, lived 38 years and 2 months. His wife and heir, Aelia Filonice, erected this for her most dutiful husband, together with their children.
EDCS-20800626.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Romanus, a military tribune of the III Augusta Legion, set up by his wife and heirs, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20800629.translation: Of Flavia Xystus, from the first rank. By law and command.
EDCS-20800629.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Flavia Xystus, possibly related to a military or legal context, with an unclear purpose and date.

EDCS-20800631.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Hyacinthus the Epistates, the loyal and most deserving servant, Julius Sabinianus, a military tribune of the III Augusta Legion, dedicated this.
EDCS-20800631.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Julius Sabinianus, a military tribune of the III Augusta Legion, to his loyal freedman or servant, Hyacinthus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20800632.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Matius Quartus, a centurion of the first rank of the III Augusta Legion, made this for his well-deserving freedman Zosimus.
EDCS-20800632.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Matius Quartus, a centurion of the III Augusta Legion, for his freedman Zosimus, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20800633.translation: For Lucius Mellonis, son of Lucius, Claudia Blando Ara, prefect of the camp of the III Augusta Legion.
EDCS-20800633.summary: This is an inscription honoring Lucius Mellonis, son of Lucius, and Claudia Blando Ara, who held the position of prefect of the camp of the III Augusta Legion, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20800634.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Munatius Macro, my most dutiful son. Munatius Venustus, his father, a tribune of the III Augusta Legion, lived nine years.
EDCS-20800634.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Munatius Macro, who died at nine years old, set up by his father, Munatius Venustus, a tribune of the III Augusta Legion, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20800635.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Septimius Septiminus, who lived 13 years. Septimius Mucatra, a military tribune of the III Augusta Legion, for his dearest son.
EDCS-20800635.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Septimius Septiminus, who died at 13, erected by his father, Septimius Mucatra, a military tribune of the III Augusta Legion, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20800637.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Aemilius Agathopus, a companion and beloved pupil of Silvanianus the tribune, who lived 19 years.
EDCS-20800637.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Aemilius Agathopus, who died at 19, dedicated by Silvanianus, a tribune, possibly his mentor, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20800639.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Valerius Valerianus, an advocate from Carthage, who lived 35 years. Valeria Saturnina, his mother, erected this for her most dutiful son. He is buried here.
EDCS-20800639.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Valerius Valerianus, an advocate from Carthage who died at 35, set up by his mother, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20800642.translation: To Ancyra Largus, from Galatia, prefect of the III Augusta Legion.
EDCS-20800642.summary: This inscription honors Ancyra Largus, from Galatia, who served as prefect of the III Augusta Legion, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-20800644.translation: Having subdued the long times, and for future times, you sought out praises; the prefect of the legion and his friends dedicated this.
EDCS-20800644.summary: This inscription is a dedication by friends and a prefect of a legion to commemorate the achievement of overcoming difficult times.

EDCS-20800645.translation: Acutius Aristo and Acutius Strabo, a centurion of Legio III Augusta, and his sons and heirs, dedicated this to Acutius Ianus.
EDCS-20800645.summary: This is a dedication by sons and heirs to Acutius Ianus, mentioning a centurion of Legio III Augusta.

EDCS-20800646.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For their most dutiful mother, who lived 70 years, Publius Aelius Hermias, aquilifer of Legio III Augusta, and his brother Polianus, set this up.
EDCS-20800646.summary: A funerary monument set up by a brother and an aquilifer of Legio III Augusta for their mother, who lived 70 years.

EDCS-20800647.translation: Aelius Maior, in charge of arms, and Aelius Speratus and Aelius Vindilus, soldiers of the same legion, his heirs, made this for him who deserved it, for 2000 sesterces. He lived 44 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-20800647.summary: A funerary monument paid for by soldiers of Legio III Augusta, including a supervisor of arms, for a fellow soldier who lived 44 years.

EDCS-20800648.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the departed. For Quintus Aelius Minucianus, beneficiary of the prefect of the legion, who lived 45 years. Quintus Aelius Lucanus, a fellow soldier and his closest kinsman, made this.
EDCS-20800648.summary: A funerary dedication to Quintus Aelius Minucianus, a beneficiary of a legionary prefect, by his kinsman and fellow soldier, dated to his lifetime of 45 years.

EDCS-20800649.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius Proculi filius. Quintinus Perennis, his heir, saw to the making of this for his most dutiful brother, a centurion of Legio III Augusta, who lived 50 years.
EDCS-20800649.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by an heir for his brother, a centurion of Legio III Augusta, who lived 50 years.

EDCS-20800650.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius Romanus, son of Publius, a centurion of Legio III Augusta, and of Legio VII Claudia XX Valeria Victrix, and of Legio I Italica, decorated with honors, a vanquisher of enemies in the province of Hispania and the region of Mazicum Montensium. Valeria Ingenua, his wife, with her children Tuberone and Romana, made this.
EDCS-20800650.summary: A funerary monument for Publius Aelius Romanus, a decorated centurion who served in multiple legions and fought enemies in Hispania, erected by his wife and children.

EDCS-20800651.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius Securus of Napoca, a decurion of Cohors II Hispanorum, who lived 35 years. Publius Aelius Arimanus, custodian of arms, made this for him who deserved well, for 200 sesterces.
EDCS-20800651.summary: A funerary monument for Publius Aelius Securus, a decurion of Cohors II Hispanorum who lived 35 years, erected by his colleague Publius Aelius Arimanus.

EDCS-20800652.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Aelius Restutus, centurion of Legio I Adiutricis, centurion of Legio III Augusta, his sons made this. He lived 50 years.
EDCS-20800652.summary: A funerary monument for Aelius Restutus, who served as a centurion in Legio I Adiutricis and Legio III Augusta, erected by his sons, noting he lived 50 years.

EDCS-20800653.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius Serapio, standard-bearer of Legio III Augusta, who lived [X] years. Caius Antonius Alexander, a veteran, made this for him who deserved well.
EDCS-20800653.summary: A funerary dedication for Publius Aelius Serapio, a standard-bearer of Legio III Augusta, made by a veteran named Caius Antonius Alexander.

EDCS-20800654.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Publius Aelius Silvanus, centurion of the III Augusta legion. [---]
EDCS-20800654.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Silvanus, a centurion of the III Augusta legion.

EDCS-20800655.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Gaius Caelis Saturninus, soldier of the III Augusta legion, lived 40 years. Aelius Surus, a cattle dealer of the same legion, a fellow citizen and heir, made this.
EDCS-20800655.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Caelis Saturninus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, erected by a fellow soldier and heir.

EDCS-20800656.translation: To Publius Aelius Taurus, groom of the pack animals, lived 25 years. Publius Aelius Arpocra, standard-bearer of the III Augusta legion, made this for his brother.
EDCS-20800656.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Taurus, a groom, erected by his brother, Publius Aelius Arpocra, a standard-bearer of the III Augusta legion.

EDCS-20800658.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Publius Aelius Vitalis, eagle-bearer of the III Augusta legion, lived 45 years. His heirs made this: Publius Aelius Mucianus, Publius Aelius Mucatra, Gaius Valerius Fortis.
EDCS-20800658.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Vitalis, an eagle-bearer of the III Augusta legion, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-20800659.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Lucius Aemilius Albus, armourer of the III Augusta legion, lived 55 years. Geminia Asella made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-20800659.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Aemilius Albus, an armourer of the III Augusta legion, erected by his wife.

EDCS-20800660.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Gaius Aemilius Demetrianus, eagle-bearer, lived 32 years. Gaius Aemilius Emeritus, his father, made this for his dutiful son and also dedicated it.
EDCS-20800660.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Aemilius Demetrianus, an eagle-bearer, erected by his father.

EDCS-20800662.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Aemilius Rogatianus, beneficiary of the III Augusta legion, lived 37 years, and Aemilius Rogatianus, his son, lived 1 year. Aurelia Valentina, his mother, made this for her son.
EDCS-20800662.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Aemilius Rogatianus, a beneficiary of the III Augusta legion, and his infant son, erected by his wife and mother of the son.

EDCS-20800663.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Quintus Aenius Rogatus, lived 30 years, served 14 years, a duplicarius. [---]
EDCS-20800663.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Aenius Rogatus, a duplicarius, who served for 14 years.

EDCS-20800664.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Gaius Agrius Dexter, veteran, formerly a beneficiary of a tribune, lived well 82 years. Gaius Iulius Nastor, veteran, his son-in-law, made this for his most excellent father-in-law and dedicated it.
EDCS-20800664.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Agrius Dexter, a veteran who served as a tribune's beneficiary, erected by his son-in-law.

EDCS-20800665.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. To Alfius Blasius, centurion of the III Augusta legion, and to Caecilius Proculeianus, soldier, candidate for centurion, a fellow centurion from Campania, made this in memory. He lived about 40 years.
EDCS-20800665.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Alfius Blasius, a centurion of the III Augusta legion, and Caecilius Proculeianus, a fellow soldier and candidate centurion, erected by a colleague.

EDCS-20800666.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Fulcinia Iusta, who lived 25 years, Alfius Ianuarius, centurion of the Legio III Augusta, made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-20800666.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a centurion to his wife, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD given the legion mentioned.

EDCS-20800667.translation: To the Divine Manes. A sacred offering. Although pitiable, I joined myself to your tomb, my dear, as much as I could, for as long as my life remains to me. I believe this will be pleasing to you if the Underworld also knows it. Ampliatus, the actor, made this.
EDCS-20800667.summary: A funerary inscription by an actor named Ampliatus for a deceased loved one, expressing enduring grief and hope for recognition in the afterlife.

EDCS-20800668.translation: To the Divine Manes. Sacred offering. Donata, pious and just, farewell. You could not bear the return of your absent husband, and keep all your loved ones. Ampliatus will be able to bring you sad gifts.
EDCS-20800668.summary: A funerary inscription for a woman named Donata, lamenting her inability to endure her husband's absence and death.

EDCS-20800669.translation: To the Divine Manes. Sacred offering. Publius Ancharius Saturninus, optio, who lived 20 years. Publius Ancharius Victorinus, a veteran, made this for his most loving brother from his own funds.
EDCS-20800669.summary: A funerary monument for a young man named Publius Ancharius Saturninus, dedicated by his veteran brother.

EDCS-20800670.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Julia Secunda, daughter of Publius, from Hadrumetum, aged 40. Annaeus Clarus, centurion of the Legio III Augusta, made this for his excellent wife.
EDCS-20800670.summary: A funerary dedication by a centurion of the Legio III Augusta for his wife, Julia Secunda, who was from Hadrumetum.

EDCS-20800671.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Sextilia Quieta, his most holy wife, who lived 27 years. Quintus Annius Clarus, centurion of the Legio X Gemina.
EDCS-20800671.summary: A funerary monument for Sextilia Quieta, dedicated by her husband, Quintus Annius Clarus, a centurion of the Legio X Gemina.

EDCS-20800673.translation: To the Divine Manes. Sacred offering. Marcus Antonius Sabinus, son of Marcus, of the Collina tribe, from Caesarea Augusta, centurion of the Legio X Fretensis, centurion of the Legio III Augusta, who lived 60 years, and his daughter Antonia Antulla, who lived 11 years. Varia Binna, his wife.
EDCS-20800673.summary: A funerary monument for Marcus Antonius Sabinus, a centurion who served in two legions and lived to 60, and his daughter Antonia Antulla, dedicated by his wife Varia Binna.

EDCS-20800674.translation: To the Divine Manes. Sacred offering. For Lucius Antonius Saturus, haruspex of the Legio Flavia Gemellina, her most sweet husband, she made this.
EDCS-20800674.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife for her husband, Lucius Antonius Saturus, who served as a haruspex (diviner) in the Legio Flavia Gemellina.

EDCS-20800675.translation: Apo Saturnianus, optio, who lived 25 years, arranged for this to be made for his most sweet wife. To the Divine Manes. Sacred offering. Apo Saturnianus, who lived 2 years, 2 months, and 19 days, arranged for this to be made for his most sweet son. Apo Saturnianus, optio.
EDCS-20800675.summary: A funerary monument with double dedication, by an optio named Apo Saturnianus for both his wife and his young son.

EDCS-20800676.translation: To the Divine Manes. Sacred offering. Buccius Antoninus, veteran, who lived 75 years. Antonius Victor, signifer, made this for his most sweet father.
EDCS-20800676.summary: A funerary inscription by a soldier named Antonius Victor for his father, Buccius Antoninus, a veteran.

EDCS-20800678.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Lucius Arruntius Saturninus, aide of the prefect of the Legio III Augustae, lived 25 years. Tonneius Victor made this for his deserving stepson.
EDCS-20800678.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Arruntius Saturninus, an aide to a prefect of Legio III Augustae, set up by his stepson.

EDCS-20800679.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Marcus Attius Festus, standard-bearer of the Legio III Augustae, lived 53 years, 2 months, and 19 days. Marcus Sulpicius Proculus, standard-bearer of the same legion, made this for his best friend.
EDCS-20800679.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Attius Festus, a standard-bearer of Legio III Augustae, set up by a colleague.

EDCS-20800680.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Publius Aufidius Felix, standard-bearer of the Legio III Augustae, lived 47 years. His mother Manilia Arbuscula and his brothers and heirs Aufidius Quartus and Amarantus, aides of the tribune, made this for him when he deserved it, for 1200 sesterces.
EDCS-20800680.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aufidius Felix, a standard-bearer of Legio III Augustae, erected by his mother and brothers, with a stated cost.

EDCS-20800682.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Aurelius Amandus, centurion of the Legio III Augustae, is buried here. His son Titus, formerly a decurion of the Emperor's personal cavalry, lived 45 years. His heirs Aurelius Amandus and Aurelius Gentilis, centurion of the same legion, took care to erect this, for 2000 sesterces.
EDCS-20800682.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Amandus, a centurion of Legio III Augustae, erected by his sons, with a stated cost.

EDCS-20800683.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, centurion of the Legio III Augustae, lived 57 years. Iulia Successa made this for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-20800683.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, a centurion of Legio III Augustae, set up by his wife.

EDCS-20800684.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Titia Primiane made this for her well-deserving husband Aurelius Apollophanus, centurion of the Legio III Augustae.
EDCS-20800684.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Apollophanus, a centurion of Legio III Augustae, set up by his wife.

EDCS-20800685.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Benigno and Eustatia, slaves. Titus Aurelius Bethus, centurion of the Legio III Augustae, made this for them when they deserved it, from his own funds.
EDCS-20800685.summary: This is a funerary inscription for two slaves, Benigno and Eustatia, set up by their owner, Titus Aurelius Bethus, a centurion of Legio III Augustae.

EDCS-20800686.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Augurinus Terentius Propinquus. Titus Aurelius Bethus, centurion of the Legio III Augustae, made this for him when he deserved it, from his own funds.
EDCS-20800686.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Augurinus Terentius Propinquus, set up by Titus Aurelius Bethus, a centurion of Legio III Augustae.

EDCS-20800687.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Marcus Aurelius Epaphroditus, aide of the tribune, who lived 35 years. Titus Pinarius Saturninus, standard-bearer of the legion, made this for his innocent parent.
EDCS-20800687.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Aurelius Epaphroditus, an aide to a tribune, erected by his son, a standard-bearer of Legio III Augustae.

EDCS-20800689.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Quintus Aurelius Processus Maddanius, optio of the Legio III Augustae, who lived 38 years. Iulia Datina, his mother, and his brother Longinus, made this.
EDCS-20800689.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Aurelius Processus Maddanius, an optio of Legio III Augustae, erected by his mother and brother.

EDCS-20800690.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Marcus Aurellius Sextianus, son of Marcus, of the Palatine tribe, a frumentarius centurion in Ostia, trainer of the Emperor's bodyguards, a hastatus of the III Augusta Legion, who lived 48 years. Paccia Prima, his wife and heir, [erected this] to her most devoted husband.
EDCS-20800690.summary: This funerary epitaph honors Marcus Aurellius Sextianus, a centurion and trainer of bodyguards, erected by his wife and heir.

EDCS-20800692.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Quintus Baebius Ianuarius, a cattle dealer of the legion, who lived 34 years and 7 months. Caecilia Fausta, his wife, and Baebia Faustina Caecilia, his heirs, made this for their father.
EDCS-20800692.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Quintus Baebius Ianuarius, a legionary cattle dealer, set up by his wife and heirs.

EDCS-20800693.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Caecilius Iovinus, a tesserarius of the III Augusta Legion, who lived 43 years. Caius Caecilius Iovinus, a beneficiarius of the legion, made this for his dearest brother.
EDCS-20800693.summary: A funerary monument for Caius Caecilius Iovinus, a tesserarius and beneficiarius of the III Augusta Legion, erected by his brother.

EDCS-20800694.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Caecilia Emerita, who lived 7 years. Caius Caelius Saturninus, a veteran and former beneficiarius, [erected this] for his innocent daughter.
EDCS-20800694.summary: This epitaph honors Caecilia Emerita, a young girl who died at age 7, dedicated by her father, a veteran.

EDCS-20800695.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Decimus Caesilius Hispanus, son of Decimus, who lived 13 years and 4 months. Caesilius Hispanus, a centurion of the III Augusta Legion, and Iulia Festa, his parents, [erected this] for their most dutiful son.
EDCS-20800695.summary: A tombstone for the young Decimus Caesilius Hispanus, son of a centurion, erected by his parents.

EDCS-20800696.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Gaius Calpurnius Clemens, an immune soldier of the III Augusta Legion, who lived 45 years. Staberia Hilara made this for her dearest husband.
EDCS-20800696.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Gaius Calpurnius Clemens, an immune soldier of the III Augusta Legion, erected by his wife.

EDCS-20800697.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Marcus Calpurnius Calpurnianus. Marcus Calpurnius Honoratianus, a centurion of the III Augusta Legion, [erected this] for his most beloved brother, who lived 60 years.
