EDCS-24500314.translation: To our lord Flavius Constantius, most noble Caesar, the council of the colony of Thamugadi, devoted to his divine power.
EDCS-24500314.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the council of Thamugadi to the Caesar Flavius Constantius, showing devotion to his divine authority.

EDCS-24500315.translation: To Galerius Valerius Maximianus, junion, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, of the Senate and the fatherland, invincible, pious, fortunate Augustus; the state of the colony of Thamugadi, devoted to his divine power and majesty. To our lord G[...], invincible, pious, fortunate Augustus; the state of the colony of Thamugadi, devoted to his divine power and majesty.
EDCS-24500315.summary: This inscription is a complex dedication by the council of Thamugadi to multiple emperors/Caesars, including Galerius Valerius Maximianus and an unnamed Augustus, expressing devotion to their divine authority and majesty.

EDCS-24500316.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius Augustus, the state of the colony of Thamugadi.
EDCS-24500316.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the state of the colony of Thamugadi to Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius Augustus.

EDCS-24500317.translation: To Flavius Valerius C[...], the state of the colony of Thamugadi, devoted to his divine power.
EDCS-24500317.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the state of the colony of Thamugadi to Flavius Valerius C[...], indicating devotion to his divine power.

EDCS-24500318.summary: This inscription is a fragmented statement identifying someone as the son of an Emperor Caesar.

EDCS-24500325.translation: Lucius Novius Crispinus, imperial legate with praetorian authority and patron of the colony, dedicated [this] with the approval of C. [...], and it was inscribed in bronze for perpetual memory.
EDCS-24500325.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Lucius Novius Crispinus, an imperial legate and patron of the colony, with approval from C. [...], likely during the reign of Emperor Julian.

EDCS-24500327.translation: By the authority of Ulpius Mariscianus, most distinguished man, promoted to consular rank, first by our lord Julian the invincible prince, the order of salutation was established and thus inscribed in bronze for perpetual memory: first, senators and their companions and former companions and administrators; second, the chief of the cornicularii of the palace; third, the crowned officials of the province; fourth, promoted officials and magistrates with their order; fifth, officials from the order. Also, how much profit should be obtained by the chief official for assigning a citizen within the city: five measures of wheat or the price of wheat for seven measures, or the price of seven measures, or even for ten thousand [sesterces], two measures, or their prices should increase if sent overseas, one hundred measures or the price of one hundred measures will be sufficient. The cornicularius and the commentariensis will receive half of this sum as profit. The scholastici in a simple petition will receive five measures of wheat or the price of five measures; in a contested case, they will receive ten measures of wheat or the price of ten measures; in an urgent case which must be concluded, fifteen measures or the price of fifteen measures. The exceptores in a simple petition will receive five measures of wheat or the price of five measures; in a contested case, twelve measures of wheat or the price of twelve measures; in a decided case, twenty measures of wheat or the price of twenty measures. For a document, single coins are sufficient for the majors; in contested cases, four; never more than six should be exacted from the litigant in a decided matter. The libellensis will have to receive two measures of wheat or the price of two measures for one petition. An official sent within the city should receive two measures of wheat or the price of two measures.
EDCS-24500327.summary: This inscription, authorized by Ulpius Mariscianus under Emperor Julian, details the establishment and benefits of a hierarchical system of official ranks and their corresponding emoluments, primarily in measures of wheat or their monetary value.

EDCS-24500329.translation: To Prastinia Pacata, daughter of Caius.
EDCS-24500329.summary: This is a funerary dedication to a woman named Prastinia Pacata, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500331.translation: To Gaius Annius Flavius, by public equestrian rank, procurator of the district of Carthage, procurator of the Cottian Alps, subprefect of the praetorian fleet of Misenum, prefect of the I Flavia Sabastena ala, tribune of the III Italica legion, prefect of the II Raetorum cohort, decorated with military honors in the Second Germanic War.
EDCS-24500331.summary: This is a career summary inscription honouring Gaius Annius Flavius, detailing his military and administrative posts and awards, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500335.translation: To Sertius Optantius, to Marcus Plotus Faustus, Roman knight, prefect of the III Ituraeorum cohort, tribune of the I Flavia Canathena cohort, prefect of the I Flavia Gallorum Tauriana Flavia ala, perpetual flamen, priest of the city. Marcus Pompeius Quintianus, Roman knight, perpetual flamen, [erected this for his] dearest father.
EDCS-24500335.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marcus Pompeius Quintianus for his father, Marcus Plotus Faustus, who held various equestrian and priestly offices, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500336.translation: To Sertia Cornelia Valentina Tucciana, wife of Marcus Plotus Faustus, from military service, perpetual flamen, a woman of honorable memory. Marcus Pompeius Quintianus, Roman knight, perpetual flamen, [erected this for his] most excellent mother.
EDCS-24500336.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Marcus Pompeius Quintianus for his mother, Sertia Cornelia Valentina Tucciana, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500339.summary: This is a dedication to Julia Vic, who held the priestly office of flaminica, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500340.summary: This is a dedication to Vocontius Potamius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500345.translation: 1st Italian Legion, 2nd Cohort. By decree of the decurions.
EDCS-24500345.summary: This inscription records a decision made by the decurions of the 1st Italian Legion, 2nd Cohort, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500347.translation: To Marcus Aurelius, son of Marcus.
EDCS-24500347.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Aurelius, son of Marcus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500348.summary: This inscription records the name and title of Julius Eralis, who was a prefect, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500380.translation: Aelia Honorata erected [this] to her most dutiful father, Aelius Secundinus.
EDCS-24500380.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aelia Honorata to her father, Aelius Secundinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500381.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Aelius Castus set this up for his deserving father, Publius Aelius Solutus.
EDCS-24500381.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by a son for his father.

EDCS-24500382.translation: Amania Sextulla, daughter of Sextus, pious, lived for 7 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24500382.summary: This is a tombstone for a young girl, recording her name, parentage, lifespan, and burial.

EDCS-24500430.translation: Sacred to the Guardian Spirits. Popilius [---].
EDCS-24500430.summary: This is a dedication to guardian spirits by an individual, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500431.translation: Sacred to the Guardian Spirits, to Jupiter Best and Greatest, to Mercury, and to the Genius of the army of Iorchobolus, the god.
EDCS-24500431.summary: This is a dedication to various deities, including the Genius of a specific army unit, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500432.translation: To all the gods. Gaius Julius Martinus, a beneficiary of a consular legate of the Third Augustan Legion in the province of Dacia.
EDCS-24500432.summary: This is a dedication to all gods by a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500433.translation: Sacred to the Geniuses of Mars the Victorious, Mercury Vazaivitanus, and the Patrons. Tiberius Claudius [---].
EDCS-24500433.summary: This is a dedication to several divine entities, including martial and local deities, by Tiberius Claudius.

EDCS-24500434.translation: To the Father Gradivus, the Genius of the Vazaivitanus station, and the Guardian Spirits. Marcus Baebius Speratus, a standard-bearer (cornicularius) of the prefect of the Third Augustan Legion, the Pious and Vindicative, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-24500434.summary: This is a votive inscription by a soldier to military deities and local spirits, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500435.translation: Sacred to the Father God. Postumius Caricus, formerly a grain official, now a beneficiary of a consular legate, willingly fulfilled his vow, [---].
EDCS-24500435.summary: This is a votive inscription by a soldier who previously held a civilian administrative role, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500437.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Vespasian, chief priest, with tribunician power...
EDCS-24500437.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to Emperor Domitian, likely from the Flavian period (AD 81-96).

EDCS-24500438.translation: To the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Pius, and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Pius, and Lucius Septimius Geta, most noble Caesar, son of our Augustus, 'prince of youth', and Julia Augusta, mother of the Augusti and of the camps. Marcus Baebius Victor, perpetual priest, as consul, made this at his own expense.
EDCS-24500438.summary: This is a monumental dedication funded by Marcus Baebius Victor, likely during the joint reign of Septimius Severus and his sons, Caracalla and Geta (c. AD 209-211).

EDCS-24500439.translation: First rank soldier of the 1st Cohort, a unit of a thousand men, of the 3rd Augustan Legion, IAN.
EDCS-24500439.summary: This inscription records the rank and unit of a soldier in the Roman army, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-24500440.translation: Lucius Pomponius Iovinus, pious, made this sacred offering to the Divine Manes; he fulfilled his vows; he lived 21 years. Priest Saturnus. Lucius Pomponius Successor, pious, made this sacred offering to the Divine Manes; he fulfilled his vows.
EDCS-24500440.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the deaths of Lucius Pomponius Iovinus and Lucius Pomponius Successor, noting their piety, fulfillment of vows, and ages, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500441.translation: Baebius Cerdo, freedman of Lucius Baebius, pious, lived 76 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-24500441.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the life and burial of Baebius Cerdo, a freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500442.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Caninius Primus lived 52 years. His wife, Iulia Ninosa, made this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-24500442.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Caninius Primus by his wife Iulia Ninosa, noting his age and their marital relationship, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500443.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Caius Iulius MR lived 22 years. His parents made this for their son.
EDCS-24500443.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Caius Iulius MR at age 22, set up by his parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500444.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Iulia Sabina, pious, in her own property, lived 60 years. N MP R instituted NI M raised.
EDCS-24500444.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication to Iulia Sabina, mentioning her piety and age, with some difficult to interpret phrases, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500445.translation: To the Divine Manes. Iulia Ingenua lived 70 years. Neinius Alumnus lived 82 years. Their heirs made this.
EDCS-24500445.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the deaths of Iulia Ingenua and Neinius Alumnus, noting their ages and that their heirs erected the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500446.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Octavia Successa lived 70 years. Caius Strabonius, her husband, and his sons made this; he is buried here.
EDCS-24500446.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Octavia Successa, erected by her husband Caius Strabonius and their sons, noting her age and burial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500447.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Volceius Ianuarius lived 95 years. We did what you commanded.
EDCS-24500447.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Volceius Ianuarius at age 95 and includes a personal message from the deceased, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500448.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to record a payment or debt settlement involving a woman named Cervia, with the context and precise meaning unclear due to damage, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24500449.translation: To the memory of the saints Silvanus, Primus, Donatus, Tunninus, Felix, Luccatis, and Iahinis.
EDCS-24500449.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the memory of several saints, likely from the later Roman period.

EDCS-24500450.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Donatus, dutiful, lived 75 years, is buried here.
EDCS-24500450.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Donatus, recording his age and burial, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24500451.translation: Cota Baliahonis, to Bona Fortuna. Iulia Fortunata Namgidde lived 60 years, is buried here.
EDCS-24500451.summary: This funerary epitaph records the burial of Iulia Fortunata Namgidde, likely from a provincial context.

EDCS-24500452.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Faonius F. T. (his freedman?) wrote this at my command. Ars Felix, son of L. N. Siivius, lived for [age missing]. Fortunata [details missing].
EDCS-24500452.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription that mentions the deceased Ars Felix and the person who commissioned the inscription, Marcus Faonius.

EDCS-24500453.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Tertia Geminia lived 81 years; her husband made this.
EDCS-24500453.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tertia Geminia, recording her age and that the monument was erected by her husband.

EDCS-24500454.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Ialeia Aurelia lived 12 years; her bones were laid here.
EDCS-24500454.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ialeia Aurelia, recording her young age and the deposit of her remains.

EDCS-24500455.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iunia Saturnina lived 80 years; she is buried here. [Erected by] d...
EDCS-24500455.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Iunia Saturnina, recording her age and burial, with an incomplete indication of the dedicator.

EDCS-24500456.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Maton Chelia lived 80 years. Baliao, his heir, made this for his excellent and dutiful father.
EDCS-24500456.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Maton Chelia at age 80 and was erected by his heir, Baliao, for his father.

EDCS-24500457.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Mustius Numida; his sons, his heirs, made this for their excellent and dutiful father, deserving of honor, who lived 42 years.
EDCS-24500457.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Mustius Numida at age 42 and was erected by his sons and heirs.

EDCS-24500458.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Stator. Lucius Septimius Carchedon fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-24500458.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a votive offering to Jupiter Stator by Lucius Septimius Carchedon.

EDCS-24500459.translation: Dedicated to Saturn Augustus, Titus Salonius Saturninus and his sons Volussius and V, and Publius, priests of the flocks, fulfilled their vow with willing heart.
EDCS-24500459.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by priests of Saturn for the flocks, dedicated to Saturn Augustus.

EDCS-24500460.translation: Lucius Caecilius Novellus made this by order.
EDCS-24500460.summary: This inscription records an individual, Lucius Caecilius Novellus, who completed a work by imperial command.

EDCS-24500461.translation: For the health of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Pius Felix, they made (this) to the god Frugiferus.
EDCS-24500461.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the god Frugiferus for the health of Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-24500463.translation: Antoninus Augustus Pius, for the honour of the perpetual priesthood, he built the curia from the ground up with his own money for Larum Caesarum.
EDCS-24500463.summary: This inscription records the construction of a curia by Antoninus Pius for the honor of the perpetual priesthood.

EDCS-24500464.translation: For our Emperors Lords Publius Licinius Valerianus and Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus, the Pious Fortunate Augusti, and for Publius Cornelius Licinius Valerianus, the most noble Caesar, and for Cornelia Salonina Augusta, the republic of the municipality of Masculitana (dedicated this).
EDCS-24500464.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the municipality of Masculitana to the emperors Valerian and Gallienus, their son Valerianus Caesar, and Empress Salonina.

EDCS-24500467.summary: This inscription is a short dedication or acclamation to an emperor referred to as Pius Felix Invictus.

EDCS-24500471.translation: To the prosperous tide(s) Sconia, happily.
EDCS-24500471.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or wish for good fortune related to tides and a place named Sconia.

EDCS-24500473.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Annia, likely wishing for good fortune or a long duration.

EDCS-24500474.summary: This inscription identifies Itus Aelius Heunus as a courier.

EDCS-24500475.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelius Valens lived 30 years. Ieius, his dearest wife, made this.
EDCS-24500475.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Valens, who lived 30 years, dedicated by his wife Ieius.

EDCS-24500476.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Ingenua lived 6 years. Her mother made this.
EDCS-24500476.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Ingenua, erected by her mother.

EDCS-24500477.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Chilia Pia lived 2 years, 2 months, and 24 days. Eutuches made this.
EDCS-24500477.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Chilia Pia, erected by Eutuches.

EDCS-24500478.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Volusia, recording her lifespan.

EDCS-24500479.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Herennia Veneriosa lived 55 years.
EDCS-24500479.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Herennia Veneriosa, recording her lifespan.

EDCS-24500480.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Hilarus Augustus lived 40 years.
EDCS-24500480.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Hilarus Augustus, recording his lifespan.

EDCS-24500481.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Julius Maximus lived 36 years.
EDCS-24500481.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julius Maximus, recording his lifespan.

EDCS-24500482.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Julius Victor lived [...] years.
EDCS-24500482.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julius Victor, recording his lifespan, though the exact number of years is lost.

EDCS-24500483.translation: [...] lived 1 year. Iulia Fortunata, his sister and heir, made this.
EDCS-24500483.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an unknown individual, erected by their sister and heir, Iulia Fortunata.

EDCS-24500484.translation: To Iulia Successa, who lived 55 years, her sons erected this for their mother.
EDCS-24500484.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulia Successa, erected by her sons.

EDCS-24500485.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Fucceia Maxima.
EDCS-24500485.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Fucceia Maxima, likely a tombstone inscription.

EDCS-24500486.translation: Decimus Muthunus lived for 100 years. Sextilia Spica made this for her husband.
EDCS-24500486.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Decimus Muthunus, erected by his wife Sextilia Spica, indicating a lifespan of 100 years.

EDCS-24500487.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Sextilius Saturninus, dutiful, lived for 75 years.
EDCS-24500487.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Sextilius Saturninus, who lived to be 75 years old.

EDCS-24500488.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Sittius Priscus the Younger lived for...
EDCS-24500488.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Lucius Sittius Priscus the Younger, recording his name and status as deceased.

EDCS-24500489.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Marcus Statilius Communus, dutiful, lived for 80 years. Neptunalis made this monument with his own money; he is buried here.
EDCS-24500489.summary: This funerary inscription records that Marcus Statilius Communus lived to 80 and that Neptunalis erected this monument for him at his own expense, and is also buried here.

EDCS-24500490.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gia Tertulia, dutiful, lived for 85 years. Her son, her heir, made this here; she is buried here.
EDCS-24500490.summary: This funerary inscription states that Gia Tertulia lived to 85 and was buried here, with the monument erected by her son and heir.

EDCS-24500491.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Valerius Rufinus lived for 75 years. Quintus Valerius, his son, made this.
EDCS-24500491.summary: This funerary inscription records that Quintus Valerius Rufinus lived to 75 and was commemorated by his son, Quintus Valerius.

EDCS-24500492.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Vibius Donatus, dutiful, lived for 30 years. Vibius Felix, his father, made this monument; he is buried here.
EDCS-24500492.summary: This funerary inscription states that Vibius Donatus lived to 30 and was buried here, with the monument erected by his father, Vibius Felix.

EDCS-24500493.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Vibius Felix lived for 14 years. Vibius Felix, his father, made this monument; he is buried here.
EDCS-24500493.summary: This funerary inscription records that Vibius Felix lived to 14 and was buried here, with the monument erected by his father, also named Vibius Felix.

EDCS-24500494.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Victoria Pia, mother of Auctus, lived for 85 years.
EDCS-24500494.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Victoria Pia, mother of Auctus, who lived to be 85 years old.

EDCS-24500495.translation: In memory of Neiminus. To his most rare wife, Limiilmenia.
EDCS-24500495.summary: This is a votive or funerary inscription dedicating a monument 'in memory' to Neiminus and 'to his most rare wife' Limiilmenia.

EDCS-24500497.summary: This is a dedication by a devoted individual, likely on a monument or object.

EDCS-24500500.summary: This inscription records the name and title of Vitalis, a bishop.

EDCS-24500501.translation: Leontia the faithful rested in peace.
EDCS-24500501.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Leontia, recording her faithfulness and peaceful repose.

EDCS-24501342.translation: Aminius Sabinianus of the 7th Claudian Legion, citizens of Saltu, made and dedicated [this].
EDCS-24501342.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Aminius Sabinianus and fellow citizens of Saltu, soldiers of the 7th Claudian Legion.

EDCS-24501360.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Cristia Ianuaria lived for 58 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24501360.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cristia Ianuaria, recording her lifespan and burial place.

EDCS-24501361.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Iulia Victoria lived for 62 years. Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Annius Saturninus lived for 65 years.
EDCS-24501361.summary: This inscription records the lifespans of two individuals, Iulia Victoria and Marcus Annius Saturninus, dedicated to the spirits of the underworld.

EDCS-24501938.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Primitivus lived for 100 years. Netunus lived for 110 years.
EDCS-24501938.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespans of Primitivus and Netunus, dedicated to the spirits of the underworld.

EDCS-24700571.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caius Nunnius Cetulicus lived for 60 years.
EDCS-24700571.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Caius Nunnius Cetulicus, dedicated to the spirits of the underworld.

EDCS-24700650.translation: Sacred to the Great Mother of the Gods, the Idaean [Cybele], and Attis. Caius Clodius Felix, quaestor and aedile, requested from the council of Assunum what was granted, and with Caecilia, his mother, and Poenia, his wife, by his own money, placed [this] with his brother.
EDCS-24700650.summary: This is a religious dedication by Caius Clodius Felix, an official, to the Great Mother of the Gods and Attis, funded by himself and placed with family members.

EDCS-24700651.translation: Sacred to Neptune Augustus. Otacilial's altar, his son... [placed it].
EDCS-24700651.summary: This is a religious dedication to Neptune Augustus, likely an altar placed by Otacilial and his son.

EDCS-24700654.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Lucius Caelius M.
EDCS-24700654.summary: A dedication to Saturn Augustus by Lucius Caelius M., likely an altar inscription.

EDCS-24700655.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Publius Furius Saturninus, the priest.
EDCS-24700655.summary: A dedication to Saturn Augustus by the priest Publius Furius Saturninus, likely an altar inscription.

EDCS-24700656.translation: Sacred to Saturn Augustus. Postimius Pudens, the priest.
EDCS-24700656.summary: A dedication to Saturn Augustus by the priest Postimius Pudens, likely an altar inscription.

EDCS-24700657.translation: Sempronius Saturninus willingly paid his vow to Saturn Augustus.
EDCS-24700657.summary: A votive inscription to Saturn Augustus by Sempronius Saturninus, indicating the fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-24700659.translation: Gaius Julius Baric, priest; Gaius Julius Victor, priest; and Gaius Julius Optatus, priest.
EDCS-24700659.summary: An inscription listing three priests: Gaius Julius Baric, Gaius Julius Victor, and Gaius Julius Optatus.

EDCS-24700660.translation: ..., Gaius Laelius Bon...; Laelia Fausta had vowed, upon completion.
EDCS-24700660.summary: A partial inscription mentioning a vow made by Laelia Fausta and initiated by Gaius Laelius Bon, likely related to a building or monument.

EDCS-24700789.translation: To the Prince of the Youth, the Mother of the Augusti and of the Camps, dedicated by Quintus Anicius Faustus, clarissimus vir, legate of the Augusti, pro praetore, consul, by the veterans of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion who began their service under Gnaeus Claudius Severus II and Tiberius Claudius Pompeianus II, consuls. [listing numerous names of veterans and their locations/ranks].
EDCS-24700789.summary: A large inscription commemorating veterans of the III Augusta Pia Vindex legion, dedicated under the legateship of Quintus Anicius Faustus during the consulship of Claudius Severus II and Claudius Pompeianus II, likely honoring their service to the imperial family and the army.

EDCS-24700803.translation: The optio and soldiers of [---], detained by the hope of succession. Nepos, detained by the hope of succession. Saturninus of Hadrumetum, equestrian. Solva. Sarapio of the camp. [...] Rogatus of Karthago. [...] Maximus of Theveste, standard-bearer. [...] Lucanus of Karthago. Aelius Arruntianus of Hadrumetum. Domitius Saturninus of Theveste. Aelius Bitus of Napoca. Aelius Sarapio of the camp, armourer. Publius Aelius Sotas of the camp. Lucius Licinius Maximus of Napoca. Titus Flavius Tarsa of Napoca. Publius Aelius Seranus of the camp, standard-bearer. Marcus Antonius Donatus of Thysdro. Titus Aelius Soter. Aelius Mucatra. Marcus Titisenius Saturninus. Publius Aelius S. Publius Aelius. Publius Aelius. Marcus Ho. Publius Aelius. Publius Aelius. Publius Aelius. Lucius Valerius. Publius Aelius. Publius Aelius. Publius Aelius. Publius Nonius. Publius Aelius. Marcus Publius I of the camp. [...] of Napoca, trumpeter. [...] toclus. Maximus of Lepcis, [---]. [...] of Napoca II, of the camp. [...] of Napoca. [...] of Napoca. [...] of Napoca. [...] cornicularius. Fuscus of Theveste. [...] of Napoca. [...] of Napoca. [...]. Caius Lucius. Publius Ma. Caius Fullonius. Lucius Aemilius. Publius Aelius. Publius Aelius. Ti Publius Aelius Marcellus IV. Publius Aelius Serenus of Theveste. Publius Aelius Dassius of the camp. Publius Aelius Iunio of Napoca. Publius Aelius Didymus of the camp. Publius Aelius Peregrinus of Hadrumetum, armourer. Publius Aelius Theodorus of the camp. Publius Aelius Maximus of the camp. Publius Aelius Serapio of the camp. Publius Aelius Nigrinus of Napoca. Caius Julius Diodorus of the camp. Publius Aelius Donatus of Napoca, trumpeter. [...]cius Martialis of Karthago. Alexander of the camp. [...] polio. Clemens of Napoca. Eutrophus of Napoca. [...]ilius of Napoca, standard-bearer. Placentus of Napoca. Victimarius Ingenus of Karthago. Aporus of the camp. Pudens of Emona. Severus of Naraggara. Rufinus of Thelepti. Ampliatus of Karthago. Rufus Ianuarius. Leonides. Dioscorus of the camp, standard-bearer. Germanus of the camp. Diodorus of the camp. [...]us of Napoca, aquilifer. S Lucianus of Lamabsa. D [...] of the camp. IVI
EDCS-24700803.summary: A lengthy inscription listing soldiers and officers, likely from a military unit, with details about their roles, locations, and possibly their status or experiences, possibly related to the administration or logistics of the Roman army.

EDCS-24700805.summary: A dedication to the personified goddess Disciplina, likely an altar erected by soldiers or officers.

EDCS-24700806.translation: To Fortuna Redux Augusta (the Returning Fortune).
EDCS-24700806.summary: A dedication to Fortuna Redux, an aspect of the goddess Fortuna associated with safe return, likely an altar or votive offering.

EDCS-24700807.translation: To the genius of the chief clerk and to Augusta Minerva.
EDCS-24700807.summary: This is a dedication to the genius of a chief clerk and to Augusta Minerva.

EDCS-24700809.translation: Cascellius Optatus willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-24700809.summary: This inscription records Cascellius Optatus fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-24700810.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the second time, imperator for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-24700810.summary: This is a titulature of Emperor Antoninus Pius, listing his official titles and honors, likely from an imperial dedication.

EDCS-24701149.translation: Sacred to Mars and to the August Pegas(us).
EDCS-24701149.summary: This inscription is a sacred dedication to the gods Mars and Pegas(us).

EDCS-24701188.summary: This is a personal name, likely an owner or dedicatee.

EDCS-24701209.summary: This inscription refers to the Salloniani and a statue or dedication to Janus Parthicus.

EDCS-24701210.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or memorial to a person named Ladius, possibly a husband.

EDCS-24701211.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription beginning with the titles of an Emperor.

EDCS-24701219.translation: Soszicus Iulianus Evassianus Dulcitius Re ZIBIR Ierditenu Evessanius Licentiarus Processaron Massamai Marius Duzatus Rusticus Mas
EDCS-24701219.summary: This inscription is a long list of names, likely belonging to a group or collegium.

EDCS-24701220.translation: Sacred to the Dis Manibus. Lucius Granius Rogatianus, a decurion, served in the cohort for 14 years, lived for 32 years. Granius Felix, his brother, made this.
EDCS-24701220.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Granius Rogatianus, a decurion who served for 14 years and lived for 32 years, dedicated by his brother.

EDCS-24701232.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Edinius Restutus, freedman of Octavius, lived 50 years.
EDCS-24701232.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Edinius Restutus, who lived 50 years.

EDCS-24701238.translation: To Martura Salva, Fortune. He/She lived...
EDCS-24701238.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Martura Salva, possibly invoking good fortune.

EDCS-24701240.translation: Dedicated to Neptune Augustus. Quintus Vettius Iustus, centurion of the III Augusta legion, commander of the Palmyrene cohort.
EDCS-24701240.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Neptune Augustus by Quintus Vettius Iustus, a military officer, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24701242.translation: For Gargilius Maximus, veteran. Maximosa and their sons made this for their husband. He lived 53 years.
EDCS-24701242.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a wife and sons for Gargilius Maximus, a veteran, who lived 53 years.

EDCS-24701243.translation: Gargilius Sextianus lived 14 years. Gargilia Mosa lived 2 years. Mucia Maximosa, mother, and the brothers Gargilius, made this.
EDCS-24701243.summary: This epitaph records the deaths of two young children, Gargilius Sextianus and Gargilia Mosa, with their mother and brothers as dedicants.

EDCS-24701245.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Caius Iulius Victor lived 50 years. Memmii Valerianus and Crescens [made this] for their most deserving father.
EDCS-24701245.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Caius Iulius Victor, who lived 50 years, and was commemorated by his sons.

EDCS-24701246.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Memmia Pinira lived 40 years. Memmii Valerianus and Crescens [made this] for their most deserving sister.
EDCS-24701246.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Memmia Pinira, who lived 40 years, and was commemorated by her brothers.

EDCS-24701247.translation: Macrobiius Secundus set up and dedicated this altar to the Holy God Malagbel, fulfilling his vow willingly with his heart.
EDCS-24701247.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to the Syrian deity Malagbel by Macrobiius Secundus, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-24701250.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Au CES A S served in the IX legion, lived 20 years. Flavia MonnVulaI [made this] for her deserving husband.
EDCS-24701250.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a soldier of the IX legion, who lived 20 years and was commemorated by his wife.

EDCS-24701251.translation: [He served in the] XXVI legion. He lived 25 years.
EDCS-24701251.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of a soldier who lived 25 years and served in the XXVI legion.

EDCS-24701252.translation: Geta lived 70 years. Fortunata placed this for her dearest mother.
EDCS-24701252.summary: A funerary epitaph for Geta, set up by her mother Fortunata, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24701253.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulius Seleucus lived 50 years. Antonia TONTANRHC made this for her deserving one.
EDCS-24701253.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulius Seleucus, dedicated by Antonia TONTANRHC, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24701254.translation: Publius Terentius lived. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-24701254.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Terentius, invoking a common Latin sentiment for the deceased, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24701255.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. NOR Hector N H.
EDCS-24701255.summary: A funerary dedication to the 'Dis Manibus' ('spirits of the departed'), mentioning Hector, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24701256.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Liontilla lived 30 years. Lucius... 20 years. ...
EDCS-24701256.summary: A funerary inscription for Liontilla and Lucius, dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus', dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24701263.translation: Aruntia Saturnina lived 30 years. Luus Lelita placed this for his dearest wife. She lies here.
EDCS-24701263.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aruntia Saturnina, erected by her husband Luus Lelita, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24701351.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sallustia Hygia lived 25 years. Sallustia, daughter of Quintus, made this for her deserving daughter. She lies here.
EDCS-24701351.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sallustia Hygia, dedicated by her mother Sallustia, daughter of Quintus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24701354.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Titus Flavius Potens and Mundicia Quieta, his grandson.
EDCS-24701354.summary: A funerary dedication to the 'Dis Manibus', naming Titus Flavius Potens and his grandson Mundicia Quieta, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24701355.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Munius Cittinus lived 73 years. His heir and husband made this.
EDCS-24701355.summary: A funerary epitaph for Munius Cittinus, erected by his heir and husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24701358.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aelia Ara made this for herself while alive.
EDCS-24701358.summary: A funerary epitaph commissioned by Aelia Ara for herself during her lifetime, dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus', Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24701359.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Claudia Venusta lived for 21 years. Caius Iulius Saturninus, a soldier of the Third Legion Augusta, made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-24701359.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudia Venusta, set up by her husband, a soldier of the Third Legion Augusta.

EDCS-24701360.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Caius Iulius Mirinas, dutiful, lived.
EDCS-24701360.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Iulius Mirinas, likely incomplete, indicating his piety and life.

EDCS-24701369.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Sabinius Felix, a veteran, lived for 68 years. His sons made this for their deserving father.
EDCS-24701369.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sabinius Felix, a veteran, erected by his sons.

EDCS-24701370.translation: From the workshop of Hai and Deunis.
EDCS-24701370.summary: This inscription indicates the origin of a product from the workshop of Hai and Deunis.

EDCS-24701371.translation: A us anus avius D II an
EDCS-24701371.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to provide a meaningful translation or summary.

EDCS-24800678.translation: To Fortuna Redux Augusta, Lucceia Euthycia willingly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-24800678.summary: This is a votive inscription to Fortuna Redux Augusta, dedicated by Lucceia Euthycia.

EDCS-24800679.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, Tiberius Memmius Ulpianus, son of Titus, of the Palatina tribe, from Rome, prefect of the Second Cohort of Roman Citizens (equestrian), Piae Fidelis, tribune of the soldiers of the Third Legion Augusta, along with his wife Veratia Athenaide and his daughter Memmia Macrina, willingly fulfilled their vow with merit.
EDCS-24800679.summary: This is a complex votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Dolichenus by Tiberius Memmius Ulpianus, detailing his military and equestrian career, along with his family.

EDCS-24800680.translation: Sacred to Hygeia Augusta, whom the state of the Gemellenses dedicated in recognition of outstanding friendship, having obtained a single grant in Latium. Fortis and Exoratus, sons of Sextus Iulius Felix, a toga-clad official and curator of the state, and Tertius I, Julius Donatus I, V, D., undertook its repair.
EDCS-24800680.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a sanctuary to Hygeia Augusta by the state of Gemellenses, honoring a friendship, and notes the repair efforts by the sons of a local official and another individual.

EDCS-24800681.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Prince of the Gods, Governor of all things, Ruler of heaven and earth, for the glory regained from barbarian tribes, Flavius Leontius, a most accomplished man and general throughout Africa, has erected this.
EDCS-24800681.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Flavius Leontius, a high-ranking official and general in Africa, celebrating a victory over barbarian tribes.

EDCS-24800682.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus.
EDCS-24800682.summary: This is a simple dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus.

EDCS-24800683.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Marcus Aurelius Iustus, prefect of the Third Legion Augusta, together with his wife Claudia Maximilla and his children Aurelia Maximina, Aurelius Iustus, Aurelia Iustina, and Aurelius Iustianus, together with all his family, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-24800683.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by a military prefect and his family, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24800684.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Valerius Rufus, centurion of the Third Legion Augusta, willingly and joyfully fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-24800684.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Dolichenus by a centurion of the Third Legion Augusta, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24800685.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the public market, Sabinius Ingenuus and Aurelius Sedatus, standard-bearers of the Third Legion Augusta, who are managing the market, together with their helpers, willingly fulfilled their vow with their hearts.
EDCS-24800685.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by two standard-bearers managing the market, possibly from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24800687.translation: For the welfare of the Augustan Genius of Lambaesis, the republic arranged for a temple to be built at the expense of 600,000 sesterces, as entrusted by Marcus Sadius Rufus, of honored memory, perpetual priest and advocate.
EDCS-24800687.summary: A public dedication of a temple to the Genius of Lambaesis, funded by the will of a prominent local official, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24800690.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar and the divine household, to Mars, Minerva, Fortuna, Victoria, and Diana, Titus Atarenus Prudens, princeps of the Third Legion Augusta, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-24800690.summary: A dedication to various deities for the emperor's well-being by a soldier of the Third Legion Augusta, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24800694.translation: To the unconquered god Sol Mithras, Decimus, a most distinguished man and president of the province of Numidia, fulfilled his vow appropriately and successfully.
EDCS-24800694.summary: A votive inscription to Sol Mithras by the provincial governor of Numidia, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24800696.summary: A dedication to an unspecified deity by a priest named Cerficius Honoratus, likely from the Roman Empire.

EDCS-24800697.summary: A dedication to the god Silvanus, possibly from a private individual or a group.

EDCS-24800698.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus, by the centurions of the Third Legion Augusta, under the supervision of Memmius Donatus, the decurion.
EDCS-24800698.summary: A dedication to Silvanus Augustus by centurions of the Third Legion Augusta, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24800699.translation: To Divine Victory, companion of the Augusti, the republic of the colony of Lambaesis.
EDCS-24800699.summary: A dedication to Victoria by the municipal government of Lambaesis, possibly from the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-24800700.translation: To Victoria Augusta, Lucius Aemilius Fortis, who held the office of duumvir, dedicated and paid for this out of 5,000 sesterces, as he had promised in the public records, because the city council and his fellow citizens conferred that office upon him, with Lucius Aemilius Simbolius Germanus Pelusius, Purpurius Fortunius, and his children overseeing the work.
EDCS-24800700.summary: This is a dedication by a local magistrate in a Roman colony, likely a public building or monument, commemorating his assumption of office.

EDCS-24800703.translation: Fulfilled vow to Saturn. Sacred offering.
EDCS-24800703.summary: This is a simple votive inscription to the god Saturn.

EDCS-24800704.translation: Sacred offering to the Divine Manes. Verivetius Veturianus, a veteran of the Legio III Augusta, placed this according to his vow.
EDCS-24800704.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the deceased by a veteran of the Roman army.

EDCS-24800707.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Father of the Fatherland, in his eleventh year of tribunician power, his third consulship, and proconsulship, by the staff of the military treasury.
EDCS-24800707.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated to Emperor Septimius Severus, likely on a building or monument related to the military administration.

EDCS-24800709.translation: For the health and safety of our lords Severus and Antoninus Pius Felix Augusti, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Augusti and the camp, Tiberius Claudius Subatianus Proculus, imperial legate and designated consul, adorned the temple of the god with mosaic pavements and the walls with marble.
EDCS-24800709.summary: This is a dedication of a temple, possibly to an imperial deity or the imperial family, by a high-ranking Roman official during the Severan period.

EDCS-24800710.translation: To Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, mother of the Emperor and Caesar and the camp, whose names are subscribed: Gaius Cassius Pudens, Marcus Galerius Silvanus, Helvidius Rogatianus.
EDCS-24800710.summary: This inscription lists the names of individuals, likely officials or benefactors, associated with honors or dedications to the Severan imperial family.

EDCS-24800712.translation: To the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Geta Parthicus Britannicus Germanicus Maximus Augusti, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor and the camp, dedicated by Quintus Anicius Faustus.
EDCS-24800712.summary: This is a dedication to multiple members of the Severan imperial family, including the sons of Septimius Severus, by an imperial legate.

EDCS-24800713.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, designated Emperor, son of Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Augustus Pius, propagator of the empire, dedicated by Quintus Anicius Faustus, imperial legate with proconsular power and patron of the municipality, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-24800713.summary: This is a dedication to the future Emperor Caracalla, made by a provincial governor and patron of the town, funded by the local council.

EDCS-24800714.translation: Under the consulship of the Emperors Caesars Marcus Aurelius Caro Pius Felix Augustus (for the second time) and Marcus Aurelius Carinus.
EDCS-24800714.summary: This is a consular dating inscription, likely the header for a larger monument or document, from the reign of the co-emperors Carus and Carinus.

EDCS-24800716.translation: To our most provident and triumphant lord, the perpetual Emperor Galerius Valerius Maximianus Iunior Constantis Caesar, invincible, pious, fortunate, victorious, who restored liberty to the world, the republic of the colony of Lambaesis.
EDCS-24800716.summary: This inscription, likely from a public building, records a dedication by the colony of Lambaesis to Emperor Galerius Maximian, celebrating his victory and the restoration of liberty.

EDCS-24800726.translation: To Lucius Julius Apronius Maenius Pius Salamallianus, tribunus laticlavius of the tenth legion Gemina, enrolled among the quaestorian officials, prefect of the Acts of the Senate, curule aedile, praetor, legate of Augustus with the power of five fasces of the province of Belgica, legate of the first legion Adiutrix, legate of Augustus pro praetore of the province of Galatia, likewise legate of Augustus pro praetore, vir clarissimus.
EDCS-24800726.summary: This is a career CIL V, 1066 listing for Lucius Julius Apronius Maenius Pius Salamallianus, a prominent Roman official.

EDCS-24800727.translation: To Lucius Julius Apronius Maenius Salamalladius, most illustrious girl, and to Maenius Avitianus, most illustrious boy, and to Maenius Pius, most illustrious boy, and to Alfena Agrippina, most illustrious girl, their children, their patrons.
EDCS-24800727.summary: This is an epitaph dedicated by patrons to their deceased children, Lucius Julius Apronius Maenius Salamalladius, Maenius Avitianus, Maenius Pius, and Alfena Agrippina.

EDCS-24900462.translation: Crescens, son of [---], a Roman equestrian, from Tiniste.
EDCS-24900462.summary: This is an inscription identifying Crescens, a Roman equestrian from Tiniste.

EDCS-24900463.summary: This is a dedication to the emperors Diocletian and Maximian.

EDCS-24900464.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the departed. Allia Saturnina, dutiful, lived 21 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900464.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Allia Saturnina, recording her age and burial place.

EDCS-24900465.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to be the name of Quintus Clodius C[---].

EDCS-24900466.translation: Aulus Cossinius Saturninus, dutiful, lived 60 years; he is buried here. Cossinia Secunda, dutiful, lived 15 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900466.summary: This is a double funerary epitaph for Aulus Cossinius Saturninus and Cossinia Secunda, recording their ages and burial places.

EDCS-24900467.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the departed. Marcus Gabinius Felix, son of Lucius, lived [---] year. Attia Concessa, his most dutiful mother, made this.
EDCS-24900467.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Gabinius Felix, made by his mother Attia Concessa.

EDCS-24900468.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the departed. Gabinia Semperusa lived 90 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-24900468.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gabinia Semperusa, recording her age and burial place.

EDCS-24900469.translation: Publius Granius Felix lived 60 years. Valeria Rogata lived 85 years.
EDCS-24900469.summary: This inscription records the lifespans of Publius Granius Felix and Valeria Rogata.

EDCS-24900470.translation: To the divine spirits. Flavia Dailua, wife, lived [X] years. Gaius Iulius Proculus lived [X] years. Titus Flavius Saturninus made this for his daughters and sons-in-law.
EDCS-24900470.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Gaius Iulius Proculus and Flavia Dailua, and erected by their son-in-law Titus Flavius Saturninus.

EDCS-24900471.translation: To the divine spirits. Quintus Morius lived 31 years. His wife Quintus M. Urbanus lived 7 years. Quintus M. lived 4 years.
EDCS-24900471.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Morius, his wife, and a child, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-24900472.summary: This is a brief funerary epitaph for Retia Saturnina, indicating her age and burial location.

EDCS-24900473.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Ofellia Matrona.

EDCS-24900475.translation: To the divine spirits. Sabinius, son of Quintus, of the Papiria tribe, pious, lived 12 years, is buried here.
EDCS-24900475.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy named Sabinius, dedicated to the divine spirits and indicating his tribal affiliation and lifespan.

EDCS-24900476.translation: To the divine spirits. Sextilius Ianuarius lived 55 years. His wife Fabia Victoria made this; she lived [X] years.
EDCS-24900476.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sextilius Ianuarius, made by his wife Fabia Victoria, who also provides her own lifespan.

EDCS-24900479.translation: [X], son of C.R., 2, pious, is buried here.
EDCS-24900479.summary: This is a very abbreviated funerary epitaph for a child, possibly a son of C.R., indicating their age and burial.

EDCS-24900585.translation: To the god and divine power Malagbel. For the safety of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Magnus Antoninus. The centuria of Honoratus. Amicus Iulius Donatus, son of Ranius Messor Petronius Rogatianus, Titus Annius Victor, Quintus Granius Donatus, Gaius Iulius Tertullus, soldiers of the Palmyrene Severian cohort, centuria of Caltonianus. Gaius Modius, the centurion written above, Lucius Rubrius Felix, Gaius Iulius Maximus, Gaius Cannius Malchus, Lucius Hostilianus Florentinus, Gaius Iulius Tonneus, Gaius Iulius T.
EDCS-24900585.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Malagbel for the emperor's safety, mentioning soldiers from the Palmyrene Severian cohort and their centurion, likely dating to the reign of Severus Alexander.

EDCS-24900589.translation: [X] Antonino AE, mother of the Augusti of the camp. [X] Caius Iulius Processus, Marcus Dixonius Faustus, centuria of Iucundus. Caius Iulius Maximus, Caius Iulius Etruscanus, Marcus Aemilius Victor, Lucius Valerius Processus.
EDCS-24900589.summary: This inscription lists several individuals, possibly soldiers or associated personnel, within a military context, mentioning a 'mother of the Augusti of the camp,' possibly dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-24900590.translation: Lucius Staius, Lucius Furius Gudus, Marcus Sextilius Peqa, Caius Pontilius II, F. Furius Victor, Caius Caius C.
EDCS-24900590.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription listing several names, possibly of soldiers or individuals associated with a military unit, with an incomplete closing.

EDCS-24900592.summary: This inscription records the name and filiation of a Roman individual.

EDCS-24900595.translation: Donatus of the Reitani tribe, from Elabesa.
EDCS-24900595.summary: This inscription records the name and tribal affiliation of a Roman individual.

EDCS-25001007.translation: Valerius Germanus, Emeritus, and Honoratus, his brothers.
EDCS-25001007.summary: This inscription lists the names of three brothers, likely as part of a dedication or funerary monument.

EDCS-25001015.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Atilius Emeritus lived for 60 years.
EDCS-25001015.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name and age at death of Lucius Atilius Emeritus.

EDCS-25100354.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, pontifex maximus, princeps iuventutis, son of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Parthicus Arabicus Adiabenicus, pontifex maximus, by decree of the decurions and with public money, the pagus Trispensis [dedicates this].
EDCS-25100354.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the pagus Trispensis to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, likely during his reign (177-192 AD).

EDCS-25500084.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Aelius Victor, standard-bearer of the III Augusta legion, lived for 40 years. Aelius Felix made this for his dearest father.
EDCS-25500084.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aelius Felix for his father, Lucius Aelius Victor, a standard-bearer in the III Augusta legion.

EDCS-25500085.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. To Publius Aemilius Pudens, quaestor of the Iulia Monnula legion, his most loving husband.
EDCS-25500085.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Publius Aemilius Pudens, a quaestor of the Iulia Monnula legion, by his husband.

EDCS-25500086.translation: If [this is] sacred to the spirits of the departed. Ananius Vitalis of the third Augusta legion.
EDCS-25500086.summary: This inscription records the name of Ananius Vitalis, a soldier of the third Augusta legion, possibly as part of a funerary dedication.

EDCS-25500087.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Arruntius Felix lived for 40 years. Cupula Arruntius Felix made this for his brother, who is also his heir.
EDCS-25500087.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Arruntius Felix, who lived for 40 years, and was commemorated by his brother and heir.

EDCS-25500088.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aufidius Faustus, centurion of the III legion, who lived for 47 years, 6 months, and 18 days. Iulia Patrofila [dedicated this] to her husband.
EDCS-25500088.summary: This funerary inscription records the death details of Marcus Aufidius Faustus, a centurion in the III legion, and states it was erected by his wife Iulia Patrofila.

EDCS-25500090.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Aurelius Fortunatus, soldier of the Third Augustan Legion.
EDCS-25500090.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Aurelius Fortunatus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion.

EDCS-25500091.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Nigrinus, soldier of the province of Moesia, of Memesis Superior, with five years of service, lived for 20 years. Aurelius Ursinus set this up for his well-deserving brother.
EDCS-25500091.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Nigrinus, a soldier of Moesia, set up by his brother, dating to the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-25500093.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Fabius Aranius made this for Marcus Caecilius Primus, optio of the Third Augustan Legion, who lived for 32 years.
EDCS-25500093.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Caecilius Primus, an optio of the Third Augustan Legion, made by Fabius Aranius.

EDCS-25500094.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Caelius Rufus, from Hadrumetum, a beneficiarius of the prefect of the Third Augustan Legion, lived for 37 years. Ofellia Procula, his most devoted wife, made this; he is buried here.
EDCS-25500094.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Caelius Rufus, a beneficiarius of the Third Augustan Legion, made by his wife Ofellia Procula.

EDCS-25500095.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Caesennius Alexander, veteran, formerly a signifer, lived for 60 years.
EDCS-25500095.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Caesennius Alexander, a veteran and former signifer.

EDCS-25500096.translation: Soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, of the century of Claudius Priscus, primus pilus, dutiful, lived for [X] years.
EDCS-25500096.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription for a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion serving under Claudius Priscus.

EDCS-25500097.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Domitius Saturninus, soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, lived for 35 years. Lucius Domitius Saturninus, a soldier of the same legion, and his heir, made this; he is buried here.
EDCS-25500097.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Domitius Saturninus, a soldier of the Third Augustan Legion, also set up by himself as heir.

EDCS-25500098.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caelia Procula made this for Gaius Domitius Secundus, veteran, who lived for 80 years, for her deserving husband, for 2000 sesterces.
EDCS-25500098.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Domitius Secundus, a veteran, paid for by his wife Caelia Procula.

EDCS-25500099.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Fabius Dadatus, veteran, lived for 73 years. Iulia Iarecto made this for her well-deserving man.
EDCS-25500099.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Fabius Dadatus, a veteran, made by his wife Iulia Iarecto.

EDCS-25500101.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulius Domitianus, veteran of the Third Augustan Legion, lived for 90 years. To the spirits of the departed. Iulius SINIII, of the Third Augustan Legion, lived for 35 years.
EDCS-25500101.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the deaths of two individuals, Iulius Domitianus and Iulius SINIII, both veterans of the Third Augustan Legion.

EDCS-25500102.translation: Saturninus lived 30 years, a soldier of the Third Legion Augusta. Gaius Julius Secundus made this for his brother.
EDCS-25500102.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Saturninus, a soldier of the Third Legion Augusta, erected by his brother Gaius Julius Secundus.

EDCS-25500103.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Julius Victor, a veteran, made this for himself in his lifetime. Gaius Julius Fortunatus lived 23 years. Gaius Julius Victor lived 22 years.
EDCS-25500103.summary: This is a funerary inscription with a self-dedication by Gaius Julius Victor, a veteran, who also records the ages of two other individuals, possibly his sons.

EDCS-25500104.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Ulpia Victoriola lived 50 years. Julius Victor, a veteran, made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-25500104.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Julius Victor, a veteran, for his wife Ulpia Victoriola.

EDCS-25500105.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Julius Vitalis, a veteran, made this for his grandfather, Iulius sacerdos Saturni. He lived 100 years.
EDCS-25500105.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Julius Vitalis, a veteran, for his grandfather, Iulius sacerdos Saturni, noting his grandfather's long life.

EDCS-25500106.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Iunius Hospitalis, a veteran, made this for O.
EDCS-25500106.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Gaius Iunius Hospitalis, a veteran, dedicated to an individual identified only as 'O'.

EDCS-25500107.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Iuventius Restutus lived 70 years, a veteran.
EDCS-25500107.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iuventius Restutus, a veteran, noting his age.

EDCS-25500108.translation: To the departed spirits. Ius Maximus, standard-bearer of the Third Legion Augusta, lived 34 years.
EDCS-25500108.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ius Maximus, a standard-bearer of the Third Legion Augusta, noting his age.

EDCS-25500109.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Gaius Papirius Aelianus, ordinary physician of the Third Legion Augusta, who lived 85 years, 7 months, and 15 days. Papiria Vitalis made this for her most worthy husband.
EDCS-25500109.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Papiria Vitalis for her husband Gaius Papirius Aelianus, an ordinary physician of the Third Legion Augusta, recording his lifespan.

EDCS-25500110.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Titus Percennius Optatus, who lived 53 years. His son Pudens, a centurion, made this.
EDCS-25500110.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Pudens, a centurion, for his father Titus Percennius Optatus.

EDCS-25500111.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Salvidienus Martimis, a soldier of the Cillaliciani Legion, lived 100 years, made this for his deserving husband for 400 sesterces.
EDCS-25500111.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Salvidienus Martimis, a soldier of the Cillaliciani Legion, for his husband, noting the cost of the monument.

EDCS-25500112.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caius Satrius Crescens, centurion of the Third Augustan Legion, made this for his freedman Caius Satrius Epictetus.
EDCS-25500112.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a centurion for his freedman, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500113.translation: Secundus, a soldier, served for 25 years, lived for 40 years. Iulius Donatus [made this] for his friend.
EDCS-25500113.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a soldier, likely set up by a friend, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500114.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Septimius Iustus, patron of the Third Augustan Legion, [made this] for his freedman Septimius Secundus.
EDCS-25500114.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a patron for his freedman, who was associated with the Third Augustan Legion, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500117.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulius the veteran made this for himself while alive and for his deserving wife. He lived for...
EDCS-25500117.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a veteran for himself and his wife during his lifetime, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500118.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iulius, heir, assistant of the record office.
EDCS-25500118.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an individual identified as an heir and assistant in a record office, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500119.translation: Polychronia, who lived 22 years, 2 months, and 10 days, for her most rare husband, Saturus, assistant of our emperor's record office, and Aelius Capito...
EDCS-25500119.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Polychronia for her husband Saturus, who was an assistant in an imperial record office, and possibly also for Aelius Capito, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500120.translation: In the golden times of our divine lords Gratian, Valentinian, and Theodosius, perpetual and divine princes, not only are worn-out structures repaired, but new ones are built for the prosperity of the city. The Senate House, which our ancestors rightly wished to be called its temple, seemed to lie ruined by age, or rather by neglect, and hated. Now it is recognized as distinguished, built on the same site with new work. For it seemed that even in so splendid a city, the flow of water was lacking, which has now been constructed from new work for the use and benefit of the same city. All these things have been completed for the splendor of the most happy city under the administration of Lucius Aemilius Metellus Flavianus, most distinguished vir consularis, sexfascalis of the province of Numidia, under the supervision of Lucius Silicius Rufus, duoviral, curator of the state, at his own expense.
EDCS-25500120.summary: This is a dedication for the reconstruction of a Senate House and the construction of a water system in a city in Numidia, under the reign of Gratian, Valentinian, and Theodosius, commissioned by Lucius Aemilius Metellus Flavianus and supervised by Lucius Silicius Rufus at his own expense, likely dating to the late 4th century CE.

EDCS-25500122.translation: ...under the supervision of Publius, son of Cicero.
EDCS-25500122.summary: This is a fragmented inscription mentioning supervision by Publius, son of Cicero, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500126.translation: M. [Name] arranged for this to be set up.
EDCS-25500126.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription indicating that someone abbreviated as 'M.' arranged for a monument to be erected, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500155.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Publius Aelius as an aedile, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500156.translation: S. Aurelianus Flavius Marinus, priest; Lucius Aelius Felix, Satrius Donatus, Antonius Donatus, Aelius Donatius,lius us.
EDCS-25500156.summary: This inscription lists names, possibly of officials or members of a college, in an unclear context, potentially related to a religious or civic function.

EDCS-25500157.translation: M. flamen perpetuus,lius Crepereius, of the citizenship.
EDCS-25500157.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions a perpetual priest,lius Crepereius, and his citizenship, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-25500158.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Here lies Quintus Rassinius Senecio, perpetual priest. He lived 43 years. His most devoted wife made this.
EDCS-25500158.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus Rassinius Senecio, a perpetual priest, erected by his wife, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-25500159.translation: niae. Servaeus A., perpetual priest of the Emperor, for the state.
EDCS-25500159.summary: This inscription records Servaeus A., a perpetual priest of the Emperor, acting for the republic, likely from a public building or dedication.

EDCS-25500163.translation: Quintus Geminius Modestus, Quintus Octavius Fortunatus, Caius Iulius Crescens, Caius Magnius Victor, Lucius Iulius Crescens, Marcus Antonius Redemtus, Marcus Magnius Crescens, Caius ius Rogatus, Marcus Iulianus Pudens, ius Maximus, s Felix Venerius Victor, etrius Fortunatus, tius Etruscus, ndius Princeps, s Donatus, s Faustus.
EDCS-25500163.summary: This inscription lists numerous names, possibly donors or members of a collegium or local council, associated with an unspecified public or private project.

EDCS-25500164.translation: Aelianus Laelius Felix, Antonius Donatus, Aeleius Donatus.
EDCS-25500164.summary: This inscription simply lists three personal names, perhaps indicating membership in a group or a dedication of some sort.

EDCS-25500166.summary: This fragmentary inscription contains two names, llinius Iulius Crescens and Aurelia Ordi, possibly related to a funerary or public dedication.

EDCS-25500168.translation: Lucius Aburius Secundinus, their heir, to his dearest parents.
EDCS-25500168.summary: This epitaph records Lucius Aburius Secundinus as the heir dedicating a monument to his beloved parents.

EDCS-25500169.translation: Aelius dem, lived 15 years. Aelius P.
EDCS-25500169.summary: This is a very fragmentary epitaph recording the age of a deceased individual named Aelius, likely a child or young person.

EDCS-25500170.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aemili For, lived 12 years.
EDCS-25500170.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the Divine Shades for Aemili For, who died at the age of 12, likely an epitaph for a child.

EDCS-25500171.translation: To the divine spirits. Publius Afranius Secundus, son of Publius, of the Ulpian tribe, from Hadrumetum, lived 17 years and 28 days. He is buried here.
EDCS-25500171.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Afranius Secundus, recording his age and place of origin, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500173.translation: Sacred to the divine spirits. Quintus Annius Martialis lived 30 years.
EDCS-25500173.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Annius Martialis, stating his age, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500174.translation: Built by Antonius, son of Proculus.
EDCS-25500174.summary: A dedication or building inscription by Antonius, son of Proculus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500175.translation: Sacred to the divine spirits. For Antonia Festa, dearest daughter. Antonius December and Flavia Festa, parents, made this.
EDCS-25500175.summary: A funerary epitaph for Antonia Festa, dedicated by her parents, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500176.translation: Sacred to the divine spirits. Attia Victoria, who was also called Vincentia, lived 50 years and 1 month. Her father made this.
EDCS-25500176.summary: A funerary epitaph for Attia Victoria (also called Vincentia), stating her age and that her father erected the monument, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500178.translation: To the divine spirits. Quintus Aufidius Gallicus lived 7 years. Aufidius Gallus, his father, made this for his son.
EDCS-25500178.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Aufidius Gallicus, stating his age and that his father erected the monument, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500179.translation: To the divine spirits. Aufidia Tertia lived 29 years. Publius Herennius Donatus made this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-25500179.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aufidia Tertia, stating her age and dedicated by her husband, Publius Herennius Donatus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500180.translation: Sacred to the divine spirits. For Manna Elia, who lived X years. Quintus Avianius Emeritus made this monument for his most dutiful mother at his own expense.
EDCS-25500180.summary: A funerary epitaph for Manna Elia, stating her age and dedicated by her son Quintus Avianius Emeritus at his own expense, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500181.translation: To the divine spirits. For Caius Aurelius Amator, who lived 55 years. His dutiful wife made this.
EDCS-25500181.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Aurelius Amator, stating his age and dedicated by his wife, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500182.translation: His wife and Aurelii Castor and Vera made this for him, as he deserved.
EDCS-25500182.summary: A funerary epitaph for an unnamed man, dedicated by his wife and Aurelii Castor and Vera, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-25500183.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Lucius Aurelius Iunior lived 9 years and 7 months. Gabinia Fortunata made this for her dearest son.
EDCS-25500183.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of a young boy, Lucius Aurelius Iunior, and made by his mother, Gabinia Fortunata.

EDCS-25500184.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Aurelia Cara lived 17 years. Tyrius Donatianus placed this for his wife.
EDCS-25500184.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Aurelia Cara and erected by her husband, Tyrius Donatianus.

EDCS-25500185.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Baebius Faustinus made a tomb for his most dutiful father, Baebius Faustus.
EDCS-25500185.summary: A funerary epitaph where Baebius Faustinus commissioned a tomb for his father, Baebius Faustus.

EDCS-25500186.summary: A dedication to a freedman named Barbarus, likely on a monument or tomb.

EDCS-25500187.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription fragment recording the lifespan of an individual.

EDCS-25600016.summary: A dedication by Adeudatus, identified as a bishop.

EDCS-25600018.translation: This is an eternal house and this inscription will be a resting place for eternal memory. Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Aelia Materna made this mausoleum for herself while alive. She lived [age].
EDCS-25600018.summary: A self-funded mausoleum inscription by Aelia Materna, intended for eternal memory.

EDCS-25600020.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus. Gaius Antonius Sergianus, decurion of the cavalry.
EDCS-25600020.summary: A dedication to the god Mars Augustus by Gaius Antonius Sergianus, an officer in the cavalry.

EDCS-25600021.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus. Marcus Licinius Valens, centurion of the 7th cohort of Lusitanians, paid his vow willingly.
EDCS-25600021.summary: A votive inscription to Mars Augustus by Marcus Licinius Valens, a centurion of a Lusitanian cohort.

EDCS-25600022.translation: Iulianus, a *beneficiarius* and *exceptores*, having completed his service with all his men, paid his vow willingly.
EDCS-25600022.summary: A votive inscription by Iulianus and his colleagues, *beneficiarii* and *exceptores*, upon completion of their military service.

EDCS-25600025.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Secundus lived 71 years. His freedman and heir made this.
EDCS-25600025.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Secundus and the person who erected it.

EDCS-25600026.translation: For the Emperors Diocletian and Maximian Augustus, the state of the Cedians, the duovirs, by the generosity of Aelius Princeps, a Roman knight and perpetual flamen, and his heirs, from their own resources.
EDCS-25600026.summary: A dedication made by the state of the Cedians, likely a public building or honor, to the emperors Diocletian and Maximian.

EDCS-25600027.translation: Sacred to the safety of the Emperor Caesar Augustus. Publius Iulius Chelia, perpetual flamen, made this with his own money, and he...
EDCS-25600027.summary: A dedication made by Publius Iulius Chelia, a perpetual flamen, for the safety of the Emperor Augustus.

EDCS-25600029.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Ialeius Donatus lived 30 years. He made this for his excellent patron, a good, dutiful man.
EDCS-25600029.summary: A funerary epitaph where Marcus Ialeius Donatus erected a monument for his patron.

EDCS-25600030.translation: Iulius Rufinus lived 80 years. Primus and Methun made this.
EDCS-25600030.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Iulius Rufinus and the individuals who erected the monument.

EDCS-25600031.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Granius Isot lived 75 years.
EDCS-25600031.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Granius Isot.

EDCS-25600032.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Ania Matrona.

EDCS-25600033.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Publius Nummius Felix.

EDCS-25600034.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Caius Iulius Florentinus lived 13 years. Caius V E, his grandson, dedicated this.
EDCS-25600034.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Caius Iulius Florentinus and its dedication by his grandson.

EDCS-25600035.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Iulius Rogatus lived 51 years.
EDCS-25600035.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Marcus Iulius Rogatus.

EDCS-25600036.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iulia Rogata lived 71 years.
EDCS-25600036.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Iulia Rogata.

EDCS-25600038.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Decimus Vitellius Saturninus lived 105 years.
EDCS-25600038.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Decimus Vitellius Saturninus.

EDCS-25600039.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. [---] lived 59 years, [---] lived 55 years. Licinianus lived 25 years.
EDCS-25600039.summary: This is a funerary epitaph that appears to record the lifespans of multiple individuals, including Licinianus.

EDCS-25600041.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Ilus Ba lived [---] years.
EDCS-25600041.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Ilus Ba, though the duration is incomplete.

EDCS-25600043.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Caerellia the freedwoman lived 40 years.
EDCS-25600043.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Caerellia, a freedwoman.

EDCS-25600044.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Caius Iulius Marcellus lived 30 years.
EDCS-25600044.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Caius Iulius Marcellus.

EDCS-25600045.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Lucius Iulius Maximus lived 15 years.
EDCS-25600045.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Lucius Iulius Maximus.

EDCS-25600046.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iulia Urbana lived [---] years and 9 months.
EDCS-25600046.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Iulia Urbana, with the years incomplete.

EDCS-25600047.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Caius Perellius Felix lived [---] years.
EDCS-25600047.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Caius Perellius Felix, though the duration is incomplete.

EDCS-25600048.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Lucius Sabinius Iunior lived 21 years.
EDCS-25600048.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Lucius Sabinius Iunior.

EDCS-25600050.translation: Fausta, daughter of Faustus. She lived 50 years. May your bones rest well here.
