EDCS-28200087.translation: To Flavius Claudius Constantinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-28200087.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Flavius Claudius Constantinus, referred to as Caesar, likely erected during his rise to power.

EDCS-28200089.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius, Invictus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, from the state of the Dianenses, seven times.
EDCS-28200089.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the community of Dianenses to Emperor Maxentius, likely dating to his reign (306-312 AD).

EDCS-28200093.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Maximianus, Invictus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, from the state of the Dianenses.
EDCS-28200093.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the community of Dianenses to Emperor Maximianus, likely dating to his co-rule with Maxentius in the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-28200094.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus, Invictus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, and to the Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Voldumianus Volusianus, Invictus, Pious, Fortunate, consuls.
EDCS-28200094.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, who were consuls in 252 AD.

EDCS-28200095.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, Pious, Fortunate, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, and to Marcus Julius Philippus the Younger.
EDCS-28200095.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Philip the Arab and his son Philip II, likely dating to their co-rule in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-28200096.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus, renewed the milestones for travelers.
EDCS-28200096.summary: This inscription records that Emperor Marcus Aurelius restored the milestones, likely dating to his reign (161-180 AD).

EDCS-28200097.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus, Invictus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the country, proconsul.
EDCS-28200097.summary: This inscription describes Emperor Tacitus, likely dating to his brief reign (275-276 AD).

EDCS-28200098.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus, Invictus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, proconsul, and to Marcus Aurelius Carinus Augustus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, son of our Augustus, and to Numerianus, most noble Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-28200098.summary: This inscription honors the emperors Carus, Carinus, and Numerian, likely dating to their co-rule between 283 and 284 AD.

EDCS-28200100.translation: To Flavius Valerius Constantinus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, from the community of Lamasba.
EDCS-28200100.summary: This inscription is a dedication by the community of Lamasba to Flavius Valerius Constantinus (Constantine the Great), likely during his early reign.

EDCS-28200101.translation: To our Lord Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate Augustus.
EDCS-28200101.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine I, likely from the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-28200102.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Julianus, ever victorious and triumphant Augustus.
EDCS-28200102.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Julian, mentioning his titles.

EDCS-28200103.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Julianus, ever victorious and triumphant Augustus.
EDCS-28200103.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Julian, mentioning his titles.

EDCS-28200104.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, greatest conqueror of Parthia, greatest conqueror of Britain, greatest conqueror of Germany, chief priest, restored the milestones, which had collapsed due to age.
EDCS-28200104.summary: This inscription records Emperor Caracalla's restoration of milestones, likely in the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28200105.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Magnus Antoninus, renewed the milestones for travelers.
EDCS-28200105.summary: This inscription records Emperor Caracalla's renewal of milestones, likely in the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28200106.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, father of the fatherland, son of the deified Magnus Antoninus, renewed the milestones for travelers.
EDCS-28200106.summary: This inscription records Emperor Severus Alexander's renewal of milestones, likely in the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28200107.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus, Invictus, Pius, Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-28200107.summary: This is an honorific inscription to Emperor Probus, listing his titles, likely from the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28200108.translation: To our lords Emperors Marcus Aurelius Carinus and Marcus Aurelius Numerianus, most noble Caesars Augusti.
EDCS-28200108.summary: This inscription is dedicated to the co-emperors Carinus and Numerian, likely in the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28200109.translation: To Emperor our lord Gaius Valerius Diocletianus, Invictus, Pius, Felix Augustus.
EDCS-28200109.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Diocletian, likely in the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-28200110.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus, Invictus, Pius, Felix Augustus.
EDCS-28200110.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Maximian, likely in the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-28200111.translation: To Emperor our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus Augustus.
EDCS-28200111.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantine the Great, likely in the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-28200112.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurellius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, conqueror of Parthia, conqueror of Britain, conqueror of Germany, chief priest, restored mile-markers worn out by age.
EDCS-28200112.summary: This inscription records that Emperor Caracalla restored old mile-markers, dating to his reign.

EDCS-28200113.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus.
EDCS-28200113.summary: This is a standard imperial titulature for Emperor Aurelian, likely found on a public monument.

EDCS-28200114.translation: To the eternal, most glorious, most indulgent Emperor, restorer of the world, Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the fifth time, consul for the second time, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-28200114.summary: This dedicatory inscription honors Emperor Aurelian as a restorer of the world, indicating his importance and likely dating to his reign.

EDCS-28200115.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus, Invictus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, greatest, holder of tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-28200115.summary: This inscription presents the full titulature of Emperor Tacitus, likely from a monument during his brief reign.

EDCS-28200116.translation: To our lords, Flavius Constantinus, Invictus Germanicus Maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, for the third time.
EDCS-28200116.summary: This inscription honors Constantius I as Caesar and Augustus, possibly a dedicatory monument from the early 4th century.

EDCS-28200118.translation: Emperor Caesar Caius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, for the fifth time.
EDCS-28200118.summary: This is the imperial titulature of Emperor Decius, likely from a public inscription during his reign in the mid-3rd century.

EDCS-28200119.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, son of the divine Antoninus, renovated mile-markers for travelers.
EDCS-28200119.summary: This inscription records Emperor Severus Alexander's renovation of mile-markers, suggesting a public works project during his reign.

EDCS-28200133.translation: To our lord, Emperor Galerius Licinianus Licinius Augustus.
EDCS-28200133.summary: This dedicatory inscription is addressed to Emperor Licinius, likely from the early 4th century.

EDCS-28200134.translation: To our lord, Flavius Claudius Constantinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-28200134.summary: This dedicatory inscription is addressed to Constantine I as Caesar, likely from the period before he became Augustus.

EDCS-28200135.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, greatest conqueror of Parthia, greatest conqueror of Britain, greatest conqueror of Germany, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the seventeenth time, emperor for the third time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and to Julia Augusta, mother of the Augustus and of the camp and of the senate and of the fatherland.
EDCS-28200135.summary: This lengthy inscription provides the full imperial titulature for Emperor Caracalla and Julia Domna, indicating a significant public monument from the period of Caracalla's reign.

EDCS-28200136.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, greatest Parthian, greatest Briton, greatest German, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 18th time, imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of his country, proconsul, and to Julia Augusta, mother of the emperor and of the camp and of the senate and of the fatherland, four thousand paces.
EDCS-28200136.summary: This inscription records the completion of a road project measuring 4000 paces, dedicated to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus and Julia Augusta.

EDCS-28200138.translation: To the perpetual, most glorious, most indulgent Emperor, restorer of the world, Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul, father of his country, proconsul.
EDCS-28200138.summary: This is an honorific inscription for Emperor Aurelian, praising his virtues and role as restorer of the world.

EDCS-28200139.summary: This is a simple titulary inscription for Emperor Licinius Augustus.

EDCS-28200142.translation: By the authority of the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, the state of the people of Cirta established milestones for the new Rusicade road, six thousand paces.
EDCS-28200142.summary: This inscription records the establishment of milestones for a new road by the city of Cirta, authorized by Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-28200143.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, father of his country, proconsul, restored the road, collapsed by rains and age, along with its bridges.
EDCS-28200143.summary: This inscription records Emperor Gordian III restoring a road and its bridges that had collapsed due to weather and age.

EDCS-28200144.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, father of his country, proconsul, restored the road, collapsed by rains and age, along with its bridges, fourteen thousand paces.
EDCS-28200144.summary: This inscription records Emperor Gordian III restoring a road and its bridges, covering a length of 14,000 paces, which had collapsed due to weather and age.

EDCS-28200145.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul, father of his country, proconsul, and Marcus Iulius Philippus restored the road, collapsed by rains and age, along with its bridges, fourteen thousand paces.
EDCS-28200145.summary: This inscription records the joint restoration of a 14,000 pace road and bridges by Emperor Philip the Arab and Marcus Iulius Philippus.

EDCS-28200146.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, proconsul, the road.
EDCS-28200146.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription for a road to Emperor Diocletian.

EDCS-28200147.translation: To Valerius Maximinus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, our most noble Caesar, fourteen [paces].
EDCS-28200147.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Caesar Valerius Maximinus, likely associated with a road or boundary marker of fourteen units.

EDCS-28200148.translation: To our lord Flavius Iulius Crispinus, most noble Caesar, fourteen [paces].
EDCS-28200148.summary: This is a dedication to the Caesar Flavius Iulius Crispinus, possibly related to a road or boundary marker of fourteen units.

EDCS-28200151.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul. He restored the road, which had collapsed with its bridges due to rains and age, [having spent] 14 [units of money].
EDCS-28200151.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road and its bridges by Emperor Carinus, likely in the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28200152.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Marcus Iulius Philippus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-28200152.summary: This inscription lists the titles and co-rule of Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, Marcus Iulius Philippus Caesar, in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28200153.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, grandson of the deified Septimius Severus, son of the deified Antoninus Magnus Pius.
EDCS-28200153.summary: This inscription records the full titulature of Emperor Severus Alexander, who reigned in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28200154.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 15th time, imperator for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, greatest, unconquered, most holy, most brave, most fortunate, and most indulgent of all princes, son of the deified Septimius Severus Pius Augustus, Augustus for the 13th time.
EDCS-28200154.summary: This inscription lists the extensive titles of Emperor Caracalla, likely from his 13th consulship and 15th tribunician power, in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28200155.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix, son of the deified Antoninus Magnus Pius, grandson of the deified Severus Pius, most fortunate and most indulgent prince, holder of tribunician power for the second time, designated consul for the third time, proconsul for the third time.
EDCS-28200155.summary: This inscription records the titles of Emperor Caracalla, likely from around 213 CE, highlighting his lineage and honors.

EDCS-28200158.translation: [...] greatest, holder of tribunician power, designated consul, imperator for the second time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, to our Lord Julianus, victorious and triumphant, ever Augustus.
EDCS-28200158.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Julian, likely from the mid-4th century CE, emphasizing his military and imperial titles.

EDCS-28200159.summary: This very brief inscription is a dedication to Emperor Valens, likely from the late 4th century CE.

EDCS-28200160.translation: By the authority of Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland. The road was fortified by the people of Milevitanus, from his generosity, [funded] by the [tax] on wheeled traffic [amounting to] 6 [denarii?].
EDCS-28200160.summary: This inscription records the fortification of a road by the people of Milevitanis under the authority and generosity of Emperor Hadrian, likely in the early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-28200162.translation: To Flavius Claudius Constantinus Iunior, [designated] nobilissimus Caesar for the 3rd time.
EDCS-28200162.summary: This inscription honors Constantius II as Caesar, likely from the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-28200163.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Iunius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus.
EDCS-28200163.summary: This inscription lists the imperial titles of Emperor Philip the Arab, who reigned in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28200164.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the country, proconsul, by the people of Milevum for 20 miles.
EDCS-28200164.summary: This is a milestone inscription from Milevum dedicating a 20-mile stretch of road to Emperor Gordian III.

EDCS-28200165.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, and to Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, the public community of Cuiculitanorum restored roads exhausted by torrents and widened them with new fortifications.
EDCS-28200165.summary: This is a public works inscription from Cuiculitanorum dedicating restored roads to Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, Caesar Philip II.

EDCS-28300491.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Diocletian, likely dating to his reign.

EDCS-28300492.translation: To the eternal, most noble Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius Invictus Pius Felix, always Augustus, the public community of the colony of Thamugadi.
EDCS-28300492.summary: This inscription from the colony of Thamugadi dedicates something to Caesar Constantius Chlorus.

EDCS-28701392.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Solomon, a former praetorian prefect, possibly related to a monument or tomb.

EDCS-28701393.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Solomon, a former praetorian prefect, possibly related to a monument or tomb.

EDCS-28701409.translation: During the reign of Justinian it was made.
EDCS-28701409.summary: This is a short inscription indicating that a work was completed during the reign of Emperor Justinian I.

EDCS-29200168.translation: To Victoria Augusta, our lord Julianus. 'Our lord, the public community and the council of the colony of Thamugad, under the supervision of Flavius Aquilinus, perpetual flamen and curator of the public community, erected and dedicated this for the splendor of the age.'
EDCS-29200168.summary: This is a dedication by the community and council of Thamugad to Emperor Julian and possibly his Augusta, with a specific official overseeing the project.

EDCS-29200169.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 3rd time, emperor for the 4th time, father of the country, and Julia Augusta Pia Felix, mother of the camps, and of the senate and of the country, the circuit of the fountain with bronze gratings enclosed, and the colonnades of the pleasure garden adorned with paintings, with doors and fore-courts open to the same colonnades, and also the work of the square from the baths up to the entrance, was completed by the public community of Thamugadenses by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-29200169.summary: This lengthy inscription details public works in Thamugad, including a fountain, gardens, and a square, completed under Emperor Caracalla and his mother Julia Domna, as decreed by the local council.

EDCS-29200192.translation: To Flavia Procilla, daughter of Titus, perpetual flaminica, wife of Marcus Coelius Saturninus, perpetual flamen. Titus Flavius Monimus, honored with the public horse, erected this for his very rare kinswoman, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-29200192.summary: This is a funerary or commemorative inscription set up by Titus Flavius Monimus for his wife, Flavia Procilla, a woman of senatorial rank (flaminica), and likely honored by the local decurions.

EDCS-29200437.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus, clarissimus vir, governor of the Augusti and president, [and] to the Genius of all Virtues, from Clodius Lucifer, prefect of the fleet, an egregius vir.
EDCS-29200437.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcus Aurelius Cominius Cassianus and the Genius of Virtues by Clodius Lucifer, a fleet prefect, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30100146.translation: For eternal security, Marcus Licinius Felix, who is called Ballantis, perpetual flamen, who lived for thirty-three years, a guest of the rich, is buried here.
EDCS-30100146.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Licinius Felix, a perpetual flamen who died at the age of 33, dated to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31200126.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Caius Munatius Felix, a veteran and perpetual flamen, erected and dedicated this to the Conservator of the Empire of our lord Caius Augustus and the Hearer of the prayers of the human race.
EDCS-31200126.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a shrine to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by the veteran Caius Munatius Felix, likely during the reign of Emperor Caracalla (211-217 CE).

EDCS-31300168.translation: Publius Flavius Pudens Pomponianus, clarissimus vir, proconsul of Crete and Cyrenaica, legate of the Augusti of Aquitanian province, prefect for the distribution of grain by decree of the Senate, curator of the Albenses Fucensium, praetor, tribune of the plebs, quaestor of the province of Sicily, by decree of the decurions of his fatherland.
EDCS-31300168.summary: This is a career summary inscription for Publius Flavius Pudens Pomponianus, detailing his extensive public service from senatorial rank upwards, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31300199.translation: Lucius Acilius Granianus gave this as a gift to Hygia, his father-in-law Lucius Iulius Ianuarius, for the adornment of the bathhouse.
EDCS-31300199.summary: This inscription records a gift from Lucius Acilius Granianus to his father-in-law Lucius Iulius Ianuarius, intended to adorn a bathhouse, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31300203.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the century. Titus Terentius Maximus, optio, made this.
EDCS-31300203.summary: This is a dedication to the Genius of a military century by Titus Terentius Maximus, an optio, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31300204.translation: Sacred to Mars Augustus, Genius of the century. Caius Aponius Saturninus, optio, willingly and happily fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31300204.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Mars Augustus and the Genius of a century by Caius Aponius Saturninus, an optio, probably in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31300205.translation: Sacred to Augustus, Genius of the century. Marcus Magullius Rufus, veteran of the Legio III Augusta, happily and willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31300205.summary: This is a dedication to Augustus and the Genius of a century by Marcus Magullius Rufus, a veteran of Legio III Augusta, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31700255.translation: Titus Roscius, military tribune of a cohort of Roman citizen volunteers of the citizenship of Pittana, lived 61 years, 10 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-31700255.summary: This inscription records the lifespan and military service of Titus Roscius, a military tribune from Pittana, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-33100003.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or ownership marker, for Quintus Iunius Victor, likely from the 3rd century CE or later.

EDCS-33100004.summary: This is a genitive inscription likely naming the owner or subject of a monument.

EDCS-33100008.summary: This inscription indicates the origin of an object, stating it was made in the workshop of Felix.

EDCS-33100012.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of a pious individual.

EDCS-33100013.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Roga.

EDCS-33100015.translation: Publius Sittius Saturninus lived for 51 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-33100015.summary: This funerary inscription records the name, lifespan, and burial place of Publius Sittius Saturninus.

EDCS-33100016.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Marcus Sittius.
EDCS-33100016.summary: This is a dedication to the Manes, likely for a tombstone, naming Marcus Sittius.

EDCS-33100017.translation: Gaius Iulius Iulus lived for 75 years; he is buried here. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-33100017.summary: This funerary inscription records the name, lifespan, burial place, and a wish for peace for Gaius Iulius Iulus.

EDCS-33100018.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Sittia Matronica lived for 70 years.
EDCS-33100018.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the Manes and records the name and lifespan of Sittia Matronica.

EDCS-33100019.summary: This inscription likely names the owner or subject of a monument, possibly funerary.

EDCS-33100021.summary: This inscription is likely a dedication or naming of a monument for Gaius Iulius Quadratus.

EDCS-33100022.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription for Lollia Modesta.

EDCS-33100023.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription for Quintus Julius Maximus.

EDCS-33700134.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription for Lucius Laeceanavius Gemellus.

EDCS-33700493.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription for Aurelius Neptunalis.

EDCS-33700502.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription for Publius Clodius Euphemus.

EDCS-33700570.translation: Caius Asutius Amandus's stacte for roughnesses, Caius Asutius Amandus's drop for eye ailments
EDCS-33700570.summary: This inscription records medical remedies or treatments for specific ailments, likely related to Caius Asutius Amandus.

EDCS-33700586.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription for Gaius Titinius Valerianus.

EDCS-33700587.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia, reflecting Christian beliefs.

EDCS-33700591.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription for Titus Aurelius Rufus.

EDCS-34000172.translation: To the ancestral gods, to the health-giving gods, to Jupiter Serapis, Augustus, by the tribune of the 15th cohort of Roman citizen volunteers, Pious and Loyal, tribune of the 3rd Parthian legion, tribune of the 3rd Augustan legion.
EDCS-34000172.summary: This inscription records dedications by a military officer holding multiple tribunate positions, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-34200084.translation: Pullus, who lived and gave, may you enjoy the province in the 422nd year.
EDCS-34200084.summary: A Latin inscription from a provincial context, possibly a tombstone or dedication, dated to the provincial year 422.

EDCS-34300775.translation: The colony of Lambaesis erected this for our most provident lord, the ever-triumphant Emperor Flavius Claudius Julianus, our lord, the unconquered, pious, fortunate, victorious Augustus, with the world having been restored to his dominion.
EDCS-34300775.summary: A public dedication by the colony of Lambaesis to Emperor Julian, celebrating his victory and restoration of order, likely in the 360s AD.

EDCS-35200310.translation: Matrona is placed here as an example, whose fame will speak that no better or more holy one could have been given.
EDCS-35200310.summary: A Latin epitaph for a woman named Matrona, praising her character and exemplary life.

EDCS-38301834.translation: To the departed spirits, Publicius Heracla lived 95 years, he is buried here. To the departed spirits, Publicia Vitalis, a most exceptional wife, lived 43 years, she is buried here. In the time of our lords Honorius and Theodosius, perpetual always Augusti, Caesius Aufidianus Polybius, a most distinguished man of the highest rank, serving as vicarius of the praetorian prefecture, honored the curia.
EDCS-38301834.summary: A tomb inscription for Publicius Heracla and his wife Publicia Vitalis, mentioning Caesius Aufidianus Polybius who held a high administrative post under Honorius and Theodosius (late 4th/early 5th century AD).

EDCS-39200513.translation: Cornelia Urbanilla lies here, having been saved from great danger, having lived 27 years, 10 months, 12 days, 9 hours. Tiberius Claudius Veitalis for his wife.
EDCS-39200513.summary: A Greek epitaph for Cornelia Urbanilla, who survived a great danger, and was mourned by her husband Tiberius Claudius Veitalis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39200514.translation: To the memory of Cornelia Urbanilla. Euterpius: I was not, I was, I am not, I do not care.
EDCS-39200514.summary: A Greek funerary inscription for Cornelia Urbanilla, possibly a second inscription or a separate epigram by Euterpius, reflecting on mortality.

EDCS-39200549.summary: A very brief Greek inscription, likely identifying a person named Hippocrates from Ephesus, possibly on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-41300004.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits of Titus Flavius Germanus, a veteran formerly a decurion, who lived 75 years, and Flavia Thuiumatdi, his wife, who lived 91 years.
EDCS-41300004.summary: A Latin tomb inscription dedicated to a veteran, Titus Flavius Germanus, and his wife Flavia Thuiumatdi.

EDCS-41300008.translation: To Cornelia Salonia Augusta, wife of our lord Publius Licinius Gallienus Augustus, mother of Publius Cornelius Licinius Valerianus, most noble Caesar Augustus, and of the army, the Senate [dedicates this].
EDCS-41300008.summary: A Latin dedication to Cornelia Salonina, wife of Emperor Gallienus, and mother of their son Valerian, likely erected by the Senate or related authorities.

EDCS-41300010.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Fabricia Silvana lived 50 years, a most worthy wife. Publius Marius Saturninus, the husband, for [her]. He built this monument with 12,000 sesterces.
EDCS-41300010.summary: A Latin tomb inscription for Fabricia Silvana, dedicated by her husband Publius Marius Saturninus, who funded the construction of the monument.

EDCS-41300203.translation: Caecilia Urbana, daughter of [---], lived for 35 years, is buried here.
EDCS-41300203.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a woman named Caecilia Urbana, stating her age and burial place.

EDCS-41500044.translation: Saturnus, a decurion, completed this farm, built by the inhabitants from the gifts of the god, with Maximo, son of Maximinus, successfully.
EDCS-41500044.summary: This inscription records the completion of a farm built by locals, dedicated to a god, with Saturnus the decurion and Maximo being involved. It likely dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-42400370.translation: Severus Arado, Aemilius Martialis of Apamea, Iulius Crispus of Sidon, Iulius Agrippa of Tripoli, Iulius Proculus of Sidon, Clodius Gaianus of Epipa, Iulius Iulianus of Nicomedia, Maximus of Parethonio, Marcianus of Nicomedia, La[---]ntianus of Claudia, Tolomaide, Fortunatus of Carthage, Valens of Sidon, Theodorus of Cirta, [---]ianus of Laodicea, [---]us, an equestrian, from the camp, [---]cus of Carthage, Arellianus of Zeugma, Macrinus of Cirta, a beneficiary of the legate, Proculus of Carthage, Quintullus of Nicomedia, Severus of Gaza, Valens of Cyrene, Clemens of Gabala, [---]lianus of Sidonia, CTI IVS of Sirmium, a candidate, [---]minus to Marcianus, Callicies of Nicomedia, s Antiochus of Prusias, Sanctus of Nicomedia, s Fronto of Sidon, Severus of Gen[---], F ius Valens, us Marianus of Sirir, an optio, ius IIIanus of Ecis, an immunis, us of Sidone, ava, Damaso, a sttranslator of the legion, Valens of Hippo, a candidate, us of Tolomaide, Rhyto of Antioch, ius Lucianus of Claudiopolis, anius Longus of Apamea, Aemilius Bassus of Apamea, Gracilis from the camp, Anicius Niger of Plusiade, Mimmius Macrinus of Laodicea, Flavius Marcellus of Larisa, nianus Severus of Tolomaide, Ammonius Valens of Sidon, Cornelius Sabinus of Nicomedia, a standard-bearer, Antonius Priscus of Antioch, Aemilius Secundus of Theveste, Quintus Domitius Caius Numonius Pot[---], Titus Axius Sabinus, Titus Flavius Safidianus of Caesarea, Quintus Otacilius Ma[---] of Carthage, Titus Papius Flaminalis of Nicomedia, Tiberius Clodius C[---] of Tyre, A Messalla, Claudia of Tolomaide, of Prusias, Caius Iulius Antoninus from the camp, Tiberius Claudius Iulianus of Tyre, Lucius Claudius Agrippa of Nicomedia, Caius Manilius Rufus of Prusias, Caius Sempronius Maternus of Lugudunum, Lucius Antonius Victor of Scythopolis, Caius Vibius Celer of Nicomedia, Marcus Appius Sossianus of Nicomedia, Fabricius N[---], Caius Iulius Proculeianus, Titus Flavius Victorinus of Damascus, Quintus Apuleius Martialis of Carthage, Marcus Domitius Valens of Heliopolis, Marcus Sossius Macrinus of Nicomedia, Publius Lucceius Marcianus of Nicomedia, Lucius Gellius Felix of Carthage, Caius Publicius Ingenuus of Hippo, Marcus Antonius Bassus of Apamea, Tiberius Claudius Restitutus of Tolomaide, Caius Marius Pedo of Sidonia, Marcus Pompeius Maximus of Tolomaide, Stertinius Maximus of Nicomedia, Cornelius Magnus, Marcus Livius Theopompus of Sidone, Lucius Antistius Lucianus of Nicomedia, Lucius Minucius Paulus of Nicomedia, Caius Annius Valens of Capitoliade, Claudius Arellianus, Caius Iulius Rogatus of Papiria, Hadrumet, Marcus Atilius Saturninus of Heliopolis, Titus Flavius Valens of Tolomaide, Quintus Decrius Severus of Nicomedia, Caius Valerius Rufus of Anthedon, Caius Lucceius Hermianus from the camp, Marcus Statius Maximus of Nicomedia, a standard-bearer, Quintus Caesonius Augurinus.
EDCS-42400370.summary: This is a lengthy inscription listing numerous individuals, likely officials, soldiers, or prominent citizens, identified by name and place of origin or affiliation, possibly related to a military or administrative context.

EDCS-42600190.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely a dedication or epitaph, honoring a person named Gureus, emphasizing his holiness.

EDCS-42600193.translation: Here is the memory of the holy Cassianus.
EDCS-42600193.summary: This inscription marks a place associated with the memory or tomb of a holy individual named Cassianus.

EDCS-42700002.translation: On these estates, of Aurelius Marcellinus, a most perfect man, leader of leaders, and of Marcella, a most noble lady, and of Egyptilla, a most noble lady, his mother, [---]
EDCS-42700002.summary: This inscription indicates ownership or association with estates belonging to Aurelius Marcellinus, his wife Marcella, and his mother Egyptilla, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-43400843.translation: Flavia Albula of good memory, placed in this sarcophagus, in which she lived for eighty-five years.
EDCS-43400843.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Flavia Albula, noting her good memory and the length of her life.

EDCS-43500031.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Elia Tertula lived for sixty years.
EDCS-43500031.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes, recording the name and age of Elia Tertula.

EDCS-43500032.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Sons made [this] for their dearest mother and father. Iulius Sae Felicitus lived for 26 years and 70 years.
EDCS-43500032.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes, commissioned by sons for their parents, and it includes ages for at least one of the deceased, Iulius Sae Felicitus.

EDCS-43500033.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Aemilia Casta made this for Caecilia Aemiliana, aged 10, her most precious daughter. She lived for 7 years.
EDCS-43500033.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes, commemorating Caecilia Aemiliana, who died at the age of 7, with the epitaph made by her mother Aemilia Casta.

EDCS-43500046.translation: To our lord and emperor Lucius Domitius Alexander.
EDCS-43500046.summary: This is an inscription dedicated to the emperor Lucius Domitius Alexander, likely on a public monument or honorific statue.

EDCS-43500052.translation: To the perpetual and most indulgent, unconquered emperor Diocletian.
EDCS-43500052.summary: This inscription honors the emperor Diocletian, portraying him as an undefeated and benevolent ruler.

EDCS-43500070.translation: To our lord and emperor, the unconquered, dutiful, fortunate Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Augustus.
EDCS-43500070.summary: This dedicatory inscription honors Emperor Constantine the Great, highlighting his virtues and titles.

EDCS-43600013.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aemilia Saturnina lived 20 years. Gaius Julius Silvanus, a cavalryman of the 6th Cohort of Commageni, made this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-43600013.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aemilia Saturnina, erected by her husband, Gaius Julius Silvanus, a member of a military cohort.

EDCS-43600014.translation: To our lord and emperor, the unconquered, dutiful, fortunate Marcus Julius Philippus, Augustus, 15 miles.
EDCS-43600014.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Philip the Arab and likely indicates the distance to a location or a measure of land.

EDCS-43600015.translation: To our lords Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, Augustuses.
EDCS-43600015.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the co-emperors Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-43600018.translation: To the unconquered, dutiful, fortunate emperor Caesar Maximian, Augustus.
EDCS-43600018.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Emperor Maximian, emphasizing his imperial titles and qualities.

EDCS-43600019.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Flavia Getula lived 25 years. Flavius Felix made this for his good daughter, an actress, and dedicated it.
EDCS-43600019.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Getula, erected by her father, Flavius Felix, who identifies her profession as an actress.

EDCS-43600020.translation: To the unconquered, dutiful, fortunate emperor Caesar, 1 mile.
EDCS-43600020.summary: This inscription is a dedication to an unnamed emperor, likely indicating a distance or measurement of one mile.

EDCS-43600021.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Constantius, the most noble Augustus.
EDCS-43600021.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantius, likely on a public monument or building.

EDCS-43600022.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Sittia Maxima lived 45 years; she is buried here.
EDCS-43600022.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sittia Maxima, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-43600024.translation: Flavius Innocentius restored [this], dedicated by T. Cresconia, an honorable woman, to her son and [---].
EDCS-43600024.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a monument by Flavius Innocentius, dedicated by T. Cresconia to her son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-43600027.translation: For the well-being of our Emperor Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Pius, the people of Alea have established and dedicated a sacred altar, under the procuratorship of Gaius Helvius Hilarius, with Gaius Julius Felix as chief official, and with Erimus and Gaius Julius Victor as curators.
EDCS-43600027.summary: This is a dedication of a sacred altar to Emperor Commodus by the people of Alea, dating to his reign (177-192 AD), with a list of officials.

EDCS-43600029.translation: To the Emperor, our lord, Magnus Magnentius, unconquered, pious, fortunate, always Augustus, for the seventh time.
EDCS-43600029.summary: This is an inscription honoring the emperor Magnentius, likely a dedication or acclamation during his reign (349-353 AD).

EDCS-43600030.translation: To our lord Valentinianus, the triumphant, always Augustus.
EDCS-43600030.summary: This is an inscription honoring Emperor Valentinian I, likely a dedication or acclamation during his reign (364-375 AD).

EDCS-43600033.translation: To the eternal Emperors Diocletian and Maximian Augusti, and to the most valiant Constantinus and Maximianus Caesares.
EDCS-43600033.summary: This inscription honors the co-emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Constantine I, and Maximian II, dating to their period of rule (c. 306-307 AD).

EDCS-43600034.translation: To the Emperor, our lord, Licinianus Licinius, pious, fortunate, always Augustus.
EDCS-43600034.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Licinius, likely a dedication or acclamation during his reign (308-324 AD).

EDCS-43600035.translation: To the perpetual and most flourishing Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus, junior, unconquered, pious, fortunate Augustus.
EDCS-43600035.summary: This inscription honors Caesar Galerius Maximian (later Emperor), likely from the period of his co-rule or Caesarship (c. 293-305 AD).

EDCS-43600037.translation: To the Emperor, our lord Julianus, the victor, always Augustus.
EDCS-43600037.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Julian, likely a dedication or acclamation during his reign (360-363 AD).

EDCS-43600038.translation: To our lord the Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus Augustus.
EDCS-43600038.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Aurelian, likely a dedication or acclamation during his reign (270-275 AD).

EDCS-43600039.translation: To our lord, the Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus.
EDCS-43600039.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Tacitus, likely from the period of his reign (275-276 AD).

EDCS-43600041.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, Unconquered, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus.
EDCS-43600041.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Philip the Arab, likely from his reign (244-249 AD).

EDCS-43600074.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Plotius Emeritus lived for 9 years; under the guardianship of his mother, he rests here.
EDCS-43600074.summary: This funerary epitaph records the short life of Lucius Plotius Emeritus and indicates his mother placed the monument.

EDCS-43600075.translation: To Hygieia Augusta, from Faustus and Valentina.
EDCS-43600075.summary: This is a votive offering to the goddess Hygieia Augusta, dedicated by Faustus and Valentina.

EDCS-43600077.translation: To Aesculapius Augustus, for the adornment of the baths, from Primitus, the administrator.
EDCS-43600077.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Aesculapius Augusta by Primitus, likely an official responsible for baths.

EDCS-43600079.translation: During the reign of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Country. Caius Prastina Messallinus, imperial legate with praetorian power of the III Augusta Legion, patron of the colony, dedicated the paved street with a decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-43600079.summary: This inscription details the dedication of a paved street in a colony by Caius Prastina Messallinus, a Roman governor, during the reign of Antoninus Pius (138-161 AD).

EDCS-43600080.translation: Dedicated to Victoria Augusta, for the safety of our lord the Emperor.
EDCS-43600080.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Victoria Augusta, made for the emperor's well-being.

EDCS-43600082.translation: To the Emperor, our lord, Flavius Julius Crispus, most noble Caesar, by the state of Thamugadis.
EDCS-43600082.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by the municipality of Thamugadis to Caesar Flavius Julius Crispus, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-43600087.translation: To Minerva Augusta, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus, Unconquered, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, by the keepers of the arms.
EDCS-43600087.summary: This is a votive inscription to Minerva Augusta dedicated by the 'keepers of the arms' for the safety of Emperor Gallienus (253-268 AD).

EDCS-43600088.translation: To the Eternity of the Emperors Augusti, under the care of Lucius Sallustius Honoratus, quaestor.
EDCS-43600088.summary: This dedication to the 'Eternity of the Emperors' was overseen by Lucius Sallustius Honoratus, a quaestor, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-43700622.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, consul for the ninth time.
EDCS-43700622.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Gordian III, dating to his reign (238-244 AD) based on his titulature.

EDCS-43700623.translation: To the most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, son of Emperor Augustus, consul for the eighth time.
EDCS-43700623.summary: This inscription honors a Caesar, likely a designated heir, dating to the period of the Roman Empire when such titles were in use.

EDCS-43700624.translation: To our lords Valentinian and Valens, victorious, ever Augusti.
EDCS-43700624.summary: This dedication is to the co-emperors Valentinian I and Valens, ruling from 364 to 375 AD, likely associated with a public building or monument.

EDCS-43700643.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Iulius Saturninus lived 75 years. Varia Matrona made and dedicated this altar to her husband while she was alive.
EDCS-43700643.summary: A funerary altar dedicated by Varia Matrona to her deceased husband, Iulius Saturninus, who lived to be 75.

EDCS-43700644.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, for the safety of our lords Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti and...
EDCS-43700644.summary: A dedication to Jupiter for the well-being of the emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, indicating it was set up during their joint reign (198-211 AD).

EDCS-43700663.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Iulius Victor lived 90 years. He made this for himself while alive and for his dearest wife.
EDCS-43700663.summary: A funerary monument set up by Iulius Victor for himself and his wife during his lifetime, recording his age as 90.

EDCS-43700664.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Protector of our lords the Emperors Gaius Valerius Diocletian Augusti.
EDCS-43700664.summary: A dedication to Jupiter, the protector of Emperor Diocletian, dating to his reign (284-305 AD) and likely associated with a public building.

EDCS-43700665.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Aniolenus Servandus Minuci built this altar. He lived 95 years.
EDCS-43700665.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Aniolenus Servandus Minuci at the age of 95 and noting he built the altar.

EDCS-43700666.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Masinia Prima lived 80 years. Masinius Conductor made this for his father VAL.
EDCS-43700666.summary: A funerary monument set up by Masinius Conductor for his father, Masinia Prima, who lived to be 80.

EDCS-43700667.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Dead. Gaius Iulius Secundus lived 81 years.
EDCS-43700667.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Gaius Iulius Secundus at the age of 81.

EDCS-43700668.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Claudia Maxima.

EDCS-43700669.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iulius Rogatianus lived 44 years. Fortunatus made this for his deserving brother.
EDCS-43700669.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Iulius Rogatianus, for whom his brother Fortunatus erected the monument.

EDCS-43700670.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iunius Nomi lived 40 years. Caius Iunius Iulus made this for his most devoted brother.
EDCS-43700670.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Iunius Nomi, for whom his brother Caius Iunius Iulus erected the monument.

EDCS-43700672.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Servandus Nuciell lived 85 years.
EDCS-43700672.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Servandus Nuciell.

EDCS-43700673.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iunius Rogatianus Iunior lived 45 years. Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iunius Rogatianus lived 80 years. The father made and dedicated this.
EDCS-43700673.summary: This funerary inscription records the deaths of two Iunius Rogatianus, father and son, with the father erecting the monument for his son and himself.

EDCS-43700674.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Quinto Acro Fortunato lived 80 years. Numissia Caelina made this for her most loving husband.
EDCS-43700674.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Quinto Acro Fortunato, for whom his wife Numissia Caelina erected the monument.

EDCS-43700675.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Marcus V Estikta lived 51 years.
EDCS-43700675.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Marcus V Estikta.

EDCS-43700676.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iulia Dativa lived 80 years.
EDCS-43700676.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Iulia Dativa.

EDCS-43700678.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Otolus Namphamo lived 75 years. Sameus for his dearest father.
EDCS-43700678.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Otolus Namphamo, for whom his son Sameus erected the monument.

EDCS-43700679.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iulius Urbanus lived 90 years. Publius Opt Par made this for his deserving.
EDCS-43700679.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Iulius Urbanus, for whom Publius Opt Par erected the monument.

EDCS-43700680.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld: Vollusinnia Aninivia.
EDCS-43700680.summary: A funerary dedication by Vollusinnia Aninivia, likely a private tomb from the Imperial period.

EDCS-43700681.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld: V. lived Oscelia Pollim. Lived years.
EDCS-43700681.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription recording the name Oscelia Pollim and incomplete lifespan information, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-43700682.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld: Paulim lived 75 years.
EDCS-43700682.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Paulim at the age of 75, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-43700683.translation: To the deified Augustus, for the health of our lord Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Augustus and Julia Augusta, his mother, the Senate and People of Rome [dedicate this].
EDCS-43700683.summary: A dedication to Emperor Caracalla and his mother Julia Domna, mentioning the Senate and People of Rome, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-43700686.translation: To our lord Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Trajanus Decius Victorious, Happy, August.
EDCS-43700686.summary: A dedication to Emperor Decius, likely from a public monument erected during his reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-43700687.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus IIII.
EDCS-43700687.summary: An inscription marking a milestone, likely a road, under the consulship or reign of Emperor Gordian III, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-43700688.translation: To the Emperor, our lord, Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar. Miles.
EDCS-43700688.summary: An inscription likely related to a road or boundary, dedicating to Emperor Philip the Arab, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-43700689.translation: To the Emperors, Caesars, Publius Licinius Valerianus and Publius Licinius Gallienus, the Unconquered Augusts IIII.
EDCS-43700689.summary: A dedication to Emperors Valerian and Gallienus, likely from a public monument or milestone during their joint rule in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-43700690.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, the Unconquered, Victorious, August.
EDCS-43700690.summary: A dedication to Emperor Aurelian, likely from a public monument or milestone during his reign in the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-43700691.summary: A dedication or inscription honouring Flavius Valerius Constantius, likely a military or administrative official, from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-43700692.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning Flavius Valerius Constantius, likely part of a dedication or honorific.

EDCS-43700693.translation: To our lords Magnus Maximus and Victor, his son, perpetual Augusti, rulers of our serenity
EDCS-43700693.summary: This inscription honors the emperors Magnus Maximus and his son Victor, referring to them as "rulers of our serenity", likely dating to their joint reign.

EDCS-43700694.translation: To the emperor, our lord Aurelius Carinus
EDCS-43700694.summary: This inscription honors the emperor Aurelius Carinus, likely a dedication from an individual or group.

EDCS-43700695.translation: To the emperor, our lord Gaius Valerius Diocletian, Invictus Pius Felix Augustus
EDCS-43700695.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Diocletian, describing him with epithets emphasizing his invincibility, piety, and good fortune, likely from the period of his reign.

EDCS-43700696.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Valerius Aurelius Maximianus Augustus, consul
EDCS-43700696.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Maximian, mentioning his consular status, dating it to one of his consulships during his reign.

EDCS-43700698.translation: To the emperors, our lords Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus and Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, six miles
EDCS-43700698.summary: This inscription honors the co-emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, indicating a distance of six miles, likely a milestone or boundary marker.

EDCS-43700699.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Maximianus Augustus
EDCS-43700699.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Maximian, likely a dedication or honorific from the period of his rule.

EDCS-43700700.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Traianus Decius Augustus
EDCS-43700700.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Decius, likely a dedication or honorific from the period of his reign.

EDCS-43700701.translation: To the emperor, our lord Marcus Aurelius Probus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, eight
EDCS-43700701.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Probus, indicating a quantity of eight (likely miles), possibly from a milestone or an honorific text.

EDCS-43700702.translation: To the emperors, Caesars Marcus Valerius Diocletian and Marcus Aurelius Maximian, Invicti Pii Felices Augusti
EDCS-43700702.summary: This inscription honors the co-emperors Diocletian and Maximian with their epithets, dating to their joint reign.

EDCS-43700703.translation: To the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, eight miles.
EDCS-43700703.summary: This inscription records a milestone indicating a distance of eight miles dedicated to the emperor, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-43700706.translation: Titus Flavius Longinus, Titus Flavius Getulicus, Caius Popilius, Caius Iulius N., Quintus Calpurnius, Quintus Valerius, Lucius Porcius, Marcus Porcius, Publius Statilius, Caius Petronius, Caius Iulius O., Publius Iug., Titus Flavius m., Claudius m., Aelius Iu., Quintus Annius, Quintus Iulius, Iulius.
EDCS-43700706.summary: This inscription lists a series of Roman male personal names, possibly related to a collegium or a group of soldiers, with an indeterminate date.

EDCS-43700707.translation: To our Lords, the ever-lasting Augusti Valentinianus, Theodosius, and Arcadius.
EDCS-43700707.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Valentinianus, Theodosius, and Arcadius, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-43700708.translation: To the Emperor, Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus.
EDCS-43700708.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Gordian III, likely from a public monument or honorary statue during his reign in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-43700709.translation: To our Lord, the most powerful Emperor, Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, our consul for the second time.
EDCS-43700709.summary: This inscription dedicates to Emperor Severus Alexander, likely an honorary inscription from his second consulship in the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-43700710.translation: To the Emperor, our Lord Flavius Claudius Julianus, the Victorious.
EDCS-43700710.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Julian, likely from a military context or public monument during his reign in the mid-4th century AD.

EDCS-43700711.translation: To the Emperor, Caesar Marcus Aurelius Alexander, the Invincible, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Severus, greatest in Parthia, greatest IV.
EDCS-43700711.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Severus Alexander, likely an honorary monument from his reign in the early 3rd century AD, referencing his Parthian victories.

EDCS-44000010.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Paulus Ianuarius lived 23 years and 5 months; he made this for his deserving father.
EDCS-44000010.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a father, set up by his son Paulus Ianuarius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44000337.translation: Saturninus made this for the Good; good things for such people.
EDCS-44000337.summary: This is a short, possibly votive or dedicatory, inscription by Saturninus, with an indeterminate date.

EDCS-44000338.translation: Saturninus made this. Speratus, do not despair; having read, depart.
EDCS-44000338.summary: This enigmatic inscription, possibly funerary or dedicatory, by Saturninus contains a direct address to someone named Speratus, with an indeterminate date.

EDCS-44000344.translation: Leander, charioteer of Favyanus, [trained with] Hercules, Achilles, Diomedes.
EDCS-44000344.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Leander, a charioteer, who apparently associated himself with famous mythical heroes. The date is uncertain but likely imperial.

EDCS-44000879.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gellia Spes lived 80 years.
EDCS-44000879.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gellia Spes, recording her lifespan of 80 years, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-44000880.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Luciusius Fortis lived 90 years. [---]
EDCS-44000880.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph for Luciusius Fortis, recording his lifespan of 90 years, with the remainder of the inscription illegible. It dates to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-44000881.translation: To our Lord Constantius, Pius, Invictus, Felix, Augustus.
EDCS-44000881.summary: This is an inscription honoring Emperor Constantius Caesar, likely dating to the Tetrarchic period (late 3rd to early 4th century AD).

EDCS-44000882.translation: To our Lord Constantius Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-44000882.summary: This is an inscription dedicating something to Emperor Constantius Caesar Augustus, likely dating to the Tetrarchic period (late 3rd to early 4th century AD).

EDCS-44000883.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus, Pius, Felix, Augustus.
EDCS-44000883.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Maximian, a co-emperor with Diocletian, likely dating to the Tetrarchic period (late 3rd to early 4th century AD).

EDCS-44000884.translation: Our Lord Emperor Caesar Maximinus, Invictus, Augustus.
EDCS-44000884.summary: This is an inscription honoring Emperor Maximinus Daza, likely dating to the Tetrarchic period (early 4th century AD).

EDCS-44000885.translation: To Emperor Gaius Valerius Licinius, Invictus, Pius, Felix, Augustus.
EDCS-44000885.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Licinius, a co-emperor with Constantine the Great, likely dating to the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-44000886.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Decima Gemellia lived 40 years.
EDCS-44000886.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Decima Gemellia, recording her lifespan of 40 years, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-44000887.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus, Invictus, Pius, Felix, Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul for the second time.
EDCS-44000887.summary: This is an imperial dedication or titulature for Emperor Probus, who reigned from 276 to 282 AD.

EDCS-44000889.translation: To our lord the most noble Emperor Caesar Constantius, prince of youth, unconquered, pious, fortunate Augustus, consul, proconsul.
EDCS-44000889.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantius, likely on a public monument, praising his titles and virtues.

EDCS-44000890.summary: This is a very brief inscription, possibly a dedication or a curse, to someone named Philadelphus.

EDCS-44000898.translation: Dedicated to the gods for the safety of Publius Flavius Pomponianus Pudentis and Caelia Procilla.
EDCS-44000898.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made for the health of two individuals, Publius Flavius Pomponianus Pudentis and Caelia Procilla.

EDCS-44000899.translation: lius Quietianus dedicated this by a vow from his military service.
EDCS-44000899.summary: This inscription records a vow made bylius Quietianus, likely after completing his military service.

EDCS-44000900.translation: Publius Cuculnius Quadratianus Pius, son of Marcus, of the Arnensian tribe, lived 11 months and 2 days; he is buried here.
EDCS-44000900.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy named Publius Cuculnius Quadratianus Pius, indicating his age at death and burial.

EDCS-44000902.translation: For the son of the Pious Fortunate Augusti and Marcia Otacilia Severa Augusta, and for the entire divine family, the state of the colony of Thamugadi.
EDCS-44000902.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication by the colony of Thamugadi, honoring the imperial family, possibly under the reign of Gordian III.

EDCS-44000903.translation: For Pompeia Fortunata and Quintus Plotius Victorinus.
EDCS-44000903.summary: This inscription is a dedication or memorial for Pompeia Fortunata and Quintus Plotius Victorinus.

EDCS-44000904.translation: Aurelia Matrona, daughter of Titus, lived 30 years.
EDCS-44000904.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelia Matrona, stating her age at death.

EDCS-44000905.translation: To Mars Augustus for the safety of our lords the Emperors Severus and Antoninus Augusti and Geta Caesar.
EDCS-44000905.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Mars Augustus for the well-being of the imperial family, specifically mentioning Emperors Severus, Antoninus, and Geta, placing it in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-44000906.translation: To the genius of the III Augusta legion, Antonius Flavianus.
EDCS-44000906.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the genius of the III Augusta legion by Antonius Flavianus, likely a soldier.

EDCS-44000909.translation: Cerelius Florentinus, Marius Ulpianus, Iulius Iulianus, Numitorius Tertius, Valerius Liberalis, Iulius Ianuarius, Aurelius Firmus, Iulius Saturninus, Grunius Victor, Lucius Calpurnianus, Pompeius Celer, Iulius Felix, Aurelius Optatus, Vibius Albosus, Aurelius Vitalis, Gelasinius Nepos, Lucretius Thubunius, Perelius, Lucius Aurelius Romanus.
EDCS-44000909.summary: This inscription lists a series of Roman personal names, possibly of individuals honored or associated with a particular monument or event.

EDCS-44000910.summary: This inscription lists two personal names, Attius Augustalis and Pescennius Ianuarius, possibly as dedicants or individuals honored.

EDCS-44000911.summary: This inscription contains fragmented personal names, likely referring to an individual named Hospitalis, possibly part of a dedication or funerary text.

EDCS-44000912.translation: Aelia Saturnina lived for 80 years and is buried here. Her son made and dedicated it.
EDCS-44000912.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the life span of Aelia Saturnina and stating that her son erected and dedicated the monument.

EDCS-44100002.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Marcia Fortunata lived for 30 years.
EDCS-44100002.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the spirits of the departed, recording the lifespan of Marcia Fortunata.

EDCS-44100012.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus [ordered the construction of the] baths.
EDCS-44100012.summary: This inscription fragment likely records Emperor Caesar Marcus as the one who ordered the construction of baths, probably in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-44100014.translation: [...] Hilarus, Flavius Germanus, Iulius Felix, Caius Antonius Ampliatus of Theveste, Marcus Homullius Lucanus, Bisi, Lucius Antonius Restutus of Carthage, Caius Arrius Rogatus, Caius Badius Saturninus of Theveste, Caius Iulius Maximus of Castra, Caius Secundius Primus of Tubursicumbursuc, Tannonius Secundus of Castra, Caius Caecilius Romanus of Tipasa, cohort IIII, Marcus Aurelius Sedatus, Furnius Iacius Namphamo of Castra, Pudens of Castra, Rufus of Castra, Valens of Theveste, Victor of Theveste, maessor Assuribus Faustus of Theveste, Cirta II, Marcus Fuficius, Titus Flavius Priscus, Marcus Silius Urbanus, Marcus Aemilius Felix, Caius Cerellius Donatus, Caius Faianius Felix, Quintus Aemilius Sarnianus, Quintus Geminius Crescens, Marcus Seius Aelianus, Caius Lusattus Tiro, Caius Annius Saturninus, Caius Sittius Saturninus, Caius Motilius Negotianus, Caius Caecilius Castus, Caius Attius Fabullianus, Titus Flavius Rusticus, Caius Sittius Silvanus, Caius Iulius Faustus, Quintus Glicius Fabricius, Caius Iulius Maximus, Titus Iulius Paulinus, Caius Caelius Felix Maximus of Castra, [...] of Theveste, [...] of Castra, [...] of Carthage, [...] of Carthage, bucinator of Castra, immunis, Cuiculo.
EDCS-44100014.summary: This long inscription lists numerous personal names, likely members of military units or officials associated with various cities, including Theveste, Carthage, and Castra, possibly related to a dedication or a list of benefactors.

EDCS-44100197.translation: [...] the consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, for the 17th time.
EDCS-44100197.summary: This fragmented inscription refers to a high-ranking official, possibly a governor or imperial legate, holding multiple titles including consul and father of the fatherland, with a reference to a regnal year or a count of seventeen.

EDCS-44100200.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Commodus lived for 50 years, ecimi et s[...].
EDCS-44100200.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the spirits of the departed, recording the lifespan of Commodus and mentioning an incomplete phrase, possibly related to his family or cause of death.

EDCS-44100203.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Marcus Annius Hyginus lived for 30 years. Clodia [his wife/dedicator].
EDCS-44100203.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the spirits of the departed, recording the lifespan of Marcus Annius Hyginus and mentioning Clodia, likely his wife or the dedicator of the monument.

EDCS-44200046.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Magnius Victor, priest, lived 85 years.
EDCS-44200046.summary: A funerary dedication to the Dis Manes for Quintus Magnius Victor, a priest, with his lifespan recorded.

EDCS-44200047.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Caius Muthunis, priest of Saturn, lived 75 years.
EDCS-44200047.summary: A funerary dedication to the Dis Manes for Caius Muthunis, a priest of Saturn, indicating his age at death.

EDCS-44200048.translation: To the god Saturn. Caius Iulius Victor, son of Rogatus, priest of Saturn.
EDCS-44200048.summary: An inscription dedicating a priest, Caius Iulius Victor, to the god Saturn.

EDCS-44200051.translation: Here lies lia Emerita. Her mother placed this.
EDCS-44200051.summary: A tombstone for lia Emerita, erected by her mother.

EDCS-44200052.translation: May this be good, fortunate, and happy. Sacred to the great, nocturnal lord Sanctus Saturninus: Marcus Cossutius Mar voluntarily fulfills a vow for his son Donatus, soul for soul, life for life, blood for blood, a substitute ram.
EDCS-44200052.summary: A votive inscription to Saturn, where Marcus Cossutius Mar dedicates a substitute offering for his son Donatus, likely in fulfillment of a vow for protection or healing.

EDCS-44200053.translation: To Faustina Augusta, wife of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, father of the fatherland, dedicated by Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus, imperial legate with the power of a praetor, consul-designate, patron of the town, with funds from the public treasury, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-44200053.summary: A public dedication to Empress Faustina, wife of Marcus Aurelius, commissioned by the governor Decimus Fonteius Frontinianus and authorized by the local council.

EDCS-44200054.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quinti Iuli I[---] lived 50 years, 1 month. He made this for himself while alive, and he [---]
EDCS-44200054.summary: A funerary monument made by Quinti Iuli I[---] for himself during his lifetime, recording his age at death.

EDCS-44200340.translation: For good things, good. Fortunatus made this for Christ Curucutus.
EDCS-44200340.summary: A dedication by Fortunatus to 'Christ Curucutus', possibly a Christian deity or figure.

EDCS-44200350.summary: A fragmentary inscription intended as a memorial for someone named Hermetica.

EDCS-44200353.summary: A tombstone recording the lifespan of Getula, who lived 28 years.

EDCS-44200354.translation: To Liboso, slave of Quintianus, who served well, by order of his master.
EDCS-44200354.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a slave named Liboso, erected by his master.

EDCS-44200355.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Titus Cocleius Maximus lived 40 years. Iulia Urbana made this for her husband; he is buried here.
EDCS-44200355.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titus Cocleius Maximus, erected by his wife Iulia Urbana.

EDCS-44200356.translation: To our lord Emperor Caesar Constantine Invictus Augustus, the state of the colony of Thamugadi.
EDCS-44200356.summary: This is an honorific inscription from the city of Thamugadi to Emperor Constantine.

EDCS-44200357.translation: To Gaius Fulvius Plautus Hortensianus, son of Gaius Fulvius Plautus, son of Quintus Plautianus, a most distinguished man and prefect of the Praetorium and confidant of our lords.
EDCS-44200357.summary: This is an inscription honoring Gaius Fulvius Plautus Hortensianus, likely part of a larger monument.

EDCS-44400132.translation: To the most blessed era of our lords Diocletian and Maximian Augusti, and Constantius and Maximian Nobilissimi Caesares, the temple of the god Mercury, which had been converted into ruins through the neglect of the times, was restored to its former state with porticoes and new public works were established, by order of the most perfect man Valerius Florus, the most perfect president of the province of Numidia Militiana, under the supervision of Iulius Lambessius, curator of the state.
EDCS-44400132.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to Mercury by the governor of Numidia Militiana, Valerius Florus, during the reign of Diocletian and Maximian, ordered during a period of neglect and executed by Iulius Lambessius.

EDCS-44400133.translation: Sacred to Ceres Augusta. Claudia Polla, priestess, made the altar with her own money.
EDCS-44400133.summary: This is a dedication of an altar to Ceres Augusta, funded by the priestess Claudia Polla.

EDCS-44400134.translation: Sacred to Arminia Paulla, daughter of Gaius.
EDCS-44400134.summary: This is a dedication, likely funerary, to Arminia Paulla.

EDCS-44400135.translation: Publius Clodius Licinianus, quaestor, and his wife Egnatia Rufina made this in memory of their sons Clodius Saprullicus, Licinianus, and Rufinus, and for themselves and their descendants.
EDCS-44400135.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Publius Clodius Licinianus and Egnatia Rufina for their sons and for themselves.

EDCS-44400136.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely honoring Emperor Severus.

EDCS-44400138.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, father of the country.
EDCS-44400138.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Philip the Arab, listing his titles.

EDCS-44400139.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Severus, 19 miles.
EDCS-44400139.summary: This is a milestone marking a distance of 19 miles to Emperor Caesar Marcus Severus.

EDCS-44400144.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Julius Sorex lived 50 years. Aemilia Optatula, his wife, made this for him as he deserved.
EDCS-44400144.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aemilia Optatula for her husband Julius Sorex, who lived 50 years.

EDCS-44400145.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Annius Venustus lived 10 years.
EDCS-44400145.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Annius Venustus, who died at the age of 10.

EDCS-44400146.translation: Iulia Fortunata lived 70 years. Pitta, her sister.
EDCS-44400146.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Iulia Fortunata at age 70, set up by her sister Pitta.

EDCS-44400148.summary: This is a very brief inscription, possibly a boundary marker or ownership record, identifying 'Calucus' as belonging to the local area.

EDCS-44400149.translation: To Flavius Iulius Caesar Augustus, our Lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-44400149.summary: This is a dedication from an unknown dedicator to Emperor Constantine (Constantinus Nobilissimus Caesar) and the Augusti.

EDCS-44400151.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Leo tianus at the age of 20.

EDCS-44400152.translation: Archilinus with his family, three measures.
EDCS-44400152.summary: This inscription may record a land measurement or a division of property for Archilinus and his household.

EDCS-44400156.summary: This is a simple ownership inscription, likely referring to property or a monument belonging to Marcus Novius Justus.

EDCS-44400161.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Sextus, soldier of the Fourth "Felicis" Legion, with 26 years of service, lived 50 years. Flavius Restutus, his heir, had this made for him as he deserved.
EDCS-44400161.summary: This is a funerary monument for Titus Flavius Sextus, a soldier with 26 years of service in the Fourth Legion, erected by his heir Flavius Restutus.

EDCS-44400321.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus.
EDCS-44400321.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Maximinus Thrax, likely from the period 235-238 CE.

EDCS-44900006.translation: Sacred to the Invincible Hercules. For the safety and victory of the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti, and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar, and Julia Augusta, mother of the Augustus. Publius Aelius Menecrates dedicated this as a gift from his fatherland in honor of Saturninus, his sister.
EDCS-44900006.summary: This is a religious dedication to Hercules for the well-being of the emperors Severus, Caracalla, Geta, and Julia Domna, made by Publius Aelius Menecrates, likely during the reign of Severus (198-211 CE).

EDCS-44900008.translation: Sacred to the Invincible Hercules. For the safety and victory of the Emperors Caesars Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti, and Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar, Julia Augusta, mother of Augustus. Publius Aelius Menecrates dedicated this as a gift from his fatherland in the name of his sister's son.
EDCS-44900008.summary: This is a religious dedication to Hercules for the well-being of the emperors Severus, Caracalla, Geta, and Julia Domna, made by Publius Aelius Menecrates, likely during the reign of Severus (198-211 CE).

EDCS-44900034.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Victoricus lived 18 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-44900034.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Victoricus, recording his age at death.

EDCS-44900072.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Audenia Salsa lived 110 years; Aemilius and Felix set this up.
EDCS-44900072.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Audenia Salsa, recording her great age and mentioning the dedicants, Aemilius and Felix.

EDCS-44900073.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. C. Caecina lived 25 years; her mother, pious, made this for her when she was a virgin, Fortunata, from her own property.
EDCS-44900073.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for C. Caecina, recording her age at death and noting that it was erected by her mother, Fortunata.

EDCS-44900074.translation: The Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul-designate for the second time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. The Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul-designate, father of the fatherland, proconsul for the thirteenth time.
EDCS-44900074.summary: This inscription records the full titles of Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and his son Volusianus, likely from their joint reign around 251-253 CE.

EDCS-44900075.translation: By decree of the Emperor Caesar of Trajan Hadrian Augustus, father of the fatherland. C. S. A. P.
EDCS-44900075.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription referencing Emperor Hadrian and likely a public works or dedication, dated to his reign (117-138 CE).

EDCS-44900079.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Alfidius Felix, veteran, lived 65 years; his heirs, while he was still alive, made this for themselves.
EDCS-44900079.summary: This tombstone inscription records the life and death of Alfidius Felix, a veteran, and notes that it was erected by his heirs during his lifetime.

EDCS-44900080.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caecilius Victor, a veteran, lived 60 years. His sons made this for their dearest father.
EDCS-44900080.summary: This funerary epitaph records Caecilius Victor, a veteran who lived 60 years, and was erected by his sons.

EDCS-44900081.translation: Caius Iunius Fuscus, a veteran, placed this for his dearest wife.
EDCS-44900081.summary: This funerary monument was erected by the veteran Caius Iunius Fuscus for his beloved wife.

EDCS-44900083.translation: To Titus, son of Agr[---] Aelia, and to Ru[---], father [---]
EDCS-44900083.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records dedications to deceased family members, possibly a son named Titus and a father named Ru[---].

EDCS-44900084.summary: This brief inscription is a dedication for 'Secunda, mother'.

EDCS-44900085.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Aurelia Lanpuitana.
EDCS-44900085.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the spirits of the underworld by Aurelia Lanpuitana.

EDCS-44900086.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caius Longeius Celsus lived 30 years. Pompeia Fortunata made this for her husband.
EDCS-44900086.summary: This epitaph records Caius Longeius Celsus, who lived 30 years, and was made by his wife Pompeia Fortunata.

EDCS-44900087.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Geminius Silvanus, 80 years old. Gemina Sabina, his devoted daughter, made this for her father.
EDCS-44900087.summary: This funerary inscription honors Lucius Geminius Silvanus, who lived 80 years, and was erected by his daughter Gemina Sabina.

EDCS-44900088.summary: This inscription marks the burial place of Terius Fortunatus.

EDCS-44900089.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. To Bryseis, his most cherished wife, Exuperans [made this]. She lived 30 years.
